1. An otherwise healthy 11-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her mother because of a 3-month history of right breast tenderness. She is at the 50th percentile for height and weight. Breast development is Tanner stage 1 on the left and Tanner stage 2 on the right. The left breast is flat and nontender, and the right breast is slightly raised, enlarged, and tender; there is no nipple discharge. There is no axillary or pubic hair. Pelvic examination shows normal-appearing external genitalia. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Reassurance  </br>b. Measurement of serum prolactin concentration  </br>c. Ultrasonography of the pelvis  </br>d. CT scan of the pituitary fossa  </br>e. Biopsy of the breast</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>a. Reassurance  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• Age 11 – within normal range for onset of female puberty (8–13 yrs).<br /></br>• Asymmetric breast development – Tanner 2 on the right (small mound/elevation of papilla), Tanner 1 on the left (flat).<br /></br>• Mild tenderness of the enlarging side – common with normal thelarche.<br /></br>• No nipple discharge, no axillary/pubic hair, otherwise healthy.<br /></br>Pattern: In early puberty one breast often “buds” first, may be tender, and the other side commonly lags by up to 6 months. Absence of other abnormal findings (mass, discharge, systemic signs) rules out pathology.  </p></br><p>Test‐taking mindset: Any breast finding in a peripubertal girl must first be judged against normal pubertal sequence. If age-appropriate and no red-flag symptoms → physiologic → reassure rather than order tests.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step 1 – Is thelarche age-appropriate? Yes (normal window 8–13 yrs).<br /></br>Step 2 – Are other pubertal milestones present? No pubic/axillary hair yet → still early.<br /></br>Step 3 – Are there pathologic signs (mass, fixation, skin changes, discharge, systemic endocrine signs)? None.<br /></br>Therefore: Physiologic unilateral breast budding. The correct management is simple reassurance and routine follow-up.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Early, asymmetric, and sometimes tender breast budding is a normal variant in girls beginning puberty; no work-up is indicated unless red flags appear.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Puberty begins with activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis → pulsatile GnRH → ↑LH/FSH → ovarian estrogen production.<br /></br>• Thelarche (breast budding, Tanner 2) is often the first visible sign, preceding pubarche/menarche.<br /></br>• Breast tissue on each side may respond to estrogen at different rates → temporary asymmetry and tenderness.<br /></br>• Tenderness results from rapid ductal and stromal proliferation.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics Review  </p></br><p>No diagnostic studies are needed.<br /></br>Counseling points to document:<br /></br>1. Normal variant; expected contralateral development within months.<br /></br>2. Return if hard fixed mass, nipple discharge, skin dimpling, rapid enlargement, or systemic symptoms develop.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“PUBERTY starts with a BUD”:<br /></br>B – Breast bud (thelarche)<br /></br>U – Usually Unilateral first<br /></br>D – Discomfort common<br /></br>→ If age 8–13 and only Bud present, Don’t order tests, just reassure.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Serum prolactin – Indicated for galactorrhea, headaches, visual changes, amenorrhea; none present here.  </p></br><p>c. Pelvic ultrasound – Used to look for estrogen-producing ovarian tumors or to evaluate precocious puberty. Single-breast budding without systemic estrogen effect does not suggest peripheral estrogen excess.  </p></br><p>d. CT of pituitary fossa – Work-up for central precocious puberty or pituitary mass (e.g., adenoma). Patient shows no signs (no pubic hair, no neurological symptoms).  </p></br><p>e. Breast biopsy – Reserved for suspicious masses (hard, fixed, non-tender, skin changes) or concerning imaging. A tender, soft, mobile Tanner-stage bud in an 11-year-old is physiologic.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line:<br /></br>Unilateral, tender breast budding in an 11-year-old girl is a normal part of pubertal development. Provide reassurance—no tests or procedures are necessary.</p>
2. A 13-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her mother because of a 10-day history of vaginal spotting. This is her first episode of vaginal bleeding. She has never been sexually active. She has mild asthma treated with an inhaled β-adrenergic agonist as needed. She is at the 75th percentile for height and 80th percentile for weight and BMI. Breast and pubic hair development are Tanner stage 4. The external genitalia are normal. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Follow-up examination in 6 months</br>b. Measurement of serum follicle-stimulating hormone concentration</br>c. Vaginal ultrasonography</br>d. CT scan of the head</br>e. Oral contraceptive therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Follow-up examination in 6 months  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Age/sexual maturity: 13-year-old, Tanner stage 4 breasts &amp; pubic hair → hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian (HPO) axis is still maturing; expected age for menarche is 11–15 yr.<br /></br>• Bleeding description: single, light (“spotting”) 10-day episode; this is her <em>first</em> menstrual bleed (menarche).<br /></br>• No red flags: not sexually active (pregnancy, STI, trauma unlikely); no heavy bleeding, anemia, weight loss, galactorrhea, hirsutism, thyroid symptoms, headaches, visual changes, or signs of coagulopathy.<br /></br>Key Diagnostic Clue → A solitary, mild first episode of bleeding in an otherwise healthy peripubertal girl = normal menarche/anovulatory bleeding.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Ask: “Is this pathologic or normal puberty?”<br /></br>2. Look for red-flag symptoms or exam findings that would mandate work-up.<br /></br>3. If none → reassure &amp; observe.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Menarche can begin with several months of irregular, painless, anovulatory spotting because the HPO axis has not yet synchronized GnRH, LH, FSH, and estrogen/progesterone surges.<br /></br>2. In a patient who is otherwise healthy and within the normal pubertal age range, watchful waiting is appropriate.<br /></br>3. Re-examine in 6 months (or sooner if bleeding becomes heavy, frequent, or symptomatic).  </p></br><p>Take-home concept: Up to the first 2–3 years after menarche, irregular or light bleeding is physiologic. Management = reassurance ± scheduled follow-up.  </p></br><p>Documentation bullets for a real encounter:<br /></br>• Pubertal stage; characteristics of bleeding episode; absence of pregnancy risk or systemic disease; counseling given; plan for interval follow-up.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Early adolescence → intermittent, <em>anovulatory</em> cycles:<br /></br>• GnRH pulses are still irregular → LH &amp; FSH surges are inconsistent.<br /></br>• No ovulation → no corpus luteum → inadequate progesterone → unopposed estrogen → random endometrial sloughing (“spotting”).<br /></br>Typically self-resolves as cycles become ovulatory within ~2 years.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>No immediate tests or pharmacologic therapy needed. If future visits reveal:<br /></br>• Heavy/iron-deficiency bleeding → CBC, TSH, coagulation tests, ± combined OCPs or tranexamic acid.<br /></br>• Secondary amenorrhea &gt;3 months, galactorrhea, or hirsutism → serum β-hCG, TSH, prolactin, FSH/LH, and pelvic US or MRI as indicated.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“2 P’s after menarche”<br /></br>• Physiologic irregular bleeding is common for “2 years Post-menarche” → Provide reassurance.<br /></br>Visualize a traffic light turning green after 2 years = “OK to go” if no red flags.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>B. Serum FSH<br /></br>• Used to evaluate ovarian failure or central hypogonadism in <em>primary</em> amenorrhea or prolonged secondary amenorrhea. Our patient has already bled; no amenorrhea yet.  </p></br><p>C. Vaginal ultrasonography<br /></br>• Looks for structural uterine/ovarian pathology (polyps, fibroids, pregnancy). Not indicated with a benign first menses and normal exam.  </p></br><p>D. CT scan of the head<br /></br>• Consider intracranial lesion if headaches, visual changes, or <em>absence</em> of menses (amenorrhea). Here, menarche has occurred; CT (MRI preferred anyway) is unnecessary.  </p></br><p>E. Oral contraceptive therapy<br /></br>• Combined OCPs are used for cycle regulation or heavy painful menses. Premature in a single, light, physiologic first bleed; exposes patient to unnecessary hormones.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Irregular or light bleeding during the first 2–3 years after menarche is usually normal anovulatory physiology. Reassure and schedule a routine follow-up visit.</p>
3. A 7-month-old boy is brought to the physician because of a 4-day history of persistent fever. He was born at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery; there was prolonged bleeding following circumcision. At the age of 3 months, he developed eczema over the face and elbows that now involves the entire body. He has also had frequent episodes of thrush and otitis media and was recently discharged following treatment of pneumococcal bacteremia. His current temperature is 38.5 C (101.3 F), pulse is 120/min, and respirations are 32/min. Examination shows a diffuse eczematous rash with areas of purulent drainage. Cervical lymph nodes are movable and range in size from 1.5 to 2 cm. There are white plaques over the oral cavity. The liver edge and spleen tip are palpated 2 cm below the costal margin. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin               10 g/dL</br>Leukocyte count          5000/mm^3</br>Segmented neutrophils    60 percent</br>Lymphocytes              30 percent</br>Monocytes                10 percent</br>Platelet count           35,000/mm^3</br>Serum</br>IgA                      100 mg/dL</br>IgG                      400 mg/dL</br>IgM                      15 mg/dL</br>IgE                      60 IU/mL</br>Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Adenosine deaminase deficiency</br>b. Ataxia telangiectasia</br>c. HIV infection</br>d. Hyper-IgE syndrome</br>e. Thymic-parathyroid dysplasia (DiGeorge syndrome)</br>f. Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome</br>g. X-linked agammaglobulinemia</br>h. X-linked lymphoproliferative disease</br>i. X-linked severe combined immunodeficiency</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>f. Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome (WAS)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Sex &amp; Age: 7-month-old boy → think of X-linked disorders that present in infancy.<br /></br>2. Bleeding after circumcision + platelet count 35 000/mm³ → micro-thrombocytopenia.<br /></br>3. Diffuse eczema beginning at 3 mo → chronic eczematous rash.<br /></br>4. Recurrent sinopulmonary &amp; opportunistic infections (otitis media, thrush, pneumococcal bacteremia).<br /></br>5. Immunoglobulins: very low IgM (15 mg/dL), relatively preserved/↑ IgA &amp; IgE → classic WAS pattern.<br /></br>6. Mild hepatosplenomegaly &amp; lymphadenopathy → chronic immune activation.</p></br><p>Pattern: “Eczema + thrombocytopenia + recurrent infections” in a male infant = Wiskott-Aldrich.</p></br><p>Test-taking pearl: When you see bleeding diathesis (esp. post-circumcision) together with eczema, your first thought should be WAS; confirm with low IgM.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise approach:</br>1. Identify life-long bleeding tendency → search for platelet disorders.<br /></br>2. Superimposed eczema suggests immune dysregulation rather than isolated platelet defect.<br /></br>3. Add recurrent infections → combined B- &amp; T-cell dysfunction.<br /></br>4. Check immunoglobulin pattern: low IgM with normal/↑ IgA &amp; IgE is highly specific for WAS.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Wiskott-Aldrich is an X-linked mutation (WAS gene) causing defective actin polymerization in hematopoietic cells → impaired platelet production/size and defective T-cell signaling → triad of thrombocytopenia, eczema, recurrent infections.</p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): bleeding history, skin exam, infection history, CBC with platelet size, quantitative immunoglobulins, referral for hematology/immunology &amp; HSCT evaluation.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Gene: WAS on X-chromosome → defective WASp protein.<br /></br>• Mechanism: impaired actin cytoskeleton re-arrangement →<br /></br>  – Platelets: produced small &amp; destroyed prematurely → quantitative &amp; qualitative defect.<br /></br>  – Lymphocytes: poor immunologic synapse → decreased T-cell/B-cell cooperation.<br /></br>• Immunoglobulins: ↓ IgM, normal/↑ IgA &amp; IgE, normal/low IgG.<br /></br>• Complications: encapsulated-organism infections, opportunistic fungi/viruses, autoimmune disease, lymphoma.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Management (high-yield)<br /></br>Definitive: Hematopoietic stem-cell transplant (HSCT).<br /></br>Supportive:<br /></br>• Prophylactic antibiotics &amp; IVIG infusion.<br /></br>• Platelet transfusions or splenectomy for severe bleeding (temporary).<br /></br>• Avoid live vaccines.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>Mnemonic “WATER”:<br /></br>W – Wiskott<br /></br>A – (platelet) Abnormalities (thrombocytopenia)<br /></br>T – T-cell dysfunction → infections<br /></br>E – Eczema<br /></br>R – Recurrent pyogenic infections</p></br><p>Think “WAITER” (adds Autoimmunity &amp; Encapsulated organisms).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Adenosine deaminase deficiency (SCID): Severe combined immunodeficiency presents earlier with profound lymphopenia and ALL immunoglobulins low; no thrombocytopenia or eczema.</p></br><p>b. Ataxia telangiectasia: Hallmark is progressive cerebellar ataxia and telangiectasias; IgA deficiency common, but platelets normal and bleeding absent.</p></br><p>c. HIV infection: Can cause thrush and infections, but does not explain neonatal bleeding or thrombocytopenia + characteristic Ig pattern; eczema not typical.</p></br><p>d. Hyper-IgE (Job) syndrome: Very high IgE (&gt;2000 IU/mL), coarse facies, “cold” Staph abscesses, retained primary teeth; platelets normal.</p></br><p>e. Thymic-parathyroid dysplasia (DiGeorge): T-cell deficiency, hypocalcemic tetany, conotruncal heart defects; platelets / IgM normal, no eczema triad.</p></br><p>g. X-linked agammaglobulinemia (Bruton): Absent B-cells → all immunoglobulins low, but platelet count normal and no eczema.</p></br><p>h. X-linked lymphoproliferative disease: Usually healthy until fulminant EBV infection in older child; platelet count normal before that.</p></br><p>i. X-linked severe combined immunodeficiency: Early severe infections &amp; lymphopenia with very low IgG/A/M/E; again, no platelet defect or eczema.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Male infant with thrombocytopenia (bleeding), eczema, and recurrent infections + ↓ IgM → Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome; curative therapy is HSCT.</p>
4. A 59-year-old woman with a 5-year history of ulcerative colitis comes to the office to discuss pharmacotherapy options. Her condition has been progressively less responsive to oral prednisone therapy. She also has Ehlers-Danlos syndrome and asthma. Other medications are mesalamine, albuterol, and tiotropium. The patient is allergic to sulfonamide drugs. She appears pale and has cushingoid facies; she is not in acute distress. Temperature is 36.9 C (98.4 F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 110/60 mm Hg. Lungs are clear to auscultation. Cardiac examination discloses a grade 2/6 systolic ejection murmur heard best at the upper left sternal border; the murmur does not radiate. Abdomen is mildly distended and tender to palpation. There is 1+ edema of the lower extremities. Results of laboratory studies are shown:</br>Blood</br>  Hematocrit                   36 percent</br>  Hemoglobin                   11.0 g/dL</br>  WBC                          13,000/mm3</br>    Neutrophils, segmented     60 percent</br>    Eosinophils                1 percent</br>    Lymphocytes                36 percent</br>    Monocytes                  2 percent</br>    Basophils                  1 percent</br>  Platelet count               197,000/mm3</br>The most appropriate pharmacotherapy for the patient is being considered. Which of the following is most appropriate for this patient to undergo before beginning this medication?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Chest x-ray</br>b. Dental examination</br>c. Echocardiography</br>d. Sulfonamide desensitization</br>e. Tuberculosis screening</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer: E.  Tuberculosis screening (PPD or interferon-γ release assay)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• “Ulcerative colitis … progressively less responsive to oral prednisone” → moderate-to-severe, steroid-dependent disease that now requires escalation to a steroid-sparing agent.<br /></br>• Mesalamine failure + steroid dependence → next step is a biologic anti-TNF-α agent (e.g., infliximab, adalimumab, golimumab) or another systemic immunosuppressant.<br /></br>• Cushingoid facies, edema, leukocytosis → chronic steroid adverse effects—further highlighting the need to transition off prednisone.<br /></br>• Allergy to sulfonamides rules out sulfasalazine, pushing physician toward anti-TNF therapy.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On exam, once you see “IBD not controlled with 5-ASA ± steroids,” immediately think biologic. Then ask, “What do I need to do before anti-TNF?” → screen for latent TB (and Hep B).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1 – Identify the drug class about to be started.<br /></br>• 5-year UC, steroid dependence → anti-TNF-α monoclonal antibody.</p></br><p>Step 2 – Recall obligatory pre-treatment tests for that class.<br /></br>• Anti-TNF drugs impair macrophage activation and granuloma maintenance → high risk of reactivating latent Mycobacterium tuberculosis.<br /></br>• Therefore, CDC/ACG guidelines: screen for latent TB with PPD or IGRA ± chest X-ray, and treat latent infection before first infusion/injection.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Any time you see “biologic anti-TNF” or “etanercept/infliximab/adalimumab” in a question stem, think “PPD first.”</p></br><p>Documentation (real world):  • TB screening results and, if positive, plan for isoniazid (± rifapentine) therapy prior to biologic initiation.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• TNF-α is crucial for formation and maintenance of granulomas that wall off mycobacteria.<br /></br>• Blocking TNF-α (monoclonal antibodies or fusion proteins) dismantles granulomas, allowing dormant bacilli to proliferate → fulminant miliary or extrapulmonary TB.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Test Review<br /></br>First-line escalation for moderate-severe UC unresponsive to steroids:<br /></br>1. Anti-TNF-α monoclonal antibodies: infliximab, adalimumab, golimumab.<br /></br>2. Integrin antagonist (vedolizumab) or JAK inhibitor (tofacitinib) if anti-TNF fails.<br /></br>Pre-therapy screening:<br /></br>• Latent TB (mandatory)<br /></br>• Chronic hepatitis B and C (recommended)<br /></br>• Up-to-date vaccines (live vaccines contraindicated once therapy starts)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“ANTI-TNF can ‘BREAK’ the wall”:<br /></br>B – Baseline vaccines<br /></br>R – Reactivation of TB → PPD/IGRA first<br /></br>E – Evaluate for Hep B<br /></br>A – Avoid live vaccines<br /></br>K – Keep an eye on malignancy / infection risk</p></br><p>Visual: Picture a brick wall (granuloma). Anti-TNF bulldozer knocks it down, releasing “TB ghosts.” Always scan (screen) the wall before driving the bulldozer.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Chest X-ray – Not routinely required before anti-TNF. You would get a chest X-ray only if PPD/IGRA is positive or the patient has pulmonary symptoms, or before amiodarone/methotrexate (pulmonary toxicity concern).<br /></br>B. Dental examination – Necessary before IV bisphosphonates (to prevent osteonecrosis of the jaw), not for anti-TNF therapy.<br /></br>C. Echocardiography – Indicated before anthracycline (doxorubicin) or trastuzumab chemotherapy because of cardiomyopathy risk; not for biologic therapy in IBD.<br /></br>D. Sulfonamide desensitization – Would be relevant if sulfasalazine were required. However, sulfasalazine contains a sulfonamide moiety and is contraindicated in sulfa-allergic patients; the physician would simply choose an alternative drug (e.g., anti-TNF), not attempt desensitization.<br /></br>E. Tuberculosis screening – Correct, mandatory prior to initiating anti-TNF-α biologic therapy to prevent latent TB reactivation.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Key Takeaway<br /></br>Before starting any anti-TNF-α agent, always screen for latent tuberculosis (PPD or IGRA).</p>
5. A 27-year-old man with sickle cell disease is admitted to the hospital because of a 2-day history of increasingly severe pain in his shoulders, back, and shins. During the past 6 months, he has been hospitalized three times for treatment of painful crises unresponsive to outpatient therapy with hydrocodone. His temperature is 37.2 C (99 F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 108/52 mm Hg. The lungs are clear to auscultation. Examination shows tenderness over the shoulders, back, and shins. His hematocrit is 30 percent, and reticulocyte count is 11 percent. In addition to pain control, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Bone scan</br>b. Antibiotic therapy</br>c. Intravenous administration of fluids</br>d. Blood transfusion</br>e. Plasmapheresis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>C. Intravenous administration of fluids  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key identity clue: “27-year-old man with sickle cell disease” → think vaso-occlusive (pain) crisis.<br /></br>• Precipitants: pain for 2 days, tenderness over long bones, shoulders, back; no focal infection signs; afebrile (T = 37.2 °C).<br /></br>• Baseline hemolysis labs: Hct 30 % (≈ patient’s baseline), retic 11 % (compensatory).<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: clear lungs, stable vitals, no high fever, no swelling/redness that would suggest osteomyelitis or septic arthritis.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When a sickle-cell vignette presents bone pain without evidence of infection, the default first-line triad is 1) analgesia, 2) aggressive IV hydration, 3) oxygen as needed. Ask yourself: “Is there anything here that forces me away from the standard crisis protocol?” If not, choose fluids.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Why fluids?<br /></br>1. Vaso-occlusion is triggered/worsened by intravascular dehydration → ↑ HbS concentration → polymerization/sickling.<br /></br>2. IV isotonic fluids lower blood viscosity, improve microvascular flow, and help flush polymerized cells.<br /></br>3. Hydration is a universal part of inpatient management; it complements opioid analgesia already being given.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: In an uncomplicated vaso-occlusive crisis, after adequate analgesia, give IV normal saline (or D5½NS if glucose is needed) at 1–1.5× maintenance.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: “Patient placed on IV NS at 125 mL/h to maintain euvolemia and decrease sickling risk.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Mutation: β-globin Glu6Val → HbS polymerizes when deoxygenated, especially with dehydration, acidosis, hypoxia, or cold.<br /></br>• Polymer → RBC sickling → microvascular occlusion → ischemic bone pain.<br /></br>• Hydration dilutes HbS and improves red-cell transit time.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Overview for a Pain Crisis<br /></br>1. Analgesia: IV opioids (e.g., morphine, hydromorphone).<br /></br>2. Hydration: IV isotonic fluids.<br /></br>3. Oxygen if SpO₂ &lt; 92 %.<br /></br>4. Search for triggers (infection, dehydration) and treat accordingly.<br /></br>5. Adjuncts: NSAIDs if renal function OK, incentive spirometry to prevent acute chest, consider hydroxyurea long-term.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“POH” for sickle crisis basics:<br /></br>P – Pain control (opioids)<br /></br>O – Oxygen if needed<br /></br>H – Hydration (IV fluids)  </p></br><p>Visual: Imagine sickled RBCs “unsickling” and floating freely in a river after a rainstorm (IV fluid bolus).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong<br /></br>A. Bone scan – Used to detect osteomyelitis or avascular necrosis. Those present with focal tenderness, fever, or chronic pain, not a symmetric acute crisis.<br /></br>B. Antibiotic therapy – Indicated only if infection suspected (fever &gt;38 °C, leukocytosis, source). Afebrile patient with benign exam doesn’t warrant empiric antibiotics.<br /></br>D. Blood transfusion – Reserved for severe anemia (Hct &lt;18 % or drop &gt;3 g/dL), acute chest syndrome, stroke, or aplastic crisis. This patient’s Hct 30 % and stable vitals do not justify transfusion.<br /></br>E. Plasmapheresis – Used for TTP, myasthenic crisis, hyperviscosity, not for sickle vaso-occlusion.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>Uncomplicated sickle-cell pain crisis = analgesia + aggressive IV hydration (and oxygen if hypoxic). Only escalate to antibiotics, imaging, or transfusion if additional red flags appear.</p>
6. A 72-year-old man comes to the emergency department because he has vomited blood three times during the past 12 hours. He has had dizziness during this period. He has a 5-year history of arthritis treated with daily ibuprofen. Over the past 4 weeks, he has increased his daily dose of ibuprofen because of worsening joint pain but is not sure how many pills he has been taking. He has been unable to take his daily walks because of the joint pain. He has not had chest pain. He had an uncomplicated myocardial infarction 10 years ago. He appears pale. His temperature is 37.5 C (99.5 F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 90/60 mm Hg. Examination shows dried blood near the mouth. The lungs are clear to auscultation. Heart sounds are normal. The stool is black, and test for occult blood is positive. His hematocrit is 22 percent, and platelet count is 215,000/mm³. Upper endoscopy shows hemorrhagic gastritis with no active site of bleeding. Thirty minutes after fluid resuscitation with crystalloid solution and transfusion of 4 units of crossmatched packed red blood cells, the patient has shortness of breath. Diffuse rhonchi and crackles are heard bilaterally. Which of the following is the most likely cause of the dyspnea?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. ABO incompatibility</br>b. Acute respiratory distress syndrome</br>c. Aspiration of gastric contents</br>d. Fluid overload</br>e. Pulmonary embolism</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Fluid overload (transfusion-associated circulatory overload, TACO)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Strategy</br>1. Key clinical clues<br /></br>   • Massive, very rapid resuscitation: “crystalloid solution and transfusion of 4 units of packed RBCs” completed within ≈30 min.<br /></br>   • Elderly patient (72 y) with possible decreased cardiac reserve (remote MI, limited activity).<br /></br>   • Acute onset dyspnea immediately after the transfusion; lung exam: “diffuse rhonchi and crackles bilaterally.”<br /></br>2. Cognitive road map<br /></br>   • Ask: “What new intervention happened just before the respiratory compromise?” → large-volume infusion.<br /></br>   • In an elderly or cardiac patient, rapid volume expansion → hydrostatic pulmonary edema → crackles/rhonchi.<br /></br>   • Timing (minutes) rules out slower processes such as ARDS or pulmonary embolism.<br /></br>   • For future questions: after any transfusion, create a mental differential of acute transfusion complications (TACO, TRALI, hemolytic reaction, bacterial contamination) and match the timeline + predominant clinical feature (volume overload vs non-cardiogenic edema vs hemolysis vs sepsis).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-by-step linkage to the correct answer:<br /></br>1. Rapid infusion of large fluid volume.<br /></br>2. Elderly patient with likely impaired LV compliance → cannot accommodate sudden preload ↑.<br /></br>3. Hydrostatic pressure rises in pulmonary capillaries → pulmonary edema.<br /></br>4. Exam: crackles/rhonchi, acute SOB within minutes.<br /></br>5. Therefore, most likely cause = fluid overload/TACO.</p></br><p>Take-home message: In the setting of massive, rapid transfusion—especially in the elderly or those with cardiac disease—think TACO (cardiogenic pulmonary edema) presenting with acute dyspnea, bilateral rales, possible S3, and often hypertension.</p></br><p>What would be documented: time &amp; amount of fluids/blood given, vital-sign change, lung findings, oxygen saturation, diuretic administration, response to therapy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• TACO = volume-mediated cardiogenic pulmonary edema.<br /></br>  – Risk factors: age &gt;65, pre-existing heart or renal disease, high infusion rate (&gt;15 mL/kg/h), total volume &gt;2 units without diuretics.<br /></br>  – Mechanism: excessive intravascular volume → ↑LV end-diastolic pressure → ↑pulmonary venous &amp; capillary hydrostatic pressure → transudation of fluid into alveoli.<br /></br>• Contrast with TRALI (transfusion-related acute lung injury) → non-cardiogenic edema due to donor anti-leukocyte antibodies; presents within 6 h but usually with hypotension, fever, and no signs of volume overload.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Management</br>Immediate:<br /></br>1. Stop transfusion.<br /></br>2. Sit patient upright, give supplemental O2.<br /></br>3. IV loop diuretic (e.g., furosemide 20–40 mg).<br /></br>4. Monitor vitals, consider CXR (vascular congestion, bilateral alveolar infiltrates with cardiomegaly).<br /></br>Prevention: slower transfusion rate, split units, prophylactic diuretics in high-risk patients.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Mnemonic</br>“TACO stuff-the-tank”<br /></br>T – Too much<br /></br>A – Acute<br /></br>C – Cardiogenic<br /></br>O – Overload<br /></br>…occurs when you “stuff the tank” too fast.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>a. ABO incompatibility (acute hemolytic reaction)<br /></br>   • Presents with fever, chills, flank/back pain, hemoglobinuria, hypotension, DIC—NOT pulmonary crackles.</p></br><p>b. Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS / TRALI variant)<br /></br>   • ARDS from sepsis/bleeding occurs hours–days later; TRALI within 6 h but is non-cardiogenic, often with fever &amp; hypotension and no volume overload history.</p></br><p>c. Aspiration of gastric contents<br /></br>   • Requires witnessed vomiting/aspiration; usually unilateral focal crackles, not diffuse bilateral findings immediately after transfusion.</p></br><p>e. Pulmonary embolism<br /></br>   • Sudden dyspnea with clear lungs or localized wheeze; crackles are uncommon, and timing to transfusion is coincidental.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tip</br>“TACO is about TOO MUCH fluid; TRALI is from an immunologic ‘attack’ on the Lungs.”<br /></br>Picture a taco shell (heart) overstuffed with fluid spilling into the lungs (pulmonary edema).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>In an elderly patient who receives large, rapid blood and crystalloid infusions, sudden dyspnea with bilateral crackles is most consistent with fluid overload/TACO—not with immunologic transfusion reactions, aspiration, ARDS, or PE.</p>
7. Six hours after admission to the hospital for management of a seizure secondary to hyponatremia, a 53-year-old man with schizophrenia is found to have iron deficiency anemia. He says he has been drinking 1 gallon of water daily during the past 2 months. He is receiving haloperidol in the hospital. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. His fluid balance has normalized. His psychosis has improved, and he consents to colonoscopy, which shows a large tumor in the ascending colon. When the physician mentions cancer, the patient immediately says, “I don't have cancer; I stopped drinking a lot of water after I was admitted to the hospital.” Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's response?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Dementia</br>b. Denial</br>c. Rationalization</br>d. Reaction formation</br>e. Regression</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Denial  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key phrases: “large tumor in the ascending colon,” “when the physician mentions cancer, the patient immediately says, ‘I don’t have cancer.’”<br /></br>• Pertinent positive: Clear pathologic finding (colon tumor) → very high likelihood of malignancy.<br /></br>• Pertinent negative: No cognitive deficits or delirium on exam; psychosis improved; patient is oriented and consents to colonoscopy.<br /></br>• Pattern: Abrupt, unqualified refusal to accept an anxiety-provoking reality despite unequivocal evidence = classic USMLE buzz-phrase for denial.<br /></br>Metacognitive tip: When a patient flat-out rejects the existence or consequences of a new diagnosis (esp. cancer, HIV, pregnancy, substance use), first think “denial,” especially if there is no intellectualization or elaborate excuse.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Reality: Biopsy-proven or endoscopically visualized colon mass.<br /></br>2. Patient’s reaction: Immediate refusal to accept (“I don’t have cancer”).<br /></br>3. Defense mechanism definitions:<br /></br>   • Denial – subconscious refusal to accept painful reality.<br /></br>   • Rationalization – creating a logical but false explanation for a reality one is willing to acknowledge.<br /></br>   • Reaction formation – transforming an unacceptable impulse into its opposite.<br /></br>   • Regression – reverting to earlier developmental behavior.<br /></br>4. The patient does NOT supply an alternative logical explanation (“colonic polyps are common, maybe benign”); he simply negates the fact. Therefore, denial fits best.<br /></br>Take-home message: Pure negation of reality = denial.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: “Patient verbally rejects the cancer diagnosis; demonstrates denial, a common initial coping response. Will provide additional education, allow time for processing, and consider psych support.”  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (Defense Mechanisms)<br /></br>• Denial is a primitive (immature) defense mechanism.<br /></br>• Operates unconsciously to block external realities that cause overwhelming anxiety.<br /></br>• Commonly first response to catastrophic illness (cancer, MI, HIV).  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Management<br /></br>• Short term: Empathic acknowledgment (“I hear this is difficult news”) + reiteration of facts.<br /></br>• Offer repeat discussion, involve support system, and consider psychiatric consult if persistent denial interferes with care.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“DENy the DENger” – when reality is too dangerous to accept, patients DENy.<br /></br>Hierarchy:<br /></br>D – Disaster news<br /></br>E – Evidence ignored<br /></br>N – “No, not me” immediate response  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Dementia – Would show chronic cognitive decline: memory loss, disorientation, aphasia; none are present.<br /></br>c. Rationalization – Patient would admit cancer but attribute it to something “logical” (“I knew this would happen because I smoked in college”). Here he flatly rejects the fact.<br /></br>d. Reaction formation – Requires adoption of the opposite attitude (e.g., over-optimistic enthusiasm about cancer screening while secretly fearful). Not seen.<br /></br>e. Regression – Would manifest as childlike behavior (thumb-sucking, temper tantrum, demanding parental attention). Not present.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>Immediate, categorical refutation of a frightening medical reality—without alternative explanation—signals the primitive defense mechanism of denial. Recognizing defenses helps anticipate patient responses and tailor communication accordingly on the wards and the exam.</p>
8. A 25-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of itching over her arms that has become worse during the past 2 months. She attributes the itching to small worms, which she reports having seen appear from under her skin intermittently. On several occasions, she has "dug them out" with a clean needle. She has brought a small box containing samples of her dry skin and debris. She reports no history of depressed mood or auditory hallucinations. There is no personal or family history of serious illness. The patient takes no medications and does not use illicit drugs. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows mildly inflamed lesions over the upper extremities with scabbing and excoriations. Examination of the samples she provides shows skin, scabs, and cotton fibers. On mental status examination, she has an anxious mood and constricted affect. Her speech is normal in rate and tone, and her thought processes are organized and coherent. She insists that she has parasites in her skin. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Bipolar disorder</br>b. Delusional disorder</br>c. Generalized anxiety disorder</br>d. Major depressive disorder with psychotic features</br>e. Schizophrenia</br></br></br><p>Correct answer selected by user: B.  Delusional disorder (somatic type)   </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Time course – ≥ 6 months of symptoms → meets DSM-5 duration criterion for delusional disorder (≥ 1 month).<br /></br>• Core symptom – Fixed, false, non-bizarre belief of “worms” living under the skin. Patient is so convinced that she excoriates the skin and brings “specimens” (classic “matchbox sign”).<br /></br>• Absence of other psychotic features – No hallucinations, disorganized speech/behavior, or negative symptoms. Thought process is coherent.<br /></br>• Mood – No manic, hypomanic, or major depressive episode reported.<br /></br>• Functioning – Aside from the skin picking, she is not globally impaired.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive pearl: When you see 1) a single, well-systematized false belief, 2) intact cognition/affect, and 3) ≥ 1 month duration, immediately think “delusional disorder.” The subtype is based on content; here it is somatic (a.k.a. delusional parasitosis/Ekbom disease).  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)<br /></br>1. Identify the prominent symptom → belief of infestation.<br /></br>2. Check duration → 6 months.<br /></br>3. Screen for mood episodes or multiple psychotic symptoms → none.<br /></br>4. Assess level of functioning → largely preserved.<br /></br>5. Apply DSM-5 criteria → single delusion for &gt; 1 month + otherwise normal → delusional disorder.  </p></br><p>Key takeaway: A solitary, entrenched delusion in a high-functioning individual without other psychotic signs = delusional disorder.  </p></br><p>Documentation note: Detail the content of the delusion, patient’s insight (poor), associated behaviors (skin picking), level of distress, and absence of additional psychotic or mood symptoms.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology is unclear; thought to involve dopaminergic dysfunction in mesolimbic pathways.<br /></br>• Somatic subtype often centers on infestation, malodor, or body deformity.<br /></br>• “Matchbox sign” – patient brings skin debris/fibers as “proof.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment<br /></br>Diagnostics: Primarily clinical. Rule out stimulant use, dementia, thyroid disease, B12 deficiency, and true parasitic infection if history suggests.  </p></br><p>Treatment:<br /></br>1. First-line – Second-generation antipsychotics (e.g., risperidone, olanzapine). Pimozide historically used but has QT-prolongation risk.<br /></br>2. Establish therapeutic alliance; patients usually lack insight and may refuse psychiatric referral.<br /></br>3. Treat skin lesions (topical antibiotics, wound care) and consider SSRI if comorbid anxiety/OC traits.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>• “Bugs in the Box”: Patient brings a box of “bugs” → B for Box = B for Belief = Delusional disorder.<br /></br>• 1 delusion, 1 month, otherwise normal = “1-1-Normal” rule for delusional disorder.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Bipolar disorder<br /></br>– Requires ≥ 1 manic or hypomanic episode. No elevated mood, decreased need for sleep, flight of ideas, etc. Psychotic features in bipolar are mood-congruent and occur during mood episodes.  </p></br><p>C. Generalized anxiety disorder<br /></br>– Characterized by excessive, uncontrollable worry about multiple domains for ≥ 6 months, plus restlessness, muscle tension, irritability. Does not produce fixed delusional beliefs.  </p></br><p>D. Major depressive disorder with psychotic features<br /></br>– Would need ≥ 2 weeks of depressive symptoms (SIGECAPS) plus mood-congruent psychosis. Patient denies depressed mood; affect is only constricted/anxious.  </p></br><p>E. Schizophrenia<br /></br>– Requires ≥ 2 of the following for ≥ 1 month: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized/catatonic behavior, negative symptoms. Patient has only one positive symptom and otherwise normal affect/cognition.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>A solitary, persistent belief of skin infestation with normal speech, cognition, and behavior is classic for delusional disorder, somatic type. Treat with antipsychotics and gentle engagement.</p>
9. A 15-year-old boy is brought to the physician by his parents for an examination prior to participating on the school basketball team. Five weeks ago, he had a 1-week history of fever, sore throat, and diffuse muscle aches. Since then, he has had decreased energy and needs at least 10 hours of sleep every night. While the parent's parents are out of the room, the patient states that he does not want to play basketball, but his parents are forcing him to join the team. He is an honor student but failed an examination last week. He does not drink alcohol, but he smokes marijuana occasionally; he does not use any other illicit drugs. He has been sad and angry since his girlfriend broke up with him 3 weeks ago after she told him that he was "too moody and not fun to be around anymore." His pulse is 88/min, and blood pressure is 120/72 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he has a sad mood and a full range of affect. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Reassurance</br>b. Assessment for suicide risk</br>c. Serologic testing for Epstein-Barr virus</br>d. Thyroid function tests</br>e. Urine toxicology screening</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>B. Assessment for suicide risk  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>Key clues pointing toward a depressive episode:<br /></br>• 3-week history of depressed/sad mood after a stressor (girlfriend broke up).<br /></br>• Loss of interest/pleasure (does not want to play basketball, formerly engaged).<br /></br>• Decreased energy, hypersomnia (needs ≥10 h sleep).<br /></br>• Cognitive difficulty (failed an exam).<br /></br>• Functionally significant change (drop in grades, family conflict).  </p></br><p>In a question stem describing possible major depressive disorder (MDD) in an adolescent, the MOST urgent issue is ALWAYS personal safety. USMLE/NBME items reward recognizing that a safety assessment (suicidal thoughts, intent, plan, means) must precede any diagnostic tests, medications, or “watch-and-wait.”  </p></br><p>Metacognitive pearl: Whenever you see depression (SIGECAPS) in a teenager, immediately ask yourself “Could this patient be suicidal?”—that is almost always step 1 in management.</p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Identify depressive symptoms ≥2 weeks ⇒ possible MDD.<br /></br>2. First management step in MDD = evaluate risk of self-harm or harm to others.<br /></br>3. Only after safety is addressed do you order labs (TSH, CBC), screen for substances, or start therapy/medication.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Safety trumps diagnostics.  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• MDD involves dysregulation of monoamine neurotransmitters (serotonin, NE, dopamine).<br /></br>• Adolescents often present with irritable or labile mood, sleep/appetite changes, academic decline.<br /></br>• Suicide is the 2nd leading cause of death in ages 10-24; recent breakup, substance use, and declining performance increase risk.</p></br><p>Treatments &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review<br /></br>Sequential approach after suicide screen:<br /></br>1. If positive risk → create safety plan, possible hospitalization, involve parents.<br /></br>2. Complete assessment (TSH, CBC, CMP, urine tox) to rule out mimics.<br /></br>3. Evidence-based therapies: CBT (cognitive-behavioral therapy) and/or SSRI (fluoxetine is FDA-approved first-line in adolescents).  </p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“SAD TEENS can S*elf-harm”<br /></br>S – Sleep change<br /></br>A – Anhedonia<br /></br>D – Dysphoria<br /></br>T – Trouble concentrating<br /></br>E – Energy low<br /></br>E – Eating/appetite change<br /></br>N – No fun (anhedonia)<br /></br>S – Suicide screen FIRST  </p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Reassurance – Downplays potential MDD and ignores safety; inappropriate before risk assessment.<br /></br>c. Serologic testing for EBV – Fatigue after a viral illness is possible, but the cardinal mood/behavior changes make depression more likely and still require suicide screening first.<br /></br>d. Thyroid function tests – Hypothyroidism can mimic depression, but again, safety assessment comes first; TFTs are done later if indicated.<br /></br>e. Urine toxicology screening – Occasional marijuana use is noted, but substance screening is second-tier; does not address immediate risk of self-harm.  </p></br><p>Bottom line: With any adolescent displaying depressive symptoms, ALWAYS assess for suicidality before anything else.</p>
10. A 16-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his mother 30 minutes after a 4-minute episode of generalized shaking of his body. One hour ago, the patient's friend brought him home after a party, at which time, the patient exhibited unusual behavior and said she was a superhero with the ability to fly. The patient subsequently became unresponsive and the episode began. The friend reports that he saw the patient "smoke weed" at the party 2 hours before the episode. On arrival, the patient is diaphoretic and difficult to arouse. His temperature is 37.0 C (98.6 F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 135/95 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 99 percent. The pupils are 6 mm and sluggishly reactive to light. There is horizontal nystagmus. Use of which of the following substances is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Diazepam</br>b. Ethanol</br>c. Marijuana</br>d. Methamphetamine</br>e. PCP (phencyclidine)</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>E. PCP (phencyclidine)</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • “Smoked weed” ➜ Street preparation could be marijuana laced with another drug (“wet” cigarettes).<br /></br>   • Bizarre behavior/ grandiose hallucination (“I’m a superhero and can fly”).<br /></br>   • Post-episode generalized tonic-clonic seizure.<br /></br>   • Vital-sign pattern: tachycardia (120), hypertension (135/95), diaphoresis.<br /></br>   • Neurologic exam: dilated (6-mm) pupils + horizontal nystagmus.<br /></br>   • Difficult to arouse yet not markedly respiratory-depressed (SpO₂ 99%, RR 24).</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives and negatives  </p></br></li></br><li>Horizontal nystagmus (classic for PCP; can be horizontal ± vertical)  </li></br><li>Psychosis/agitation with relative analgesia (patient had a seizure but no complaint of pain)<br /></br>   – No conjunctival injection or “munchies” commonly seen with isolated cannabis use.<br /></br>   – Not profoundly sedated or bradypneic as with ethanol/benzodiazepines.<br /></br>   – Stimulant-like vitals but nystagmus separates PCP from pure amphetamine/cocaine.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: In any tox question, the combination of violent psychosis + nystagmus (especially horizontal or rotatory) should immediately put PCP at the top of the differential.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Identify the pathognomonic sign → nystagmus.  </li></br><li>Match vitals (HTN, tachycardia) &amp; neuro findings (dissociative hallucinations, seizure) with known toxidrome.  </li></br><li>Recognize that PCP is often smoked with marijuana, explaining the friend’s observation.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Horizontal/vertical nystagmus + psychosis/violence + sympathetic surge = PCP intoxication.</li></br></ol></br><p>Documentation bullets (real patient): mental status, eye findings, vitals trend, evidence of trauma (often pain-insensitive), need for chemical/physical restraints, administration of benzodiazepines.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• PCP = dissociative anesthetic; NMDA receptor (glutamate) antagonist → cortical sensory dissociation, hallucinations, analgesia.<br /></br>• Increases dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin reuptake inhibition → sympathetic overdrive (HTN, tachycardia).<br /></br>• Lowers seizure threshold; can precipitate rhabdomyolysis, hyperthermia.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><p>First-line:<br /></br>1. Benzodiazepines (e.g., lorazepam) for agitation &amp; seizures.<br /></br>2. Cooling and IV fluids for hyperthermia/rhabdo if present.<br /></br>3. Minimize external stimulation; consider haloperidol only if severe psychosis unresponsive to benzos (may worsen seizures).<br /></br>Diagnostics: CMP (CK, renal function), CPK for rhabdo, urine tox screen (PCP detectable ≥ 4–7 days).</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“PCP = Pupils wide, Crazy strength, Paddling eyes (nystagmus).”<br /></br>Alternate: “Angel dust makes eyes DUST off side-to-side.”</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Diazepam (benzodiazepine)<br /></br>   • Produces CNS depression, ataxia, slurred speech, normal-to-low BP/HR, miosis or normal pupils.<br /></br>   • Horizontal nystagmus and sympathetic surge are NOT typical. Seizures occur in withdrawal, not intoxication.</p></br><p>B. Ethanol<br /></br>   • Similar to benzos: sedation, ataxia, dysarthria, possible nystagmus but with bradycardia/hypotension, not HTN/tachycardia. Grandiose hallucinations/seizures uncommon at this blood level in a 16-year-old 2 h after ingestion.</p></br><p>C. Marijuana<br /></br>   • Findings: conjunctival injection, mild tachycardia, increased appetite, dry mouth, possible paranoia.<br /></br>   • Does NOT cause severe hypertension, dilated pupils, nystagmus, or seizures in typical doses.</p></br><p>D. Methamphetamine<br /></br>   • Sympathetic hyperactivity (HTN, tachycardia, agitation, mydriasis) fits, but nystagmus is absent.<br /></br>   • Hallucinations are more persecutory, not dissociative; seizures less common acutely.</p></br><p>E. PCP (Correct)<br /></br>   • Only option that explains the triad: violent psychosis, nystagmus, autonomic instability ± seizures.<br /></br>   • Often co-smoked with marijuana (“wet” joints).</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>Horizontal or vertical nystagmus + violent, dissociative behavior + sympathetic vitals = PCP intoxication; treat primarily with benzodiazepines and supportive care.</p>
11. A 38-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her husband 30 minutes after she sustained a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. Her husband reports that she has had worsening headache during the past 2 days and had the gradual onset of confusion 1 hour prior to the seizure. The patient has no history of serious illness. Her only medication is an oral contraceptive. She has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 20 years. On arrival, she is sleepy but arousable. Her temperature is 37.8 C (100 F). Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she is oriented to person but not to place or time. She is distractible and recalls zero of three objects after 5 minutes. A T2-weighted MRI of the brain is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Brain biopsy</br>b. Cerebral angiography</br>c. EEG</br>d. Intracranial MR venography</br>e. Lumbar puncture</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer (as provided by user): e. Lumbar puncture  </p></br><hr /></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues:<br /></br>  – Subacute prodrome: 2-day “worsening headache” → infectious or inflammatory rather than vascular rupture.<br /></br>  – Mild fever (37.8 °C) → supports infectious etiology.<br /></br>  – New-onset confusion, disorientation, distraction, poor recall → encephalopathy.<br /></br>  – Generalized tonic-clonic seizure → cortical irritation.<br /></br>  – No focal neurologic deficits on exam.<br /></br>  – T2-weighted brain MRI image (classic USMLE stem): in HSV encephalitis the picture shows hyperintense signal in one or both medial temporal lobes/insulae.<br /></br>  – Risk factors listed (OCP, smoking) are red herrings to make you think of venous sinus thrombosis; always match the IMAGE with clinical course.  </p></br><p>Approach tip: When a stem supplies an MRI, FIRST decide what the picture is showing. If you recognize medial-temporal hyperintensity, immediately think “HSV encephalitis → lumbar puncture for CSF PCR” and start acyclovir empirically.</p></br><hr /></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Diagnose the process: encephalitis (behavioral changes + seizures + fever).<br /></br>2. Most common/sporadic encephalitis in adults = HSV-1.<br /></br>3. Characteristic MRI: asymmetric T2/FLAIR hyperintensity or swelling in the temporal lobes/insula.<br /></br>4. Gold-standard diagnostic test = HSV PCR of cerebrospinal fluid.<br /></br>5. Therefore next step in work-up → obtain CSF via lumbar puncture (while simultaneously starting IV acyclovir—management point, though not asked).  </p></br><p>Take-home message: For suspected HSV encephalitis, lumbar puncture with HSV PCR is mandatory and should not be delayed by other imaging once mass-effect has been excluded.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls if this were a real patient: mental-status findings, seizure description, imaging result, informed consent for LP, empirical acyclovir initiation.</p></br><hr /></br><p>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)<br /></br>• HSV-1 reactivation in trigeminal ganglion → travels to limbic structures.<br /></br>• Necrotizing, hemorrhagic inflammation primarily of medial temporal lobes and inferior frontal lobes.<br /></br>• Results in fever, headache, focal deficits, personality change, seizures.<br /></br>• CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, ↑protein, normal glucose, and RBCs from hemorrhagic necrosis.<br /></br>• Untreated mortality ≈70 %; early acyclovir decreases mortality to &lt;20 %.</p></br><hr /></br><p>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics Snapshot<br /></br>1. Emergent therapy: IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8 h as soon as HSV suspected—do NOT wait for PCR.<br /></br>2. Diagnostic confirmation: CSF HSV-1 DNA PCR (sensitivity &amp; specificity &gt;95 %).<br /></br>3. EEG may show periodic lateralized epileptiform discharges but is NOT diagnostic.<br /></br>4. Follow-up MRI after therapy if clinical course atypical.  </p></br><hr /></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Temporal lobe + Fever + Seizure = Think ‘Herpes — Tap it!’”<br /></br>(Temporal → Herpes; Tap → lumbar puncture).<br /></br>Picture a giant thermometer jammed into the temporal lobe to remember fever + temporal involvement + LP.</p></br><hr /></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Brain biopsy<br /></br>   – Reserved for suspected tumor, vasculitis, or unknown encephalitis after exhaustive non-invasive studies. Too invasive; PCR on CSF is virtually diagnostic for HSV.</p></br><p>b. Cerebral angiography<br /></br>   – Used for aneurysm/SAH work-up or vasculitis. MRI did not show subarachnoid blood or large-vessel narrowing, and patient’s presentation is subacute, not thunderclap.</p></br><p>c. EEG<br /></br>   – Helpful adjunct (may show periodic discharges in HSV), but it is not confirmatory and would not replace CSF PCR. Seizure etiology must still be identified.</p></br><p>d. Intracranial MR venography<br /></br>   – Evaluates cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (CVST). CVST risk factors include OCPs and smoking, but CVST usually produces severe headache, papilledema, focal deficits, and MRI/CT signs of venous infarct or hemorrhage; the provided MRI instead shows temporal-lobe parenchymal changes characteristic of HSV.</p></br><hr /></br><p>Bottom Line<br /></br>Temporal-lobe abnormalities on MRI in a febrile, confused adult with seizures = HSV encephalitis. Next diagnostic step is lumbar puncture for CSF HSV PCR while starting empiric IV acyclovir.</p>
12. A 54-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department because she was unable to move her right side or speak when she awoke this morning. Her husband reports that he found her in bed with these symptoms 25 minutes ago, when he returned home from working an overnight shift. He says she understood what he was saying to her but was unable to respond. He says she felt well the night before. He adds that she fainted three times during the past 2 years. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She appears to be in distress. She seems to understand what is said to her but is unable to respond. She is 165 cm (5 ft 5 in) tall and weighs 82 kg (180 lb); BMI is 30 kg/m². Her temperature is 36.4 C (97.5 F), pulse is 80/min and regular, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 142/89 mm Hg. Examination shows weakness of the right lower aspect of the face. Muscle strength is 3/5 in the right extremities. Babinski sign is present on the right. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. MRI of the brain shows an infarct in the anterior branches of the left middle cerebral artery. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Alteplase</br>b. Aspirin</br>c. Dabigatran</br>d. Heparin</br>e. Warfarin</br></br></br><p>Correct answer (per key): B. Aspirin  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>VIGNETTE ANALYSIS<br /></br>• “Found … when he returned home from working an overnight shift” → the exact time she became symptomatic is unknown; the LAST‐KNOWN-WELL time is the night before.<br /></br>• Right face/arm &gt; leg weakness + expressive aphasia → dominant-hemisphere MCA distribution (confirmed by MRI).<br /></br>• No current evidence of atrial fibrillation (pulse 80 regular) or other cardio-embolic source identified.<br /></br>• Blood pressure &lt; 185/110 mm Hg (so no need for emergent BP lowering).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>Step 1 – Establish stroke type with imaging.<br /></br>Step 2 – Determine eligibility for reperfusion (tPA and/or thrombectomy) by LAST-KNOWN-WELL time.<br /></br>Step 3 – If not eligible, initiate secondary prevention (antiplatelet, statin, risk-factor control).</p></br><p>Here, the patient is beyond the 4.5-hour IV alteplase window → move directly to antiplatelet therapy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>CLINICAL CRITICAL REASONING (why aspirin?)<br /></br>1. Guideline-directed therapy: For an acute ischemic stroke NOT treated with IV alteplase, give aspirin 160–325 mg within 24 hours of presentation (American Heart Association/American Stroke Association).<br /></br>2. Aspirin irreversibly inhibits platelet cyclooxygenase-1 → ↓thromboxane A2 → prevents further platelet aggregation and limits extension of the thrombus.<br /></br>3. Early aspirin reduces recurrent stroke and improves neurological outcome with a very low risk of hemorrhagic conversion relative to anticoagulation.<br /></br>Take-home: “Unknown or &gt;4.5 h onset = no tPA → give aspirin ASAP (after a hemorrhage has been excluded on imaging).”</p></br><p>Documentation pearls: note last-known-well time, NIH Stroke Scale, imaging results, inclusion/exclusion criteria for tPA, and time aspirin was administered.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>PATHOPHYSIOLOGY REVIEW<br /></br>• Large-artery thrombotic (often atherosclerotic or in situ thrombosis of MCA branch) ischemic stroke.<br /></br>• Neurons become irreversibly injured within minutes; penumbra can be salvaged with reperfusion or at least protected from further thrombosis with antiplatelet agents.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>TREATMENT &amp; DIAGNOSTICS SUMMARY<br /></br>Acute phase (no IV tPA):<br /></br>1. Aspirin 325 mg chewed → then 81 mg daily.<br /></br>2. High-intensity statin (e.g., atorvastatin 40–80 mg) once dysphagia screen passed.<br /></br>3. BP permissive up to 220/120 mm Hg unless other indications.<br /></br>4. Evaluate for long-term secondary prevention: carotid imaging, echo, telemetry for occult AF.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>MEMORIZATION TIPS<br /></br>“TIME” mnemonic:<br /></br>T – Time last normal → &lt; 4.5 h = tPA; &gt; 4.5 h = no tPA.<br /></br>I – Image to rule out bleed.<br /></br>M – Meds: if no tPA → M = Mouthful of aspirin.<br /></br>E – Evaluate for endovascular therapy (6-24 h window in selected large-vessel occlusions).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>INCORRECT ANSWER ANALYSIS<br /></br>a. Alteplase (tPA): Requires symptom onset ≤ 4.5 h (or up to 6 h in some extension trials) from last-known-well. Here onset is unknown (likely &gt; 8 h). Giving tPA markedly increases intracerebral hemorrhage risk without proven benefit.<br /></br>c. Dabigatran: Direct thrombin inhibitor used for long-term stroke prevention in non-valvular atrial fibrillation or venous thromboembolism. Not indicated acutely and no current evidence of AF.<br /></br>d. Heparin (unfractionated or LMWH): Anticoagulation in the hyper-acute phase of ischemic stroke has not shown benefit and increases risk of hemorrhagic transformation. Reserved for specific indications (e.g., venous sinus thrombosis, moving DVT/PE, mechanical valves).<br /></br>e. Warfarin: Long-term oral anticoagulation for AF, mechanical valves, hypercoagulable states. Takes days to achieve therapeutic INR; not useful acutely and increases bleeding risk.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Core takeaway:<br /></br>If ischemic stroke patient is NOT eligible for IV thrombolysis, give aspirin within 24 hours after excluding intracranial hemorrhage.</p>
13. A previously healthy 47-year-old man comes to the emergency department because he has been unable to close his right eye during the past 4 hours. He noticed the problem while shaving this morning. During breakfast, he had difficulty closing his mouth to chew and could not taste his food. His temperature is 37.2 C (99 F), pulse is 84/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 132/80 mm Hg. Examination of the face shows a loss of the right labial fold. There is no movement of the right side of the mouth when the patient is asked to smile, and he is unable to wrinkle the forehead on the right. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. CT scan of the head with contrast</br>b. MRI of the cervical spine</br>c. Lumbar puncture</br>d. Electromyelography and nerve conduction studies</br>e. No further testing is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. No further testing is indicated  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• “Unable to close his right eye … difficulty closing his mouth … could not taste” → isolated unilateral facial muscle weakness plus loss of anterior-two-thirds taste = CN VII (facial nerve) lesion.<br /></br>• “Unable to wrinkle the forehead on the right” → lower-motor-neuron pattern; a cortical (upper-motor-neuron) stroke would spare the forehead because of bilateral cortical innervation.<br /></br>• Onset “during the past 4 hours,” no fever, trauma, rash, ear pain, parotid mass, or neurologic deficits elsewhere → classic presentation of idiopathic Bell palsy.<br /></br>• Age 47, previously healthy → common demographic; no red-flag signs to suggest tumor, stroke, otitis media, Lyme disease, or Ramsay-Hunt syndrome.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When you see isolated unilateral LMN facial paralysis that involves BOTH the forehead and lower face and begins abruptly, immediately think “Bell palsy—no tests needed, start steroids.” Ask yourself: “Is anything else wrong neurologically?” If the answer is no, you’re done.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Identify CN VII palsy pattern (forehead + lower face + taste).<br /></br>2. Screen for exclusion criteria (bilateral involvement, other cranial neuropathies, vesicular ear rash, stroke symptoms, parotid mass, trauma). None are present → idiopathic Bell palsy.<br /></br>3. Guidelines (AAFP, AAN) state that routine imaging or labs are unnecessary in an uncomplicated, first-episode Bell palsy. Therefore, “No further testing” is the appropriate next step.  </p></br><p>Chart-worthy items if this were a real patient: complete neuro exam documentation, absence of vesicles/ear pathology, counsel on prognosis, initiate prednisone, recommend ocular protection.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Uncomplicated, acute, isolated LMN facial paralysis = Bell palsy → treat empirically; diagnostics reserved for atypical or recurrent cases.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Thought to be reactivation of latent HSV-1 in the geniculate ganglion → inflammation → edema → compression of CN VII in the facial canal.<br /></br>• Risk factors: diabetes, pregnancy, recent viral illness, family history.<br /></br>• 80–85 % experience full recovery within 3 months.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Treatment Snapshot (not asked but high-yield)<br /></br>1. Prednisone 60–80 mg/day × 5 days, then taper (start within 72 h).<br /></br>2. +/- Valacyclovir 1 g TID × 7 days (added benefit in some trials).<br /></br>3. Eye care: artificial tears, nighttime eye patch or taping to prevent exposure keratitis.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Bell’s PASTIC”<br /></br>P – Peripheral pattern (forehead + lower face)<br /></br>A – Acute onset<br /></br>S – Steroids first-line<br /></br>T – Taste loss anterior 2/3<br /></br>I – Idiopathic (HSV)<br /></br>C – Cannot close eye → protect Cornea  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Why Other Options Are Wrong<br /></br>A. CT head with contrast: Indicated for intracranial mass, abscess, hemorrhage, or stroke. Stroke would spare forehead; masses cause gradual, progressive deficits. None fit this vignette.<br /></br>B. MRI cervical spine: Used for spinal cord pathology, multiple sclerosis plaques, or high cervical tumors—irrelevant to isolated CN VII palsy.<br /></br>C. Lumbar puncture: Used for meningitis, encephalitis, Guillain-Barré (bilateral facial palsy), carcinomatous meningitis. No meningeal signs, fever, or bilateral involvement.<br /></br>D. Electromyography &amp; nerve conduction studies: Helpful in severe or recurrent palsy for prognostication, not required acutely and would not change immediate management.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Isolated, acute, unilateral LMN facial nerve paralysis without red flags = Bell palsy → treat empirically; no diagnostic tests needed.</p>
14. A 32-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department because of a 1-day history of worsening confusion. Two months ago, she underwent successful gastric bypass for morbid obesity, but she has not adhered to her physician’s instructions for health maintenance since she was discharged from the hospital. She has been taking no regular medications or supplements and has not followed the recommended diet. She has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 15 years, and she drinks two glasses of wine daily. On arrival, she is oriented to person but not to place or time. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows disconjugate gaze and marked nystagmus. Muscle strength is normal bilaterally. She cannot stand with her feet together; she stands unsteadily with her feet 12 inches apart. Mental status examination shows moderately impaired attention. She is admitted to the hospital, and medical and physical therapy are begun. Her condition improves, and she is reexamined 1 week later in preparation for discharge home. The patient agrees to take all necessary medications and follow the recommended diet. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to prevent future similar episodes in this patient?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Abstinence from alcohol</br>b. Adherence to a low-fat diet</br>c. Adherence to a low-protein diet</br>d. Vitamin B1 (thiamine) supplementation</br>e. Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin) supplementation</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Vitamin B1 (thiamine) supplementation  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Approach  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>   • 2 mo status-post gastric bypass with POOR diet/supplement compliance<br /></br>   • Acute/subacute neuro-psychiatric changes: confusion, impaired attention<br /></br>   • Ocular findings: disconjugate gaze, marked nystagmus (ophthalmoplegia)<br /></br>   • Gait problem: cannot stand with feet together; wide-based stance (ataxia)<br /></br>   • Rapid improvement after hospital admission (likely received IV thiamine)  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pattern recognition: Confusion + Ophthalmoplegia + Ataxia = classic Wernicke encephalopathy.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Cognitive roadmap for similar questions:<br /></br>   • Always look for the “CAN” triad: Confusion, Ataxia, Nystagmus (or any ophthalmoplegia).<br /></br>   • Search the stem for a precipitating setting of thiamine depletion (alcoholism, hyperemesis, bariatric surgery, prolonged IV dextrose without vitamins, anorexia).<br /></br>   • If the patient improves once hospitalized, assume they got empiric IV thiamine → prevention strategy is ongoing thiamine replacement.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-by-step:<br /></br>1. Bariatric surgery dramatically decreases oral intake and intestinal absorption (especially of water-soluble vitamins).<br /></br>2. Without prescribed daily multivitamin, thiamine stores (≈2–3 wk) are quickly exhausted.<br /></br>3. Neurons with high metabolic demand (mammillary bodies, medial thalamus, cerebellar vermis) develop energy failure → Wernicke encephalopathy.<br /></br>4. Hospital team likely corrected deficiency with IV thiamine → neurologic improvement.<br /></br>5. Recurrence is highly likely unless she receives lifelong oral thiamine. Therefore, the single best discharge intervention is daily vitamin B1 supplementation.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: ANY patient with Wernicke encephalopathy or at high risk (alcohol use disorder, bariatric surgery, hyperemesis, malnutrition) must receive continuous thiamine replacement; give BEFORE or WITH glucose.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Record neurologic findings, response to IV thiamine, discharge prescription for oral thiamine (100 mg PO daily) and inclusion in bariatric multivitamin regimen.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)  </p></br><p>• Thiamine (B1) → cofactor for PDH, α-ketoglutarate DH, branched-chain ketoacid DH, and transketolase.<br /></br>• Deficiency → impaired cerebral glucose utilization → lactate accumulation → neuronal necrosis in characteristic areas.<br /></br>• Manifestations:<br /></br>  – Wernicke (acute): CAN triad.<br /></br>  – Korsakoff (chronic): anterograde/retrograde amnesia, confabulation.<br /></br>• Precipitants: glucose load, malnutrition, alcohol, bariatric surgery.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Review  </p></br><p>Diagnosis: Clinical; MRI may show mamillary body/T2 changes but not required.<br /></br>Acute treatment: IV thiamine 100 mg before glucose; then daily until PO intake adequate.<br /></br>Long-term prevention: Oral thiamine 100 mg daily (typically as part of bariatric multivitamin).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“Wernicke CAN be Reversible with B-ONE.”<br /></br>C – Confusion<br /></br>A – Ataxia<br /></br>N – Nystagmus/ophthalmoplegia<br /></br>B-ONE = Vitamin B1  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Abstinence from alcohol<br /></br>   • Alcoholism is a COMMON cause, but here the root cause is malabsorption after gastric bypass. Moderate wine intake (2 glasses/day) is insufficient alone to explain the profound deficiency; stopping alcohol would not by itself prevent recurrence without thiamine replacement.  </p></br><p>b. Adherence to a low-fat diet<br /></br>   • Fat intake does not influence thiamine levels; low-fat diet would not correct or prevent deficiency.  </p></br><p>c. Adherence to a low-protein diet<br /></br>   • Protein restriction is unrelated; could even worsen postoperative nutrition.  </p></br><p>e. Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin) supplementation<br /></br>   • B12 deficiency after gastric bypass causes megaloblastic anemia, peripheral neuropathy, and subacute combined degeneration over months-years, NOT acute Wernicke triad. It should be monitored but would not prevent recurrence of thiamine-dependent encephalopathy.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Post-bariatric patients without adequate vitamin supplementation are at high risk for thiamine deficiency and Wernicke encephalopathy. Lifelong vitamin B1 supplementation is essential to prevent future episodes.</p>
15. An 8-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department by her father because of swelling of the right side of her neck. The father first noticed a small lump on her neck last week and says it seems larger today; it also has become discolored and now resembles a bruise. The patient has not had fever or changes in energy or appetite. She has had no recent infections or contact with anyone known to be ill. The family has not traveled outside of the United States, but they spend weekends on the patient's grandparents' ranch. The patient is at the 50th percentile for height, weight, and BMI. Vital signs are within normal limits. She appears well. Physical examination shows a 3 × 5-cm firm, mobile mass in the right anterior cervical chain region; there is no erythema and the mass is minimally tender. A purplish hue is noted over the mass There is no other lymphadenopathy, and no hepatomegaly or splenomegaly is noted. Excisional biopsy specimen of the mass shows the presence of multiple granulomas. Which of the following infectious agents is the most likely cause of this patient's condition?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Cytomegalovirus</br>b. Epstein-Barr virus</br>c. Haemophilus influenzae, type b</br>d. Mycobacterium avium-intracellulare</br>e. Staphylococcus aureus</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Mycobacterium avium-intracellulare (nontuberculous mycobacteria, “MAC”)</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• Child (8 y) with a 1-week history of a slowly enlarging, firm, mobile, minimally tender unilateral cervical node.<br /></br>• Overlying skin now has a violaceous/“bruise-like” discoloration.<br /></br>• Afebrile, no systemic symptoms, no diffuse lymphadenopathy, hepatomegaly, or splenomegaly.<br /></br>• Excisional biopsy: multiple granulomas.</p></br><p>How the clues narrow the differential<br /></br>1. Gradual onset + minimal tenderness → rules out typical pyogenic bacterial lymphadenitis (Staph/Strep).<br /></br>2.
