Unilateral node, violaceous skin, well-appearing child → classic for atypical (nontuberculous) mycobacterial cervicofacial lymphadenitis (“scrofula”).<br /></br>3. Granulomatous histology → strongly favors organisms handled by a Th1/cell-mediated response (mycobacteria, fungi).<br /></br>4. Age 1-5 y is most common, but older children can be affected, esp. with environmental exposure (soil/well water at grandparents’ ranch).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Anytime you see a well child with a single, firm cervical node + violaceous skin + granulomas, think “atypical mycobacteria.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why choice D is correct)</p></br><p>Step-wise approach<br /></br>• Presentation = chronic, subacute, unilateral cervicofacial lymphadenitis.<br /></br>• Histology = granulomas → intracellular pathogen.<br /></br>• Epidemiology = children; environmental water/soil exposure.<br /></br>• Mycobacterium avium-intracellulare complex (or M. scrofulaceum) is the prototypical cause.<br /></br>Take-home: Nontuberculous mycobacteria cause painless or minimally painful lymph node enlargement with violaceous skin and granulomatous inflammation.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient)<br /></br>HPI details, node characteristics, lack of systemic symptoms, environmental exposures; plan for complete excision (both diagnostic &amp; therapeutic) ± clarithromycin/azithromycin if incomplete resection.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review</p></br><p>• Nontuberculous mycobacteria (NTM) are ubiquitous in water/soil.<br /></br>• Infection usually occurs via ingestion or minor skin breaks → local lymph node seeding.<br /></br>• Cell-mediated immunity walls off bacteria → caseating or non-caseating granulomas.<br /></br>• Node becomes firm and violaceous; may eventually drain through sinus tract if untreated.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics</p></br><ol></br><li>Best initial diagnostic step: Excisional biopsy (already done).  </li></br><li>Management: Complete surgical excision is curative in &gt;90%.<br /></br>   – If complete excision not possible ► macrolide (clarithromycin or azithromycin) ± rifampin/ethambutol for several months.  </li></br><li>Routine labs/imaging not required unless systemic disease suspected.</li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids</p></br><p>“Viola-NTM”: Violaceous skin over Node = Think Non-Tuberculous Mycobacteria.<br /></br>Kids with “MAC” on their neck = M. Avium Complex cervical node.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</p></br><p>A. Cytomegalovirus<br /></br>• Causes a mononucleosis-like illness: fever, malaise, diffuse lymphadenopathy, atypical lymphocytes.<br /></br>• No isolated node, no violaceous skin, histology would show viral cytopathic changes, not granulomas.</p></br><p>B. Epstein-Barr virus<br /></br>• Classic infectious mononucleosis → diffuse tender LAD, fever, sore throat, splenomegaly; histology shows reactive hyperplasia, not granulomas.</p></br><p>C. Haemophilus influenzae type b<br /></br>• Pre-vaccine era cause of acute, toxic-appearing bacterial cervical adenitis or cellulitis; presents with high fever, erythema, marked tenderness, neutrophilic abscess formation—not granulomas. Current immunization makes this exceedingly rare.</p></br><p>E. Staphylococcus aureus<br /></br>• Most common cause of acute, unilateral, painful, erythematous cervical adenitis with systemic symptoms; biopsy would show acute suppurative inflammation/abscess, not granulomatous inflammation. Violaceous discoloration is atypical.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Key Takeaway</p></br><p>A slow-growing, unilateral cervical node with violaceous skin and granulomatous histology in a well child = nontuberculous mycobacterial lymphadenitis (M. avium-intracellulare complex).</p>
16. A 55-year-old woman with a 10-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus returns to the clinic for a follow-up examination of a right foot ulcer diagnosed 8 weeks ago. At that time, she was treated with hydrocolloid dressings. Today, the patient reports a 1-week history of chills and moderate mid back pain. She has not had cough, pain with urination, or diarrhea. She also has hypertension. Medications are metformin and lisinopril. The patient is 160 cm (5 ft 2 in) tall and weighs 88 kg (195 lb); BMI is 35 kg/m2. Her temperature is 38.2 C (100.8 F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 132/80 mm Hg. Examination shows a stage 2 foot ulcer that measures 2 cm in diameter and point tenderness over the T12 spinous process. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following studies is most likely to confirm the cause of this patient’s fever and back pain?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Blood cultures</br>b. Determination of erythrocyte sedimentation rate</br>c. Echocardiography</br>d. MRI of the thoracic spine</br>e. Ultrasonography of the abdomen</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. MRI of the thoracic spine  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>VIGNETTE ANALYSIS  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• Long-standing diabetes mellitus → immunocompromise &amp; higher risk of deep infection/hematogenous spread.<br /></br>• Chronic foot ulcer (potential portal of entry for bacteria).<br /></br>• New systemic symptoms: 1-week history of chills + fever 38.2 °C.<br /></br>• Focal, reproducible point tenderness over the T12 spinous process.<br /></br>• No GU, pulmonary, or intra-abdominal symptoms.  </p></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives &amp; pattern recognition:<br /></br>Fever + focal vertebral tenderness in a patient with a potential bacteremic source = classic Step-2 pattern for vertebral osteomyelitis/discitis. The spine is the most common skeletal site of hematogenous osteomyelitis in adults, and diabetes is a major risk factor. The thoracic level of tenderness localizes the suspected infection.  </p></br><p>Exam strategy tip:<br /></br>Whenever you see (1) fever, (2) a bacteremia risk factor, and (3) focal back pain/tenderness, immediately think “vertebral osteomyelitis → order MRI.”  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>CLINICAL CRITICAL REASONING  </p></br><ol></br><li>Suspect diagnosis: Vertebral osteomyelitis/discitis at ~T12.  </li></br><li>Most sensitive &amp; specific diagnostic test: MRI with gadolinium of the affected spinal segment.<br /></br>   • Detects early marrow edema, disc-space involvement, and epidural/ paravertebral abscesses.  </li></br><li>Blood cultures and ESR/CRP are useful adjuncts but cannot localize or confirm the spinal source; imaging is required for confirmation and to plan biopsy/drainage.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home message: In adults with suspected vertebral osteomyelitis, MRI of the spine is the gold-standard first test to confirm infection and delineate extent.  </p></br><p>Documentation must include: suspected vertebral osteomyelitis, focal tenderness level, fever, risk factors (diabetes, foot ulcer), and rationale for urgent MRI.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>PATHOPHYSIOLOGY SNAPSHOT  </p></br><p>• Etiology: Hematogenous seeding (Staphylococcus aureus most common) or contiguous spread from soft-tissue infection.<br /></br>• Risk factors: Diabetes, IV drug use, bacteremia, endocarditis.<br /></br>• Mechanism: Bacteria lodge in end-arterial metaphyseal vessels of vertebral bodies → marrow infection → extends to disc → possible epidural abscess.<br /></br>• Clinical triad: Back pain, fever, neurologic deficits (late).  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>TREATMENT &amp; DIAGNOSTICS REVIEW  </p></br><ol></br><li>Imaging:<br /></br>   • MRI with contrast → confirm diagnosis, evaluate for abscess.<br /></br>   • CT-guided biopsy afterward for culture if blood cultures negative or unusual organism suspected.  </li></br><li>Labs: ESR/CRP markedly elevated (monitor response). Blood cultures often positive.  </li></br><li>Management:<br /></br>   • Empiric IV antibiotics (vancomycin + ceftriaxone/cefepime) after cultures.<br /></br>   • Surgical decompression if neurologic compromise or large abscess.  </li></br></ol></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>MEMORIZATION PEARL  </p></br><p>“MRI for a hot spine”:<br /></br>Hot (febrile) patient + spine pain/tenderness → MRI ASAP.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>INCORRECT OPTIONS  </p></br><p>a. Blood cultures<br /></br>   • Helpful for identifying organism and guiding antibiotics, but they do NOT confirm the anatomic source; 30–50 % may be negative.  </p></br><p>b. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate<br /></br>   • Sensitive but nonspecific; elevated ESR only supports inflammation, cannot localize the infection.  </p></br><p>c. Echocardiography<br /></br>   • Consider for endocarditis (fever + bacteremia), but endocarditis does not cause isolated focal spinous tenderness; no murmurs or vascular phenomena given.  </p></br><p>e. Ultrasonography of the abdomen<br /></br>   • Useful for cholecystitis/renal pathology; the patient lacks abdominal pain or GU symptoms.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core concept locked in: Fever + focal vertebral tenderness in a patient with a bacteremic risk factor → think vertebral osteomyelitis → order MRI of the spine.</p>
17. A 67-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 1-week history of moderate pain and swelling, redness, and warmth of her right knee. She has a 10-year history of moderate pain and progressive stiffness of her hands and a 1-year history of swelling and warmth of her digits and wrists. She says she has generalized stiffness for 1 hour after awakening in the morning. She has no history of trauma to the hands or knees. She has no history of serious illness and takes ibuprofen for the pain as needed. Vital signs are within normal limits. On examination, the metacarpophalangeal joints are boggy bilaterally and tender on squeezing. There is erythema and swelling of the right knee; the patella is ballotable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. X-ray of the right knee</br>b. Oral naproxen therapy</br>c. Oral oxycodone therapy as needed for pain</br>d. Oral prednisone therapy</br>e. Arthrocentesis of the right knee</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Arthrocentesis of the right knee  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Key phrases that jump out:<br /></br>   • “1-week history of moderate pain and swelling, redness, and warmth of her right knee” → acute, inflammatory, monoarticular arthritis.<br /></br>   • “Patella is ballotable” → large effusion.<br /></br>   • History of symmetric MCP stiffness ≥1 h in the morning → classic for rheumatoid arthritis (RA).  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Why these clues matter:<br /></br>   • Patients with underlying RA (or any chronically damaged joint) are at markedly higher risk for septic arthritis.<br /></br>   • An acutely hot, swollen single joint in any patient—especially one with RA—must be assumed to be septic arthritis or a crystal arthropathy until proven otherwise.<br /></br>   • The only way to rapidly rule in/out infection or crystals is to obtain and analyze synovial fluid.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>   • “Hot joint? TAP joint.”<br /></br>   • Never give steroids or opioids or simply order imaging before fluid analysis; doing so can delay diagnosis and destroy the joint.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Identify acute monoarthritis.<br /></br>2. Recognize high-risk host (RA, effusion).<br /></br>3. Understand that untreated septic arthritis threatens cartilage within hours → emergent procedure.<br /></br>4. Therefore, perform diagnostic (and partially therapeutic) arthrocentesis:<br /></br>   • Cell count with differential<br /></br>   • Gram stain &amp; culture<br /></br>   • Crystal analysis  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: In any acutely inflamed solitary joint—particularly in an immunocompromised or chronically arthritic patient—arthrocentesis is the first and most important step.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): onset, duration, systemic symptoms, risk factors (RA), exam findings, informed consent, synovial fluid results, early empiric antibiotics if septic arthritis confirmed.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Septic arthritis: Hematogenous seeding of damaged synovium; staph aureus most common.<br /></br>• Crystal arthropathies: CPPD (pseudogout) common in older adults; can mimic infection.<br /></br>• RA joints are chronically inflamed, making bacterial seeding easier and presentation less obvious—hence low threshold to aspirate.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Relevant Diagnostics &amp; Treatment<br /></br>Diagnostics:<br /></br>• Synovial WBC &gt;50,000 with &gt;75% PMNs → strongly suggests infection.<br /></br>• Positive Gram stain/culture → definitive.<br /></br>• Rhomboid, weakly birefringent crystals → CPPD; needle, negatively birefringent → gout.  </p></br><p>Management after tap:<br /></br>• Septic → IV vancomycin + 3rd-gen cephalosporin (or tailored therapy) and surgical lavage.<br /></br>• Crystals → NSAIDs, colchicine, or intra-articular steroids once infection excluded.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Hot red joint? Do NOT delay – aspirate today.”<br /></br>“TAP before you TREAT.”<br /></br>Picture a flashing red stop sign on the joint reminding you to stop everything and aspirate first.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. X-ray of the right knee<br /></br>   • May show effusion or erosions but cannot diagnose infection or crystals and does not change emergent management.  </p></br><p>b. Oral naproxen therapy<br /></br>   • Symptomatic care only; masks infection, delays definitive therapy.  </p></br><p>c. Oral oxycodone therapy<br /></br>   • Pure analgesia; no diagnostic value, risks delaying life-saving intervention.  </p></br><p>d. Oral prednisone therapy<br /></br>   • Potent immunosuppression could rapidly worsen an undiagnosed septic joint. Steroids are contraindicated until infection is ruled out.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>An acutely swollen, warm monoarticular joint in a patient with RA is septic arthritis until proven otherwise. Emergency arthrocentesis is mandatory.</p>
18. A 22-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 6-week history of mild pain and swelling in the front of her right ankle. She says her symptoms began after she awkwardly stepped off a curb. There is no personal or family history of serious illness. Her only medication is ibuprofen as needed for pain. She does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcohol. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows a 1-cm abrasion and mild ecchymosis over the anterolateral aspect of the right ankle. There is warmth and swelling at the right anterior ankle joint; passive dorsiflexion produces pain. Anterior drawer test is negative bilaterally. Muscle strength is 5/5 on inversion, eversion, dorsiflexion, and plantar flexion of the right ankle. Dorsalis pedis pulses are 2+ bilaterally. Sensation to light touch is intact over the lower extremities. X-rays of the right ankle are shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Bone biopsy</br>b. Complete blood count</br>c. Joint aspiration of the right ankle</br>d. MRI of the right tibia</br>e. Ultrasonography of the right lower extremity</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. MRI of the right tibia  </p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Framework<br /></br>1. Chronicity &amp; Mechanism<br /></br>   • “6-week history” of pain/swelling that started after a low-energy misstep → think occult (eg, stress) injury rather than acute ligament tear or septic process.<br /></br>2. Physical exam<br /></br>   • Mild warmth/swelling but full strength, intact ROM except pain at extreme dorsiflexion → points away from high-grade sprain, rupture, or septic arthritis (which would cause marked pain with any motion and systemic signs).<br /></br>   • Negative anterior drawer → lateral ankle ligaments likely intact.<br /></br>3. Imaging provided (plain films)<br /></br>   • NBME typically shows either a completely normal film or an equivocal cortical line; question stem implies that x-ray did NOT give a definitive answer (“next step in diagnosis”).<br /></br>4. Classic USMLE pattern<br /></br>   • Persisting focal bone pain + non-diagnostic x-ray → order MRI to look for a stress fracture or other occult bony pathology.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Flag any musculoskeletal vignette in which (A) pain persists &gt;4–6 weeks, (B) x-ray is normal/indeterminate, and (C) exam is otherwise benign. The next move is almost always an MRI (occasionally a bone scan if MRI unavailable).  </p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Suspect a tibial (or talar) stress fracture given subacute focal pain that worsens with use, minimal trauma, and a normal ligament exam.<br /></br>2. Plain radiographs have poor sensitivity (≈15% in early weeks).<br /></br>3. MRI is the most sensitive and specific modality for cortical stress reactions, early fracture lines, and periosteal edema.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Persisting bone pain with a negative x-ray → MRI for occult/stress fracture.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient)<br /></br>• Mechanism &amp; timeline of pain, failure of conservative therapy, focal tenderness.<br /></br>• Plan: MRI of tibia/ankle to evaluate for stress fracture; counsel on activity modification pending results.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review – Tibial Stress Fracture<br /></br>• Etiology: Repetitive sub-maximal loading → micro-trabecular cracks &gt; bone remodeling capacity.<br /></br>• RFs: Sudden ↑ activity, improper footwear, low vitamin D, female athlete triad.<br /></br>• Early x-ray: often normal; periosteal callus may appear ≥3 wks.<br /></br>• MRI findings: T2/STIR hyperintense periosteal edema ± linear cortical hypointensity (fracture line).  </p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Management Snapshot (after diagnosis)<br /></br>1. Activity modification, protected weight-bearing (CAM boot or pneumatic brace) 4–6 wks.<br /></br>2. Analgesia (acetaminophen/NSAIDs), optimize calcium/vit D.<br /></br>3. Gradual return-to-sport protocol once pain-free and repeat imaging shows healing if high-risk site.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>“MRI Finds Stress”:<br /></br>• Microfracture<br /></br>• Repetitive load<br /></br>• Inconclusive x-ray → order MRI<br /></br>• Finds Stress fracture (or other occult bone lesion)  </p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Bone biopsy – Reserved for suspected malignancy or chronic osteomyelitis seen on imaging. No lytic/sclerotic mass, systemic signs, or chronic drainage described.<br /></br>b. Complete blood count – Would help in infection or anemia work-up, but the patient is afebrile with normal vitals and localized symptoms. A normal or abnormal CBC would not change immediate management.<br /></br>c. Joint aspiration – Indicated for septic arthritis or crystal arthropathy (acute, severe pain, marked loss of motion, fever). Here pain is mild, ROM preserved, and process is subacute.<br /></br>e. Ultrasonography of the lower extremity – Appropriate to evaluate for DVT (calf swelling, erythema, risk factors) or tendon rupture, neither of which fits this presentation.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────<br /></br>Key Exam Pearl<br /></br>• Persisting focal bone pain + negative x-ray → MRI (or bone scan) to rule in/out stress fracture. Don’t jump to invasive tests or labs unless systemic/infectious red flags are present.</p>
19. A 16-year-old girl is brought to the physician because of generalized weakness for the past 3 days. She states that during the past month, she has been following a strict diet and taking laxatives and diuretics to lose weight. She has no history of serious illness. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows diffuse, generalized weakness; there is no pain on muscle palpation. Deep tendon reflexes are 1+ in the upper and lower extremities. A decreased concentration of which of the following serum electrolytes is the most likely cause of this patient's muscle weakness?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Calcium</br>b. Chloride</br>c. Magnesium</br>d. Potassium</br>e. Sodium</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Potassium (hypokalemia)</p></br><p>────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – 16-year-old female with disordered-eating behavior (severe dieting, laxative and diuretic abuse).<br /></br>  – 3-day history of generalized, painless weakness.<br /></br>  – Diffuse ↓ deep-tendon reflexes (1+).<br /></br>  – No abnormal vital signs or focal neurologic deficits.</p></br><p>• Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>  – No muscle tenderness → argues against inflammatory myopathy.<br /></br>  – Normal vitals → makes acute adrenal crisis or severe dehydration less likely.<br /></br>  – Laxatives → GI K⁺ loss; loop/thiazide-like diuretics → renal K⁺ wasting.<br /></br>  – Strict diet → low K⁺ intake.<br /></br>  – Altogether, triple hit for hypokalemia.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Whenever you see laxatives + diuretics + weakness on NBME/Step 2, immediately think “check potassium.”</p></br><p>────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Mechanism: K⁺ sets the resting membrane potential (RMP) of skeletal muscle.<br /></br>2. Low extracellular K⁺ → membrane hyperpolarization → ↓ excitability → flaccid weakness and depressed reflexes.<br /></br>3. Patients can progress to paralysis, ileus, rhabdomyolysis, or life-threatening arrhythmias (look for U waves, flattened T waves on ECG).</p></br><p>Take-home message: Hypokalemia is the classic electrolyte cause of painless, generalized weakness with decreased reflexes, especially after diuretic or laxative abuse.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life)<br /></br>• Symptoms/signs of weakness, dietary history, medication/laxative use.<br /></br>• Serum electrolytes, ECG.<br /></br>• Plan for K⁺ repletion and cardiac monitoring.</p></br><p>────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiologies: GI loss (vomiting, diarrhea, laxatives), renal loss (loop &amp; thiazide diuretics, hyperaldosteronism), decreased intake, intracellular shift (insulin, β-agonists).<br /></br>• Clinical picture: Weakness → paralysis, hyporeflexia; arrhythmias; metabolic alkalosis often co-exists (diuretics/laxatives).  </p></br><p>────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment<br /></br>• Labs: BMP showing K⁺ &lt;3.5 mEq/L ± metabolic alkalosis.<br /></br>• ECG: U waves, flattened T, ST depression, PVCs.<br /></br>• Management:<br /></br>  – Mild-moderate: Oral KCl (20–40 mEq).<br /></br>  – Severe or symptomatic: IV KCl with cardiac monitoring (≤10 mEq/h peripheral, ≤20 mEq/h central).<br /></br>  – Address precipitants: Stop laxatives/diuretics, treat eating disorder, consider adding potassium-sparing diuretic if diuretic required clinically.  </p></br><p>────────────────────</br>Memory Aids<br /></br>• “Low ‘K’ = Low Kraft” (muscle power).<br /></br>• LooP &amp; Thiazide “Take the ‘K’ out of you.”<br /></br>• ECG: “U have two letters after T” → U wave follows flattened T in hypokalemia.</p></br><p>────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Calcium<br /></br>   – Hypocalcemia causes tetany, Chvostek/Trousseau signs (hyperreflexia, cramps), not flaccid weakness. Hypercalcemia causes weakness but classically with constipation, polyuria, bone pain. Laxative/diuretic abuse does not preferentially lower Ca²⁺.  </p></br><p>b. Chloride<br /></br>   – Hypochloremia accompanies metabolic alkalosis but by itself does not produce neuromuscular weakness.  </p></br><p>c. Magnesium<br /></br>   – Hypomagnesemia leads to hyperreflexia, tremor, tetany, torsades; depressed reflexes are more typical of low K⁺.  </p></br><p>e. Sodium<br /></br>   – Hypo- or hypernatremia mainly cause CNS symptoms (confusion, seizures) rather than isolated motor weakness; reflexes usually remain intact.  </p></br><p>────────────────────</br>High-Yield Core Concept<br /></br>Potassium is the primary determinant of resting membrane potential. Losses from laxatives and diuretics commonly precipitate hypokalemic flaccid weakness with depressed reflexes—an NBME favorite.</p>
20. A 61-year-old man with ischemic cardiomyopathy is admitted to the hospital for management of community-acquired pneumonia. His medications are furosemide, spironolactone, and lisinopril. He has a dietary sodium restriction of less than 1500 mg daily. His temperature is 38.8 C (101.8 F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 128/74 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 88 percent. Crackles are heard in the middle lobe of the right lung. There is no edema of the lower extremities. A chest x-ray shows a right middle lobe infiltrate. His ejection fraction is 30 percent. Which of the following is the most appropriate intravenous fluid maintenance therapy?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. 5 percent Dextrose in 0.45 percent saline</br>b. 5 percent Dextrose in 0.9 percent saline</br>c. 5 percent Dextrose in water</br>d. 0.9 percent Saline</br>e. No intravenous fluids are indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. No intravenous fluids are indicated  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues in the stem:<br /></br>• Known systolic heart failure – EF only 30% (severely reduced)<br /></br>• Already on loop (furosemide) + potassium-sparing diuretic (spironolactone) + ACE inhibitor, AND on a &lt;1500-mg Na⁺ diet → tells you his baseline volume status must be carefully controlled.<br /></br>• No physical signs of volume depletion: BP normal (128/74), HR only mildly elevated from fever, mucous membranes/lower extremities not described as dry or edematous.<br /></br>• Presenting problem is pneumonia, not gastroenteritis or bowel obstruction – nothing suggests ongoing fluid loss.<br /></br>• Able to take oral medications, so presumably can drink.<br /></br>Take-home pattern: Heart-failure patient + no volume loss → default is fluid and often sodium restriction, not automatic “maintenance IV fluids.”</p></br><p>Cognitive/Metacognitive tip: On test day first decide “Does this patient truly need maintenance fluids?” Unless the vignette demonstrates (1) inability to take PO OR (2) overt volume depletion, do not reach for an IV bag—especially in CHF, renal failure, or cirrhosis where extra volume can quickly decompensate the patient.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why “no IVFs” is right)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Goal in systolic HF is strict fluid/SODIUM restriction to avoid pulmonary edema.  </li></br><li>None of the stem data show hypovolemia (orthostasis, dry mucosa, prerenal labs, etc.).  </li></br><li>Fever from pneumonia does increase insensible loss, but that can be replaced with PO water as tolerated.  </li></br><li>Any crystalloid solution would introduce 0.45–0.9% NaCl (≈77–154 mEq/L) and &gt;1 L/day of free volume, directly opposing HF management and risking acute decompensated heart failure (flash pulmonary edema).  </li></br><li>Therefore the safest “maintenance” plan is no routine IV fluids; simply use a saline lock for medication administration and flushes.</li></br></ol></br><p>Core takeaway: IV maintenance fluids are NOT a default order. In patients with fluid-sensitive states (CHF, ESRD, cirrhosis) give fluids only when a clear indication (volume loss, inability to take PO, hypotension) exists.</p></br><p>Documentation (real life): “Patient euvolemic on exam. With HFrEF (EF 30%) will avoid maintenance IVF; saline lock only for meds. Encourage PO intake to goal ≤1.5 L/day; continue daily weights.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Brief Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• HFrEF → ↓ forward flow → ↑ RAAS &amp; ADH → water + Na⁺ retention.<br /></br>• Extra IV fluid quickly raises LV end-diastolic volume → pulmonary capillary hydrostatic pressure → pulmonary edema.<br /></br>• Pneumonia adds inflammatory cytokines that already increase vascular permeability, further lowering the threshold for edema.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls  </p></br><p>• Community-acquired pneumonia: empiric abx (e.g., ceftriaxone + azithro) and O₂ to keep SpO₂ &gt;92%.<br /></br>• Continue outpatient HF meds unless hypotension/AKI.<br /></br>• Monitor weight, I&amp;O, and daily chemistries (esp. K⁺ with ACEI + spironolactone + furosemide).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“CHF = Can’t Handle Fluids.”<br /></br>When you see EF &lt;40% on exam questions, ask: “Do I give fluid or do I restrict?”<br /></br>If no clear reason for fluid → answer is restrict.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. 5% Dextrose in 0.45% saline (D5 ½NS)<br /></br>   • Standard maintenance fluid (77 mEq/L Na⁺). Still delivers ≈1.5–2 L/day of volume and Na⁺ → precipitates volume overload in HF.</p></br><p>b. 5% Dextrose in 0.9% saline (D5NS)<br /></br>   • Even higher Na⁺ load (154 mEq/L). Would worsen HF fastest; generally reserved for hypoglycemic patients needing isotonic resuscitation.</p></br><p>c. 5% Dextrose in water (D5W)<br /></br>   • Initially isotonic but becomes free water (metabolism of dextrose) → expands total body water, dilutes serum Na⁺, and still stresses a failing LV.</p></br><p>d. 0.9% Saline (NS)<br /></br>   • Pure salt water, high chloride; routine use risks HF exacerbation and hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis.</p></br><p>Each incorrect choice ignores the cardinal rule: avoid unnecessary volume and sodium in systolic heart failure patients who are not volume-depleted.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line:<br /></br>In euvolemic or hypervolemic heart-failure patients who can drink, the safest “maintenance fluid” is none; give only what is necessary for medication administration.</p>
21. A 64-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department because of a 3-hour history of shortness of breath, nausea, and vomiting. She has not had fever or chest or abdominal pain. She has not had any sick contacts. She has hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Her medications are lisinopril, insulin, and aspirin. On arrival, she is in acute distress. Her pulse is 64/min with occasional premature beats, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 90/50 mm Hg. The lungs are clear to auscultation. On cardiac examination, S₁ and S₂ are normal and an S₄ is heard; there are no murmurs. The abdomen is soft and nontender. There is no peripheral edema. An ECG is shown. Chest x-ray shows no pulmonary edema. Administration of which of the following is contraindicated in this patient?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Heparin  </br>b. Nitroglycerin  </br>c. 0.9 percent Saline  </br>d. Simvastatin  </br>e. Streptokinase</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Nitroglycerin  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that immediately raise suspicion for an acute right-ventricular (RV) (i.e., “inferior”) myocardial infarction (MI):<br /></br>• 64-year-old with cardiac risk factors (HTN, diabetes).<br /></br>• Acute‐onset dyspnea, nausea, vomiting—but no chest pain (classic “silent” MI presentation in diabetics).<br /></br>• Hypotension (BP 90/50) with relatively low-normal heart rate (64/min) and clear lungs → cardiogenic shock without pulmonary congestion → points to RV failure rather than LV failure.<br /></br>• S4 (stiff, ischemic ventricle).<br /></br>• ECG (not shown here, but in the actual test) typically demonstrates ST-segment elevation in the inferior leads (II, III, aVF) ± ST elevation in V₁ and V₄R or ST depression in V₂–V₃ (posterior pattern)—classic for RV involvement.<br /></br>• Chest x-ray: no pulmonary edema (again favors isolated RV infarct).  </p></br><p>Cognitive road map for exam success:<br /></br>1. “Hypotension + clear lungs + inferior ECG changes” → think RV infarct.<br /></br>2. RV infarct = preload dependent. Anything that reduces preload (e.g., nitrates, diuretics, morphine) can precipitate profound hypotension → contraindicated.<br /></br>3. Give fluids to augment preload.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning &amp; Step-wise Approach  </p></br><ol></br><li>Identify cardiogenic shock pattern without pulmonary congestion.  </li></br><li>Infer RV failure → requires higher filling pressures to maintain LV preload and systemic output.  </li></br><li>Nitroglycerin is a potent venodilator that drops preload → would worsen hypotension and reduce cardiac output in an RV MI. Therefore, it is contraindicated.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home “golden rule”: In suspected or confirmed RV MI, avoid preload-reducing agents (nitrates, diuretics, aggressive opioids) and instead give volume (NS bolus) ± inotropes if needed.  </p></br><p>Documentation if this were a real patient: “Patient presents with probable inferior/posterior RV MI. Will avoid nitrates/diuretics; initiate IV isotonic saline bolus, antiplatelet therapy, anticoagulation, statin, and arrange for emergent PCI.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• The RV has thin walls and is highly preload dependent.<br /></br>• Ischemia → RV cannot generate adequate pressure, so LV underfills → systemic hypotension.<br /></br>• Venodilators further reduce RV preload → precipitous drop in BP.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management Pearls  </p></br><p>First-line management of inferior/RV MI:<br /></br>1. Aspirin ± P2Y12 inhibitor (clopidogrel).<br /></br>2. Anticoagulation (heparin).<br /></br>3. High-intensity statin (e.g., atorvastatin 80 mg).<br /></br>4. Reperfusion (PCI preferred; fibrinolytics if PCI unavailable).<br /></br>5. Maintain preload: isotonic saline bolus 250-500 mL; consider inotropes if necessary.<br /></br>6. Avoid: nitroglycerin, nitroprusside, morphine, diuretics.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aid  </p></br><p>“RV = Requires Volume.”<br /></br>If you see: Inferior leads + Hypotension + Clear lungs → think “No Nitrates, Needs Normal Saline.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Heparin<br /></br>• Used routinely in STEMI/NSTEMI to prevent further thrombosis. Does not worsen preload. Appropriate, not contraindicated.  </p></br><p>c. 0.9 % Saline<br /></br>• ACTUALLY indicated to augment preload in RV infarct–related hypotension.  </p></br><p>d. Simvastatin<br /></br>• High-intensity statin therapy (usually atorvastatin or rosuvastatin) is standard post-MI. No contraindication.  </p></br><p>e. Streptokinase<br /></br>• A fibrinolytic agent. Acceptable if PCI not immediately available and no contraindications (e.g., recent bleed). RV involvement does not preclude its use.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>In an RV (inferior) MI the heart is preload dependent; venodilators such as nitroglycerin can precipitate catastrophic hypotension and are therefore contraindicated.</p>
22. A 57-year-old woman comes to the physician as a new patient. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus that was poorly controlled with glyburide for 10 years; it has been better controlled with the addition of metformin for the past 2 years. She does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcohol. She is 152 cm (5 ft) tall and weighs 63 kg (140 lb); BMI is 27 kg/m2. Her blood pressure is 165/102 mm Hg on three measurements. One year ago, her blood pressure was 144/90 mm Hg. Examination today shows no abnormalities. Serum studies show electrolyte concentrations within the reference ranges and a creatinine concentration of 0.9 mg/dL. Urinalysis shows 4+ protein. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism of this patient’s increased blood pressure?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Aldosterone-secreting adrenal tumor</br>b. Atherosclerosis of the renal arteries</br>c. Epinephrine-secreting adrenal tumor</br>d. Renal parenchymal disease</br>e. Renin-secreting adrenal tumor</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>d. Renal parenchymal disease (diabetic nephropathy)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key phrases:</br>  – “Type 2 diabetes mellitus … poorly controlled for 10 years” → classic risk factor for diabetic nephropathy.<br /></br>  – “Urinalysis shows 4+ protein” → nephrotic-range proteinuria strongly suggests glomerular damage (most commonly diabetic nephropathy in adults).<br /></br>  – Rising blood pressure over 12 months (144/90 → 165/102) → new-onset or worsening secondary HTN.<br /></br>  – Normal creatinine and normal electrolytes today → early-stage renal disease (structural injury precedes loss of GFR).</p></br><p>• Pertinent positives: long-standing diabetes, nephrotic proteinuria, progressive HTN.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: no hypokalemia, no episodic symptoms, no renal bruits, no mass findings—these argue against adrenal or renovascular causes.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On Step 2 questions, “proteinuria + diabetes + HTN” should immediately prime you to think “diabetic nephropathy → renal parenchymal disease–mediated secondary hypertension.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>1. Long-standing DM damages glomeruli (mesangial expansion, GBM thickening, nodular sclerosis).<br /></br>2. Glomerular injury → loss of albumin (4+ proteinuria).<br /></br>3. Nephron loss ➜ decreased GFR ➜ renal hypoperfusion of remaining nephrons ➜ ↑ renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS).<br /></br>4. RAAS activation and impaired Na⁺/water excretion generate sustained systemic hypertension.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Chronic intrinsic kidney disease—especially diabetic nephropathy—is the single most common cause of secondary hypertension.</p></br><p>Documentation note: You would record the degree of proteinuria, BP values, suspected diabetic nephropathy, and plan for ACE-inhibitor/ARB, statin, tight glycemic and BP control, and nephrology follow-up.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>Diabetic nephropathy:</br>• Etiology: Hyperglycemia → non-enzymatic glycosylation of glomerular basement membrane + mesangial expansion.<br /></br>• Hallmarks: Microalbuminuria progressing to macroalbuminuria (&gt;300 mg/day or 4+ dipstick), nodular (Kimmelstiel-Wilson) glomerulosclerosis.<br /></br>• Consequences: Nephrotic-range proteinuria, progressive loss of GFR, secondary HTN.</p></br><p>Hypertension mechanism: Loss of functioning nephrons → impaired Na⁺/water excretion + intrarenal ischemia → ↑renin secretion → angiotensin II vasoconstriction and aldosterone-mediated volume expansion.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Management &amp; Diagnostics (high-yield)</br>1. ACE inhibitor (lisinopril) or ARB (losartan): slows proteinuria progression and lowers BP by blocking RAAS.<br /></br>2. Annual urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio, serum creatinine, eGFR.<br /></br>3. Tight glycemic control (HbA1c ≤ 7 %), statin therapy, low-salt diet.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“Three Ds”: Diabetes → Damaged glomeruli → Diastolic &amp; systolic HTN.<br /></br>Visual: Picture a leaky “sugar-coated” glomerulus dumping protein into a bucket labeled “BP ↑.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why Other Choices Are Wrong</br>a. Aldosterone-secreting adrenal tumor (Conn syndrome)</br>   • Would produce hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis; electrolytes are normal. Proteinuria is not a feature.</p></br><p>b. Atherosclerotic renal artery stenosis</br>   • Causes refractory HTN with abdominal bruit, flash pulmonary edema, or sudden creatinine rise after ACE-I. Proteinuria is typically mild or absent.</p></br><p>c. Epinephrine-secreting adrenal tumor (pheochromocytoma)</br>   • Presents with paroxysmal headaches, palpitations, diaphoresis, episodic HTN, and possible hyperglycemia, not heavy proteinuria.</p></br><p>e. Renin-secreting adrenal (juxtaglomerular cell) tumor</br>   • Extremely rare; causes severe HTN with markedly elevated plasma renin but no nephrotic proteinuria.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>In a diabetic patient, new heavy proteinuria + progressive hypertension = diabetic renal parenchymal disease driving secondary hypertension.</p>
23. A 25-year-old woman comes to the office for a health maintenance examination. She is on active duty in the US Air Force, and her primary duty is flying drones. She reports no symptoms. Medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. She does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcoholic beverages. Her paternal grandmother had a myocardial infarction at the age of 62 years. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination discloses no abnormalities. Results of fasting serum lipid studies obtained 1 year ago are shown:</br>Cholesterol</br>    Total 205 mg/dL</br>    HDL-cholesterol 45 mg/dL</br>    LDL-cholesterol 150 mg/dL</br>    Triglycerides 50 mg/dL</br>Which of the following is the most appropriate screening laboratory study at this time?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. LDL-receptor activity assay</br>b. Measurement of serum apolipoprotein(a) concentration</br>c. Measurement of serum C-reactive protein concentration</br>d. Repeat measurement of fasting serum lipid studies</br>e. No further testing is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. No further testing is indicated  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Strategy<br /></br>1. Patient profile: 25-year-old, asymptomatic, normotensive, nonsmoker, normal BMI, no diabetes, no meds.<br /></br>2. Prior data: A complete fasting lipid panel was performed 1 year ago.<br /></br>   • LDL 150 mg/dL is mildly elevated but &lt;190 mg/dL (the usual familial-hypercholesterolemia cutoff).<br /></br>   • No other lipid abnormalities.<br /></br>3. Family history: MI in a grandmother at 62 y is NOT considered “premature” (&lt;55 y in male first-degree or &lt;65 y in female first-degree). Therefore, the patient has no major ASCVD (atherosclerotic CV disease) risk enhancers.<br /></br>4. USPSTF &amp; ACC/AHA screening interval: In adults ≥20 y with low ASCVD risk, repeat fasting or non-fasting lipid profile every 5 years (sooner only if risk factors emerge).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When a screening test was done within the recommended interval and nothing has changed clinically, the safest NBME answer is often “no further testing.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• The core concept tested: appropriate use of screening tests—ordering only what changes management, at evidence-based intervals.<br /></br>• Because the last lipid panel was recent (1 year), repeating it or ordering ancillary lipoprotein tests would not alter current management; lifestyle counseling (diet, exercise) is already standard for everyone.<br /></br>• Document in the note: “Lipid panel done 1 y ago—within 5-y guideline interval; continues to be low ASCVD risk; no repeat labs indicated today.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)<br /></br>ASCVD risk is driven by cumulative exposure to LDL-C plus modifiable factors (smoking, HTN, DM). When LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL or premature ASCVD in a first-degree relative is present, familial hypercholesterolemia is suspected and more aggressive evaluation/therapy is warranted. That threshold is not met here.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Guideline Snapshot (Adults 20-39 y)<br /></br>• Baseline fasting or non-fasting lipid profile.<br /></br>• If low risk (10-y ASCVD risk &lt;7.5% and no risk enhancers): repeat q5 y or sooner if risk factors develop.<br /></br>• Risk enhancers (e.g., family history of premature ASCVD, metabolic syndrome, chronic inflammatory disease) justify earlier repeat or more advanced testing.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memory Tips<br /></br>“2-5-20 Rule” for lipids:<br /></br>• Start at 20 years.<br /></br>• Screen every 5 years if low risk.<br /></br>If LDL ≥190 or family history of MI &lt;55 M / &lt;65 F, think familial hypercholesterolemia and break the rule.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>A. LDL-receptor activity assay<br /></br>   • Used only when LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL or clear premature ASCVD suggests familial hypercholesterolemia. Her LDL 150 + no premature FHx makes this unnecessary.  </p></br><p>B. Serum apolipoprotein(a) [Lp(a)] concentration<br /></br>   • Optional in intermediate-risk patients or strong FHx of premature ASCVD. Not indicated in a low-risk 25-y-o.  </p></br><p>C. High-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hs-CRP)<br /></br>   • Considered for risk reclassification in intermediate-risk adults (age 40-75). Adds no value here.  </p></br><p>D. Repeat fasting lipid panel now<br /></br>   • Contrary to guidelines (should wait up to 5 y unless clinical status changes).  </p></br><p>Key difference: None of these alternatives would change current management or are guideline-endorsed at this time, so the correct action is to refrain from further testing.</p>
24. A 57-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 3-month history of an open wound on the left leg; the wound has been increasing in size during this time. He also has had mild pain and the sensation of heaviness in the entire left leg during this time; the pain is worst at the end of the day and improves with leg elevation. His medications are inhaled albuterol as needed for asthma and acetaminophen as needed for the leg pain. He has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 40 years. He lives in the southwestern USA and works as a long-distance truck driver. Vital signs are within normal limits. A photograph of the left leg is shown. The left ankle brachial index is 0.75 (N>1). The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely cause of these findings?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Brown recluse spider bite</br>b. Malignant melanoma</br>c. Peripheral artery disease</br>d. Squamous cell carcinoma</br>e. Venous insufficiency</br></br></br><p>Correct answer: e. Venous insufficiency  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Chronic, non-healing “open wound” on the lower leg for 3 months → think chronic ulcer rather than an acute bite or malignancy.<br /></br>• “Mild pain and sensation of heaviness … worst at the end of the day and improves with leg elevation” → classic venous congestion pattern. Arterial pain is classically worse with elevation and relieved by dependency.<br /></br>• Long-distance truck driver = prolonged sitting → promotes venous hypertension/valve incompetence.<br /></br>• Photograph (not shown here) on NBME typically demonstrates a shallow, irregular ulcer over the medial malleolus surrounded by brown pigmentation and edema—hallmark of venous stasis ulcers.<br /></br>• ABI 0.75 (moderately reduced) shows he does have some PAD, but the symptom pattern points strongly to venous disease. (Mixed arterial/venous disease is common; always let the clinical picture guide you.)  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip:<br /></br>1. Identify ulcer location + wound bed description.<br /></br>2. Ask whether pain is better “UP” (elevation) → venous, or better “DOWN” (dependency) → arterial.<br /></br>3. Look for edema &amp; hyperpigmentation → venous; shiny hairless skin &amp; diminished pulses → arterial.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1: Localize the problem to the venous system (symptoms relieved by elevation + heaviness after standing) → chronic venous hypertension.<br /></br>Step 2: Venous hypertension → valve incompetence → edema, RBC extravasation, hemosiderin deposition (brown skin), tissue hypoxia → shallow medial-ankle ulcer.<br /></br>Take-home: On exams, “medial malleolar ulcer + edema + better with elevation” = venous stasis ulcer due to chronic venous insufficiency.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): describe ulcer size/depth, surrounding induration, edema grade, skin pigmentation, pulses, ABI, and patient functional limitations.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Etiology: valve incompetence (post-thrombotic damage, prolonged standing, obesity, pregnancy, or hereditary weakness) → ↑ venous pressure.<br /></br>Mechanism:<br /></br>• Persistent venous hypertension → capillary leak of proteins &amp; RBCs → hemosiderin deposits → brown discoloration.<br /></br>• Chronic edema &amp; inflammation → fibrosis (lipodermatosclerosis) → skin breakdown.<br /></br>• Resulting ulcers are shallow, irregular, exudative, usually around the medial malleolus.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>1. Confirm venous etiology: duplex Doppler ultrasound (reflux, obstruction) if needed.<br /></br>2. Mainstay therapy:<br /></br>   • Graduated compression stockings or multilayer compression bandaging.<br /></br>   • Leg elevation and calf-muscle exercises.<br /></br>3. Wound care: moist dressings, debridement of devitalized tissue.<br /></br>4. Address risk factors: smoking cessation, weight control, periodic walking/stretch breaks for truck drivers.<br /></br>5. Refractory cases: endovenous ablation (radiofrequency, laser) or surgical vein stripping.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“VENOUS ulcers live MEDIALLY and love ELEVATION.”<br /></br>—V: Varicose veins/Valve failure<br /></br>—E: Edema, Erythema, brown “Earthy” pigmentation<br /></br>—N: Night swelling/Nocturnal relief with elevation<br /></br>—O: Open, Oozing, Shallow ulcer<br /></br>—U: Up-elevation makes it feel better<br /></br>—S: Saphenous area (medial malleolus)  </p></br><p>Compare with “ARTERIAL = dOwn dependency relieves pain; dorsal/lateral ‘punched-out’ painful ulcers.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Brown recluse spider bite: painful necrotic eschar within days, systemic flu-like symptoms possible; not a chronic 3-month enlarging ulcer.<br /></br>b. Malignant melanoma: pigmented lesion with ABCDE changes; ulceration possible in advanced lesions but usually begins as a pigmented nodule/patch, not a heavy, edema-related ulcer.<br /></br>c. Peripheral artery disease: ABI &lt; 0.90 confirms PAD, but PAD ulcers are typically on toes, lateral malleolus, or pressure points, are sharply demarcated, painful with elevation, and accompanied by diminished pulses, cool skin, and hair loss—none of which are described.<br /></br>d. Squamous cell carcinoma: chronic non-healing ulcer, often sun-exposed or associated with chronic scars/burns (Marjolin ulcer). Usually firm, raised, everted edges, not improved by leg elevation or associated with dependent heaviness.  </p></br><p>Key difference: only venous insufficiency matches the positional pain relief + edema + typical ulcer location/picture.</p>
25. A previously healthy 4-year-old boy is brought to the physician because of a 3-day history of fever, cough, and runny nose. He has not had wheezing, vomiting, or diarrhea. He is at the 75th percentile for height and 10th percentile for weight. His temperature is 37.5 C (99.5 F). The skin is warm and pink. Capillary refill time is 2 seconds. Examination shows clear rhinorrhea. Breath sounds are normal. Cardiac examination is shown. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Congestive heart failure</br>b. Idiopathic pulmonary hypertension</br>c. Pericarditis</br>d. Upper respiratory tract infection</br>e. Ventricular septal defect</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Upper respiratory tract infection (URI)</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key positives: 3-day history, low-grade fever (99.5 °F/37.5 °C), cough, clear rhinorrhea, normal lung &amp; heart exams, normal perfusion (warm skin, cap refill 2 s).<br /></br>• Key negatives: no wheezing, no dyspnea, no GI symptoms, no abnormal heart sounds/murmur, no hepatomegaly, no edema.<br /></br>• Pattern: Short-duration, mild constitutional symptoms + isolated “cold” symptoms in a child = classic viral URI.<br /></br>Cognitive approach for future questions:<br /></br>1. Separate “common viral syndrome” versus “cardiopulmonary pathology” by focusing on vitals, perfusion, and exam.<br /></br>2. Ask: “Is there any objective evidence of heart, lung, or systemic compromise?” If not, default to benign URI.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1 – Localize: Symptoms are confined to upper airway (nose + oropharynx) with no lower-respiratory findings.<br /></br>Step 2 – Severity: Normal temperature, normal work of breathing, good perfusion.<br /></br>Step 3 – Match pattern: Viral URI in children typically lasts &lt;1 week, features clear or mucoid rhinorrhea, mild cough, ± low-grade fever, but normal chest exam.<br /></br>Core takeaway: In the absence of focal lung findings or hemodynamic compromise, the most likely diagnosis for an afebrile to low-grade febrile child with runny nose and cough is a self-limited viral URI.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real-life): history of present illness, ROS negative for SOB/wheezing, normal vitals/oxygenation, normal lung &amp; heart sounds, assessment: “viral URI,” plan: supportive care, return precautions.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: Most commonly rhinovirus, adenovirus, coronavirus, RSV, parainfluenza.<br /></br>• Mechanism: Viral replication in nasopharyngeal epithelium → inflammatory mediators → rhinorrhea &amp; cough.<br /></br>• Clinical manifestations: Nasal congestion/discharge, sore throat, cough, low-grade fever; should not produce focal lung signs or significant systemic illness in healthy children.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>• No lab or imaging needed if classic presentation.<br /></br>• Supportive care: hydration, antipyretic (acetaminophen or ibuprofen), saline nose drops, bulb suction, humidified air.<br /></br>• Avoid antibiotics (viral).<br /></br>• Red-flag follow-up: fever ≥38.5 °C beyond 3–4 days, respiratory distress, ear pain, persistent vomiting, lethargy.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Cold = COOL”:<br /></br>C – Clear rhinorrhea<br /></br>O – Onset recent (&lt;1 week)<br /></br>O – Otherwise normal exam<br /></br>L – Low-grade or no fever  </p></br><p>If all four boxes check, think benign common cold/URI.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Congestive heart failure<br /></br>   • Would show tachycardia, tachypnea, hepatomegaly, S₃ gallop, crackles, poor weight gain/failure to thrive, sweating with feeds—not present here.  </p></br><p>b. Idiopathic pulmonary hypertension<br /></br>   • Presents with exertional dyspnea, chest pain, syncope, loud P₂, right-sided lift; chronic process, not an acute 3-day illness with runny nose.  </p></br><p>c. Pericarditis<br /></br>   • Sharp chest pain relieved by leaning forward, pericardial friction rub, tachycardia, often higher fever. None of these findings are given.  </p></br><p>e. Ventricular septal defect<br /></br>   • Classically a holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border ± thrill; may cause failure to thrive, frequent respiratory infections, or signs of CHF. Cardiac exam “shown” (and implied normal) argues strongly against a murmur.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>A short course of mild fever, cough, and clear rhinorrhea with normal cardiopulmonary exam in a healthy child = uncomplicated viral upper respiratory tract infection.</p>
26. An asymptomatic 37-year-old man who has been HIV positive for 1 year comes to the physician for a follow-up examination. His CD4+ T-lymphocyte count is 650/mm3 (Normal≥500). A PPD skin test produces 6 mm of induration at 48 hours. An x-ray of the chest shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Repeat PPD skin test in 10 weeks</br>b. Repeat PPD skin test in 1 year</br>c. Second strength PPD skin test (250 tuberculin units) now</br>d. Treatment of active tuberculosis now</br>e. Treatment of latent tuberculosis now</br>f. No further testing or therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>E. Treatment of latent tuberculosis now  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>1. Patient profile<br /></br>   • 37-year-old man with documented HIV infection.<br /></br>   • CD4 count 650 / mm³ (still immunocompromised by definition).  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>TB screening result<br /></br>   • PPD (tuberculin skin test, TST) shows 6 mm induration at 48 h.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Imaging<br /></br>   • Chest-x-ray is normal → no radiographic evidence of active disease.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Key testing pearl: In patients with HIV infection, a PPD induration ≥5 mm is considered POSITIVE, regardless of CD4 count.<br /></br>Therefore the patient has latent TB infection (LTBI) by CDC / USPSTF / IDSA criteria.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-by-step thought process for test day:</br>1. Determine the positivity cut-off for the population.<br /></br>   • ≥5 mm: HIV+, recent TB contact, fibrotic CXR lesions, organ transplant, immunosuppressed.<br /></br>   • ≥10 mm: recent immigrants, IVDU, healthcare workers, children &lt;4 y, etc.<br /></br>   • ≥15 mm: no risk factors.<br /></br>2. CXR rules out active disease.<br /></br>3. Positive test + no symptoms + normal CXR = latent TB infection.<br /></br>4. HIV infection is the strongest risk factor for progression to active TB → treat LTBI.<br /></br>   • First-line: Isoniazid (INH) + pyridoxine for 9 months (alternatives: INH–rifapentine weekly x 3 mo, or rifampin daily x 4 mo if INH-intolerant).</p></br><p>Take-home message: Any HIV-positive patient with ≥5 mm TST or positive IGRA receives LTBI therapy after active disease is excluded.</p></br><p>Documentation in real life: “Positive TST (6 mm) meeting ≥5 mm cut-off for HIV. Asymptomatic, CXR negative → initiate INH 300 mg daily + pyridoxine 25–50 mg daily for 9 months.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• TB infection is contained within granulomas (latent) but can reactivate when cellular immunity wanes.<br /></br>• HIV depletes CD4 T-cells, the very cells needed to maintain granuloma integrity, so reactivation risk is ~10% per year without prophylaxis versus 10% lifetime in immunocompetent hosts.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review</br>1. Diagnostics already done: TST and CXR. Further tests (sputum AFB, IGRA, CT) are only warranted if symptoms or radiographic findings suggest active TB.<br /></br>2. LTBI treatment options (CDC 2020):<br /></br>   • INH 300 mg daily x 9 mo (preferred for HIV+ on ART)<br /></br>   • INH + rifapentine weekly x 12 wk (not for patients on many ART regimens due to drug–drug interactions)<br /></br>   • Rifampin daily x 4 mo (if INH resistant/intolerant)<br /></br>3. Always add pyridoxine (vitamin B6) to prevent INH-induced neuropathy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“5-10-15 Rule”<br /></br>• 5 mm → HIV, recent contact, CXR scar, transplant/immunosuppressed.<br /></br>• 10 mm → High-risk but not immunosuppressed.<br /></br>• 15 mm → Everyone else.<br /></br>Visual: Picture a stoplight—red light at 5 mm for HIV (highest urgency to stop progression).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>A. Repeat PPD in 10 weeks<br /></br>   • Used for people recently exposed who may still be in the “window period” with an initially negative test. Our patient is already positive.</p></br><p>B. Repeat PPD in 1 year<br /></br>   • Annual screening is for those with an INITIAL NEGATIVE test who remain at risk (e.g., healthcare workers). Not appropriate after a positive result.</p></br><p>C. Second-strength PPD (250 TU) now<br /></br>   • The “second-strength” test (old anergy testing) is obsolete; current practice uses the same strength for two-step baseline testing in elderly or healthcare workers, not to redefine a positive result.</p></br><p>D. Treatment of ACTIVE TB now<br /></br>   • Active disease requires 4-drug regimen (INH, rifampin, pyrazinamide, ethambutol) and is indicated only if CXR or symptoms suggest active infection—absent here.</p></br><p>F. No further testing or therapy<br /></br>   • Would leave an HIV patient with untreated latent TB at high risk for reactivation; contrary to all guidelines.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>In HIV-positive patients, ≥5 mm PPD is positive. After ruling out active disease with a normal CXR, initiate latent TB therapy (INH + B6 for 9 months).</p>
27. An 18-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital because of a 3-day history of fever, shortness of breath, and cough productive of sputum. She received the diagnosis of asthma at the age of 12 years. She is currently in acute respiratory distress. Her temperature is 37.3 C (99.1 F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 30/min, and blood pressure is 120/70 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on 4 L/min of oxygen by nasal cannula shows a decrease in oxygen saturation from 98 percent on admission to 90 percent. Examination shows use of accessory muscles of respiration. Breath sounds are decreased bilaterally. Her leukocyte count is 18,000/mm3. A chest x-ray shows hyperinflation, which is consistent with x-ray findings on admission. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's current condition?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Aspiration</br>b. Bronchospasm</br>c. Pneumonia</br>d. Pneumothorax</br>e. Pulmonary edema</br>f. Pulmonary embolus</br></br></br><p>Correct answer (given by user): B. Bronchospasm  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• 18-year-old with a known history of asthma → puts bronchospasm at the top of the differential for any sudden respiratory decompensation.<br /></br>• “Currently in acute respiratory distress” with RR 30/min and use of accessory muscles → classic signs of a severe asthma exacerbation.<br /></br>• Bilaterally decreased breath sounds (“quiet chest”) → ominous clue that airflow through constricted bronchi is now so limited that little air movement is heard.<br /></br>• Pulse ox fell from 98 % → 90 % despite 4 L O2 → worsening V/Q mismatch from obstructed airways.<br /></br>• Chest x-ray shows hyperinflation identical to the film on admission → rules out new parenchymal process (e.g., infiltrate, edema, PTX).<br /></br>• Leukocytosis (18 000/µL) can be stress- or steroid-induced; not necessarily infection.  </p></br><p>Cognitive / metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Ask: “Is there anything new on CXR?” If no, parenchymal diseases (pneumonia, edema) drop down the list.<br /></br>2. Next: “Does the exam suggest focal vs diffuse pathology?” Bilateral, diffuse findings with quiet chest → diffuse bronchospasm.<br /></br>3. Always recall that the most common reason for sudden hypoxia in an asthmatic is progression of the attack itself.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thinking that leads to choice B:<br /></br>1. Acute asthma patient deteriorates → most common mechanism = worsening bronchoconstriction.<br /></br>2. Bilaterally decreased breath sounds + hyperinflation → severe air trapping.<br /></br>3. No new infiltrate, no unilateral absent breath sounds, no cardiogenic findings → rules out other listed etiologies.<br /></br>Take-home message: In an asthmatic who suddenly worsens with an unchanged hyperinflated CXR, think severe bronchospasm (“status asthmaticus”) first.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl (real life): You would record work of breathing, accessory muscle use, auscultatory findings, SpO2 trend, and immediate escalation of bronchodilator/ corticosteroid therapy ± consideration for intubation.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Asthma = chronic airway inflammation → exposure to trigger → bronchial smooth-muscle contraction, mucosal edema, ↑ mucus → airflow obstruction, ↑ airway resistance. In severe attacks, dynamic hyperinflation occurs, producing the “silent chest” and significant hypoxemia.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment and Diagnostics<br /></br>• Immediate: Re-dose rapid-acting β2-agonist (albuterol 2.5 mg nebulized q20 min × 3), add ipratropium, systemic corticosteroids (e.g., IV methylprednisolone), O2 to keep SpO2 &gt; 92 %.<br /></br>• If impending respiratory failure: prepare for intubation + mechanical ventilation with low tidal volume and long expiratory time to prevent auto-PEEP.<br /></br>• Ancillary tests: arterial blood gas if mental status changes (look for rising PaCO2, sign of fatigue).  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>• “Quiet chest = Tight chest” → very decreased breath sounds in an asthmatic means severe bronchospasm.<br /></br>• Hyperinflation + no new infiltrate on CXR → think asthma, COPD, or bronchiolitis (“air-trappers”), not pneumonia or edema.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Aspiration<br /></br>– Would show new infiltrate in dependent lung zones on CXR shortly after event; often follows witnessed vomiting or altered mental status. Not present here.  </p></br><p>C. Pneumonia<br /></br>– Typically causes new focal or diffuse infiltrate and often fever &gt;38 °C. Chest film unchanged; temperature only 37.3 °C.  </p></br><p>D. Pneumothorax<br /></br>– Produces unilateral absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation (if tension), sudden pleuritic pain. CXR would show collapsed lung and pleural line, not symmetric hyperinflation.  </p></br><p>E. Pulmonary edema<br /></br>– CXR: bilateral perihilar opacities (“bat-wing”), possible cardiomegaly; exam: crackles, S3. None seen.  </p></br><p>F. Pulmonary embolus<br /></br>– Usually normal CXR or wedge-shaped infarct; acute pleuritic chest pain and tachypnea without productive cough. PE is possible in differential for sudden hypoxia, but in an asthmatic with bilateral wheeze/air trapping and unchanged hyperinflation, bronchospasm is far more likely.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>When an asthmatic hospitalized for exacerbation suddenly worsens and the CXR is unchanged except for hyperinflation, the primary culprit is progression of bronchospasm leading to severe airflow limitation—manage as status asthmaticus.</p>
28. A 51-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 1-year history of restless sleep and fatigue in the morning. He says that his wife has asked him to sleep in a separate bedroom because of his excessive snoring during this time. The patient has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He is 191 cm (6 ft 3 in) tall and weighs 124 kg (273 lb); BMI is 34 kg/m2. His pulse is 82/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 150/90 mm Hg. Pulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. On cardiac examination, a loud S2 is heard at the second left intercostal space. Which of the following is most likely to be seen on echocardiography?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Decreased left ventricular ejection fraction</br>b. Decreased right ventricular ejection fraction</br>c. Increased pulmonary artery pressure</br>d. Increased pulmonary venous pressure</br>e. Left atrial hypertrophy</br>f. Right atrial hypertrophy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer:<br /></br>C. Increased pulmonary artery pressure  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• “Excessive snoring,” “restless sleep,” and morning “fatigue” in an obese (BMI 34) middle-aged man → classic red flags for obstructive sleep apnea (OSA).<br /></br>• Loud, single S2 heard best at the 2nd left intercostal space (pulmonic area) really means a loud P2 component → key physical-exam clue for pulmonary hypertension (PH).<br /></br>• No pulmonary crackles/rales, no lower-extremity edema, and normal lung exam → argues against left-sided heart failure or intrinsic lung parenchymal disease.<br /></br>Cognitive strategy: Any NBME stem that pairs OSA with a loud P2 is almost always testing your recognition that chronic nocturnal hypoxemia → pulmonary vasoconstriction → PH (cor pulmonale if advanced).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1: Diagnose the underlying problem → OSA.<br /></br>Step 2: Recall pathophysiology → repetitive upper-airway obstruction → intermittent hypoxia &amp; hypercapnia → reflex pulmonary vasoconstriction → remodeling of pulmonary arterioles → PH.<br /></br>Step 3: Translate finding to echo result → echocardiography measures elevated estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure (via TR jet velocity). Hence “increased pulmonary artery pressure.”<br /></br>Core takeaway: OSA → chronic hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction → pulmonary hypertension → loud P2.  </p></br><p>Documentation must include: symptoms/signs of OSA, BMI, BP, physical finding of loud P2, and plan for formal sleep study ± CPAP; echo ordered to quantify PH.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Intermittent hypoxia (every apneic episode) increases sympathetic tone and causes pulmonary vascular smooth-muscle constriction.<br /></br>• Long-term effect: medial hypertrophy &amp; intimal fibrosis of pulmonary arterioles → PH.<br /></br>• Loud P2 arises from forceful closure of a hypertensive pulmonary valve.<br /></br>• Untreated PH eventually leads to right-ventricular (RV) hypertrophy/failure (cor pulmonale).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>1. Diagnostic tests<br /></br>   – Polysomnography (gold standard for OSA).<br /></br>   – Transthoracic echo to estimate pulmonary artery pressure and assess RV size/function.<br /></br>2. Treatment of OSA<br /></br>   – CPAP (continuous positive airway pressure) at night—first-line.<br /></br>   – Weight loss; avoid alcohol/sedatives; possible mandibular advancement device, UPPP surgery if CPAP intolerant.<br /></br>3. Treatment of PH secondary to OSA<br /></br>   – Primarily treat underlying OSA; PH often improves once hypoxia is corrected.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Tip<br /></br>“OSA makes the PA loud”: Obstructive Sleep Apnea → Pulmonary Artery (pressure) ↑ → loud P2. Visualize a person snoring (Z-Z-Z) while a pressure gauge on the pulmonary artery climbs.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Explanations<br /></br>A. Decreased left ventricular ejection fraction<br /></br>   – Would produce S3, crackles, orthopnea; no loud P2. OSA does not directly impair LV systolic function.<br /></br>B. Decreased right ventricular ejection fraction<br /></br>   – RV dysfunction occurs late in untreated PH; early echo more commonly shows RV hypertrophy with preserved EF. Loud P2 points to pressure overload, not yet failure.<br /></br>D. Increased pulmonary venous pressure<br /></br>   – Reflects left-sided failure/mitral disease → pulmonary congestion, crackles; absent here.<br /></br>E. Left atrial hypertrophy<br /></br>   – Seen in chronic HTN, mitral stenosis, or LV diastolic dysfunction; again no supporting findings.<br /></br>F. Right atrial hypertrophy<br /></br>   – Also a late cor pulmonale finding; the primary abnormality is the elevated pulmonary artery pressure.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Obstructive sleep apnea causes chronic intermittent hypoxia → pulmonary vasoconstriction → pulmonary hypertension, manifested clinically by a loud P2 and confirmed on echocardiography as increased pulmonary artery pressure.</p>
29. A 77-year-old man comes to the office because of a 1-day history of bloody diarrhea and moderate abdominal pain. He has had a decreased appetite and four to five bloody stools daily during this period. He was discharged from the hospital 1 week ago following management of an anterior wall myocardial infarction. He has not had fever, chills, vomiting, or recent exposure to sick contacts since he left the hospital. He has not traveled outside of the USA during the past year. His medications are enalapril, atorvastatin, metoprolol, and daily aspirin. He appears moderately ill. His pulse is 95/min, and blood pressure is 105/72 mm Hg. Cardiac examination shows a grade 2/6 systolic ejection murmur. Abdominal examination shows diffuse tenderness that is most prominent in the left lower quadrant; there is voluntary guarding but no rebound tenderness, and no masses are palpated. Results of laboratory studies are shown:</br>Hematocrit         39 percent</br>Leukocyte count    12,000/mm³</br>Platelet count     175,000/mm³</br>Serum</br>Na⁺                139 mEq/L</br>K⁺                 3.9 mEq/L</br>Cl⁻                106 mEq/L</br>HCO₃⁻              22 mEq/L</br>Urea nitrogen      29 mg/dL</br>Creatinine         1.2 mg/dL</br>Fecal leukocytes   0/mm³</br>Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Angiodysplasia</br>b. Colon cancer</br>c. Cytomegalovirus proctitis</br>d. Diverticulitis</br>e. Escherichia coli O157:H7 infection</br>f. Ischemic colitis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>f. Ischemic colitis  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Age &amp; vascular history: 77-year-old man, discharged 1 week ago after an acute MI → indicates diffuse atherosclerosis and recent low-flow states.<br /></br>• Timeline: Sudden onset (1 day) of crampy abdominal pain followed by hematochezia—classic for ischemic colitis (pain usually precedes bleeding by minutes-hours).<br /></br>• Location of pain: Left lower quadrant → matches the typical watershed areas (splenic flexure, rectosigmoid junction).<br /></br>• Exam/labs: Mild leukocytosis, modestly ↑ BUN/Cr, no peritoneal signs. Stool NO fecal leukocytes (mucosal slough rather than neutrophil-rich invasive infection).<br /></br>• Vital signs: Borderline pressure (105/72) after recent MI = potential hypoperfusion trigger.  </p></br><p>Test-taking pearls (cognitive/metacognitive):<br /></br>1. In any elderly patient with cardiovascular disease who acutely develops abdominal pain + bloody stool, think “ischemia” first.<br /></br>2. Left-sided pain + hematochezia = ischemic colitis; right-sided pain + severe metabolic acidosis/hematochezia = acute mesenteric ischemia.<br /></br>3. Fecal leukocytes/PCR positive → invasive infectious colitis; absent leukocytes steers away from infection.  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. Identify precipitants of hypoperfusion (MI, CHF, sepsis, hypotension, surgery).<br /></br>2. Localize pain: LLQ or lower abdomen = colon watershed zones.<br /></br>3. Assess bleeding pattern: Hematochezia appearing shortly after pain = mucosal sloughing.<br /></br>4. Exclude common mimics: infection (positive stool WBCs/culture), diverticulitis (fever, usually no bleeding), angiodysplasia (painless).  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Ischemic colitis is the most common form of intestinal ischemia; presents with rapid-onset LLQ pain and hematochezia in older, atherosclerotic patients after a hypotensive event.  </p></br><p>Documentation must include: character of pain, onset relative to bleeding, hemodynamics, past vascular events, abdominal exam, stool findings, plan for CT abdomen w/ contrast or colonoscopy if stable.  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: Transient low flow in small mesenteric arteries → nonocclusive ischemia of watershed zones.<br /></br>• Mechanism: Mucosal hypoxia → reperfusion injury → segmental mucosal necrosis &amp; superficial ulceration → bleeding.<br /></br>• Risk factors: Age &gt;60, atherosclerosis, MI/CHF, hypotension, vasoconstrictive drugs, hemodialysis.<br /></br>• Clinical manifestations: Crampy pain (often LLQ) → urge to defecate → bloody or maroon stools within 24 h; mild leukocytosis, metabolic acidosis if severe.  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management<br /></br>Diagnostics (after initial stabilization):<br /></br>1. CT abdomen with IV contrast: segmental colonic wall thickening, “thumbprinting.”<br /></br>2. Colonoscopy (definitive): edematous, friable mucosa with longitudinal ulcerations.  </p></br><p>Management:<br /></br>• Supportive = first-line: IV fluids, bowel rest, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone + metronidazole) to cover trans-mural bacterial translocation, close hemodynamic monitoring.<br /></br>• Surgery: indicated for peritonitis, transmural gangrene, perforation, persistent bleeding, or strictures.  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Memory Aids<br /></br>“IsCHEMIC”:<br /></br>I – Increased age/Ill cardiac output<br /></br>s – Sudden pain<br /></br>C – Crampy LLQ<br /></br>H – Hematochezia shortly after pain<br /></br>E – Event (MI, surgery, shock) precedes symptoms<br /></br>M – Mucosal sloughing (no fecal WBC)<br /></br>I – Imaging: Thumbprinting<br /></br>C – Conservative care first  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Angiodysplasia – Causes painless episodic bleeding, usually right colon; no abdominal tenderness.<br /></br>b. Colon cancer – Bleeding is chronic/occult; presents with weight loss, iron-deficiency anemia, change in bowel habits, not acute pain + blood.<br /></br>c. CMV proctitis – Seen in severely immunocompromised (AIDS, post-transplant). This patient is immunocompetent; CMV causes fever, deep ulcerations, tenesmus.<br /></br>d.
