Diverticulitis – LLQ pain with fever/leukocytosis; bleeding is uncommon during acute infection. CT would show inflamed diverticula; stool blood rare.<br /></br>e. E. coli O157:H7 – Hemorrhagic colitis usually in younger patients after undercooked beef exposure; prominent abdominal pain, often no/low fever; can have fecal WBCs and risk of HUS. This patient lacks exposure history and is elderly with vascular risk factors.  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>An elderly, atherosclerotic patient with acute LLQ pain followed rapidly by hematochezia and no fecal leukocytes has ischemic colitis—manage supportively and search for precipitating hypoperfusion events.</p>
30. A 46-year-old woman, gravida 3, para 3, comes to the emergency department because of a 4-day history of “crampy” lower abdominal pain. She voids six times daily and once at night. Her last bowel movement was 3 days ago. She has not had fever, nausea, vomiting, or changes in appetite. She has hypertension, hypothyroidism, chronic back pain, and overactive bladder. Medications are enalapril, levothyroxine, tramadol, mirabegron, and acetaminophen. She is sexually active with her husband. She appears mildly uncomfortable. Temperature is 37.5 C (99.5 F), pulse is 64/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 132/86 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 98 percent. No abdominal tenderness is noted. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Adverse effect of which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's presenting symptom?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Acetaminophen</br>b. Enalapril</br>c. Levothyroxine</br>d. Mirabegron</br>e. Tramadol</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Tramadol  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Strategy<br /></br>1. Key clues<br /></br>   • 4-day history of “crampy” lower abdominal pain<br /></br>   • No bowel movement for 3 days (constipation)<br /></br>   • Taking an opioid analgesic (tramadol) for chronic back pain<br /></br>   • Physical exam essentially benign (no peritoneal signs, afebrile)  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Pattern recognition<br /></br>   Constipation + vague colicky abdominal discomfort in a patient on an opioid = classic “opioid-induced constipation.”<br /></br>   • Pertinent positives → constipation, abdominal cramps<br /></br>   • Pertinent negatives → no N/V, no fever, normal vitals → argues against infectious or inflammatory GI pathology.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Cognitive “shortcut” for future questions<br /></br>   Whenever you see chronic use of any μ-opioid agonist (morphine, oxycodone, hydrocodone, codeine, tramadol) think:<br /></br>   Pain relief, miosis, respiratory depression, CONSTIPATION.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (why tramadol?)<br /></br>Step-wise approach<br /></br>1. Identify symptom complex: Constipation + mild crampy pain.<br /></br>2. Scan medication list for drugs that slow GI motility.<br /></br>3. μ-opioid agonists → ↓ acetylcholine release in myenteric plexus → ↓ peristalsis, ↑ water reabsorption → hard stool, infrequent BMs, colicky pain.<br /></br>4. Patient has no other drug or disease explanations as compelling.  </p></br><p>Take-home message<br /></br>Opioid analgesics are the most common iatrogenic cause of new-onset constipation in otherwise stable adults.  </p></br><p>Documentation point (“real life”)<br /></br>Document bowel habits, opioid regimen, and start a bowel regimen (senna + docusate ± polyethylene glycol). Consider peripherally acting μ-opioid receptor antagonists (methylnaltrexone, naloxegol) if refractory.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Tramadol = weak μ-opioid receptor agonist + inhibits NE &amp; serotonin reuptake.<br /></br>• μ-receptor activation in GI tract:<br /></br>  - Inhibits excitatory cholinergic neurons → decreased intestinal propulsive contractions.<br /></br>  - Increases anal sphincter tone.<br /></br>  Result → stool desiccation, delayed transit, abdominal cramping.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>First-line bowel regimen for any chronic opioid:<br /></br>• Stimulant laxative (senna or bisacodyl) + stool softener (docusate).<br /></br>• Add osmotic agent (polyethylene glycol, lactulose) if needed.<br /></br>• Refractory cases → PAMORAs (methylnaltrexone, naloxegol, naldemedine).<br /></br>No imaging is required unless red-flag symptoms (severe pain, vomiting, fever, peritonitis).  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids<br /></br>“Opioids STOP the 3 P’s: Pain, Pooping, and Pupils (they constrict).”<br /></br>“Tra-MA-dOL makes the bowels crawl… SLOW.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>A. Acetaminophen – Major toxicity = hepatic necrosis (↑AST/ALT) after overdose; does not slow gut motility.<br /></br>B. Enalapril – ACE inhibitor → cough, angioedema, hyperkalemia, creatinine rise; GI motility unaffected.<br /></br>C. Levothyroxine – Excess dosing produces hyperthyroid symptoms (diarrhea, weight loss, palpitations), not constipation.<br /></br>D. Mirabegron – β3-agonist for overactive bladder. Adverse effects: hypertension, urinary retention, nasopharyngitis. Constipation can occur but is uncommon and far less characteristic than with opioids.<br /></br>E. Tramadol – Classic, high-yield cause of opioid-induced constipation → matches vignette.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>New-onset or worsening constipation plus vague lower abdominal cramps in a patient on any opioid (including “weak” tramadol) should prompt immediate consideration of opioid-induced constipation and initiation of a prophylactic bowel regimen.</p>
31. A 69-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 6-day history of blood in her urine. She has not had pain. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no abnormalities. Urinalysis shows many RBCs. Cystoscopy shows a 2-cm mass in the bladder. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Bladder polyp</br>b. Leiomyosarcoma</br>c. Papilloma</br>d. Transitional cell carcinoma</br>e. Vesicle calculus</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Transitional cell (urothelial) carcinoma  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key words/phrases: “69-year-old,” “6-day history of blood in her urine,” “no pain” (painless gross hematuria), “cystoscopy shows a 2-cm mass in the bladder.”<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: advanced age, painless hematuria, visible mass. Classic “bladder cancer until proven otherwise.”<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: no dysuria, no suprapubic pain, no history of recurrent infections (argues against calculus or infection).<br /></br>• Cognitive approach:<br /></br>  1. Any painless gross hematuria → think malignancy first.<br /></br>  2. In the bladder, &gt;90 % of malignant tumors arise from the urothelium = transitional cell carcinoma (TCC).<br /></br>  3. A small, solitary papillary mass on cystoscopy fits the common endoscopic appearance of TCC.<br /></br>Metacognitive tip: Whenever you see painless hematuria in an older patient, place “urothelial carcinoma” at the top of your differential and hunt for confirmatory clues (mass on cystoscopy, smoking history, occupational exposures, etc.).  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise linkage to choice D:<br /></br>1. Symptom → painless hematuria → triggers work-up for malignancy.<br /></br>2. Location → bladder mass visualized.<br /></br>3. Epidemiology → urothelial carcinoma is by far the most common primary bladder malignancy in Western countries.<br /></br>Take-home message: Painless gross hematuria in an older adult = transitional cell (urothelial) carcinoma until proven otherwise.  </p></br><p>Real-world documentation: gross hematuria onset/duration, risk factors (tobacco, chemical exposures, cyclophosphamide), cystoscopy findings, plan for biopsy + staging CT urography.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Origin: Urothelial/transitional epithelium lining renal calyces → ureter → bladder → proximal urethra.<br /></br>• Risk factors: Cigarette smoking (#1), aniline dyes, aromatic amines (rubber, dye, textile industries), phenacetin, cyclophosphamide, chronic Schistosoma haematobium infection, radiation.<br /></br>• Gross patterns: Papillary, sessile/flat, or mixed.<br /></br>• Natural course: Multifocal, high recurrence; field‐effect along urothelium.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Pearls<br /></br>Diagnostics:<br /></br>1. Cystoscopy with biopsy = gold standard.<br /></br>2. Urine cytology (high specificity for high-grade lesions).<br /></br>3. CT urography or MRI pelvis for staging.  </p></br><p>Treatment (stage dependent):<br /></br>• Non-muscle-invasive (Ta/T1/CIS): Transurethral resection of bladder tumor (TURBT) + intravesical therapy (BCG immunotherapy or mitomycin C).<br /></br>• Muscle-invasive (≥T2): Radical cystectomy + urinary diversion ± neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemo.<br /></br>• Metastatic: Systemic cisplatin-based chemotherapy, immunotherapy (PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors).  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Painless Peeing of Blood in a Person Past middle age → think Transitional Cell Carcinoma.”<br /></br>Mnemonic “Pee SAC” for risk factors:<br /></br>P – Phenacetin,<br /></br>S – Smoking,<br /></br>A – Aniline dyes/Aromatic amines,<br /></br>C – Cyclophosphamide.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Bladder polyp (inflammatory polyp): Uncommon, usually arises in younger pts after infections or catheter trauma; would still be visualized but is far less likely than carcinoma in a 69-year-old.<br /></br>b. Leiomyosarcoma: Rare malignant smooth-muscle tumor; presents with bulky intramural mass, often causes irritative voiding symptoms; incidence in bladder extremely low compared with TCC.<br /></br>c. Papilloma: Benign urothelial papillomas occur, but they are exceedingly rare (&lt;1 % of bladder tumors). In an elderly patient, a urothelial neoplasm is overwhelmingly more likely to be malignant.<br /></br>e. Vesical (bladder) calculus: Would appear as a stone, typically with irritative voiding symptoms, suprapubic pain, or urinary tract infection; cystoscopy would note a hard, mobile calculus rather than a soft tissue mass.  </p></br><p>Key distinguishing feature: Only transitional cell carcinoma matches the epidemiologic likelihood and classic presentation of painless hematuria plus a visible intraluminal mass in an elderly patient.</p>
32. An 18-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of severe pelvic and lower abdominal pain 30 minutes after he was involved in a motorcycle collision in which he struck a parked car. He was wearing a helmet and did not have loss of consciousness. He is alert. His pulse is 120/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 130/50 mm Hg. The airway is clear and open, and breath sounds are normal. The abdomen is nontender. There are ecchymoses over the pelvis and scrotum. The pelvis is unstable. There is blood at the urethral meatus. Pulses are 2+ in all extremities. An x-ray of the pelvis shows an anteroposterior compression fracture. A pelvic binder is placed. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management of this patient's urinary tract injury?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis</br>b. Cystography</br>c. Placement of a urinary catheter</br>d. Pyelography</br>e. Retrograde urethrography</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>E. Retrograde urethrography  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Mechanism of injury: high-energy pelvic trauma (motorcycle collision) → anteroposterior compression (“open-book”) pelvic fracture on x-ray.<br /></br>2. Key clues pointing to urethral injury:<br /></br>   • Blood at the urethral meatus (most specific single sign).<br /></br>   • Scrotal/pelvic ecchymosis → pelvic hematoma tracking into perineum.<br /></br>   • Unstable pelvis (posterior urethra tethers to the pubic symphysis → easily torn).<br /></br>3. Pertinent negatives: abdomen nontender (makes intraperitoneal hemorrhage less likely), normal breath sounds &amp; airway secured.<br /></br>4. Vital signs: tachycardic but normotensive—he is hemodynamically acceptable for imaging.  </p></br><p>High-yield cognitive rule:<br /></br>“Blood at the meatus after pelvic trauma? STOP—no catheter until you prove the urethra is intact.”<br /></br>First test = retrograde urethrogram.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. Primary survey: ABCs already addressed.<br /></br>2. Pelvic binder controls venous bleeding.<br /></br>3. Suspect posterior urethral injury → must delineate urethra before instrumentation.<br /></br>4. Perform retrograde urethrography; if contrast extravasates you manage urethral injury (suprapubic tube ± repair). If no extravasation, you can safely pass a Foley catheter and proceed to cystography for bladder evaluation.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Do NOT blindly insert a Foley in the setting of pelvic fracture with blood at the meatus—perform retrograde urethrography first.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): mechanism, hemodynamics, binder placement, gross hematuria/ blood at meatus, decision for RUG, results, subsequent catheter plan.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Posterior urethral injury (membranous urethra) occurs when the puboprostatic ligament shears during pelvic fracture.<br /></br>• Classic findings: inability to void, high-riding or non-palpable prostate on DRE, perineal/scrotal ecchymosis, blood at meatus.<br /></br>• Anterior urethral injury (bulbar) usually from straddle injury, presents with perineal “butterfly” hematoma.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Pearls<br /></br>Retrograde urethrogram: </br>– Inject contrast into distal urethra under fluoroscopy/plain films.<br /></br>– If extravasation → place suprapubic catheter and plan urethral repair (delayed primary or staged).<br /></br>– If no leakage → pass Foley then perform cystography to assess bladder for concomitant rupture.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>Mnemonic: “Pelvic fracture + Meatus red → ‘RUG’ ahead.” (RUG = Retrograde UrethroGram).<br /></br>Visualization: Picture a STOP sign on the Foley tray until a contrast picture of the urethra is obtained.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Incorrect<br /></br>a. CT scan of abdomen/pelvis: Excellent for solid-organ injury and bladder rupture but does not reliably define urethral integrity; catheter required for contrast filling of bladder, which you cannot place yet.<br /></br>b. Cystography (CT or plain film): Evaluates bladder rupture, also needs catheterization first; contraindicated before urethra is cleared.<br /></br>c. Placement of a urinary catheter: Blind catheterization risks converting a partial tear into a complete transection, worsening hemorrhage and stricture formation.<br /></br>d. Pyelography: Used for renal collecting system/ureter evaluation, irrelevant initial test here; still requires intact lower urinary tract access.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Pelvic fracture with blood at the urethral meatus mandates retrograde urethrography prior to any catheterization or further urinary tract imaging.</p>
33. A 27-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 1, at 14 weeks' gestation comes to the physician for her first prenatal visit. She is concerned because 2 years ago, during her first pregnancy, she delivered a stillborn at 38 weeks' gestation 24 hours after she had noticed decreased fetal movement. She has no history of serious illness. Examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Monthly 1-hour glucose tolerance tests</br>b. Nonstress testing in the third trimester</br>c. Fetal Doppler ultrasonography at 36 weeks' gestation</br>d. Amniocentesis for karyotype analysis</br>e. Cesarean delivery at 37 weeks' gestation</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Nonstress testing in the third trimester  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test‐Taking Strategy<br /></br>1. Key clue: “Delivered a stillborn at 38 weeks after noticing ↓ fetal movement.”<br /></br>   • Unexplained term stillbirth is a red flag for recurrent placental insufficiency or occult cord accident.<br /></br>2. Current pregnancy is otherwise normal (G2P1, 14 weeks, normal exam).<br /></br>3. Question asks for “most appropriate NEXT step.” At 14 weeks, no intervention changes fetal outcome now; instead, plan evidence-based antepartum surveillance later in gestation.<br /></br>4. High-yield rule: A history of unexplained fetal demise = automatic indication for antenatal testing (NST or biophysical profile) beginning at 32–34 weeks (earlier if the loss occurred earlier).  </p></br><p>Cognitive road map for similar questions:<br /></br>• Identify past poor obstetric outcomes ⟶ classify the pregnancy as “high risk.”<br /></br>• Recall guidelines: NST or BPP q1–2 wk (± umbilical artery Doppler if growth restriction) starting 32 wk.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1: Classify risk. Previous unexplained stillbirth = “previous adverse pregnancy outcome” per ACOG → high-risk category.<br /></br>Step 2: Choose the surveillance modality. NST is the least invasive, easiest, and standard first-line test for fetal well-being.<br /></br>Step 3: Timing. Begin at 32 wk (third trimester) because earlier testing does not improve outcomes and increases false-positives.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Prior unexplained intrauterine fetal demise warrants third-trimester antepartum surveillance with weekly/biweekly NST (or BPP).  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: “History of term IUFD—plan NSTs q1 wk starting 32 wk; counsel re fetal movement counting.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology in 60 sec<br /></br>• Most late stillbirths are due to placental insufficiency, cord accident, or unrecognized maternal disease.<br /></br>• These conditions recur in up to 10 % of subsequent pregnancies.<br /></br>• NST detects episodic fetal heart rate accelerations, an indirect marker of intact autonomic function and adequate oxygenation.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Relevant Tests &amp; Management Ladder<br /></br>1. Primary surveillance:<br /></br>   • NST weekly/biweekly from 32 wk.<br /></br>   • If non-reactive → BPP, contraction stress test, or Doppler studies.<br /></br>2. Delivery timing: If surveillance remains reassuring, deliver at 39 wk; earlier (37–38 wk) only if testing becomes abnormal.<br /></br>3. Additional work-up (not asked here) can include thrombophilia panel, maternal diabetes screen, and autopsy/placental path in the index loss, but these are not acute management for the current pregnancy.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids<br /></br>“Stillbirth? Start Surveillance at 32” → S-alliteration helps recall start time.<br /></br>NST “No Stress to the baby”—non-invasive, belts only.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Monthly 1-h glucose tolerance tests<br /></br>   • GDM screening is one-time (24–28 wk); only high-risk patients get early screening, not monthly repeats.<br /></br>c. Fetal Doppler US at 36 wk<br /></br>   • Umbilical artery Dopplers are for known IUGR or maternal HTN, not routine after isolated IUFD.<br /></br>d. Amniocentesis for karyotype analysis<br /></br>   • Indicated for fetal genetic diagnosis (AMA, abnormal screen). Prior stillbirth alone is not an indication.<br /></br>e. Cesarean delivery at 37 wk<br /></br>   • Route and timing of delivery depend on current fetal status; prophylactic pre-labor cesarean is not recommended for prior unexplained stillbirth.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: Thinking an immediate invasive or interventional step is needed. The key is preventive surveillance at the gestational age when risk recurs.</p>
34. A 25-year-old woman comes to the physician for a follow-up examination because she continues to have urinary frequency and pain with urination despite a 1-week course of trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole followed by a 1-week course of nitrofurantoin. A urine culture at the onset of her symptoms 2 weeks ago showed vaginal flora but no specific organism. She is sexually active and uses an oral contraceptive. Examination shows a mildly erythematous, tender urethra. Purulent material can be expressed from the periurethral glands. Vaginal examination shows no abnormalities, and there is no vaginal discharge. Bimanual examination shows a nontender cervix and uterus. The cervix is friable and bleeds easily after a Pap smear. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Bartholin gland abscess</br>b. Chlamydia trachomatis infection</br>c. Interstitial cystitis</br>d. Pseudomonal urinary tract infection</br>e. Pyelonephritis</br>f. Urethral diverticulum</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Chlamydia trachomatis infection  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Strategy  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key positive clues<br /></br>   • 25-year-old, sexually active woman (high prevalence age group).<br /></br>   • Dysuria + frequency mimicking “UTI.”<br /></br>   • Urine culture grew only “vaginal flora” (essentially negative → sterile pyuria).<br /></br>   • Symptoms persisted after 2 different empiric UTI regimens (TMP-SMX, nitrofurantoin) → not an E. coli cystitis.<br /></br>   • Erythematous tender urethra with purulent material from peri-urethral glands = urethritis.<br /></br>   • Friable cervix that bleeds with minimal manipulation = cervicitis.<br /></br>   • No vaginal discharge, no flank pain, afebrile → rules out vaginitis and pyelonephritis.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pattern recognition<br /></br>   Dysuria + sterile urine culture + urethritis/cervicitis in a young sexually active woman → think sexually transmitted pathogen, especially Chlamydia (or Gonorrhea). A friable ectopy-like cervix is classic for Chlamydia cervicitis.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Any “UTI” that 1) has negative cultures or 2) does not improve with standard antibiotics should prompt you to pivot to an STI differential (Chlamydia/Gonorrhea, HSV).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (why choice B is correct)  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Identify site of infection: urethra + cervix, NOT bladder/kidney.<br /></br>2. Culture negative → most common cause of nongonococcal urethritis is Chlamydia trachomatis (serovars D-K).<br /></br>3. Typical UTI drugs (TMP-SMX, nitrofurantoin) have no activity against intracellular Chlamydia, explaining treatment failure.<br /></br>4. Therefore the persistent dysuria is due to Chlamydia urethritis/cervicitis.</p></br><p>Take-home concept: “Sterile pyuria or negative culture UTI symptoms in a young woman = screen for Chlamydia/Gonorrhea.”  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Record GU ROS, sexual history, chlamydia–gonorrhea NAAT testing, partner notification, and empiric treatment (single-dose azithromycin 1 g PO or doxycycline 100 mg PO bid × 7 days, plus ceftriaxone if gonorrhea not ruled out).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology High-Yield Review  </p></br><p>• Chlamydia trachomatis = obligate intracellular gram-negative organism.<br /></br>• Infects columnar epithelium of cervix, urethra, endometrium; triggers inflammation → friability, mucopurulent discharge.<br /></br>• Presents as urethritis, cervicitis, PID, or asymptomatic infection.<br /></br>• Complications: infertility, ectopic pregnancy, conjunctivitis/newborn pneumonia.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management Snapshot  </p></br><p>First-line therapy (cover both CT &amp; GC empirically unless GC ruled out):<br /></br>• Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM once (N. gonorrhoeae) PLUS<br /></br>• Doxycycline 100 mg PO bid × 7 days  (or azithromycin 1 g PO once if pregnant/non-adherent).<br /></br>Advise abstinence × 7 days and treat all partners from last 60 days.</p></br><p>Diagnostic test: NAAT (nucleic acid amplification test) of urine or cervical/urethral swab.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“C-DUF” for Chlamydia in women:<br /></br>C – Cervicitis (friable)<br /></br>D – Dysuria w/ negative culture<br /></br>U – Urethritis (purulent at meatus)<br /></br>F – Failure of TMP-SMX / nitrofurantoin  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Bartholin gland abscess – Painful fluctuant mass at 4 or 8 o’clock of vestibule; no urethral discharge or cervicitis.<br /></br>c. Interstitial cystitis – Chronic (&gt;6 wk) suprapubic pain relieved by voiding; bladder culture negative but no purulent discharge or friable cervix.<br /></br>d. Pseudomonal UTI – Would grow Pseudomonas on culture; nitrofurantoin ineffective but TMP-SMX often partially active; no cervicitis.<br /></br>e. Pyelonephritis – Fever, flank pain, CVA tenderness, positive urine culture; not present here.<br /></br>f. Urethral diverticulum – Post-void dribbling, dyspareunia, tender anterior vaginal wall mass; does not cause friable cervix or respond to STI therapy.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway  </p></br><p>Persistent “UTI” symptoms with a negative culture and a friable cervix = Chlamydia trachomatis urethritis/cervicitis until proven otherwise. Always test and empirically treat both the patient and recent partners.</p>
35. A 37-year-old woman comes to the physician for a routine follow-up examination. She has a 12-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus treated with glipizide and diet. She has urinary frequency when she does not follow her diet. She is otherwise healthy. There is no family history of diabetes mellitus. Her pulse is 72/min, and blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. Funduscopic examination shows scattered microaneurysms. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is most likely to determine this patient's risk for diabetic nephropathy?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Measurement of hemoglobin A1c</br>b. Measurement of serum albumin concentration</br>c. Measurement of serum glucose concentration</br>d. Measurement of urine microalbumin concentration</br>e. Measurement of urine protein by dipstick</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Measurement of urine microalbumin concentration  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues: 12-year history of type 2 diabetes, scattered retinal microaneurysms (background diabetic retinopathy = microvascular disease already present), otherwise healthy physical exam.<br /></br>• Question stem: “Which of the following is most likely to determine this patient’s risk for diabetic nephropathy?” → They are asking for the single best screening test that predicts EARLY kidney involvement before an overt decline in GFR or heavy proteinuria develops.<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: duration of diabetes (&gt;10 yrs), microvascular eye disease → puts her in the time window where nephropathy can begin.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: normal BP (130/80) and no edema → nephropathy, if present, is still in the incipient stage.  </p></br><p>Test-taking mindset: Whenever a diabetic pt with any microvascular complication (retinopathy, neuropathy) appears, think, “Do I need to screen for microalbuminuria?” The USMLE loves this association.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Diabetic nephropathy begins with glomerular hyperfiltration and mesangial expansion → loss of negative charge on the basement membrane → leakage of SMALL amounts of albumin (30–300 mg/day).<br /></br>2. This stage (microalbuminuria) is still reversible with strict glycemic and BP control (ACE inhibitors / ARBs).<br /></br>3. Therefore, detecting microalbumin is the earliest and most sensitive way to quantify risk.<br /></br>Take-home: Annual urine microalbumin (albumin-to-creatinine ratio) in all type 2 DM pts starting at diagnosis.  </p></br><p>What would be documented: “Urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio ordered to screen for incipient diabetic nephropathy.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Chronic hyperglycemia → non-enzymatic glycosylation of GBM &amp; efferent arteriolar hyalinosis → ↑ intraglomerular pressure → microalbumin leak.<br /></br>• Progression: microalbuminuria (30–300 mg/day) → macroalbuminuria (&gt;300 mg/day) → declining GFR → ESRD.<br /></br>• ACEi/ARB ↓ intraglomerular pressure &amp; slow this cascade.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>• First-line BP agent in diabetic pts with microalbuminuria: ACE inhibitor (lisinopril) or ARB (losartan).<br /></br>• Labs: Spot urine albumin/creatinine ratio is preferred over 24-h collection. Repeat 2× to confirm.<br /></br>• Tight glycemic control (target HbA1c &lt;7%) &amp; BP &lt;130/80 further lower risk.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aid<br /></br>“Micro beats Macro”:<br /></br>Micro-albumin screen beats macro-protein dipstick for EARLY detection.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Hemoglobin A1c<br /></br>• Reflects average glycemia over 3 mo; important for overall control but does NOT specifically quantify kidney damage or current nephropathy risk. Two pts with same A1c can differ in renal risk depending on BP, genetics, etc.  </p></br><p>b. Serum albumin concentration<br /></br>• Falls only after heavy protein loss or liver disease; by the time hypoalbuminemia appears, nephropathy is advanced with edema—too late for risk prediction.  </p></br><p>c. Serum glucose concentration<br /></br>• Single point-in-time value; tells nothing about chronic microvascular damage or nephropathy risk.  </p></br><p>e. Urine protein by dipstick<br /></br>• Standard dipstick detects albumin only when &gt;300 mg/day (macroalbuminuria). It will be negative during the early reversible stage (30–300 mg/day). Therefore, it misses the very patients we want to identify.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Urine microalbumin measurement (albumin-to-creatinine ratio) is the most sensitive and earliest indicator of diabetic nephropathy risk and should be obtained annually in all patients with type 2 diabetes starting at diagnosis.</p>
36. Two months after successful resection of a benign adrenal pheochromocytoma, a 47-year-old man comes for a follow-up examination. He has had occasional  </br>episodes of mild diarrhea over the past 6 weeks but is otherwise well. There is a family history of endocrine tumors. His blood pressure is 100/60 mm Hg. Examination shows a 2-cm, firm nodule in the right lobe of the thyroid gland. Laboratory studies are most likely to show which of the following serum concentrations?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Decreased calcitonin  </br>b. Decreased calcium  </br>c. Decreased thyroid-stimulating hormone  </br>d. Increased calcitonin  </br>e. Increased calcium  </br>f. Increased magnesium  </br>g. Increased thyroid-stimulating hormone  </br>h. Increased triiodothyronine (T3)</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Increased calcitonin  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>“Two months after successful resection of a benign adrenal pheochromocytoma … family history of endocrine tumors”<br /></br>   • Pheochromocytoma + positive family history immediately raises suspicion for a Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia (MEN) syndrome.  </li></br><li>“2-cm, firm nodule in the right lobe of the thyroid”<br /></br>   • In the MEN syndromes that include pheochromocytoma, the associated thyroid tumor is almost always medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC).  </li></br><li>“Occasional episodes of mild diarrhea”<br /></br>   • Classic systemic symptom of MTC; caused by hormone/peptide (calcitonin, vasoactive intestinal peptide, serotonin-like substances) secretion.  </li></br><li>Normal/low blood pressure now (100/60 mm Hg)<br /></br>   • Supports successful pheochromocytoma removal; residual hypertension would make recurrent/metastatic pheo more likely.</li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive road map for similar questions<br /></br>Step 1: Spot the key tumor in the stem (pheochromocytoma).<br /></br>Step 2: Ask yourself “What heritable syndromes include this tumor?” → MEN2A, MEN2B, von Hippel-Lindau, NF1.<br /></br>Step 3: Look for the second or third characteristic tumor. A solitary thyroid nodule in this setting is MTC until proven otherwise.<br /></br>Step 4: Recall the hallmark lab abnormality for MTC → high serum calcitonin.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• MEN2A and MEN2B are caused by germ-line activating mutations in the RET proto-oncogene.<br /></br>• Both subtypes share:<br /></br>  – Medullary thyroid carcinoma (parafollicular C-cell origin → secretes calcitonin).<br /></br>  – Pheochromocytoma (adrenal medulla catecholamine tumor).<br /></br>• MEN2A additionally has parathyroid hyperplasia (→ hypercalcemia), whereas MEN2B has mucosal/intestinal neuromas and marfanoid habitus.<br /></br>• The patient’s diarrhea + thyroid nodule after a pheochromocytoma all line up with MTC → check calcitonin.</p></br><p>Take-home message: In any patient with a personal or family history of pheochromocytoma and a thyroid nodule, the single best screening/confirmatory lab is serum calcitonin.</p></br><p>Documentation points (real-life clinic note)<br /></br>– FHx of MEN-type tumors.<br /></br>– Prior adrenal pathology report confirming pheochromocytoma.<br /></br>– Physical exam: solitary thyroid nodule.<br /></br>– Plan: serum calcitonin, neck ultrasound, genetic counseling, RET testing, consider prophylactic/therapeutic total thyroidectomy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)  </p></br><p>Medullary Thyroid Carcinoma<br /></br>• Cell of origin: Parafollicular C cells (neural crest derivatives).<br /></br>• Hormone: Calcitonin (reduces serum Ca²⁺ by ↑ renal excretion and ↓ osteoclastic bone resorption; in practice has minimal Ca²⁺ effect but is an excellent tumor marker).<br /></br>• Secreted peptides can cause: watery diarrhea, flushing, abdominal pain.<br /></br>• Sporadic vs familial: Familial cases (MEN2A/2B, familial MTC) are almost always RET-mutated and bilateral/multifocal.<br /></br>• Treatment: Total thyroidectomy with central lymph-node dissection.<br /></br>• Surveillance: Serum calcitonin and carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) post-op.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>First diagnostic step in this scenario:<br /></br>• Serum calcitonin (and CEA) to confirm hormonally active MTC.  </p></br><p>Further work-up:<br /></br>• High-resolution neck ultrasound.<br /></br>• RET genetic testing (patient &amp; first-degree relatives).<br /></br>• If MEN2A suspected, check serum Ca²⁺/PTH and screen for hyperparathyroidism.<br /></br>• If MEN2B phenotype present, screen for mucosal neuromas and skeletal anomalies.  </p></br><p>Management:<br /></br>• Definitive: Total thyroidectomy (often prophylactic in gene-positive patients, timing based on RET mutation risk category).<br /></br>• Lifelong monitoring with calcitonin and CEA.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“MEN 2 = Pheochromocytoma + ‘C’-Cell tumor”<br /></br>• Think “2 C’s” → Calcitonin from C-cells.  </p></br><p>“Pheo before ‘Thy-oh’”<br /></br>• Always rule out/control pheochromocytoma first (alpha-blockade / adrenalectomy) before thyroid surgery to avoid hypertensive crisis.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Decreased calcitonin<br /></br>   • Opposite of what MTC produces. A decreased level would argue against medullary carcinoma.  </p></br><p>b. Decreased calcium<br /></br>   • Calcitonin can slightly lower calcium, but clinically serum Ca²⁺ usually remains normal. Significant hypocalcemia would suggest postsurgical hypoparathyroidism or acute pancreatitis, neither present here.  </p></br><p>c / g. Thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) abnormalities<br /></br>   • MTC does not arise from follicular cells; TSH is typically normal. Elevated TSH would be seen in primary hypothyroidism; suppressed TSH in hyperthyroidism. No hyper-/hypo-thyroid symptoms are present.  </p></br><p>e. Increased calcium<br /></br>   • Would fit primary hyperparathyroidism, seen in MEN2A (parathyroid hyperplasia). This patient’s only MEN components mentioned are pheochromocytoma and MTC; no kidney stones, bone pain, constipation, or biochemical clues to hypercalcemia provided.  </p></br><p>f. Increased magnesium<br /></br>   • Not specifically associated with any MEN syndrome.  </p></br><p>h. Increased triiodothyronine (T3)<br /></br>   • Would point toward hyperthyroidism (e.g., Graves disease, toxic adenoma). The patient lacks weight loss/anxiety/tachycardia, and the nodule is pathognomonic for MTC in this setting rather than a toxic adenoma.  </p></br><p>Key differentiator: Only medullary thyroid carcinoma explains BOTH the thyroid nodule and the hormone-mediated diarrhea, and its laboratory hallmark is elevated calcitonin.</p>
37. A 27-year-old man with ulcerative colitis is admitted to the hospital because of a 5-day history of increasingly severe bloody diarrhea and abdominal cramps, fatigue, and generalized weakness. He receives infliximab infusions every 2 weeks. His only other medication is a daily multivitamin. His temperature is 37.2 C (99 F), pulse is 100/min, and blood pressure is 100/70 mm Hg. Examination shows a soft abdomen; there is moderate tenderness to deep palpation of the left lower quadrant. Laboratory studies show a hematocrit of 36 percent and leukocyte count of 4000/mm3. Colonoscopy shows diffusely erythematous mucosa and superficial ulcers extending contiguously from the rectum to the proximal descending colon. Biopsies of the colonic mucosa show intracellular inclusion bodies. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Administration of adalimumab</br>b. Administration of ganciclovir</br>c. Administration of hydrocortisone</br>d. Administration of metronidazole</br>e. Transfusion of 2 units of packed red blood cells</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Administration of ganciclovir  </p></br><p>=============================<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues:<br /></br>  – Known ulcerative colitis (UC) already on infliximab → significant immunosuppression (anti-TNF-α).<br /></br>  – New 5-day surge in bloody diarrhea, LLQ pain.<br /></br>  – Afebrile/low-grade temp but tachycardic; BP mildly low; WBC 4,000/mm³ (leukopenia) → think infectious complication rather than ordinary UC flare.<br /></br>  – Colonoscopy: “diffusely erythematous mucosa and superficial ulcers extending contiguously” = baseline UC picture, BUT biopsies show intracellular inclusion bodies (classic “owl’s eyes”).<br /></br>  – Inclusion bodies on colonic biopsy in an immunocompromised host = cytomegalovirus (CMV) colitis.  </p></br><p>Approach for similar questions:<br /></br>1. Any UC/Crohn flare on biologic → always ask “infection vs inflammatory flare?”<br /></br>2. Look for atypical labs (leukopenia) or path findings (inclusion bodies, pseudomembranes).<br /></br>3. Know the buzz-words: CMV = large cells + intranuclear inclusions; treat with IV ganciclovir.  </p></br><p>=============================<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-by-step logic to the correct choice:<br /></br>1. Patient is already receiving a potent biologic; additional immunosuppression is risky.<br /></br>2. CMV superinfection commonly complicates UC treated with steroids, azathioprine, infliximab, etc.<br /></br>3. Histology clinches diagnosis (owl’s-eye inclusions).<br /></br>4. First-line therapy for tissue-invasive CMV in immunocompromised hosts = IV ganciclovir (or oral valganciclovir once improved).  </p></br><p>Take-home message: In IBD patients on biologics who deteriorate and have CMV inclusions on biopsy, treat the infection (ganciclovir) before considering any escalation of IBD therapy.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real world): diagnosis CMV colitis; start IV ganciclovir 5 mg/kg q12 h; hold infliximab; monitor CBC &amp; renal function.  </p></br><p>=============================<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• CMV is a dsDNA β-herpesvirus. Latent in mononuclear cells; reactivates with immunosuppression.<br /></br>• Produces cytopathic “owl-eye” intranuclear inclusions.<br /></br>• In colon, causes patchy or diffuse mucosal ischemia-like ulcerations that can mimic/overlap IBD.<br /></br>• Untreated disease can lead to toxic megacolon, perforation, hemorrhage.  </p></br><p>=============================<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>• First line: IV ganciclovir → after clinical improvement, transition to oral valganciclovir to complete 2–3-week course.<br /></br>• Alternatives if resistance or severe myelosuppression: foscarnet, cidofovir.<br /></br>• Suspend immunosuppressive biologic until infection controlled.<br /></br>• Follow-up colonoscopy or PCR monitoring is sometimes performed in severe cases.  </p></br><p>=============================<br /></br>Memory Tips<br /></br>“Inflixi-virus”: If a UC patient on infliximab worsens, think viral (CMV) → treat with ganci-loVIR.<br /></br>CMV = “Cytomega-‘owl’ virus” → owl-eye inclusions prompt ganci-lovir.  </p></br><p>=============================<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>A. Adalimumab (another anti-TNF-α)<br /></br>   – Would add further immunosuppression, worsening CMV infection; no benefit until infection cleared.<br /></br>C. Hydrocortisone<br /></br>   – Steroids are standard for severe UC flare, but here the biopsy proves CMV; steroids will exacerbate viral replication.<br /></br>D. Metronidazole<br /></br>   – Used for C. difficile, anaerobes, Giardia. CMV is a DNA virus, not anaerobic bacteria or protozoa.<br /></br>E. Transfusion of 2 units PRBCs<br /></br>   – Hct 36% is only mildly low; no hemodynamic instability or symptomatic anemia. Transfusion does nothing for underlying cause.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: equating any bloody diarrhea in a UC patient with an autoimmune flare. Always reconsider infection—CMV, C. difficile, bacterial pathogens—especially when the patient is immunosuppressed.</p>
38. A 54-year-old woman with hypertension is admitted to the hospital because of a 1-week history of nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. She says she has been vomiting four to five times daily and has not been able to keep any food or liquids down. She has had up to 10 nonbloody loose stools daily. She reports no fever or chills. She says she previously had been well, but she noticed that her blood pressure had been higher than usual during the past 2 weeks, and she was planning to see her primary care physician. She is a pediatric nurse and had been caring for several patients with unspecified viral gastroenteritis 1 week before she became ill. She has hyperlipidemia. Medications are hydrochlorothiazide and atorvastatin. The patient is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 73 kg (162 lb); BMI is 26 kg/m2. Temperature is 37.3 C (99.2 F), pulse is 110/min, and blood pressure is 126/68 mm Hg. Abdominal examination discloses mild, diffuse tenderness to palpation without rebound or guarding. Results of complete blood count and urinalysis are within the reference ranges. Results of serum chemistry profile and urine electrolyte concentrations are shown:</br>Serum</br>Urea nitrogen 22 mg/dL</br>Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL</br>Na+ 136 mEq/L</br>K+ 3.1 mEq/L</br>Cl- 95 mEq/L</br>HCO3- 34 mEq/L</br></br>Urine</br>Na+ <10 mmol/L</br>K+ 30 mmol/L</br>Cl- 12 mmol/L</br></br>Arterial blood gas analysis on room air:</br>Po2 98 mm Hg</br>Pco2 49 mm Hg</br>pH 7.51</br></br>Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s laboratory results?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Addison disease</br>b. Adrenal adenoma</br>c. Diarrhea</br>d. Diuretic use</br>e. Vomiting</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Vomiting  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Acid-base status<br /></br>   • pH 7.51 (alkalemia)<br /></br>   • HCO₃⁻ 34 mEq/L (elevated)<br /></br>   • PCO₂ 49 mm Hg (respiratory compensation)<br /></br>   ⇒ Primary metabolic alkalosis.</p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Electrolytes<br /></br>   • ↓K⁺ 3.1 mEq/L (hypokalaemia)<br /></br>   • ↓Cl⁻ 95 mEq/L (hypochloraemia)<br /></br>   • Normal Na⁺ 136 mEq/L.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Urine studies<br /></br>   • Urine Na⁺ &lt;10 mmol/L (marked volume depletion)<br /></br>   • Urine Cl⁻ 12 mmol/L (&lt;20 mmol/L = “chloride-poor” urine)<br /></br>   • Urine K⁺ 30 mmol/L (aldosterone-mediated K⁺ wasting).</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>History clues<br /></br>   • 4–5 episodes/day of emesis for a week.<br /></br>   • Unable to keep food or liquids down → volume contraction.<br /></br>   • Diarrhoea is present but diarrhoea alone would produce metabolic ACIDosis (loss of HCO₃⁻), contradicting the lab pattern.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Meta-strategy: For metabolic alkalosis, always check urine chloride.<br /></br>   • Urine Cl⁻ &lt;20 mmol/L → vomiting or prior diuretic use (after drug stopped).<br /></br>   • Urine Cl⁻ &gt;20 mmol/L → current diuretic use, Bartter, Gitelman, hyperaldosteronism, etc.</p></br><p>Because the patient is actively vomiting and still producing chloride-poor urine, vomiting is the best fit.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)<br /></br>1. Identify alkalosis.<br /></br>2. Determine etiology with urine Cl⁻.<br /></br>3. Link volume depletion (tachycardia, low urine Na⁺) and hypokalemia to secondary hyperaldosteronism from vomiting-induced volume loss.<br /></br>4. Exclude other causes by matching or mismatching expected urine Cl⁻, BP, and history.</p></br><p>Key takeaway: Metabolic alkalosis + low urine chloride + clear history of gastric fluid loss = vomiting.</p></br><p>Documentation points: volume status, frequency of emesis, electrolyte results, plan for IV isotonic saline + KCl repletion.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Gastric fluid is rich in H⁺ and Cl⁻ (HCl). Repeated vomiting → direct loss of H⁺ (alkalosis) and Cl⁻ (hypochloraemia).<br /></br>• Volume loss activates RAAS → ↑aldosterone → renal H⁺ and K⁺ secretion (hypokalemia) and Na⁺, HCO₃⁻ reabsorption, maintaining alkalosis.<br /></br>• Kidney avidly reabsorbs the remaining Cl⁻ to accompany Na⁺, so urine Cl⁻ falls (&lt;20 mmol/L).</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Treatment Snapshot<br /></br>1. Isotonic saline (0.9 % NaCl) to correct volume and supply chloride.<br /></br>2. Potassium chloride replacement.<br /></br>3. Antiemetics (ondansetron, metoclopramide) to stop further losses.<br /></br>4. Stop contributing agents (e.g., HCTZ) until stable.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Vomiting = Very low urine Chloride.”<br /></br>Think “HCl up the esophagus, Cl⁻ nowhere left to pee.”</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Addison disease (primary adrenal insufficiency)<br /></br>   • Expected metabolic acidosis, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia; urine Na⁺ high due to salt wasting. None match.</p></br><p>b. Adrenal adenoma (primary hyperaldosteronism)<br /></br>   • Metabolic alkalosis &amp; hypokalemia, but: hypertension (&gt;140/90), urine Cl⁻ &gt;20 mmol/L (cannot reabsorb all Cl⁻ in setting of mineralocorticoid excess). Patient’s BP normal-low and urine Cl⁻ low.</p></br><p>c. Diarrhea<br /></br>   • Loss of HCO₃⁻ → non-anion gap metabolic ACIDosis, hyperchloremia; opposite of vignette findings.</p></br><p>d. Diuretic use<br /></br>   • Thiazides cause metabolic alkalosis with high urine Cl⁻ while drug is on board. Chronic non-adherence for a week with poor PO intake would lower urine Cl⁻, but the question provides a stronger, current emesis trigger; plus she likely missed doses because she “couldn’t keep anything down.”</p></br><p>e. Vomiting<br /></br>   • Exactly matches metabolic alkalosis, low urine Cl⁻, low urine Na⁺, hypokalemia, and clinical history.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: In metabolic alkalosis, a chloride-poor urine (&lt;20 mmol/L) almost always points to vomiting (or remote diuretic use). In the setting of ongoing, profuse emesis, vomiting is the definitive cause.</p>
39. A case-control study is conducted to assess the relationship between childhood infection with varicella zoster virus and lymphoma. It is most appropriate to use which of the following methods to minimize recall bias in this study?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Choose cases and controls from the same population</br>b. Ensure that the investigators gathering exposure information are unaware of participant outcome status</br>c. Form a panel of blinded investigators to assign case and control definitions</br>d. Randomly select the controls from the population</br>e. Validate the exposure information with blood tests</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer: E. Validate the exposure information with blood tests</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>1. Study design: Case-control (start with outcome → look back for exposure).</br>2. Threat identified by the stem: “recall bias” (systematic difference in how accurately cases vs controls remember a past exposure).</br>3. Key clue: Exposure = childhood varicella-zoster infection, something that can be documented objectively (lifelong VZV IgG antibodies).</p></br><p>Approach for future questions:</br>• When a question asks how to limit recall bias, immediately think “replace memory with an objective source (medical records, serology, pharmacy database, etc.).”</br>• If a biologic marker persists for life (e.g., IgG), serology is often the cleanest solution.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise logic:</br>1. In case-control studies, exposure is usually ascertained retrospectively.</br>2. Cases (lymphoma patients) may ruminate more about childhood illnesses than controls → differential misclassification of exposure (recall bias).</br>3. Objective validation (serologic confirmation of VZV IgG) removes the need for participant memory, equalizing exposure ascertainment in cases and controls.</p></br><p>Take-home concept: Replace subjective recall with objective data to eliminate recall bias.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: If this were an IRB protocol you would specify “childhood VZV infection will be ascertained via serologic testing (VZV IgG) or childhood immunization records rather than participant self-report.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (brief)</br>• Primary VZV infection (chickenpox) triggers durable IgG antibodies detectable for life.</br>• Therefore, a single blood draw provides evidence of prior exposure regardless of memory.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatments &amp; Diagnostics Review (relevant to methodology)</br>Diagnostic test: Serum VZV IgG ELISA.</br>• High sensitivity/specificity for previous infection.</br>• Not affected by acute illness or vaccination status decades later.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“ReCAll bias? Use Real-Concrete-Answer.”</br>R-C-A = Records, Charts, Antibody tests.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>a. Choose cases and controls from the same population<br /></br>   • Addresses selection bias, not recall bias. Participants could still recall differently.</p></br><p>b. Ensure investigators gathering exposure info are unaware of outcome status<br /></br>   • Prevents interviewer/observer bias (differential probing), but participants’ own faulty recall remains.</p></br><p>c. Form a panel of blinded investigators to assign case/control definitions<br /></br>   • Ensures proper classification of outcome, fights misclassification of the outcome, not the exposure.</p></br><p>d. Randomly select the controls from the population<br /></br>   • Improves representativeness (selection bias). Doesn’t change how accurately people remember childhood chickenpox.</p></br><p>e. Validate the exposure information with blood tests<br /></br>   • Only option that directly neutralizes recall differences by eliminating reliance on memory → minimizes recall bias. (Correct)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>In a case-control study, recall bias is best minimized by confirming past exposures through objective sources—here, a persistent serologic marker for VZV infection.</p>
40. A cohort study is conducted to evaluate the impact of fish consumption on the risk for developing cardiovascular disease. Participants complete a questionnaire about dietary habits, including fish consumption; responses are obtained from 2000 participants (80 percent response rate). The study participants are observed for 20 years, and cases of cardiovascular disease are identified through a data registry. Study results show:</br>Eating Fish (Frequency)    Cases with Cardiovascular Disease    Cases without Cardiovascular Disease    Total</br>Never                      160                                 1440                                   1600</br>Almost Daily               20                                  380                                    400</br>Total                      180                                 1820                                   2000</br></br>Based on these data, which of the following best represents the attributable risk for development of cardiovascular disease caused by lack of fish consumption in this population?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. 0.01</br>b. 0.04</br>c. 0.05</br>d. 0.07</br>e. 0.09</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>C. 0.05  </p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key data and clues:<br /></br>• Study design: Prospective cohort → allows calculation of incidence (risk).<br /></br>• Exposure of interest: Eating fish “almost daily” (protective) vs “never” (lack of fish = risk factor).<br /></br>• We are asked for attributable risk (AR) <em>caused by lack of fish consumption</em>.<br /></br>• Incidence (risk) in each group can be calculated directly from the 20-year follow-up table.</p></br><p>Cognitive road map for similar questions:<br /></br>1. Identify study design (case-control vs cohort).<br /></br>2. Pick correct effect measure (risk ratio, odds ratio, AR, etc.).<br /></br>3. Plug numbers → simplify.<br /></br>4. Keep straight which group is exposed vs unexposed if wording is tricky (here “lack of fish” is the exposure).</p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Attributable Risk (Risk Difference) formula:<br /></br>AR = Incidence in Exposed − Incidence in Unexposed  </p></br><p>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. Define “exposed”: individuals who <em>lack fish consumption</em> (“Never”).<br /></br>2. Define “unexposed”: individuals who <em>almost daily eat fish</em> (reference group).<br /></br>3. Calculate incidence (cumulative risk) in each group:<br /></br>   • Never eat fish: 160 cases / 1600 total = 0.10 (10%)<br /></br>   • Almost daily fish: 20 cases / 400 total = 0.05 (5%)<br /></br>4. Subtract to obtain AR:<br /></br>   0.10 − 0.05 = 0.05 (5%)  </p></br><p>Interpretation: In this population, an additional 5 cardiovascular events per 100 individuals over 20 years are attributable to not eating fish. If everyone ate fish almost daily, we would expect 5% fewer people to develop cardiovascular disease.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Attributable risk = risk difference; it quantifies the <em>absolute</em> excess risk due to the exposure.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl (real patient encounter): record dietary intake as part of cardiovascular risk assessment; counseling on diet can be justified by quantifying risk reduction.</p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>Fish (especially oily fish) is rich in omega-3 fatty acids (EPA, DHA). Proposed cardioprotective mechanisms:<br /></br>• ↓ plasma triglycerides<br /></br>• Anti-inflammatory effects<br /></br>• Anti-arrhythmic membrane stabilization<br /></br>• Endothelial function improvement<br /></br>Reduced fish intake removes these benefits, potentially increasing atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) risk.</p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Treatment / Prevention Nuggets  </p></br><p>Lifestyle modification (diet rich in fish, fruits, vegetables, whole grains) is first-line ASCVD risk reduction. If insufficient, add pharmacologic therapy (e.g., statins, icosapent ethyl in selected patients).</p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Risk DIFFERENCE is the DIFFerence”: just subtract.<br /></br>“Attributable” sounds like “add a bit” → how much extra risk can be added to the exposure.  </p></br><p>Quick visual:<br /></br>Never fish 10%<br /></br>Daily fish   5%<br /></br>Difference   5%  → answer 0.05</p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. 0.01 – Would arise if incidence were 6% vs 5%. The actual gap is larger; underestimates excess risk.<br /></br>b. 0.04 – From 9% vs 5% (not our numbers).<br /></br>d. 0.07 – Would require 12% vs 5%.<br /></br>e. 0.09 – Would require 14% vs 5%.<br /></br>All ignore the exact incidence values provided, illustrating the importance of calculating, not guessing.</p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Attributable risk = Risk in exposed – Risk in unexposed.<br /></br>Here: 0.10 − 0.05 = 0.05 → an absolute 5% of cardiovascular events over 20 years are attributable to not eating fish almost daily.</p>
41. A study is conducted to determine if there is an association between tampon use and toxic shock syndrome (TSS). A total of 50 patients with TSS and 450 patients without TSS are enrolled in the study. The patients are matched for age and race. All patients are interviewed to determine if they use tampons. When analyzing the data, which of the following is the most appropriate statistical method to use for this study?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Analysis of variance</br>b. Chi-square test</br>c. Correlation coefficient</br>d. Mann-Whitney test</br>e. Regression analysis</br>f. Unpaired t-test</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Chi-square test  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Study design: Case-control (50 “cases” with toxic shock syndrome vs 450 “controls” without).<br /></br>• Exposure variable: Tampon use (categorical, yes/no).<br /></br>• Outcome variable: Presence of TSS (categorical, yes/no).<br /></br>• Goal: Test whether the proportion of tampon users differs between the two categorical groups.<br /></br>⇒ Whenever you compare two independent groups on a dichotomous (or nominal) variable, the classic test of association is the χ² (chi-square) test; if any expected cell is &lt;5 you would use Fisher exact, a special case of χ².  </p></br><p>Cognitive / Metacognitive Tip<br /></br>1. Identify variable types first (categorical vs continuous).<br /></br>2. Ask: “Am I comparing means, medians, proportions, or correlation?”<br /></br>   – Proportions → χ² (or Fisher).<br /></br>   – Means (continuous) → t-test/ANOVA.<br /></br>   – Medians (non-parametric) → Mann-Whitney/Kruskal-Wallis.<br /></br>   – Relationship between two continuous vars → correlation/regression.  </p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Statistical)<br /></br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. “Case-control” signals retrospective comparison of exposures.<br /></br>2. Both exposure and outcome are categorical → contingency table (2×2).<br /></br>3. χ² provides a p-value showing whether observed differences in proportions deviate from what would be expected by chance.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Use χ² to test association between two independent categorical variables.  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review (brief)<br /></br>Toxic shock syndrome is usually caused by Staphylococcus aureus exotoxins acting as superantigens; historically linked to high-absorbency tampon use. The study aims to quantify that association, but for the statistics question, pathophysiology is peripheral.  </p></br><p>Diagnostic / Treatment Review (context)<br /></br>Not required for the stats question but remember: management includes fluid resuscitation, source control (remove tampon), and antistaphylococcal antibiotics (e.g., clindamycin + vancomycin or nafcillin depending on MRSA risk).  </p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Two categories? Go χ².”<br /></br>Think of the χ as an “X”—it resembles the 2×2 table you’ll build.  </p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Analysis of variance (ANOVA): Compares means of ≥3 groups on a continuous variable. Our outcome &amp; exposure are categorical, not continuous.<br /></br>c. Correlation coefficient (Pearson or Spearman): Quantifies linear relationship between two continuous variables (e.g., height vs weight). Not applicable.<br /></br>d. Mann-Whitney test (Wilcoxon rank-sum): Non-parametric comparison of medians between two independent groups for an ordinal/continuous variable that is not normally distributed. Not for categorical data.<br /></br>e. Regression analysis: Can model binary outcomes (logistic regression) but the question asks for the “most appropriate” simple test of association; χ² is sufficient and standard for a 2×2 table.<br /></br>f. Unpaired t-test: Compares means of a continuous variable between two groups assuming normal distribution. Not suited for categorical variables.  </p></br><p>Key Take-Home Message<br /></br>When both variables are categorical (especially yes/no), default to a χ² test (or Fisher exact for small samples).</p>
42. A 22-year-old man is admitted to the intensive care unit after he sustained a severe head injury in a motorcycle collision. Three days later, he remains intubated and has had no spontaneous respirations. His temperature is 37 C (98.6 F). His pupils are fixed and dilated, and he is unresponsive to all physical and verbal stimuli. He has no cranial nerve reflexes, and repeated EEG shows no electrical activity. He is receiving no medications. Toxicology screening is negative. Which of the following is the most accurate statement regarding continuation of mechanical ventilation for this patient?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Mechanical ventilation may be withdrawn only if a do-not-resuscitate order has been signed by the next of kin</br>b. Mechanical ventilation may be withdrawn only if the patient has a living will</br>c. Mechanical ventilation may be withdrawn only with court approval</br>d. Mechanical ventilation may be withdrawn without the family's permission</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>D. Mechanical ventilation may be withdrawn without the family’s permission</p></br><p>Vignette Analysis</br>Key findings that legally establish brain death:</br>• No spontaneous respirations off the ventilator (apnea test)<br /></br>• Absent brain-stem reflexes (fixed, dilated pupils; no corneal, gag, cough, oculocephalic, or vestibulo-ocular responses)<br /></br>• Confirmatory study: repeated electroencephalogram (EEG) with no electrical activity<br /></br>• Normal core temperature and no sedative, paralytic, or intoxicating drugs—rules out reversible causes of unresponsiveness  </p></br><p>Once these criteria are satisfied, every U.S. jurisdiction and the Uniform Determination of Death Act (UDDA) regard the patient as legally dead. The ventilator is no longer “life support”; it is simply oxygenating a cadaver.</p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-by-step thought process:</br>1. Recognize the pattern of brain death (apnea + absent brain-stem reflexes + confirmatory study).<br /></br>2. Recall that brain death = legal death; continuing treatment has no physiologic benefit to the patient.<br /></br>3. Because the patient is already dead, removing a ventilator is not “ending life” but discontinuing futile treatment. No consent—family, surrogate, or court—is legally required.<br /></br>Core takeaway: In brain-dead patients, mechanical ventilation can be stopped without permission; good practice is to inform the family, but their authorization is not a legal prerequisite.</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review</br>Brain death reflects complete and irreversible cessation of all brain and brain-stem function. Cerebral perfusion pressure falls to zero; neuronal destruction is total. Spinal reflexes (e.g., triple flexion) may persist because the cord can remain perfused, but this has no impact on the diagnosis.</p></br><p>Treatments and Diagnostic Tests Review</br>Diagnosis requires:</br>• Clinical exam demonstrating coma, absence of brain-stem reflexes, and positive apnea test<br /></br>• Exclusion of confounders (hypothermia, drug intoxication, metabolic derangements)<br /></br>Confirmatory tests (institution-specific): EEG silence, cerebral angiography showing absent flow, TCD ultrasound, or nuclear brain scan.</p></br><p>Management once brain death is confirmed:</br>1. Communicate findings empathetically to the family.<br /></br>2. Discuss potential organ donation.<br /></br>3. Discontinue futile interventions per institutional policy.</p></br><p>Memorization Tips</br>“BRAiN DEAD” mnemonic for prerequisites:</br>B – Brain-stem reflexes absent<br /></br>R – Reversible causes (drugs, hypothermia) ruled out<br /></br>A – Apnea test positive<br /></br>i – Isoelectric EEG (optional confirmatory)<br /></br>N – Normothermia &amp; Normotension  </p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>A. “Only if a DNR is signed by next of kin”<br /></br>• DNR addresses withholding CPR after cardiac arrest, not ventilator withdrawal. Moreover, next-of-kin consent is unnecessary because the patient is already legally dead.</p></br><p>B. “Only if the patient has a living will”<br /></br>• Advanced directives guide care for living, decision-incapacitated patients. They do not apply once death has been declared.</p></br><p>C. “Only with court approval”<br /></br>• Courts become involved when there is dispute about whether someone is dead or when surrogates disagree about care for living patients. No court order is required to withdraw futile treatment from a person who is legally dead.</p></br><p>Common Pitfalls</br>• Confusing persistent vegetative state or minimally conscious state (still alive) with brain death (legally dead).<br /></br>• Assuming that ventilator withdrawal equates to euthanasia; in brain death, there is no life to end.</p></br><p>Document in the Chart</br>• Full neurologic exam findings<br /></br>• Results of apnea and confirmatory tests<br /></br>• Time of death (time brain death criteria met)<br /></br>• Family notification and discussion  </p></br><p>Bottom Line</br>Once brain death is declared, the patient is legally dead. Mechanical ventilation may be stopped without family permission, though clear, compassionate communication with the family is ethically essential.</p>
43. A 77-year-old woman is brought to the physician by her son because of palpitations for the past month. She had a cerebral infarction 5 years ago and has residual partial paralysis of her right leg. She uses an orthotic brace on her right ankle and a walker. She has fallen three times during the past year but has not sustained any injuries. She has a 5-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus treated with metformin. Her vision is 20/60 in her left eye, but she is able to read large print. Her pulse is 120/min and irregularly irregular, and blood pressure is 135/78 mm Hg. Crackles are heard at the lung bases. A grade 2/6 systolic murmur is heard best at the lower left sternal border. Abdominal examination shows no abnormalities. There is 2+ edema below the knees. Neurologic examination shows unchanged hemiparesis. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hematocrit 37 percent</br>Leukocyte count 4700/mm^3 with a normal differential</br>Serum</br>Urea nitrogen 27 mg/dL</br>Creatinine 1.4 mg/dL</br>An ECG shows atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate between 90/min and 140/min. Echocardiography shows an ejection fraction of 40 percent. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Measurement of serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration</br>b. Tilt test</br>c. 24-Hour ambulatory ECG monitoring</br>d. Electrophysiologic studies</br>e. Placement of a cardiac pacemaker</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a.  Measurement of serum thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) concentration  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• 77-year-old woman with 1-month history of palpitations → new-onset sustained arrhythmia<br /></br>• Pulse 120/min, irregularly irregular; ECG confirms atrial fibrillation (AF) → diagnosis already made<br /></br>• Echocardiogram ordered and completed (EF 40 %) → structural heart disease assessed<br /></br>• No mention of prior AF, thyroid testing, or rate/rhythm control therapy → initial work-up still incomplete  </p></br><p>Approach to any newly diagnosed AF:<br /></br>1. Confirm rhythm on ECG (done).<br /></br>2. Look for reversible precipitants: hyperthyroidism, acute ischemia, pulmonary disease, alcohol (“holiday heart”), surgery, sepsis, etc.<br /></br>3. Assess for structural heart disease (echo done) and stroke risk (CHA₂DS₂-VASc).<br /></br>A TSH level is therefore a mandatory next step.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive pearl: As soon as the arrhythmia is captured on a 12-lead ECG (so you no longer need monitoring), the test you order is the one that can uncover a treatable, underlying cause. On NBME/Step questions, “Order TSH in new AF” is a classic reflex.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why TSH?<br /></br>• Hyperthyroidism increases β-adrenergic sensitivity and atrial automaticity, often presenting only as AF in the elderly (“apathetic hyperthyroidism”).<br /></br>• Identifying thyrotoxicosis changes management: treat the thyroid disease ± temporary β-blockade, which may restore sinus rhythm.<br /></br>• Guidelines (ACC/AHA/HRS) list TSH (and free T4 if abnormal) as part of the baseline evaluation of all patients with newly diagnosed AF.  </p></br><p>Documentation points in a real chart: “Atrial fibrillation confirmed on ECG. Will obtain TSH to evaluate for thyroid disease as reversible etiology.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Hyperthyroidism → ↑ catecholamine receptor density &amp; direct chronotropic effect → shortened atrial refractory period → triggers &amp; maintains AF. Up to 15 % of older patients with new AF have unrecognized thyrotoxicosis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review (high yield summary)  </p></br><p>Initial AF work-up:<br /></br>• ECG → diagnosis &amp; ventricular rate.<br /></br>• Transthoracic echo → chamber size, valvular disease, EF.<br /></br>• Labs: TSH ± free T4, CBC, BMP/Mg, +/- troponin if ischemia suspected.<br /></br>• Rate control (β-blocker, diltiazem, or digoxin), anticoagulation per CHA₂DS₂-VASc (this patient scores ≥5 → anticoagulate), possible rhythm control in selected cases.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“AF = C A F É T”<br /></br>C – Confirm with ECG<br /></br>A – Assess structure (Echo)<br /></br>F – Find reversible cause (TSH, lytes, ischemia)<br /></br>É – Evaluate stroke risk (CHA₂DS₂-VASc)<br /></br>T – Treat (Rate, Rhythm, Thromboembolism prophylaxis)  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Tilt test – Used to evaluate unexplained syncope (vasovagal, orthostatic). This patient has palpitations with documented AF, not unexplained fainting.  </p></br><p>c. 24-Hour ambulatory ECG monitoring – Helpful when arrhythmia is suspected but not caught on standard ECG (paroxysmal AF, SVT, PVC burden). Here AF is already documented on a 12-lead; monitoring adds nothing to etiology work-up.  </p></br><p>d. Electrophysiologic studies – Indicated for mechanism delineation or ablation planning in complex tachyarrhythmias when noninvasive tests fail. Not first-line in straightforward new AF.  </p></br><p>e. Placement of a cardiac pacemaker – Reserved for symptomatic bradyarrhythmias, high-grade AV block, or tachy-brady syndrome. AF is a tachyarrhythmia; a pacemaker would not treat it and is not indicated.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>In every patient with newly diagnosed atrial fibrillation, always check a TSH to exclude hyperthyroidism—a reversible and treatable precipitant of the arrhythmia.</p>
44. A 37-year-old man comes to the physician 12 hours after the onset of vomiting and abdominal cramps and swelling. He has had constipation for the past 4 days. He was diagnosed with Crohn disease 7 years ago. His symptoms of diarrhea and right lower quadrant abdominal pain have been well controlled with mesalamine for the past 3 years. His temperature is 36 C (96.8 F), pulse is 98/min and regular, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Examination shows a diffusely distended, tympanitic abdomen and visible peristalsis; high-pitched bowel sounds are heard. Rectal examination shows no stool in the rectal vault. An x-ray of the abdomen shows a small-bowel obstruction. Which of the following is the most likely cause of these findings?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Colon cancer</br>b. Ileocecal fistula</br>c. Small-bowel adhesions</br>d. Small-bowel fibrotic stricture</br>e. Small-bowel intussusception</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Small-bowel fibrotic stricture  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Approach<br /></br>1. Key phrases that signal a mechanical obstruction:<br /></br>   • “Constipation for 4 days,” “diffusely distended, tympanitic abdomen,” “visible peristalsis,” “high-pitched bowel sounds,” and an x-ray “shows a small-bowel obstruction.”<br /></br>2. Long-standing Crohn disease (7 years) → think chronic transmural inflammation → scarring and fibrosis.<br /></br>3. No prior abdominal surgery or trauma is mentioned (makes adhesions unlikely).<br /></br>4. Afebrile, WNL vitals, symptoms evolved over hours to days rather than acutely severe bleeding or perforation (argues against fistula, intussusception, or malignancy).<br /></br>Metacognitive tip: In a Crohn patient with SBO and no surgical history, always put “fibrotic stricture” high on your differential; Crohn = “S” for Skip lesions &amp; Strictures.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>• Crohn disease → transmural, relapsing inflammation.<br /></br>• Chronic healing cycles deposit collagen → segmental fibrosis → progressive luminal narrowing, especially in the terminal ileum.<br /></br>• Once the lumen narrows enough, proximal small bowel dilates and peristalsis becomes vigorous (visible waves, high-pitched sounds) → classic SBO picture.<br /></br>Take-home: Fibrotic stricture formation is the most common chronic obstructive complication of Crohn disease.</p></br><p>Documentation (real life): note obstructive symptoms, prior Crohn history, absence of surgery, abdominal exam findings, imaging showing SBO, plan for NG decompression, IV fluids, CT enterography vs MR enterography, and surgical consult for possible strictureplasty/resection.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)<br /></br>• Crohn disease = transmural granulomatous inflammation (Th1) anywhere mouth-to-anus, ileum most common.<br /></br>• Recurrent inflammation → ulcer → healing with fibrosis → concentric scar (“string sign” on barium) → stricture.<br /></br>• Strictures present with crampy pain, post-prandial bloating, nausea/vomiting, obstipation, and, if untreated, complete obstruction.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment Snapshot<br /></br>Diagnostics:<br /></br>• CT/MR enterography: delineates length, degree, and number of strictures; rules out abscess.<br /></br>• Colonoscopy with ileoscopy: evaluates for inflammation vs fixed scar (but avoid during acute obstruction).</p></br><p>Management:<br /></br>1. Acute obstruction: NPO, NG tube, IV fluids, electrolyte correction.<br /></br>2. Chronic fibrotic stricture: medical therapy rarely effective; requires endoscopic balloon dilation, strictureplasty, or resection.<br /></br>3. Inflammatory (non-fibrotic) stricture: try corticosteroids or anti-TNF (infliximab/adalimumab). Mesalamine does not reverse fibrosis.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids<br /></br>“Crohn CREEPS and SCARS”:<br /></br>C – Cobblestone<br /></br>R – RLQ pain<br /></br>E – Extra-GI manifestations<br /></br>E – Entire GI tract<br /></br>P – Perianal disease<br /></br>S – Strictures/Skip lesions → SBO  </p></br><p>“String sign = Stricture in Crohn.”</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Colon cancer<br /></br>   • IBD-associated cancer risk is higher with long-standing ulcerative colitis; colon cancer would cause large-bowel obstruction or bleeding, not a small-bowel obstruction in the ileum.  </p></br><p>b. Ileocecal fistula<br /></br>   • Fistulas create abnormal connections that usually cause diarrhea, sepsis, or malabsorption—not obstructive symptoms with an empty rectal vault.  </p></br><p>c.
