Small-bowel adhesions<br /></br>   • #1 cause of SBO overall, but typically follow prior laparotomy or intra-abdominal infection. This patient has no surgical history.  </p></br><p>e. Small-bowel intussusception<br /></br>   • Rare in adults; presents with intermittent, colicky pain ± “currant-jelly” stool, palpable mass. Imaging shows “target sign,” not classic SBO distention with visible peristalsis; Crohn is not a common trigger.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Key Takeaway<br /></br>In a patient with established Crohn disease and no surgical history who develops signs of small-bowel obstruction, think chronic fibrotic stricture until proven otherwise.</p>
45. A 62-year-old man comes to the physician to discuss the results of an abdominal CT scan that was performed 4 days ago. He is undergoing evaluation for a 2-month history of increasing back pain and a 6.8-kg (15-lb) weight loss. An initial physical examination showed no abnormalities. The CT scan showed a 4-cm solid mass in the body of the pancreas, multiple defects in the liver consistent with metastases, and a 7-cm abdominal aortic aneurysm with involvement of the renal arteries and no evidence of leakage. A CT scan–guided biopsy of the mass is performed. Examination of the specimen shows a pancreatic adenocarcinoma. After listening to the physician's explanation of his prognosis, the patient says, “So you think I have a pancreatic cancer that is too advanced to remove. But what about the aneurysm? Should I have something done about that?” Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation with respect to repair of this patient's aneurysm?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. The aneurysm should be repaired because of its large size</br>b. The aneurysm should be repaired only if the procedure can be performed with minimally invasive endovascular techniques</br>c. The aneurysm should not be repaired because of the location of the pancreatic cancer</br>d. The aneurysm should not be repaired because of the patient's poor prognosis</br>e. The aneurysm should not be repaired because the operation would be likely to stimulate the growth of the pancreatic cancer</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. The aneurysm should not be repaired because of the patient’s poor prognosis.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Approach<br /></br>• “4-cm solid mass in the body of the pancreas … multiple defects in the liver” → metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma (stage IV).<br /></br>• Median survival with metastatic pancreatic cancer ≈ 3-6 months (rarely &gt;12 months).<br /></br>• “7-cm abdominal aortic aneurysm with involvement of the renal arteries and no evidence of leakage” → a very large, but currently asymptomatic, infrarenal/juxtarenal AAA.<br /></br>High-yield cognitive steps:<br /></br>1. Recognize the life-limiting diagnosis (metastatic pancreatic cancer).<br /></br>2. Recall the decision rule for elective AAA repair: size &gt;5.5 cm OR rapid growth OR symptomatic AND reasonable life expectancy (&gt;1–2 y).<br /></br>3. Weigh perioperative mortality/morbidity (open ≈5-7 %; complex juxtarenal even higher) against the limited time the patient has to benefit.<br /></br>4. Conclude that surgery offers no meaningful survival or quality-of-life advantage.</p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Annual rupture risk of a 7-cm AAA ≈ 15–20 %.<br /></br>• Expected life span with metastatic pancreatic cancer is shorter than the time frame in which the aneurysm is likely to rupture.<br /></br>• Elective repair (open or EVAR) provides benefit only if the patient is likely to live at least 1–2 years; otherwise perioperative risk and recovery time outweigh potential gain.<br /></br>Take-home: Elective procedures with significant risk are contraindicated in patients with a terminal illness unless they palliate symptoms.</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Abdominal aortic aneurysm<br /></br>• Degeneration of aortic media → dilation.<br /></br>• Rupture risk correlates sharply with diameter (&gt;5.5 cm) and growth rate (&gt;0.5 cm/6 mo).<br /></br>Pancreatic adenocarcinoma<br /></br>• Aggressive tumor; liver metastases define stage IV.<br /></br>• Median survival with modern chemotherapy ~6 months; 5-year survival &lt;5 %.</p></br><p>Management Principles<br /></br>AAA<br /></br>1. Surveillance: q6–12 mo ultrasound/CT if &lt;5.5 cm &amp; asymptomatic.<br /></br>2. Elective repair (open or EVAR):<br /></br>   – Diameter &gt;5.5 cm (men) or &gt;5.0 cm (women).<br /></br>   – Rapid expansion (&gt;0.5 cm/6 mo).<br /></br>   – Symptomatic.<br /></br>   – Life expectancy &gt;1–2 y and acceptable operative risk.<br /></br>Palliative focus in terminal malignancy: emphasize symptom control, avoid non-beneficial operations.</p></br><p>Mnemonic<br /></br>“AAA Fix if Big &amp; the Patient will BE around”<br /></br>B = Big (&gt;5.5 cm)<br /></br>E = Expected survival &gt; Expect rupture window (~2 y)</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Review<br /></br>A. “Repair because of large size” – Size alone is normally an indication, but only when the patient is expected to survive long enough to benefit. This patient’s prognosis supersedes size.<br /></br>B. “Only if minimally invasive EVAR” – Procedure type doesn’t override the prognosis problem; EVAR still has peri-op risk and lifelong maintenance.<br /></br>C. “Not repaired because of the location of the pancreatic cancer” – Tumor location is irrelevant; the issue is metastatic disease and limited survival.<br /></br>E. “Operation would stimulate growth of the pancreatic cancer” – No evidence that AAA repair accelerates cancer growth; this is not a valid surgical contraindication.</p></br><p>Key Memory Hook<br /></br>Before scheduling elective vascular surgery, always ask:<br /></br>1. Does the aneurysm meet objective repair criteria?<br /></br>2. Will the patient live long enough to realize the benefit?<br /></br>If the answer to #2 is “no,” the correct choice is conservative management.</p>
46. A 52-year-old man comes to the emergency department 3 days after he sustained an injury to his left foot while walking barefoot on the beach. He says he initially thought he only had a minor cut, but he now has severe pain that radiates from his left foot to his groin. Ten years ago, he underwent cadaveric liver transplantation for hepatitis C. Current medications include mycophenolate and prednisone. On arrival, he appears diaphoretic and ill. His temperature is 39.2 C (102.6 F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 110/60 mm Hg. Examination shows erythema and hemorrhagic bullae over the left calf and foot. The skin is warm and tender to touch from over the left mid calf to the foot. Pulses in the distal lower extremities are equal bilaterally. His leukocyte count is 16,000/mm3 with 82 percent segmented neutrophils, 15 percent bands, and 3 percent eosinophils. Results of blood cultures are pending. In addition to intravenous administration of antibiotics, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Discontinue prednisone</br>b. Hyperbaric oxygen therapy</br>c. Intravenous immune globulin therapy</br>d. Surgical debridement</br>e. Amputation</br>f. No additional management is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct answer: d. Surgical debridement</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette analysis</br>1. Key clues:</br>   • “Minor cut while walking barefoot on the beach” → marine water exposure (think Vibrio vulnificus).</br>   • Rapid progression in 3 days to “severe pain that radiates … warm, tender, erythema, hemorrhagic bullae.”<br /></br>   • Pain out of proportion to exam + systemic toxicity (T = 39.2 °C, HR = 120, leukocytosis with bandemia).<br /></br>   • Immunocompromised host (solid-organ transplant, prednisone, mycophenolate).<br /></br>   Pattern screams necrotizing soft-tissue infection (necrotizing fasciitis).</p></br><ol></br><li>Pertinent positives: sudden severe pain, skin discoloration/bullae, sepsis.<br /></br>   Pertinent negatives: distal pulses intact → vascular occlusion less likely; no mention of gas crepitus but absence does not rule out nec fasc.</li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive pearl: On exams, “pain out of proportion” + systemic toxicity + bullae = act immediately; do not “watch,” image, or use exotic adjuncts before heading to the OR.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical critical reasoning</br>Necrotizing fasciitis = surgical disease.  Antibiotics are essential but cannot penetrate devitalized fascia; mortality rises rapidly if fascial debridement is delayed &gt;6 h.  Therefore, once suspected, next step is emergent surgical exploration and aggressive debridement (choice D).</p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): impression of suspected necrotizing fasciitis, sepsis; informed consent for emergent operative debridement; broad-spectrum coverage started (e.g., doxycycline + ceftazidime for Vibrio, or piperacillin-tazobactam + clindamycin + vancomycin when polymicrobial cause unclear).</p></br><p>Take-home message: “Suspect nec fasc? Cut first, ask questions later.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology review</br>• Organisms: </br>  – Marine/salt water: Vibrio vulnificus (esp. chronic liver disease, iron overload, immunosuppression).<br /></br>  – Classic polymicrobial (type I) or group A Strep (type II).<br /></br>• Toxins &amp; bacterial proliferation along fascial planes → thrombosis of perforating vessels, tissue ischemia, rapid spread → disproportionate pain, bullae, skin necrosis, systemic inflammatory response.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; diagnostic tests</br>1. Immediate:</br>   • Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics targeting gram-positives, gram-negatives, anaerobes, and in this vignette, Vibrio (doxycycline plus ceftazidime or a carbapenem).<br /></br>   • Emergent surgical exploration &amp; debridement ± repeat debridements.<br /></br>2. Adjuncts: IVF resuscitation, hemodynamic support; consider ICU.<br /></br>3. Optional tests: CT/MRI may show fascial gas or edema, but do not delay surgery if clinical suspicion is high.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization tips</br>• “Fasciitis = Fast to the OR.”<br /></br>• Beach + Bullae + Bad pain = Vibrio nec fasc → Doxy + Debridement.<br /></br>• Pain &gt; findings = Out-of-proportion → think nec fasc or compartment syndrome; nec fasc needs scalpel.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the other choices are wrong</br>a. Discontinue prednisone<br /></br>   – Stopping immunosuppression doesn’t address the life-threatening infection; delay in source control kills. Steroids may even need peri-op stress dosing.</p></br><p>b. Hyperbaric oxygen therapy<br /></br>   – Possible adjunct after debridement but never replaces early surgical exploration; evidence inconclusive.</p></br><p>c. Intravenous immune globulin<br /></br>   – Used for streptococcal toxic shock/nec fasc to neutralize exotoxin, but only as adjunct after debridement. Does not supplant surgery and is not first step.</p></br><p>e. Amputation<br /></br>   – Reserved for extensive non-salvageable limb involvement or failure of debridement. Initial attempt is radical fascial debridement while preserving viable tissue.</p></br><p>f. No additional management is indicated<br /></br>   – Nec fasc untreated surgically → &gt;70 % mortality. Doing nothing is fatal.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom line: Suspected necrotizing fasciitis—start broad antibiotics, then go straight to the OR for emergent surgical debridement.</p>
47. A 72-year-old man has decreased urine output 2 days after admission to the hospital for treatment of cholecystitis. His urine output has been 15 mL/h over the past 3 hours. On admission, results of laboratory studies were consistent with gram-negative bacteremia and disseminated intravascular coagulation. He is currently receiving intravenous fluids, cefoxitin, and gentamicin. His temperature is 38.5  C (101.3  F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 90/64 mm Hg. Abdominal examination shows mild right upper quadrant tenderness. His serum creatinine concentration has increased from 1.5 mg/dL 2 days ago to 3 mg/dL. This patient is most likely to have which of the following sets of urinalysis findings?</br>Blood      Protein   RBC (/hpf)       WBC (/hpf)      Casts                 Other Microscopic Findings	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. None     none      none             none            hyaline               none</br>b. None     4+        none             none            none                  oval fat bodies</br>c. Trace    1+        0–5              >50             none                  WBC clumps</br>d. 1+       1+        0–5              0–5             pigmented granular    renal tubular epithelial cells</br>e. 1+       1+        5–10             10–20           none                  eosinophils</br>f. 3+       1+        >50              none            RBC                   none</br>g. 4+       1+        none             none            pigmented granular    squamous epithelial cells</br>h. 4+       1+        10–20 dysmorphic none            none                  none</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. 1+ blood 1+ protein 0–5 RBC/hpf 0–5 WBC/hpf pigmented granular casts renal tubular epithelial cells  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• Elderly man with gram-negative sepsis → systemic hypotension (BP 90/64) and endothelial injury.<br /></br>• DIC on admission and now receiving an aminoglycoside (gentamicin) → added nephrotoxic risk.<br /></br>• Oliguria developing 48 h after insult (urine 15 mL/h; creatinine rose from 1.5 → 3 mg/dL).  </p></br><p>Pattern: “ICU patient + hypotension/aminoglycoside + rising creatinine at 48-72 h” → classic for intrinsic acute kidney injury due to Acute Tubular Necrosis (ATN).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip:<br /></br>1. Decide prerenal vs intrinsic vs postrenal.<br /></br> • Prerenal = hypoperfusion but structurally normal kidney → bland urine, hyaline casts.<br /></br> • Intrinsic (ATN, AIN, GN) = tubular or glomerular injury → cellular debris or casts.<br /></br>2. Look for the pathognomonic urinary sediment.<br /></br> • ATN → “muddy brown” (pigmented granular) casts ± renal tubular epithelial cells.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. Sepsis + hypotension + nephrotoxin → ischemic &amp; nephrotoxic ATN.<br /></br>2. ATN damages tubular epithelium → cells slough into lumen → pigmented granular casts and free renal tubular epithelial cells.<br /></br>3. Damaged tubules leak a small amount of protein and heme pigment (1+ blood, 1+ protein) but few intact RBCs or WBCs.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Muddy brown (pigmented granular) casts = ATN until proven otherwise.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls: oliguria volume, urine sediment description, drug list (aminoglycoside), vitals, creatinine trend, assessment “intrinsic AKI most consistent with ATN secondary to sepsis/aminoglycoside.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Ischemic ATN: prolonged hypoperfusion → tubular cell necrosis, especially in proximal tubule and thick ascending limb (high O₂ demand).<br /></br>Nephrotoxic ATN: drugs (aminoglycosides, radiocontrast, cisplatin), pigments (myoglobin, hemoglobin), toxins (ethylene glycol).<br /></br>• Tubular cells die → obstruction, back-leak of filtrate, ↓GFR, ↑FeNa (&gt;2%), muddy brown casts.<br /></br>Phases: inciting event → maintenance (oliguric, ↑K⁺/↑BUN/creatinine, metabolic acidosis) → recovery (polyuric).  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment  </p></br><p>Diagnostics (not required for answer but high-yield):<br /></br>• UA: muddy brown casts, FeNa &gt;2%, urine osmolality &lt;350 mOsm/kg, BUN:Cr &lt;15.<br /></br>• Exclude obstruction with bladder scan/renal ultrasound if uncertain.  </p></br><p>Management:<br /></br>1. Remove/treat underlying cause (stop gentamicin, control sepsis, maintain MAP).<br /></br>2. Optimize volume status; avoid further nephrotoxins.<br /></br>3. Dialysis if AEIOU criteria (Acidosis, Electrolytes, Intoxication, Overload, Uremia).  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Muddy brown - think ground”<br /></br>Picture a muddy brown river flowing through broken tubes → Acute Tubular Necrosis.  </p></br><p>Hyaline = “high and dry” (prerenal).<br /></br>Fatty casts/oval fat bodies = “Fatty nephrotic cat.”<br /></br>RBC casts = “Red cell GN shed.”<br /></br>WBC casts/eosinophils = “White cells are pyo/interstitial.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Bland urine with only hyaline casts = PRErenal azotemia (functional). Patient is already hypotensive, but intrinsic damage is evident (creatinine doubled despite fluids; on nephrotoxin). Hyaline casts lack pigmented granular appearance.  </p></br><p>B. 4+ protein + oval fat bodies (“Maltese crosses”) = Nephrotic syndrome (minimal change, FSGS, etc.), not acute oliguria from sepsis.  </p></br><p>C. Numerous WBC (&gt;50/hpf) + WBC clumps suggest acute pyelonephritis or severe lower UTI; would have flank pain, bacteriuria, not sepsis-induced AKI.  </p></br><p>E. Eosinophiluria (10–20/hpf) suggests acute interstitial nephritis (often β-lactams, NSAIDs). Gentamicin classically causes ATN, not AIN; plus eosinophils would be highlighted with Hansel stain.  </p></br><p>F. Heavy hematuria with RBC casts (&gt;50 RBC/hpf) = acute glomerulonephritis (e.g., post-strep, IgA). Would see dysmorphic RBCs, possible hypertension, not typical in sepsis with aminoglycoside.  </p></br><p>G. 4+ blood but none on microscopy (“heme-positive dipstick, no RBCs”) plus pigmented granular casts &amp; squamous cells → myoglobinuria/hemoglobinuria (e.g., rhabdo). Not suggested here.  </p></br><p>H. Dysmorphic RBCs 10–20/hpf = glomerular hematuria. Again doesn’t fit ischemic/nephrotoxic context.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line:<br /></br>In a septic, hypotensive, aminoglycoside-exposed patient who develops oliguria and rising creatinine within 48-72 h, think Acute Tubular Necrosis, classically showing muddy brown (pigmented granular) casts with renal tubular epithelial cells on urinalysis.</p>
48. A 17-year-old patient, gravida 1, para 0, aborta 1, comes to the physician for a follow-up examination. Four weeks ago, she underwent dilatation and curettage (D&C) following a spontaneous abortion at 13 weeks' gestation. She has had no vaginal bleeding or pelvic pain. The patient has no history of serious illness. Her only current medication is an oral contraceptive; she adheres to her medication regimen. Today, pelvic examination shows no abnormalities. Prior to the D&C, her serum β-hCG concentration was 50,326 mIU/mL. Since the D&C, serial measurements of serum β-hCG concentrations have shown:</br>Week   β-hCG (mIU/mL)</br>1      18,275</br>2      8,157</br>3      8,356</br>4      14,589</br>Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Endometritis</br>b. Gestational trophoblastic neoplasia</br>c. Hydatidiform mole</br>d. Retained products of conception</br>e. Septic abortion</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Gestational trophoblastic neoplasia (GTN)</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Timeline &amp; key event: Four weeks ago the patient had a suction D&amp;C for a spontaneous abortion at 13 weeks.<br /></br>• Serial β-hCG pattern:<br /></br>  – Week 1 → 18,275 mIU/mL (appropriate drop)<br /></br>  – Week 2 →  8,157 mIU/mL (continued drop)<br /></br>  – Week 3 →  8,356 mIU/mL (plateau)<br /></br>  – Week 4 → 14,589 mIU/mL (rise)<br /></br>• Symptoms: No pain, no bleeding, afebrile—so uterine infection less likely.</p></br><p>Testing “rules of thumb” for GTN (FIGO criteria):<br /></br>1. Plateau of β-hCG (± 10%) on ≥4 values over ≥3 weeks OR<br /></br>2. Rise of β-hCG ≥10% on ≥3 values over ≥2 weeks OR<br /></br>3. Detectable β-hCG ≥6 months after any pregnancy<br /></br>This patient meets criterion 1 (plateau) and criterion 2 (subsequent rise).</p></br><p>Take-home pattern: After evacuation of any pregnancy (mole, term, ectopic, or miscarriage), β-hCG should fall to zero. Plateau or rise = persistent trophoblastic tissue → GTN until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: As soon as you see post-pregnancy serial β-hCG values that stop falling, think “gestational trophoblastic neoplasia.”</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)<br /></br>1. Normal expectation: β-hCG should decline by ~50% every 48–72 h and become undetectable within 4–8 weeks after first-trimester pregnancy loss.<br /></br>2. Plateau at weeks 2–3 signals abnormal persistence.<br /></br>3. Rise at week 4 confirms active trophoblastic proliferation.<br /></br>4. Absence of infection signs or retained tissue symptoms points away from infectious or mechanical etiologies.<br /></br>Core concept: Persistent or rising β-hCG after pregnancy = GTN (most commonly an invasive mole or choriocarcinoma).</p></br><p>Documentation (real life): serial β-hCG values, absence of symptoms, planned imaging (pelvic US, chest X-ray for metastases), oncology referral, contraception counseling.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• GTN = spectrum (invasive mole, choriocarcinoma, placental-site trophoblastic tumor, epithelioid trophoblastic tumor).<br /></br>• Originates from any antecedent pregnancy (50% follow molar pregnancies, but up to 25% follow term or spontaneous abortions).<br /></br>• Trophoblastic cells continue proliferating → β-hCG production.<br /></br>• High vascularity → hematogenous spread (first to lungs).</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment<br /></br>1. Confirmatory work-up:<br /></br>   – Quantitative β-hCG weekly until three negatives, then monthly × 6–12 mo.<br /></br>   – Pelvic ultrasound.<br /></br>   – Baseline CXR/CT chest (lungs = most common metastasis).<br /></br>2. First-line therapy (non-metastatic or low-risk metastatic): Single-agent methotrexate ± leucovorin OR actinomycin D.<br /></br>   – High-risk metastatic disease: multi-agent chemotherapy (EMA-CO: etoposide, methotrexate, actinomycin D, cyclophosphamide, vincristine).<br /></br>3. Contraception: Reliable contraception (OCPs, depot shot) to avoid pregnancy during surveillance period.<br /></br>4. Cure rates &gt;90% when detected early.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Mole Makes hCG that Must Move”:<br /></br>M – Monitor hCG<br /></br>M – Months (at least 6)<br /></br>M – Methotrexate if hCG rises<br /></br>If β-hCG “plateaus or climbs, you’re out of line” → treat for GTN.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>A. Endometritis<br /></br>• Presents with postpartum/post-procedure fever, uterine tenderness, foul discharge.<br /></br>• β-hCG trends irrelevant; infection, not trophoblastic proliferation.</p></br><p>C. Hydatidiform mole<br /></br>• Would have been identified on pathology or ultrasound at the time of D&amp;C (snow-storm appearance, vesicular tissue).<br /></br>• Initial hCG (50,326 mIU/mL at 13 weeks) is within normal pregnancy range, not the markedly high levels (&gt;100,000) typical of complete moles.</p></br><p>D. Retained products of conception (RPOC)<br /></br>• Would cause persistent bleeding, cramping, possible enlarged vascular mass on ultrasound.<br /></br>• β-hCG usually declines slowly but does not first plateau then rise; symptoms expected.</p></br><p>E. Septic abortion<br /></br>• Acute presentation: fever, chills, abdominal pain, purulent discharge within days of miscarriage.<br /></br>• Not compatible with asymptomatic patient 4 weeks post-procedure.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>After any pregnancy ends, β-hCG should drop to zero. A plateau followed by a rise signals gestational trophoblastic neoplasia—treatable, but requires prompt recognition, chemotherapy, and continued β-hCG surveillance.</p>
49. A previously healthy 23-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 5-day history of fever, malaise, and headache. He has not had nausea, vomiting, cough, sore throat, diarrhea, pain with urination, or penile discharge. Medications include pseudoephedrine and ibuprofen for sinus headaches. He has no known recent exposure to illness and has not traveled recently outside the USA. He has been sexually active with four female partners over the past year and uses condoms inconsistently. He drinks two six-packs of beer weekly. He lives near tick-infested woods but has not noted a tick bite. He is 183 cm (6 ft) tall and weighs 95 kg (210 lb); BMI is 29 kg/m2. His temperature is 38.3 C (101 F), pulse is 72/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 126/68 mm Hg. Examination shows a nonpruritic, painless, pigmented, maculopapular rash over the palms and soles. There is diffuse lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is most likely to have prevented this patient's symptoms?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Abstinence from alcohol</br>b. Avoidance of ibuprofen use</br>c. Avoidance of soaps containing perfume</br>d. Consistent use of condoms</br>e. Immediate removal of ticks</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Consistent use of condoms  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • Age 23, previously healthy, sexually active with multiple partners, inconsistent condom use<br /></br>   • 5-day history of fever, malaise, headache (viral-like prodrome)<br /></br>   • Non-pruritic, painless, pigmented maculopapular rash on PALMS &amp; SOLES<br /></br>   • Generalized (diffuse cervical, axillary, inguinal) lymphadenopathy<br /></br>   • No GI, GU, or respiratory symptoms; no known tick bite  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pattern recognition<br /></br>   Palms-and-soles rash + systemic “flu-like” illness + generalized LAD in a young adult with risky sexual behavior = classic SECONDARY SYPHILIS.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Critical positives/negatives<br /></br>   • Pos: palms/soles rash, constitutional symptoms, diffuse LAD, sexual risk factors<br /></br>   • Neg: no tick bite, no petechial rash beginning on wrists/ankles, no severe myalgias (argues against RMSF); no drug eruption history  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive strategy for future questions<br /></br>   Whenever you see a palms-and-soles rash, run through “TERPS”:<br /></br>   T — TSS / Typhus,<br /></br>   E — Endocarditis (Janeway),<br /></br>   R — RMSF,<br /></br>   P — Palms/Soles Syphilis,<br /></br>   S — Scarlet fever (rare) / Smallpox (historic).<br /></br>   Combine with sexual history → Syphilis rises to the top.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thinking:<br /></br>1. Identify the hallmark rash (palms/soles).<br /></br>2. Evaluate epidemiology (multiple partners, inconsistent protection).<br /></br>3. Match constitutional symptoms and LAD to the secondary phase of Treponema pallidum infection.<br /></br>4. Ask: “What would have prevented acquisition?” → barrier protection (male latex condom).  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Consistent condom use is the single most effective, practical strategy to prevent sexual transmission of Treponema pallidum and thereby secondary syphilis.  </p></br><p>Medical documentation pearls<br /></br>   • Sexual history (partners, practices, protection, past STIs)<br /></br>   • Rash description (location, morphology, distribution)<br /></br>   • Lymph node exam<br /></br>   • Plan: RPR/VDRL, confirm with treponemal test, treat with IM benzathine penicillin G.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)  </p></br><p>• Primary syphilis: painless chancre 3 wk after exposure.<br /></br>• Secondary syphilis (6-12 wk): hematogenous spread → generalized LAD, condyloma lata, maculopapular rash incl. palms/soles.<br /></br>• Latent → Tertiary (gummas, aortitis, neurosyphilis).  </p></br><p>Treponema pallidum is transmitted via direct contact with mucocutaneous lesions or blood during sexual activity. Latex condoms greatly reduce (≈98%) transmission by providing a physical barrier.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostics Snapshot  </p></br><p>Diagnostics (not needed to answer prevention question but test-relevant):<br /></br>   • Screening: RPR or VDRL → confirm: FTA-ABS or TPPA.<br /></br>Treatment:<br /></br>   • 1 dose IM benzathine penicillin G for primary &amp; secondary disease.<br /></br>   • Desensitize if pregnant and penicillin-allergic.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“Secondary Syphilis is Systemic” → rash everywhere, including palms &amp; soles.<br /></br>“Condoms Cover the Corkscrew” (Treponema’s shape) → barrier protection prevents infection.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Abstinence from alcohol<br /></br>   • Alcohol does not predispose to syphilis; reducing alcohol would not prevent infection.  </p></br><p>b. Avoidance of ibuprofen use<br /></br>   • NSAIDs can cause drug eruptions but usually pruritic, diffuse, NOT palms-specific and NOT associated with generalized LAD &amp; sexual risk.  </p></br><p>c. Avoidance of scented soaps<br /></br>   • Contact dermatitis produces pruritic, erythematous rash localized to area of exposure; no fever, LAD, or systemic symptoms.  </p></br><p>e. Immediate removal of ticks<br /></br>   • Early tick removal prevents RMSF &amp; Lyme. RMSF rash starts wrists/ankles, becomes petechial, severe HA &amp; high fever; diffuse LAD uncommon. Lack of tick bite history and strong sexual risk makes RMSF far less likely.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Secondary syphilis presents with a systemic illness and palms-soles rash in sexually active patients. Consistent condom use would have prevented Treponema pallidum transmission and the patient’s current symptoms.</p>
50. A 30-year-old primigravid woman with systemic lupus erythematosus comes to the physician for a prenatal visit at 34 weeks’ gestation. Examination shows that her disease is in remission. She is concerned about the possible effects of her disease on her fetus. The fetus is at greatest risk for which of the following?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Atrial septal defect</br>b. Complete atrioventricular block</br>c. Endocarditis</br>d. Supraventricular tachycardia</br>e. Ventricular septal defect</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>B. Complete atrioventricular (AV) block  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• “Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE)” – Think of maternal autoantibodies (anti-Ro/SSA ± anti-La/SSB).<br /></br>• “Primigravid … 34 weeks … disease in remission” – Active inflammation is not the issue; transplacental antibodies are.<br /></br>• Question stem: “possible effects of her disease on her fetus” – asking for the classic fetal/neonatal complication of maternal SLE.  </p></br><p>High-yield pattern: Maternal anti-Ro/SSA (and/or anti-La/SSB) IgG crosses the placenta → damages fetal conduction tissue → congenital heart block, almost always a 3° (complete) AV block that appears late 2nd to 3rd trimester or at birth.  </p></br><p>Exam strategy: Whenever a vignette mentions maternal SLE (or Sjögren), immediately put “complete congenital AV block” near the top of the differential for fetal problems.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Identify maternal condition that can affect the fetus independent of current disease activity.<br /></br>2. Recall that SLE is associated with anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB antibodies.<br /></br>3. Understand these IgG antibodies freely cross the placenta beginning ≈12–16 weeks.<br /></br>4. Know the organ most vulnerable is the fetal cardiac conduction system → fibrosis and calcification of AV node → irreversible complete heart block.<br /></br>5. Translate to answer choice: “complete AV block.”  </p></br><p>Take-home: Neonatal lupus = photosensitive rash + congenital heart block (most specific, highest morbidity).  </p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): Counsel about potential congenital heart block; plan serial fetal echocardiography / Doppler for PR interval; document antibody titers, counseling, and peripartum monitoring plan.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Autoantigen: Ro (SSA) and La (SSB) ribonucleoproteins.<br /></br>• Mechanism: Transplacental IgG binds fetal cardiac tissue → immune-mediated inflammation → fibrosis of the AV node and surrounding myocardium.<br /></br>• Timing: Injury occurs between ≈18–24 weeks; block is usually permanent.<br /></br>• Risk: 1–3 % in anti-Ro–positive mothers; ↑ to 15–20 % if prior child had the lesion.<br /></br>• Other neonatal lupus findings: Cutaneous annular rash, hepatobiliary disease, cytopenias—most resolve as maternal IgG wanes (by 6 mo), unlike heart block.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>Antenatal:<br /></br>• Serial fetal echocardiography q1-2 wks starting 16–18 wks to detect PR prolongation or evolving block.<br /></br>• If 1°/2° block detected, maternal fluorinated steroids (e.g., dexamethasone) may limit progression.  </p></br><p>Postnatal:<br /></br>• ECG confirms complete AV block (dissociated P &amp; QRS).<br /></br>• Neonates often require permanent pacemaker if symptomatic (bradycardia, heart failure).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Lupus Loves to Knock the Line”:<br /></br>LUpus (anti-LU) → damages the conduction Line (AV node) → Heart’s electrical line is cut = complete block.  </p></br><p>Picture mnemonic: Mom wolf (lupus) handing baby a broken electrical cord labeled “AV.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Atrial septal defect – Structural defect from 4–6 wk embryogenesis; not linked to maternal antibodies or SLE. No conduction abnormality expected.  </p></br><p>C. Endocarditis – Infective process, not congenital; requires microbial seeding, not transplacental autoantibodies.  </p></br><p>D. Supraventricular tachycardia – Most common fetal arrhythmia overall but not specifically associated with SLE; autoantibody-mediated injury causes bradyarrhythmia (block), not tachyarrhythmia.  </p></br><p>E. Ventricular septal defect – Like ASD, arises during cardiogenesis; incidence not increased in maternal SLE.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: Students think “heart disease” → assume structural defects; remember SLE causes an electrical (conduction) problem, not a septal hole.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core “Shelf/Step 2” Takeaway<br /></br>Maternal anti-Ro/SSA (± anti-La/SSB) antibodies from SLE or Sjögren cross the placenta and most commonly produce irreversible complete congenital AV block in the fetus/neonate.</p>
51. A 9-month-old boy is brought to the physician by his mother for a well-child examination. She is concerned because over the past 4 weeks, her son has cried, screamed, and rubbed his ears whenever one of her friends holds him. She is the only person who can console him, and it now takes longer to soothe him. He was previously a happy, easygoing baby. When the physician approaches him, he begins to cry. He is at the 50th percentile for height and 75th percentile for weight. Examination shows no abnormalities except for clear rhinorrhea. He sits up independently, pulls himself to standing, and crawls along furniture. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Reassure his mother that this is age-appropriate behavior</br>b. Notify child protective services of possible abuse by one of his mother's friends</br>c. Order a CT scan of the head</br>d. Begin amoxicillin therapy</br>e. Begin low-dose lorazepam therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Reassure his mother that this is age-appropriate behavior</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Strategy<br /></br>1. Key age clue: 9-month-old.<br /></br>2. New behavior: cries, screams, rubs ears only when unfamiliar people approach; soothed only by mother.<br /></br>3. Physical exam: normal ear exam, afebrile, development on track (sitting, pulling to stand, cruising).<br /></br>4. Pattern recognition: “stranger anxiety” classically begins at 6–9 months, peaks at ~12 months, and can last until 18 months. It may be accompanied by ear tugging or head turning purely as a self-soothing maneuver, not infection.<br /></br>5. Cognitive approach for future questions: Whenever a well-appearing 6- to 12-month child shows distress around unfamiliar adults with an otherwise normal exam, think “normal socio-emotional milestone → reassure.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>• Age 9 mo → milestone window for stranger anxiety.<br /></br>• Behavior occurs only with strangers; immediately soothed by primary caregiver → fits textbook description.<br /></br>• No fever, no erythematous TM, normal exam → rules out otitis media or other pathology.<br /></br>• Therefore the most appropriate “management” is education and reassurance, not diagnostic work-up or medication.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Stranger anxiety is a normal developmental milestone, not pathology.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl (real-life chart): “Discussed normal stranger anxiety expected 6–18 mo; provided anticipatory guidance on comforting strategies; no signs of infection or abuse.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology High-Yield Review<br /></br>• Stranger anxiety results from maturation of object permanence and recognition memory; infant now differentiates primary caregivers from others and mounts a fear response to perceived threat.<br /></br>• Peaks around 9–12 mo, then gradually declines.<br /></br>• Separation anxiety follows (peaks 12–18 mo).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management &amp; Follow-Up<br /></br>• Reassure and give anticipatory guidance: allow gradual exposure to new people, keep caregiver in sight, offer transitional objects.<br /></br>• No imaging, antibiotics, or anxiolytics indicated.<br /></br>• Re-evaluate at routine health maintenance visits unless red-flag symptoms (fever, otalgia, developmental regression, signs of abuse) arise.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Mnemonic / Memory Aid<br /></br>“Stranger Danger starts at 6–9.”<br /></br>If the baby is in the crawling phase (9 mo) and cries with anyone new, it’s probably normal stranger danger.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>B. Notify child protective services of possible abuse<br /></br>• Abuse might present with bruises, fractures, developmental regression, or fearful behavior toward a specific person—not a generalized reaction to all strangers. No physical or historical findings suggest maltreatment.</p></br><p>C. Order a CT scan of the head<br /></br>• No neurological signs (vomiting, seizure, bulging fontanelle) or trauma history. Imaging would expose unnecessary radiation.</p></br><p>D. Begin amoxicillin therapy<br /></br>• Acute otitis media requires middle-ear effusion plus bulging, erythematous TM, decreased mobility, fever, or ear pain. None are present; clear rhinorrhea alone suggests a viral URI.</p></br><p>E. Begin low-dose lorazepam therapy<br /></br>• Benzodiazepines are never used for normal developmental behaviors in infants; risks include respiratory depression and dependence.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In a 6- to 12-month well-appearing child who cries around unfamiliar people, the correct response is reassurance: this is classic stranger anxiety, a normal developmental milestone.</p>
52. A previously healthy 27-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a "crawling" sensation over her skin that began 1 hour ago after she ate a candy bar containing peanuts. She has a peanut allergy but did not realize the bar contained peanuts. She says that she used an epinephrine auto-injector as soon as symptoms developed but still does not feel well. She notes that her lips have become swollen and feel especially "itchy and tingly." She has had three episodes of diarrhea. She appears flushed. Her temperature is 37 C (98.6 F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 27/min, and blood pressure is 85/42 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 92 percent. Examination shows slightly swollen lips and cheeks and erythematous areas over the face and extremities. Breath sounds are decreased, and expiratory wheezes are heard throughout the lungs. An ECG shows sinus tachycardia. In addition to epinephrine, which of the following should be administered?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Dopamine</br>b. Phenylephrine</br>c. 0.9 percent Saline</br>d. Theophylline</br>e. Vasopressin</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>C. 0.9 percent saline (large-volume isotonic crystalloid infusion)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Key “buzz” words that signal anaphylaxis:<br /></br>   • Peanut exposure in a known allergic patient<br /></br>   • Seconds–minutes onset of pruritus (“crawling sensation”), lip swelling, urticaria/flushing, wheezing, diarrhea (histamine‐mediated GI symptoms)<br /></br>   • Vital-sign triad of anaphylactic shock: tachycardia, hypotension (85/42 mm Hg), tachypnea with ↓SpO₂  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives: mucocutaneous involvement, respiratory compromise, hypotension—meets NIAID criteria for anaphylaxis.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negative: no fever, no focal lung consolidation—rules out septic or cardiogenic etiology.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Anytime you see distributive shock with rapid allergic exposure, think “Epi first, fluids second, then vasopressors if still hypotensive.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why 0.9 % saline?)<br /></br>Step-wise management after epinephrine:<br /></br>1. IM epinephrine (0.3–0.5 mg of 1:1,000) is first-line. She already used her auto-injector.<br /></br>2. Anaphylaxis causes profound vasodilation and ↑capillary permeability → 30–50 % of intravascular volume can third-space within minutes. Hypotension is therefore primarily due to absolute/relative hypovolemia.<br /></br>3. Immediate aggressive isotonic crystalloid boluses (1–2 L in adults, repeat as needed) restore preload, stroke volume, and blood pressure while additional epinephrine doses, H₁/H₂ blockers, and steroids are arranged.</p></br><p>Documentation note: “Administered 2 L IV normal saline bolus for anaphylactic shock; reassessed vitals q5 min.”</p></br><p>Take-home: After epinephrine, give fluids early; most patients respond to crystalloids and do not require pressors.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• IgE-mediated mast-cell/basophil degranulation → histamine, tryptase, prostaglandins, leukotrienes<br /></br>• Vascular effects: arterial/venous dilation + ↑vascular permeability → distributive + hypovolemic shock → hypotension<br /></br>• Pulmonary effects: bronchoconstriction, airway edema → wheezing, hypoxia<br /></br>• Cutaneous/GI effects: urticaria, angioedema, diarrhea</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatments (high-yield outline)<br /></br>Immediate:<br /></br>1. IM epinephrine (may repeat every 5–15 min)<br /></br>2. High-flow oxygen; airway equipment ready<br /></br>3. Large-bore IV access → 1–2 L NS bolus<br /></br>Adjuncts:<br /></br>• H₁ blocker (diphenhydramine) + H₂ blocker (ranitidine/famotidine)<br /></br>• Glucocorticoid (methylprednisolone) to reduce biphasic reaction<br /></br>• Inhaled β₂-agonist (albuterol) for persistent bronchospasm<br /></br>Refractory hypotension after 2–3 L fluids + multiple epi doses → start IV epinephrine infusion, then consider other pressors.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“EPI-FAST” for anaphylaxis:<br /></br>E – Epinephrine IM first<br /></br>P – Position supine, legs up<br /></br>I – IV access → Isotonic fluids<br /></br>F – Fluids 1–2 L<br /></br>A – Antihistamines (H1/H2)<br /></br>S – Steroid<br /></br>T – β₂ inhaler (Terbutaline/albuterol)  </p></br><p>Picture: flood the leaky vessel “pipes” (fluids) before you tighten them with extra pressors.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Dopamine – Second-line vasopressor for septic or cardiogenic shock. In anaphylaxis, treat volume loss first; dopamine adds chronotropy/vasoconstriction but does not correct intravascular depletion and has slower onset than IV epi.  </p></br><p>b. Phenylephrine – Pure α-agonist; useful if tachyarrhythmia precludes β-agonists. Lacks bronchodilation and, again, does not address capillary leak–mediated hypovolemia; fluids come first.  </p></br><p>d. Theophylline – Methylxanthine bronchodilator formerly used in asthma/COPD exacerbation; narrow therapeutic window, no role in acute anaphylaxis once albuterol/epi available.  </p></br><p>e. Vasopressin – V₁-mediated vasoconstrictor reserved for refractory septic shock or vasodilatory shock after fluids + catecholamines. Not first-line after initial epi dose.  </p></br><p>Key discriminator: Only isotonic saline directly corrects the primary hemodynamic problem (relative hypovolemia) that underlies the patient’s hypotension immediately following epinephrine administration.</p>
53. A previously healthy 27-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department 48 hours after the onset of confusion. She has a 1-week history of temperatures to 39.3 C (102.8 F) and yellow eyes. Her only medication is an oral contraceptive. She is oriented to person but not to place or time. Her temperature now is 38.6 C (101.5 F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Examination shows scleral icterus and scattered petechiae over the trunk and lower extremities. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hematocrit 25 percent</br>Leukocyte count 13,000/mm^3</br>Platelet count 11,000/mm^3</br>Prothrombin time 11 sec (INR=1.0)</br>Partial thromboplastin time 30 sec</br>Serum</br>  Creatinine 4.5 mg/dL</br>  Bilirubin, total 6 mg/dL</br>  Direct 0.9 mg/dL</br>Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Aspirin therapy</br>b. Immune globulin therapy</br>c. Glucocorticoid therapy</br>d. Plasmapheresis</br>e. Splenectomy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>d. Plasmapheresis (therapeutic plasma exchange)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>   • Fever to 39 °C<br /></br>   • Confusion (neurologic deficit)<br /></br>   • Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia – Hct 25 % with indirect-predominant hyperbilirubinemia (total 6, direct 0.9)<br /></br>   • Severe thrombocytopenia – platelets 11 000/µL<br /></br>   • Acute renal injury – creatinine 4.5 mg/dL<br /></br>   • Normal PT and PTT (rules out DIC)<br /></br>   • Scattered petechiae (bleeding from low platelets)</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pattern recognition: the “FAT RN” pentad—Fever, Anemia, Thrombocytopenia, Renal failure, Neurologic findings—screams Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP).</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives: normal coagulation studies exclude DIC; no isolated thrombocytopenia (rules out ITP); no prolonged hemolytic course (rules out autoimmune hemolytic anemia).</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive/Metacognitive tip: When you see thrombocytopenia + MAHA + normal PT/PTT, think TTP/HUS first. Then scan for fever or neuro signs; if present, choose TTP and treat immediately.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• TTP is caused by acquired (autoantibody-mediated) or congenital deficiency of the vWF-cleaving protease ADAMTS13 → large vWF multimers → platelet aggregation in small vessels → microthrombi.<br /></br>• Mortality is &gt;90 % if untreated; plasma exchange removes the autoantibody and replenishes ADAMTS13.<br /></br>• Therefore, the single most life-saving, time-critical intervention is emergent plasmapheresis.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): “Impression: TTP vs HUS. Plan: STAT plasma exchange, send ADAMTS13 level but do not delay therapy. Admit to ICU.”</p></br><p>Core takeaway: In TTP, do plasma exchange first and fast.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)  </p></br><p>Etiology:<br /></br>• 90 % acquired – IgG antibody against ADAMTS13 (often idiopathic, postpartum, or drug-associated)<br /></br>• 10 % hereditary (Upshaw-Schulman)  </p></br><p>Mechanism: UN-cleaved ultralarge vWF multimers → platelet microthrombi → shear stress → schistocytes, hemolysis → organ ischemia.</p></br><p>Lab pattern:<br /></br>• ↓ Hgb/Hct, ↑ LDH, ↑ indirect bilirubin, ↑ retics<br /></br>• ↑ creatinine<br /></br>• Extreme ↓ platelets<br /></br>• Normal PT/PTT &amp; fibrinogen (distinguish from DIC)<br /></br>• Peripheral smear: schistocytes, few/no platelets.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><p>Immediate:<br /></br>1. Therapeutic plasma exchange (until platelets &gt;150 000 for 2 consecutive days)<br /></br>2. High-dose IV glucocorticoids are commonly added after exchange begins, but plasma exchange is FIRST and most important.<br /></br>3. Rituximab for refractory/relapsing cases.<br /></br>4. Splenectomy only if refractory to medical therapy.  </p></br><p>No platelet transfusions unless life-threatening bleed (can fuel thrombosis).</p></br><p>Confirmation tests (do NOT delay therapy): ADAMTS13 activity &lt;10 %, inhibitor assay.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic “FAT RN needs a PLASMA exchange”:<br /></br>F – Fever<br /></br>A – Anemia (MAHA)<br /></br>T – Thrombocytopenia<br /></br>R – Renal failure<br /></br>N – Neurologic changes<br /></br>Treatment = PLASMA exchange.</p></br><p>Visual: Picture a nurse (“RN”) who is “FAT” rushing a bag of plasma to the bedside.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Aspirin therapy<br /></br>   • Antiplatelet drugs help prevent arterial thrombosis but do not address the pathogenesis of TTP and do not rapidly reverse life-threatening disease.</p></br><p>b. Immune globulin therapy (IVIG)<br /></br>   • First-line for immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) where isolated thrombocytopenia is seen. Here, anemia, renal failure, and normal PT/PTT argue against ITP.</p></br><p>c. Glucocorticoid therapy<br /></br>   • Steroids are adjunctive in TTP but cannot replace plasmapheresis. Starting steroids alone delays definitive care and increases mortality.</p></br><p>e. Splenectomy<br /></br>   • Curative for hereditary spherocytosis or refractory ITP/TTP, but only after failure of plasma exchange and medical therapy. Not appropriate as the initial step.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: The presence of the FAT RN pentad with normal coagulation studies = Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura → initiate emergent plasmapheresis immediately to remove ADAMTS13 autoantibodies and supply functional enzyme.</p>
54. A 9-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her parents because of increasingly strange behavior during the past 6 months. They report that she touches and smells household objects and repeatedly turns the lights on and off in her room. She has been worried because of frequent thoughts of her dog being hit by a car and her parents being involved in a motor vehicle collision even though these things have never happened. She has begun to tap her foot three times when she experiences these thoughts to prevent them from happening. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Mental status examination shows an anxious mood and a constricted affect. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: G</br></br>a. Clonidine</br>b. Clozapine</br>c. Dextroamphetamine</br>d. Haloperidol</br>e. Naloxone</br>f. Nortriptyline</br>g. Sertraline</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer (per answer key)</br>g. Sertraline  </p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Approach<br /></br>1. “Frequent thoughts of her dog being hit…parents in a car crash” = intrusive, unwanted, anxiety-provoking thoughts → obsessions.<br /></br>2. “Touches and smells household objects…turns lights on and off…taps her foot three times to prevent them from happening” = repetitive behaviors performed to reduce anxiety or prevent a dreaded event → compulsions.<br /></br>3. No psychotic features, normal physical exam, onset in a school-age child.<br /></br>→ Classic presentation of obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) in a pediatric patient.</p></br><p>Metacognitive pearl: As soon as you see intrusive thoughts + ritualistic behaviors performed for anxiety relief, jump to OCD. The stem may add superfluous detail; ignore distractors that target ADHD (stimulants, α-agonists) or psychosis (antipsychotics).</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>First-line treatment for OCD in children and adults:<br /></br>• Cognitive-behavioral therapy with exposure-and-response prevention (ERP)<br /></br>• Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI)</p></br><p>Among the choices, sertraline is the only SSRI. Therefore it is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy.</p></br><p>Take-home “exam tag line”: OCD → SSRI (fluoxetine, fluvoxamine, sertraline, paroxetine) ± CBT; clomipramine is second-line.</p></br><p>Documentation if this were a real patient: intrusive thoughts, compulsions, time spent (&gt;1 h/day), functional impairment, onset, comorbidities, suicide screen, parental education, treatment plan (CBT referral, start SSRI, informed consent including black-box warning).</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Pathophysiology High-Yield Review<br /></br>• Etiology: Dysregulation of serotonergic pathways involving cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical circuits.<br /></br>• Risk factors: Family history of OCD or Tourette disorder, perinatal complications, streptococcal infections (PANDAS).<br /></br>• Manifestations: Obsessions (thoughts, images, urges) + compulsions (behaviors or mental acts) that are time-consuming or impairing.<br /></br>• Course: Mean onset 9–11 y in boys, slightly later in girls; chronic if untreated.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>1. Non-pharm: CBT with ERP is gold standard; can be monotherapy for mild cases.<br /></br>2. Pharm:<br /></br>   • Start SSRI (e.g., sertraline 25 mg daily in children, titrate up).<br /></br>   • Full therapeutic response may require higher doses and 10–12 wk.<br /></br>3. Second-line / augmentation: Clomipramine (TCA), antipsychotic add-on (risperidone) if tics or psychosis.<br /></br>4. Lab monitoring: Baseline weight, BP; monitor for SSRI adverse effects (GI upset, activation, SI).  </p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Memory Aids<br /></br>“OCD = Only needs a Serotonin pill + Compulsion-Breaking Therapy”<br /></br>Visual: Picture a “SERial light switch” (sertraline) that the child turns on and off.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Clonidine – α2-agonist used for ADHD hyperactivity or Tourette tics, not for intrusive thoughts/compulsions.<br /></br>b. Clozapine – Reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia with suicidality; major risk of agranulocytosis.<br /></br>c. Dextroamphetamine – Stimulant for ADHD/narcolepsy; would worsen anxiety and has no effect on OCD rituals.<br /></br>d. Haloperidol – Typical antipsychotic; sometimes for severe Tourette tics or acute agitation, not first-line for OCD and carries EPS risk.<br /></br>e. Naloxone – Opioid antagonist for opioid overdose; irrelevant here.<br /></br>f. Nortriptyline – Secondary-amine TCA; not as effective as clomipramine for OCD and more side effects than SSRIs; not first-line in children.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Key Takeaway<br /></br>Pediatric OCD → start with CBT (ERP) ± an SSRI (sertraline, fluoxetine, fluvoxamine). Recognize obsessions + compulsions early, avoid distractors aimed at ADHD or psychosis.</p>
55. A 44-year-old man comes to the office with his girlfriend because she is concerned about his mood. He recently told her it would be a relief if he were hit by a truck and died. He reports frequent fatigue and says he has been persistently unhappy for 9 years. He has not had problems with sleep or appetite. He has type 1 diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease, hypertension, and peripheral neuropathy. His medications are furosemide, gabapentin, insulin, metoprolol, and simvastatin. He lost joint custody of his two children 1 month ago, which he says is “a real downer.” He finds his work as a convenience store clerk tedious and uninteresting, but he enjoys playing poker and watching movies. He has used cocaine once monthly during the past 5 years. His pulse is 84/min, and blood pressure is 146/88 mm Hg. Physical examination shows retinal hemorrhages. On mental status examination, he has a dysphoric mood and sarcastic affect. There is evidence of interpersonal friction with his girlfriend. He reports no current suicidal ideation or intent. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Adjustment disorder with depressed mood</br>b. Borderline personality disorder</br>c. Major depressive disorder</br>d. Persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia)</br>e. Substance-induced mood disorder</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Persistent depressive disorder (dysthymia)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Duration – “persistently unhappy for 9 years.”<br /></br>   • ≥ 2 years of chronic low-grade depression is the single strongest clue for persistent depressive disorder (PDD).<br /></br>2. Symptom profile – He lacks classic vegetative symptoms (no change in sleep or appetite) and still finds pleasure in poker/movies, so he does not meet the full 5/9 SIGECAPS criteria for an episode of major depressive disorder (MDD).<br /></br>3. Functional impact – Fatigue, hopeless comments (“would be a relief if he were hit by a truck”), sarcastic/dysphoric affect, friction with girlfriend → typical low-grade but enduring impairment seen in PDD.<br /></br>4. No full remission – Nothing in the stem suggests any 2-month symptom-free interval over the past 9 years, another DSM-5 requirement for PDD.<br /></br>5. Substance/cocaine – Uses only monthly; mood symptoms pre-date and persist between uses, making a substance-induced etiology unlikely.<br /></br>Metacognitive tip: Any vignette with chronic (&gt;2 y) “always been somewhat depressed” mood should immediately raise PDD to the top of the differential; quickly scan for full SIGECAPS episode to decide between PDD alone or PDD + MDD (“double depression”).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)<br /></br>Step 1 – Ask, “How long has the low mood lasted?” → &gt; 2 years.<br /></br>Step 2 – Do they meet full SIGECAPS (5 of 9) for at least 2 weeks? → No.<br /></br>Step 3 – Any ≥ 2-month stretches without symptoms? → Not mentioned, so assume none.<br /></br>Step 4 – Rule out substances, medical illness, bipolar swings → Cocaine use infrequent; multiple chronic medical problems but long-standing mood disorder predates/worsens independently.<br /></br>Conclusion – DSM-5 criteria for persistent depressive disorder satisfied.</p></br><p>Take-home concept:<br /></br>PDD = chronic, low-grade depression lasting ≥ 2 years (≥ 1 year in children/teens) with at least 2 of the following:<br /></br>• poor appetite/overeating<br /></br>• insomnia/hypersomnia<br /></br>• low energy/fatigue<br /></br>• low self-esteem<br /></br>• poor concentration/indecisiveness<br /></br>• feelings of hopelessness<br /></br>No symptom-free period &gt; 2 months.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Thought to involve chronic dysregulation of monoamine neurotransmission (serotonin, norepinephrine).<br /></br>• Often begins in adolescence/early adulthood and is strongly associated with chronic medical illness, substance use, and interpersonal difficulties.<br /></br>• High risk of progressing to MDD (“double depression”) and for suicidality despite milder day-to-day impairment compared with MDD.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>1. Psychotherapy – Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) or interpersonal therapy is first-line.<br /></br>2. Pharmacotherapy – SSRIs (e.g., sertraline, fluoxetine), SNRIs (e.g., venlafaxine), or bupropion. Combination of meds + therapy shows best remission rates.<br /></br>3. Screen for comorbid MDD episodes and suicidality at every visit.<br /></br>4. Monitor chronic medical illnesses (diabetes, CKD) that may exacerbate mood symptoms and complicate drug choice (e.g., renal dosing of SSRIs).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“2 + 2 + Hopeless Blue” mnemonic for PDD:<br /></br>• 2 years or more<br /></br>• ≥ 2 depressive symptoms<br /></br>• No 2-month break<br /></br>• Hopeless mood, low-grade Blue feeling all the time  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Incorrect<br /></br>a. Adjustment disorder with depressed mood<br /></br>   • Onset within 3 months of identifiable stressor and resolves ≤ 6 months after stressor ends. His low mood predates recent custody loss by years.  </p></br><p>b. Borderline personality disorder<br /></br>   • Requires pervasive instability in relationships, self-image, impulsivity, recurrent self-harm. Only “sarcastic affect” and minor interpersonal friction are noted—insufficient.  </p></br><p>c. Major depressive disorder<br /></br>   • Needs ≥ 5 SIGECAPS for ≥ 2 weeks with clear change from baseline. He lacks sleep/appetite change and anhedonia (enjoys poker/movies) and appears chronically rather than episodically depressed.  </p></br><p>e. Substance-induced mood disorder<br /></br>   • Depressive symptoms occur during/soon after intoxication or withdrawal and resolve when substance clears. Mood disturbance precedes cocaine use and persists between monthly episodes, so criteria not met.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line: Chronic, subthreshold depression lasting ≥ 2 years without significant symptomatic remission = persistent depressive disorder.</p>
56. A 14-year-old boy is brought to the physician by his parents because he has been increasingly irritable, disrespectful, and uncooperative during the past 4 years. He refuses to do any household chores and does not obey curfew. He may be expelled from school because he is rude and threatening to teachers and has been cutting classes. He is tutored for reading but earns C’s and D’s in his classes. He has been suspended three times during the past year for talking back to his teachers. He repeated first grade due to poor academic readiness. He used to have trouble with homework but now he does not complete any homework. He is at the 65th percentile for height and 50th percentile for weight. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. During the examination, the patient is minimally cooperative and frequently rolls his eyes at questions. Urine toxicology screening is positive for nicotine. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient’s behavior?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder  </br>b. Borderline cognitive functioning  </br>c. Conduct disorder  </br>d. Oppositional defiant disorder  </br>e. Reading disorder  </br>f. Normal adolescent behavior</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>D. Oppositional defiant disorder (ODD)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>1. Time course &amp; pervasiveness<br /></br>   • “Increasingly irritable, disrespectful, and uncooperative … during the past 4 years.”<br /></br>   • Occurs at home (refuses chores/curfew) and at school (rude, threatening, cutting classes).</p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Pattern of behavior<br /></br>   • Argumentative/defiant: “talking back,” “threatening teachers,” eye-rolling.<br /></br>   • Angry/irritable: constant irritability noted by parents and physician.<br /></br>   • No serious aggression, property destruction, theft, or cruelty.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Functional impairment<br /></br>   • Academic failure (C’s &amp; D’s, repeated grade).<br /></br>   • Social impairment (multiple suspensions, possible expulsion).</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No physical fights, animal cruelty, fire-setting, theft → argues against conduct disorder.<br /></br>   • No history of hyperactivity/inattention since early childhood → argues against ADHD.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Metacognitive tip<br /></br>   • Ask: “Is the patient breaking MAJOR societal rules or committing assault/theft?” If no → think ODD; if yes → think conduct disorder.<br /></br>   • Duration ≥6 months + impairment in one setting = DSM-5 threshold for ODD.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise approach:</br>1. Identify persistent defiance/hostility toward authority (&gt;6 mo).<br /></br>2. Verify impairment (school suspensions, strained family relations).<br /></br>3. Rule out more severe conduct disorder (no violence/cruelty/theft).<br /></br>4. Exclude ADHD or mood disorder as primary explanation.<br /></br>Conclusion → Oppositional defiant disorder.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: ODD = chronic pattern of angry, argumentative, vindictive behavior toward authority figures without the severe rule-breaking or aggression required for conduct disorder.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Record frequency, settings, and consequences of oppositional behaviors; note absence of aggression or property destruction.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Multifactorial: genetic vulnerability + harsh / inconsistent parenting, family conflict.<br /></br>• Neurobiological overlap with ADHD (frontostriatal circuitry dysregulation), but behavioral expression centers on defiance rather than inattention or impulsivity.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics</br>1. First-line:<br /></br>   • Parent-management training (evidence-based behavioral therapy).<br /></br>   • Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) to build problem-solving &amp; anger-management skills.</p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Adjuncts:<br /></br>   • Treat comorbidities (e.g., ADHD→ stimulant such as methylphenidate).<br /></br>   • School-based behavioral interventions.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Medications: No specific drug for ODD; pharmacotherapy targets comorbid disorders or severe aggression (e.g., atypical antipsychotics), but not first-line.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Memory Aid</br>“ODD kids are O.D.D.” → Openly Disobedient &amp; Disrespectful.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>A. Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder<br /></br>   • Would require a childhood history of inattention/hyperactivity in at least two settings. No mention of distractibility, fidgeting, or hyperactivity.</p></br><p>B. Borderline cognitive functioning<br /></br>   • IQ 71–84 with global learning difficulties. A single repeated grade plus C/D average does not establish borderline IQ; does not explain pervasive defiance.</p></br><p>C. Conduct disorder<br /></br>   • Must include serious violations: physical fights, cruelty to people/animals, weapon use, theft, property destruction. Not present here.</p></br><p>E. Reading disorder (specific learning disorder with impairment in reading)<br /></br>   • Explains academic problems, not chronic oppositional behavior across settings.</p></br><p>F. Normal adolescent behavior<br /></br>   • Some rebellion is normal, but not 4 years of persistent defiance causing school suspensions and potential expulsion.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Summary</br>Persistent, impairing pattern of argumentative, defiant, and vindictive behavior without severe rule violations → Oppositional defiant disorder. Recognize ODD early, differentiate from conduct disorder, and initiate family-based behavioral therapy to prevent escalation.</p>
57. A 72-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 2-year history of increasingly severe episodic pain in both legs. The pain is triggered by walking and increases as he walks longer distances. He also has weakness and numbness in both legs when he walks farther than 4 blocks. He says that he now has difficulty walking through the grocery store, but the pain decreases when he leans forward on the cart while walking. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Muscle strength and deep tendon reflexes are normal, and sensation is intact. Femoral, posterior tibial, and pedal pulses are equal bilaterally. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Lower extremity arterial insufficiency</br>b. Lower extremity venous insufficiency</br>c. Lumbar degenerative joint disease</br>d. Lumbar disc herniation</br>e. Lumbar spinal stenosis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Lumbar spinal stenosis  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key phrases: “72-year-old,” “2-year history,” “episodic pain in both legs,” “triggered by walking,” “weakness and numbness when he walks farther than 4 blocks,” “pain decreases when he leans forward on the cart.”<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: bilateral leg pain + neurologic symptoms that are distance-dependent (claudication) and posture-dependent (better with lumbar flexion).<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: normal pulses, no skin trophic changes, normal baseline strength/reflexes → argue against peripheral arterial disease or peripheral neuropathy.<br /></br>• Cognitive road map:<br /></br>  1. Claudication pattern? ⇒ think vascular vs neurogenic.<br /></br>  2. Check pulses. Normal pulses → vascular claudication unlikely.<br /></br>  3. Does flexion help? If yes → classic for neurogenic claudication from lumbar spinal stenosis (shopping-cart sign).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When a stem highlights relief by leaning over a grocery cart, go straight to lumbar spinal stenosis unless pulses are clearly abnormal or other red flags appear.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Flexion of the lumbar spine widens the spinal canal and intervertebral foramina, reducing compression of the cauda equina or exiting nerve roots; extension (standing upright or walking) narrows the canal, reproducing symptoms. That positional element is the key discriminator.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Neurogenic claudication = leg pain ± paresthesias brought on by walking/standing, relieved by flexion or sitting, with normal distal pulses.  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real clinic): gait description, distance to symptom onset, positional relief, baseline neuro exam, pulse exam, functional limitation.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Lumbar spinal stenosis is usually degenerative:<br /></br>• Facet joint osteoarthritis → osteophytes encroach on canal.<br /></br>• Hypertrophy/calcification of ligamentum flavum.<br /></br>• Degenerative disc disease with bulging annulus.<br /></br>These changes narrow the central canal and lateral recesses, compressing multiple lumbar nerve roots (often L3-S1), producing bilateral symptoms.  </p></br><p>Risk factors: age &gt;60 yr, chronic axial loading, prior lumbar surgery, congenital narrow canal.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management<br /></br>1. MRI of the lumbar spine = imaging of choice; shows canal diameter &lt;10 mm, ligamentum flavum thickening, facet hypertrophy.<br /></br>2. Initial therapy: weight loss, NSAIDs (e.g., naproxen), physical therapy focusing on flexion-based exercises, epidural steroid injections if persistent.<br /></br>3. Definitive: surgical decompressive laminectomy ± fusion for severe or progressive neurologic deficits.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Shopping-cart sign” → you lean over the cart in the store = stenosis relief.<br /></br>“Spinal STENosis feels better when you beND (STeN ↔ beND).”  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Lower extremity arterial insufficiency (peripheral arterial disease)<br /></br>   • Pain also induced by walking, but relief occurs simply by resting; posture doesn’t matter.<br /></br>   • Exam: diminished/absent pulses, cool extremities, bruits, trophic skin changes, ABI &lt;0.9. None are present here.  </p></br><p>b. Lower extremity venous insufficiency<br /></br>   • Causes leg heaviness, swelling, stasis dermatitis, ulcers that worsen with prolonged standing and improve with leg elevation, not with walking distance or spinal flexion. Pulses normal but pain pattern wrong.  </p></br><p>c. Lumbar degenerative joint disease (facet osteoarthritis)<br /></br>   • Produces chronic low-back pain worsened with extension/rotation, not exertional leg claudication with neurologic deficits.  </p></br><p>d. Lumbar disc herniation<br /></br>   • Produces acute or subacute radicular pain in a dermatomal pattern, often worsened by flexion (opposite of this case) and cough/Valsalva; usually unilateral; may show focal neurologic deficits.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Episodic bilateral leg pain and paresthesias provoked by walking/standing and relieved by lumbar flexion with normal distal pulses = neurogenic claudication from lumbar spinal stenosis.</p>
58. An 11-year-old boy is brought to the office by his mother as a new patient because of a 3-year history of increasing weakness of his arms and legs. His mother says the weakness is now interfering with her son’s ability to participate in physical education classes at school. He has no history of serious illness and receives no medications. His older sister is healthy. He has several male and female cousins who are uncoordinated and fall frequently. Examination of the patient shows scoliosis of the thoracic and lumbosacral spine. There is atrophy and muscle weakness of both shoulders and pelvic girdles. Deep tendon reflexes are trace throughout. Sensation is intact throughout. His gait is waddling, and he must extend his back to walk smoothly. Results of a complete blood count and serum electrolyte concentrations are within the reference ranges. His serum creatine kinase activity is 387 U/L. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Duchenne muscular dystrophy</br>b. Juvenile spinal muscular atrophy</br>c. Myasthenia gravis</br>d. Myotonic dystrophy type 2</br>e. Polymyositis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>B. Juvenile spinal muscular atrophy (SMA type 3, Kugelberg-Welander disease)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Age/onset: Weakness began at 8 yr (current age = 11). SMA type 3 typically presents after age 2 with slowly progressive proximal weakness.<br /></br>• Distribution of weakness: Both shoulder and pelvic girdles (“proximal” pattern).<br /></br>• Physical exam:<br /></br>  – Atrophy of affected muscles → lower motor-neuron (LMN) process.<br /></br>  – Trace deep-tendon reflexes → LMN lesion (anterior horn cell).<br /></br>  – Sensation intact → argues against peripheral neuropathy; fits an anterior horn cell disease.<br /></br>  – Waddling gait + lumbar lordosis &amp; scoliosis → chronic proximal weakness.<br /></br>• Laboratory: CK only mildly elevated (387 U/L; normal &lt; 200). In myopathies (e.g., Duchenne, polymyositis) CK is usually &gt;5–10× upper limit of normal; in SMA it is normal or only mildly ↑ because the primary problem is neuronal, not muscle membrane breakdown.<br /></br>• Family history: “Several male and female cousins” affected suggests an autosomal-recessive inheritance (SMN1 gene mutation). X-linked conditions would spare females.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip:<br /></br>Step back and classify childhood weakness questions by 3 axes—(1) age of onset, (2) pattern (proximal vs distal, LMN vs UMN, fluctuating vs constant), (3) laboratory CK level.  Matching those three quickly narrows the differential.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>1. Identify proximal LMN weakness with preserved sensation → anterior horn cell disease.<br /></br>2. Rule out Duchenne (very high CK, calves hypertrophied, X-linked, onset &lt; 5 yr).<br /></br>3. Rule out neuromuscular-junction, myotonic, or inflammatory causes (see below).<br /></br>4. Autosomal-recessive inheritance pattern → SMA.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Slowly progressive, proximal, symmetric weakness beginning in childhood with decreased reflexes, intact sensation, mild/normal CK = spinal muscular atrophy.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): Document pattern of weakness, reflex exam, scoliosis severity, gait description, family pedigree, and plan for genetic SMN1 testing and multidisciplinary supportive care.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• SMA = degeneration of anterior horn cells in spinal cord due to homozygous deletion/mutation of the SMN1 gene → loss of survival motor neuron (SMN) protein → LMN degeneration → muscle denervation and atrophy.<br /></br>• Type 3 (juvenile) = least severe of childhood-onset forms; patients usually achieve ambulation but may lose it later in adolescence.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests</br>1.
