Confirmatory test: SMN1 gene testing (molecular analysis).<br /></br>2. Disease-modifying therapy:<br /></br>   – Nusinersen (antisense oligonucleotide)<br /></br>   – Onasemnogene abeparvovec (AAV9-SMN gene therapy)<br /></br>   – Risdiplam (oral SMN2 splicing modifier)<br /></br>3. Supportive: PT/OT, orthopedic management of scoliosis, respiratory monitoring, nutritional support.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“SMA = Sensation Maintained, Atrophy, Minimal CK”<br /></br>“Kugelberg kids Keep walking (late onset, milder)”<br /></br>“Anterior horn = motor only, so sensory stays normal.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>A. Duchenne muscular dystrophy<br /></br>• Onset ≤ 5 yr, calves pseudohypertrophy, Gowers sign, X-linked (boys only), CK often 5,000–15,000 U/L. Reflexes preserved until late. Doesn’t fit age, sex distribution (female cousins), CK level, or findings.</p></br><p>C. Myasthenia gravis<br /></br>• Fluctuating fatigable weakness, ocular/bulbar symptoms, normal reflexes, no atrophy early, CK normal. Reflexes here are decreased and atrophy present.</p></br><p>D. Myotonic dystrophy type 2<br /></br>• Myotonia (delayed muscle relaxation), distal &gt; proximal weakness early, facial weakness, cataracts, endocrine issues. Reflexes usually normal. Not seen here.</p></br><p>E. Polymyositis<br /></br>• Autoimmune inflammatory myopathy; typically adults; proximal weakness with NORMAL reflexes, markedly ↑CK (&gt;1,000), pain uncommon; no scoliosis or LMN signs.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────</br>Key Take-Home Message</br>Child with slowly progressive proximal weakness, muscle atrophy, hyporeflexia, intact sensation, mild CK elevation, and autosomal-recessive family pattern = Juvenile spinal muscular atrophy (SMA type 3).</p>
59. A 32-year-old woman comes to the office for a health maintenance examination. She feels well. She has mild myopia and wears contact lenses. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She appears well. Vital signs are within normal limits. A photograph of the right eye is shown. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Incision and drainage</br>b. Recommendation that she wear sunglasses</br>c. Serum antinuclear antibody assay</br>d. Surgical excision</br>e. Use of antibiotic eye drops</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Recommendation that she wear sunglasses</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues in the stem &amp; photograph (classic findings even if the image is not shown here):<br /></br>• Healthy, young adult with no systemic symptoms<br /></br>• Asymptomatic, slightly raised yellow-white lesion located on the bulbar conjunctiva, adjacent (but not adherent) to the cornea<br /></br>• Remainder of the examination is normal  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition: A small, painless, yellowish conjunctival nodule in a sun-exposed patient = pinguecula. These lesions are benign degenerative proliferations of collagen fibers in the conjunctiva due to chronic ultraviolet (UV) light exposure and environmental irritation (wind, dust). They do not grow onto the cornea (unlike a pterygium) and rarely require any intervention other than UV protection and lubrication.</p></br><p>Testing strategy:<br /></br>1. Identify a benign, asymptomatic finding.<br /></br>2. Ask “Does this need treatment, diagnostic work-up, or preventive counseling?”<br /></br>3. If it is a pinguecula, the USMLE favorite answer is “reassurance ± sunglasses/artificial tears.”</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why B is correct)  </p></br><p>Step-by-step thought process:<br /></br>1. Localize the lesion: conjunctiva, not eyelid or cornea.<br /></br>2. Characterize the lesion: yellowish, flat or slightly raised, not inflamed, painless.<br /></br>3. Consider common benign conjunctival lesions: pinguecula vs early pterygium.<br /></br>4. Ask if lesion threatens vision or causes symptoms → No.<br /></br>5. Management for asymptomatic pinguecula = conservative UV protection → advise sunglasses (and artificial tears if dryness/irritation).  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Pinguecula is a benign “UV sun spot” on the conjunctiva. Primary management is prevention of progression with broad-brim hat/sunglasses and ocular lubrication.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real practice): “Benign pinguecula OD. No corneal involvement. Counselled on UV protection and artificial tears prn. RTC if growth, redness, pain, or visual changes.”</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Etiology: Elastotic degeneration of conjunctival collagen and subepithelial tissue from chronic UV light, wind, and dust exposure.<br /></br>• Location: Nasal &gt; temporal bulbar conjunctiva (exposed interpalpebral zone).<br /></br>• Clinical course: Remains confined to conjunctiva; can become inflamed (“pingueculitis”) but does not encroach on cornea.<br /></br>• Contrast with pterygium: fibrovascular growth beginning as pinguecula that crosses the limbus onto the cornea; may induce astigmatism or obscure vision.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>Pinguecula<br /></br>1. First-line:<br /></br>   • UV protection (wrap-around sunglasses, wide-brim hat)<br /></br>   • Lubricating eye drops for irritation<br /></br>2. Second-line (rare):<br /></br>   • Short course topical NSAID or mild steroid for pingueculitis<br /></br>3. Surgical excision: Only for cosmetic reasons or if persistent inflammation unresponsive to medical therapy (very uncommon).  </p></br><p>No labs or imaging are required.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Pinguecula = ‘PING-yellow-cular.’<br /></br>• P = Protected by Polarized sunglasses<br /></br>• Yellow spot that Pings at you but never Pokes into cornea (pterygium does).”</p></br><p>Visualization: Picture a tiny yellow ping-pong ball resting on the white part of the eye, shielded by sunglasses.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Incision and drainage<br /></br>• Appropriate for an acute hordeolum (painful red eyelid nodule) or large chalazion.<br /></br>• Conjunctival pinguecula is superficial, avascular, and not fluctuant—no I&amp;D needed.  </p></br><p>C. Serum antinuclear antibody assay<br /></br>• Ordered when suspecting autoimmune conjunctivitis/episcleritis/scleritis or systemic rheumatologic disease (e.g., SLE, RA).<br /></br>• Asymptomatic localized conjunctival lesion without systemic complaints offers no indication.  </p></br><p>D. Surgical excision<br /></br>• Indicated for pterygium encroaching on visual axis, cosmetic concern, or recurrent severe pingueculitis.<br /></br>• Not first-line for a small asymptomatic pinguecula.  </p></br><p>E. Use of antibiotic eye drops<br /></br>• Used for bacterial conjunctivitis, hordeolum, corneal abrasion with contact lens wear (pseudomonal coverage), etc.<br /></br>• Pinguecula is non-infectious; antibiotics provide no benefit.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: Mistaking pinguecula for pterygium and jumping to surgery—remember, pterygium must invade cornea.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>A painless, yellowish conjunctival nodule that does NOT cross onto the cornea is a pinguecula. Management is simple: reassure, recommend UV protection (sunglasses), and lubricate if needed.</p>
60. A 4-year-old boy is brought to the physician because of a lesion on his scalp for the past 6 weeks. It has continued to increase in size despite use of over-the-counter antibacterial creams and 7 days of oral cephalexin that his mother had leftover from a previous illness. The lesion began as a round area approximately the size of a quarter. He has no history of serious illness. A photograph of the lesion is shown. Infection with which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's lesion?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Microsporum gypseum</br>b. Mycobacterium ulcerans</br>c. Pseudomonas aeruginosa</br>d. Staphylococcus aureus</br>e. Streptococcus pyogenes (group A)</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Microsporum gypseum  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Patient profile<br /></br>   • 4-year-old boy (tinea capitis is almost exclusively a pediatric infection).  </li></br><li>Time course &amp; appearance<br /></br>   • 6-week, slowly enlarging, round patch on the scalp → chronic, indolent process typical of dermatophytes, not pyogenic bacteria.  </li></br><li>Treatment history<br /></br>   • Failed OTC antibacterial creams and a 7-day course of cephalexin (covers Staph aureus &amp; Strep pyogenes). Lack of response argues strongly against common pyogenic skin bacteria and points toward a non-bacterial etiology.  </li></br><li>Photo (not shown to you) in NBME/Step-style questions usually depicts a circular area of alopecia with scaling ± mild erythema—classic for tinea capitis and sometimes a boggy “kerion.”  </li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive road-map for similar questions<br /></br>• Scalp lesion in a child + patchy alopecia + chronic course + no response to antibacterials = think dermatophyte (tinea capitis).<br /></br>• Dermatophytes = Microsporum, Trichophyton, Epidermophyton species.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why Microsporum gypseum?)  </p></br><p>Step-wise linkage:<br /></br>1. Scalp dermatophyte infection = tinea capitis.<br /></br>2. Most common U.S. organism: Trichophyton tonsurans, but NBME often tests Microsporum species (classically fluorescence with Wood lamp).<br /></br>3. Among the answer choices, Microsporum gypseum is the only dermatophyte listed.<br /></br>4. Failure of cephalexin confirms it is not a gram-positive bacterial skin infection.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: A chronic, scaly/alopecic scalp lesion in a child = tinea capitis caused by a dermatophyte (Microsporum/Trichophyton) → requires oral antifungal therapy.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls if this were real life:<br /></br>• Extent and characteristics of alopecia, scaling, inflammation (± kerion).<br /></br>• Exposure history (pets, soil, infected classmates).<br /></br>• Plan for KOH prep / fungal culture and oral antifungal prescription (griseofulvin).  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Dermatophytes digest keratinized tissue (stratum corneum, hair, nails) using keratinases.<br /></br>• Microsporum gypseum: geophilic fungus found in soil; infects hair shafts → perifollicular inflammation → patchy alopecia &amp; scale.<br /></br>• Host response can range from mild scale to intense inflammatory kerion.  </p></br><p>Risk factors: age 3-10 yr, contact with contaminated soil/pets or infected peers, sharing combs/hats.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Diagnosis &amp; Treatment  </p></br><p>Diagnosis<br /></br>• KOH wet mount of hair: septated hyphae/spores.<br /></br>• Fungal culture on Sabouraud agar.<br /></br>• Wood lamp: Microsporum species often fluoresce blue-green.  </p></br><p>Treatment (must be systemic—topicals don’t penetrate follicles)<br /></br>1. First-line: Oral griseofulvin microsize 20–25 mg/kg/day × 6–8 wk (FDA-approved and tested on Step).<br /></br>2. Alternative: Oral terbinafine, itraconazole, or fluconazole for resistant cases.<br /></br>3. Adjunct: Selenium sulfide or ketoconazole shampoo twice weekly to reduce spore shedding.<br /></br>Counsel: Treat close contacts if symptomatic; disinfect combs, hats.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Tinea CAPitis needs oral meds to CAPture the fungus.”<br /></br>“Microsporum &amp; Trichophyton make your scalp ‘M-T’ (Empty) of hair.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>B. Mycobacterium ulcerans<br /></br>• Causes Buruli ulcer (necrotic painless ulcer in tropical regions). Lesion is ulcerative, not scaly alopecia; mycobacterial, not fungal.  </p></br><p>C. Pseudomonas aeruginosa<br /></br>• Think ecthyma gangrenosum in neutropenic patients or green-tinged moist lesions (hot-tub folliculitis). Would present acutely, not a chronic scaly alopecic patch, and often with systemic illness.  </p></br><p>D. Staphylococcus aureus<br /></br>• Causes impetigo, folliculitis, cellulitis, abscess. Would likely improve with cephalexin; lesions are pustular/crusted (“honey-colored”) or fluctuant, not scaly alopecia.  </p></br><p>E. Streptococcus pyogenes (group A)<br /></br>• Impetigo/cellulitis/erysipelas—again, usually respond to cephalosporins; lesions erythematous, edematous, not alopecic scaling patches.  </p></br><p>Key discriminator: Bacterial etiologies respond to cephalexin and produce erythema, warmth, purulence; dermatophytes do not—leading to chronic, scaly, hair-loss lesions.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>A chronic, enlarging, scaly patch of alopecia on a child’s scalp that fails antibacterial therapy is tinea capitis. The only dermatophyte option given is Microsporum gypseum. Treat with oral griseofulvin.</p>
61. A 67-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of a 2-day history of fever and progressive pain in his right knee. He says that he had a cold 5 days ago. He has end-stage renal failure secondary to type 2 diabetes mellitus and has required hemodialysis for the past 2 years. Current medications include insulin, erythropoietin, and a daily multivitamin. His temperature is 37.9 C (100.2 F). The right knee is swollen and warm. Range of motion is severely limited due to pain. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s current symptoms?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a.  Hematogenous infection</br>b.  Hemochromatosis</br>c.  Human leukocyte antigen–B27 haplotype</br>d.  Sequelae of a viral upper respiratory tract infection</br>e.  Uremia</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>A. Hematogenous infection (acute septic arthritis from blood-borne spread—most often Staphylococcus aureus)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key phrases:</br>  – “67-year-old man … fever and progressive pain in his right knee … swollen and warm … ROM severely limited due to pain” → classic description of an acutely inflamed mono-arthritic joint.<br /></br>  – “End-stage renal failure … hemodialysis for the past 2 years” → repeated vascular access and immunocompromise → very high risk of bacteremia.<br /></br>  – “2-day history” → hyper-acute time course typical of septic arthritis rather than crystalline or autoimmune disease.<br /></br>  – Mild fever (100.2 °F) – septic arthritis in older or immunosuppressed patients often produces only low-grade fever.</p></br><p>Pertinent negatives:</br>• No polyarticular involvement, conjunctivitis, urethritis, or back pain (makes spondyloarthropathies less likely).<br /></br>• No mention of skin lesions, chronic liver disease, or hemochromatosis stigmata.</p></br><p>Cognitive/metacognitive takeaway: </br>With ANY acutely swollen painful joint + fever, always rule out septic arthritis first, especially when the patient has a source for bacteremia (indwelling lines, IV drug use, dialysis access).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>1. Acute mono-arthritis → septic arthritis, crystal arthropathy, hemarthrosis, or reactive arthritis.<br /></br>2. Fever + immunocompromise/dialysis → prioritize septic arthritis.<br /></br>3. Most septic joints are seeded hematogenously (≈70% of cases); S. aureus is most common.<br /></br>4. Therefore, “hematogenous infection” is the most likely cause.</p></br><p>Core concept (“golden nugget”): End-stage renal disease patients on hemodialysis have frequent line accesses → high incidence of transient bacteremia → hematogenous seeding of joints → septic arthritis.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Must record joint appearance, ROM, presence of systemic symptoms, vascular access site status, and that urgent arthrocentesis/empiric IV antibiotics were initiated.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Synovium is highly vascular and lacks a limiting basement membrane → bacteria circulating in blood easily seed the joint.<br /></br>• Bacterial proliferation triggers neutrophil influx, protease release, and rapid cartilage destruction (can destroy joint in ≤48 h).<br /></br>• Risk factors: age &gt;60, diabetes, chronic kidney disease, rheumatoid arthritis, prosthetic joints, IV drug use, hemodialysis catheters.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment (board-relevant)</br>1. URGENT arthrocentesis before antibiotics if possible:<br /></br>   – WBC &gt;50,000/µL (mostly PMNs), low glucose, high protein, positive Gram stain/culture.<br /></br>2. Empiric IV antibiotics: vancomycin + third-gen cephalosporin (e.g., ceftriaxone) until culture results.<br /></br>3. Surgical washout / serial needle drainage.<br /></br>Delay in therapy → irreversible cartilage loss.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“Hot, red joint + line access? Think ‘LINE = BACTEREMIA = SEPTIC joint.’”<br /></br>Mnemonic: DIALYSIS<br /></br>D – Diabetic<br /></br>I – Immunocompromised<br /></br>A – Acute joint pain<br /></br>L – Line access<br /></br>Y – Yield bloodborne bugs<br /></br>S – Staph aureus #1<br /></br>I – Immediate tap<br /></br>S – Save the cartilage (urgent antibiotics)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>B. Hemochromatosis<br /></br>• Can cause arthropathy (often MCPs, wrists) and ↑risk of pseudogout, but typically chronic, not explosive. No bronze diabetes, hepatomegaly, or iron labs given.</p></br><p>C. Human leukocyte antigen–B27 haplotype<br /></br>• Associated with reactive arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, etc. Reactive arthritis follows GI/GU infections, presents with asymmetric oligoarthritis, conjunctivitis, urethritis, mucocutaneous lesions. Patient lacks these features and has a stronger risk for septic arthritis.</p></br><p>D. Sequelae of a viral upper respiratory tract infection<br /></br>• Post-viral reactive arthralgia is usually polyarticular, mild, and self-limited; onset 1–3 weeks after infection. Here the URI was only 5 days prior, and severe joint inflammation with fever suggests infection, not post-viral immune reaction.</p></br><p>E. Uremia<br /></br>• Uremic toxins cause pericarditis, encephalopathy, platelet dysfunction, but not acute mono-arthritis with warmth and fever.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>In an older dialysis patient, an acutely swollen painful joint with even low-grade fever is septic arthritis until proven otherwise—nearly always seeded hematogenously. Prompt arthrocentesis and IV antibiotics are lifesaving.</p>
62. A 62-year-old man comes to the physician because he has noticed bloody urine intermittently during the past 2 months. He occasionally passes large clots through his urethra. During the past 2 weeks, he has had pain and swelling over his distal radial forearm. Examination shows tenderness, erythema, and edema over the radius 10 cm proximal to the wrist joint. An x-ray of the left upper extremity shows a lytic lesion of the radius with a pathologic fracture. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Giant cell tumor</br>b. Leukemia of bone</br>c. Malignant fibrous histiocytoma</br>d. Metastatic disease</br>e. Multiple myeloma</br>f. Osteosarcoma</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer: D. Metastatic disease  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>VIGNETTE ANALYSIS<br /></br>• Demographics: 62-year-old man (primary bone tumors are uncommon in this age group; metastases are common).<br /></br>• Key “red flag” symptom: Intermittent gross hematuria with passage of clots → strongly suggests a genitourinary malignancy (renal cell carcinoma or urothelial carcinoma of the bladder).<br /></br>• Orthopedic finding: Acute pain, swelling, and pathologic fracture of the distal radius. X-ray shows a lytic (bone-destroying) lesion.<br /></br>• Pattern recognition: In an older adult, a solitary or multifocal lytic lesion—especially in an atypical site (distal radius)—is metastatic disease until proven otherwise. The presence of another cancer “signal” (gross hematuria) seals the diagnosis.</p></br><p>Metacognitive pearl: On NBME/Step questions, whenever you see a cancer “clue” (e.g., hematuria, chronic cough, breast mass) plus a destructive bone lesion in an older patient, immediately elevate metastatic disease to the top of the differential.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>CLINICAL CRITICAL REASONING<br /></br>1. Step 1: Recognize the likely primary tumor.<br /></br>   • Renal cell carcinoma and bladder (urothelial) carcinoma both produce painless hematuria with clots and commonly metastasize hematogenously to bone.<br /></br>   • Renal cell carcinoma is classically purely lytic.<br /></br>2. Step 2: Match bone lesion pattern.<br /></br>   • Lytic lesions (bone destruction without reactive bone formation) are typical of metastases from kidney, thyroid, lung, GI tract, and melanoma (“Lytic = Kidney, Thyroid, Lung, GI, Melanoma”).<br /></br>   • Prostate metastases are usually blastic; breast can be mixed.<br /></br>3. Step 3: Age &amp; site.<br /></br>   • Primary aggressive bone tumors (e.g., osteosarcoma, Ewing) cluster in children &amp; young adults, or arise in Paget disease but usually in the axial skeleton or around the knee, not the distal radius.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: In adults &gt;40, most destructive bone lesions are metastatic, not primary.</p></br><p>Documentation point: “Suspect secondary bone involvement from underlying renal/urothelial malignancy; will obtain CT abdomen/pelvis, bone scan, and refer to oncology and orthopedics.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>PATHOPHYSIOLOGY REVIEW<br /></br>• Metastatic spread to bone occurs via hematogenous seeding through vertebral venous plexus and arterial circulation.<br /></br>• Tumor cells disrupt normal bone remodeling by stimulating osteoclasts (lytic lesions) or osteoblasts (blastic lesions).<br /></br>• Clinical consequences: bone pain, pathologic fractures, hypercalcemia (lytic), spinal cord compression.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>DIAGNOSTIC &amp; TREATMENT OVERVIEW<br /></br>Diagnostics:<br /></br>• CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast → evaluate kidneys/ureters/bladder.<br /></br>• Cystoscopy (if bladder source suspected).<br /></br>• Whole-body bone scan or PET-CT → stage metastatic burden.<br /></br>• Biopsy of bone lesion if primary source unclear.</p></br><p>Treatment (principles):<br /></br>• Treat underlying malignancy (e.g., nephrectomy or cystectomy + systemic therapy).<br /></br>• Bone-directed therapy: bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid) or denosumab to reduce skeletal-related events.<br /></br>• Orthopedic fixation or palliative radiation for unstable/pathologic fractures.<br /></br>• Analgesia, consider chemotherapy/immunotherapy targeted to primary tumor.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>MEMORIZATION TIPS<br /></br>“Old Man + Red Pee + Broken Bone = METS.”<br /></br>“BLT with Kosher Pickle” (Breast, Lung, Thyroid, Kidney, Prostate) → common bone mets. Remember: BLT + K are often lytic, P (prostate) is blastic.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>INCORRECT ANSWER QUICK REVIEW<br /></br>a. Giant cell tumor: 20-40 y/o; epiphysis (distal femur/proximal tibia); soap-bubble appearance; no hematuria.<br /></br>b. Leukemia of bone: Diffuse bone pain, cytopenias, systemic symptoms; radiographs show diffuse osteopenia or periosteal reaction, not isolated lytic lesion with clots in urine.<br /></br>c. Malignant fibrous histiocytoma (undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma): Rare, usually soft-tissue mass in older adults; not linked to hematuria.<br /></br>e. Multiple myeloma: Older adults with lytic “punched-out” lesions, anemia, renal failure, hypercalcemia; hematuria would be microscopic (Bence-Jones protein) not gross with clots.<br /></br>f. Osteosarcoma: Bimodal (teens, elderly with Paget); metaphyseal, sunburst pattern, Codman triangle; produces mixed lytic/blastic bone, usually around the knee. Gross hematuria does not fit.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom line: An older patient with gross hematuria and an isolated lytic bone lesion most consistent with metastatic disease from a genitourinary primary.</p>
63. A 72-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of left knee pain. The pain increases when she walks up or down stairs. She rates her pain as a 3 on a 10-point scale. She has not had fever or morning stiffness. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She is 160 cm (5 ft 3 in) tall and weighs 89 kg (196 lb); BMI is 35 kg/m2. Her temperature is 37 C (98.6 F), pulse is 68/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 128/70 mm Hg. Examination of the left knee shows crepitus on passive flexion and extension; there is no synovitis. An x-ray of the left knee is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Acetaminophen</br>b. Hydroxychloroquine</br>c. Ketorolac</br>d. Oxycodone</br>e. Prednisone</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>A. Acetaminophen  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Key clues<br /></br>   • Age 72 y and BMI 35 → common demographics for osteoarthritis (OA).<br /></br>   • 6-month, slowly progressive unilateral knee pain.<br /></br>   • Pain worsens with stair climbing (weight-bearing, flexion/extension).<br /></br>   • No fever, no morning stiffness, no systemic symptoms → argues against inflammatory arthritis (e.g., RA, SLE, crystal).<br /></br>   • Exam: crepitus; no synovitis/effusion.<br /></br>   • Knee x-ray (not shown here) on NBME typically demonstrates joint-space narrowing, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis.<br /></br>   Pattern: Typical “wear-and-tear” OA.  </p></br><ol></br><li>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>   a. Identify if the process is inflammatory or degenerative. Absence of warmth, redness, prolonged morning stiffness, systemic signs → degenerative.<br /></br>   b. Ask: “What is the first pharmacologic step AFTER non-drug measures (weight loss, quad-strengthening, PT)?” For NBME, the safest, lowest toxicity oral agent is acetaminophen.  </li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• OA pain is largely mechanical; prostaglandin-mediated inflammation is minor early on.<br /></br>• Acetaminophen (paracetamol) provides mild-to-moderate analgesia, has excellent safety at ≤3–4 g/day, and is recommended before NSAIDs in many elderly patients because it lacks GI bleeding, renal, and cardiovascular risks.<br /></br>Take-home message: In degenerative joint disease with mild pain, start with acetaminophen (or topical NSAID if specifically listed). Escalate to oral NSAIDs, intra-articular steroids, or opioids only if pain persists.</p></br><p>Documentation points (real clinic)<br /></br>• Functional limitations (stairs, walking distance)<br /></br>• Severity (3/10)<br /></br>• Trial of non-pharm therapy discussed (weight loss, exercise)<br /></br>• Plan: acetaminophen ≤3 g/day; schedule follow-up  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (OA)<br /></br>• Etiology: progressive loss of articular cartilage, subchondral bone remodeling, osteophyte formation.<br /></br>• Risk factors: age, obesity, female sex, prior joint injury.<br /></br>• Symptoms: activity-related pain, brief (&lt;30 min) stiffness, crepitus, bony enlargement.<br /></br>• Imaging: asymmetric joint-space narrowing, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis/cysts.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment Ladder for OA (knee)<br /></br>1. Non-pharmacologic: weight loss, low-impact exercise (swimming, cycling), quad strengthening, PT, braces.<br /></br>2. First-line pharmacologic:<br /></br>   • Acetaminophen (oral, scheduled)<br /></br>   • OR topical NSAID (diclofenac gel) if listed<br /></br>3. Second-line: oral NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen; consider PPI co-therapy), intra-articular corticosteroid injections.<br /></br>4. Refractory: duloxetine, tramadol; short-course opioids only if surgery not an option.<br /></br>5. Definitive: total knee arthroplasty if severe structural disease and failed conservative therapy.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“ACETAMINOPHEN for ACHY cartilage” – two A’s line up for initial OA treatment.<br /></br>Think “NSAID = Next Step (if Tylenol fails).”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>B. Hydroxychloroquine – Disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD) for inflammatory conditions (RA, SLE). Ineffective for OA.<br /></br>C. Ketorolac – Potent parenteral/oral NSAID with high GI/renal toxicity; not first-line, especially for chronic mild pain.<br /></br>D. Oxycodone – Opioid reserved for moderate-severe pain that fails non-opioids or when surgery contraindicated; unnecessary risk (sedation, dependence) in mild OA.<br /></br>E. Prednisone – Systemic glucocorticoid useful for acute flares of inflammatory arthritis, not for chronic OA (no inflammation to suppress, long-term side effects).  </p></br><p>Common Pitfall<br /></br>Jumping directly to NSAIDs or opioids without trying acetaminophen (or topical NSAIDs) first in an elderly patient. Always start with the safest option that adequately treats pain.</p>
64. A 28-year-old primigravid woman at 7 weeks’ gestation is admitted to the hospital because of a 2-day history of shortness of breath, palpitations, and light-headedness. Pregnancy had been uncomplicated. She has no history of serious illness, and her only medication is a prenatal vitamin. Two months ago, she visited her parents’ farm in Wisconsin. The patient’s temperature is 37.8 C (100 F), pulse is 38/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 96 percent. Forehead movement is decreased. There is left facial droop, and the patient is unable to close her left eye. An ECG is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Administration of ceftriaxone</br>b. Administration of doxycycline</br>c. Administration of mifepristone</br>d. Administration of prednisone</br>e. Placement of a pacemaker</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Administration of ceftriaxone  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis (How we know it is Lyme carditis with facial-nerve palsy)</p></br><ol></br><li>Geographic clue: “Visited her parents’ farm in Wisconsin” → Upper Midwest is an Ixodes tick/Lyme endemic area.  </li></br><li>Timeline: Symptoms began ≈2 mo after exposure → consistent with early-disseminated Lyme disease (weeks–months after bite).  </li></br><li>Neurologic finding: Unilateral peripheral facial palsy (inability to close eye + loss of forehead movement) → classic cranial nerve VII involvement of Lyme (often bilateral, but unilateral is common).  </li></br><li>Cardiac finding: Pulse 38/min + light-headedness + palpitations; ECG (not shown here but in the exam) almost certainly demonstrates an AV conduction block → hallmark of Lyme carditis.  </li></br><li>Mild fever and otherwise normal oxygenation support an infectious rather than primary pulmonary or structural cardiac etiology.  </li></br><li>Pregnancy (7 weeks) eliminates tetracycline use and makes fetal safety paramount.</li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive pearl: When you see “facial palsy + profound bradycardia + recent trip to New England/Upper Midwest,” immediately think “Lyme carditis → IV ceftriaxone (or penicillin G), especially if pregnant.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</p></br><p>Step 1: Recognize disseminated Lyme disease (CN VII palsy + AV block).<br /></br>Step 2: Decide on therapy based on disease stage/severity and pregnancy status.<br /></br>• Early localized (erythema migrans, no systemic involvement): doxycycline unless pregnant (then amoxicillin).<br /></br>• Early disseminated with neurologic or cardiac manifestations OR late Lyme: IV third-generation cephalosporin (ceftriaxone) or penicillin G.<br /></br>Step 3: Contraindications: Doxycycline is contraindicated in pregnancy (risk of permanent fetal tooth discoloration, bone growth inhibition).</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Pregnant patient with disseminated Lyme involving heart/cranial nerves → IV ceftriaxone.</p></br><p>Documentation note if this were a real patient: “Suspect Lyme disease with AV block and CN VII palsy. Plan: start IV ceftriaxone 2 g daily x 14–28 days; serial ECG monitoring; fetal considerations discussed.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)</p></br><p>• Organism: Borrelia burgdorferi (spirochete) transmitted by Ixodes scapularis tick; reservoir = white-footed mouse; deer carry adult ticks.<br /></br>• Early localized (days–weeks): erythema migrans ± flu-like illness.<br /></br>• Early disseminated (weeks–months): </br>  – Neurologic: unilateral/bilateral CN VII palsy, meningoradiculitis.<br /></br>  – Cardiac: fluctuating AV block, myocarditis.<br /></br>• Late (months–years): migratory arthritis (large joints), encephalomyelitis, polyneuropathy.</p></br><p>Why bradycardia? Spirochete infiltration of the conduction system causes varying degrees of AV nodal block, often high-grade or complete, producing HR &lt; 50 bpm.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Points</p></br><p>First-line choices by stage:<br /></br>1. Localized (no neuro/cardio): doxycycline 10–21 d OR amoxicillin if pregnant/child &lt;8 y.<br /></br>2. Neuro/cardio disease or late disease: IV ceftriaxone 14–28 d (safe in pregnancy).  </p></br><p>Follow-up:<br /></br>• Serial ECGs until AV conduction normalizes.<br /></br>• Temporary pacing only if hemodynamically unstable block despite antibiotics.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips</p></br><p>“FACE” for early-disseminated Lyme:<br /></br>F – Facial nerve palsy<br /></br>A – AV block (carditis)<br /></br>C – Constitutional flu-like symptoms<br /></br>E – Erythema migrans history  </p></br><p>Mnemonic for treatment choices:<br /></br>“Doxy for rashes, Pen/cef for HEART/FACE—unless Pregnant: NO Doxy, give Cef.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong</p></br><p>B. Doxycycline<br /></br>• Correct drug for most non-pregnant adults with Lyme.<br /></br>• Contraindicated in pregnancy; also oral doxy may be inadequate for carditis/neuro involvement, which requires IV therapy.</p></br><p>C. Mifepristone<br /></br>• Progesterone antagonist used for medical abortion; irrelevant to Lyme disease or AV block.</p></br><p>D. Prednisone<br /></br>• Steroids might be used in idiopathic Bell palsy, but Lyme palsy requires antibiotic eradication. Steroids alone do not treat Borrelia infection and would not address AV block.</p></br><p>E. Pacemaker placement<br /></br>• AV block in Lyme is usually reversible with antibiotics; pacing reserved only for severe, unstable block unresponsive to antibiotics. Initial management is antimicrobial therapy.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Take-Home Message</p></br><p>Pregnancy modifies Lyme treatment. Disseminated Lyme with facial palsy + AV block in a pregnant patient mandates IV ceftriaxone (safe in pregnancy, effective against CNS/heart involvement).</p>
65. A previously healthy 53-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department 2 hours after the onset of severe pressure and pain in her chest radiating down her left arm. She says she fell off her bicycle last week and bruised the left side of her rib cage. She has been taking naproxen for pain since then. She takes no other medications. On arrival, she is in severe distress. Her pulse is 110/min, respirations are 25/min, and blood pressure is 80/50 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on 2 L/min of oxygen by nasal cannula shows an oxygen saturation of 85 percent. Examination shows cool, mottled skin. Jugular venous pulsations are present 8 cm above the sternal angle. Examination of the chest shows healing ecchymoses and mild tenderness over the ribs on the left. Crackles are heard at both lung bases. S1 and S2 are normal; an S3 is heard. An ECG shows ST-segment depression and a left bundle branch block. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Acute pericarditis</br>b. Cardiac tamponade</br>c. Myocardial infarction</br>d. Pneumothorax</br>e. Pulmonary embolism</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Myocardial infarction (acute coronary syndrome with cardiogenic shock)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Key “MI” pain description<br /></br>   • Sudden, severe “pressure” radiating to the left arm – classic anginal/ischemic pattern.<br /></br>2. Hemodynamic collapse<br /></br>   • Hypotension (80/50 mm Hg), cool mottled skin = cardiogenic shock.<br /></br>3. Volume-overload findings<br /></br>   • Elevated JVP (8 cm above sternal angle), bibasilar crackles, and an S3 → high left-sided filling pressures and acute LV failure.<br /></br>4. ECG clues<br /></br>   • ST-segment depression = subendocardial ischemia.<br /></br>   • NEW left bundle branch block (LBBB) is treated like a STEMI equivalent on boards/exams.<br /></br>5. Timeline &amp; risk profile<br /></br>   • 53-year-old woman; coronary disease becomes increasingly common after menopause. The bike-injury history is a distractor.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On test day, chest pain + sympathetic findings + ischemic ECG = think ACS first, then look for shock/CHF markers (S3, crackles) to decide if cardiogenic shock is present.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1: Identify life-threatening chest-pain causes (MI, PE, tamponade, tension PTX, aortic dissection).<br /></br>Step 2: Match hemodynamics &amp; lung findings. Pulmonary edema → LV pump failure → cardiogenic shock.<br /></br>Step 3: Correlate ECG. ST changes or new LBBB seal the diagnosis of MI.<br /></br>Core takeaway: New LBBB or ST changes in a patient with classic ischemic pain and cardiogenic shock = acute myocardial infarction requiring immediate reperfusion and pressor support.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): onset/character/radiation of pain, vitals, Killip class, ECG interpretation, cardiology activation, MONA therapy (morphine, oxygen, nitrates, aspirin) plus anticoagulation/reperfusion plan.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Coronary plaque rupture → platelet aggregation &amp; thrombosis → transmural (ST-elevation) or subendocardial (ST-depression) ischemia.<br /></br>• Loss of contractile myocardium → ↓stroke volume → ↑LV end-diastolic pressure → pulmonary congestion (crackles, S3) &amp; ↑right-sided pressures (JVD).<br /></br>• ↓Cardiac output → hypotension, peripheral vasoconstriction → cool mottled skin.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Management Snapshot (board level)<br /></br>1. Immediate: Aspirin, P2Y12 inhibitor (clopidogrel), heparin, high-flow O₂ if hypoxic, nitrates if not hypotensive, morphine for pain.<br /></br>2. Reperfusion: PCI within 90 min (or fibrinolysis if PCI unavailable and no contraindication). New LBBB + shock qualifies as emergent PCI.<br /></br>3. Hemodynamic support: IV fluids cautiously, vasopressors/inotropes (dopamine, norepinephrine, dobutamine), possible intra-aortic balloon pump.<br /></br>4. Post-MI: β-blocker, ACE inhibitor, statin, aldosterone antagonist if EF &lt; 40 % and symptomatic.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“MI SHOCK” for cardiogenic shock signs in MI:<br /></br>M – Mottled skin<br /></br>I – Increased JVP<br /></br>S – S3 gallop<br /></br>H – Hypotension<br /></br>O – rales (pulm. congestion) “Over-flowing lungs”<br /></br>C – Cool extremities<br /></br>K – (new) LBBB = Killer ECG finding</p></br><p>New LBBB on an exam? Treat it as an ST-elevation until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Acute pericarditis<br /></br>   • Pain is sharp/pleuritic, better leaning forward; ECG shows diffuse ST-elevation + PR depression, not isolated ST-depression/LBBB. No crackles/S3.</p></br><p>b. Cardiac tamponade<br /></br>   • Beck triad: hypotension, JVD, muffled heart sounds. Lungs are usually clear (no crackles) because LV filling is preserved; S3 uncommon. ECG: low voltage, electrical alternans.</p></br><p>d. Pneumothorax (including tension)<br /></br>   • Unilateral absent breath sounds &amp; hyperresonance; JVD only if tension. Crackles and S3 would not be present. ECG generally normal or shows nonspecific changes.</p></br><p>e. Pulmonary embolism<br /></br>   • Pleuritic chest pain, sudden dyspnea, clear lungs, possible tachycardia, JVD from acute RV strain. ECG may show sinus tachy, S1Q3T3, or T-wave inversions—not new LBBB with ST-depression; S3 and crackles uncommon.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Classic ischemic chest pain + ischemic ECG changes + cardiogenic shock findings = acute myocardial infarction until proven otherwise. Early recognition and reperfusion therapy save myocardium and lives.</p>
66. A 23-year-old man comes to the physician for an examination prior to employment. He feels well. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He is in his first year of law school and sleeps 4 to 5 hours nightly; he reports feeling stressed frequently because of the demanding course load. He has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 2 years and drinks one beer daily. He exercises by running for 30 minutes three times weekly. He is 178 cm (5 ft 10 in) tall and weighs 91 kg (200 lb); BMI is 29 kg/m2. His pulse is 80/min, and blood pressure is 132/96 mm Hg. Serum electrolyte concentrations are within the reference ranges. Urinalysis shows no abnormalities. In addition to advising smoking cessation, which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation to manage this patient's increased blood pressure?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Decreased alcohol consumption</br>b. Increased amount of sleep</br>c. Weight loss</br>d. Biofeedback</br>e. Hydrochlorothiazide therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>C. Weight loss  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Key data points<br /></br>   • Age 23, otherwise healthy → no compelling comorbidities requiring immediate drug therapy<br /></br>   • BP 132/96 mm Hg → Stage 1–2 range (JNC-7: stage 1 = 140–159 systolic or 90–99 diastolic; ACC/AHA 2017: stage 2 = ≥140 systolic or ≥90 diastolic)<br /></br>   • BMI 29 kg/m² (overweight, approaching obesity) → strong, modifiable driver of elevated BP<br /></br>   • Alcohol only 1 beer/day → not a major contributor<br /></br>   • Sleeps 4–5 h/night, feels stressed → possible contributors but less potent for BP reduction than weight loss<br /></br>   • No abnormal labs or urinalysis → secondary causes unlikely at this time  </p></br><ol></br><li>Pertinent positives: elevated diastolic BP, elevated BMI<br /></br>   Pertinent negatives: no heavy alcohol, no end-organ damage, no chronic disease, normal electrolytes  </li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive approach: On test day, immediately ask, “Is drug therapy mandated now, or should I start with lifestyle?” For a young patient without end-organ disease, always scan the answer set for the single most effective lifestyle intervention—as long as it is feasible and directly addresses a modifiable risk factor shown in the stem.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• First-line management of uncomplicated stage 1 hypertension in a young adult is intensive lifestyle modification.<br /></br>• Among lifestyle interventions, weight reduction has the largest average blood-pressure–lowering effect (≈5–20 mm Hg systolic per 10 kg lost).<br /></br>Core takeaway: “If the patient is overweight and doesn’t yet need urgent pharmacotherapy, weight loss is step #1.”  </p></br><p>Documentation note (real patient): Record BMI, counseling on diet/exercise plan, goal weight/BP targets, and plan for repeat BP check in 1–3 months.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Excess adipose tissue raises BP via increased circulating insulin, activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, sympathetic overactivity, and structural vascular changes.<br /></br>• Even modest weight reduction decreases peripheral resistance and improves arterial compliance.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Review<br /></br>Non-pharmacologic BP-lowering hierarchy (average systolic ↓):<br /></br>1. Weight loss (5–20 mm Hg per 10 kg)<br /></br>2. DASH diet (8–14 mm Hg)<br /></br>3. Dietary Na⁺ restriction (2–8 mm Hg)<br /></br>4. Aerobic exercise (4–9 mm Hg)<br /></br>5. Limited alcohol (2–4 mm Hg)  </p></br><p>If lifestyle measures fail after ~3 months or if BP &gt;160/100, start pharmacologic therapy (e.g., thiazide diuretic, ACE inhibitor, ARB, or CCB).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“WEIGHTS DASH Down the Pressure”:<br /></br>W = Weight loss (strongest)<br /></br>E = Exercise<br /></br>I = Intake of salt lowered<br /></br>G = Grains/Fruits/veg (DASH diet)<br /></br>H = Hydro (limit alcohol)<br /></br>T = Toss the smokes  </p></br><p>Remember: “10 kg = 10 mm Hg.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Decreased alcohol consumption<br /></br>   • He drinks 1 beer/day (~14 g ethanol). Clinically significant BP reduction is seen when heavy intake (&gt;2 drinks/day) is curtailed. Expected benefit here: minimal (≈2 mm Hg).  </p></br><p>b. Increased amount of sleep<br /></br>   • Poor sleep can raise BP, but data on BP reduction through sleep extension alone are limited; magnitude is smaller than with weight loss and not guideline-endorsed as a primary strategy.  </p></br><p>d. Biofeedback<br /></br>   • May transiently reduce stress-related sympathetic tone, but effect size on chronic BP is small and not first-line.  </p></br><p>e. Hydrochlorothiazide therapy<br /></br>   • Drug therapy is reasonable once lifestyle changes fail or if BP were ≥160/100 or evidence of target-organ damage existed. In this initial employment screen of a young, otherwise healthy man, guidelines direct providers to attempt lifestyle modification first.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: In an overweight young adult with newly discovered stage 1 hypertension and no end-organ disease, weight loss is the single most effective and guideline-endorsed initial intervention.</p>
67. Five days after admission to the hospital because of inoperable descending thoracic aortic aneurysm with dissection, a 73-year-old man has moderate back pain. He has been treated with acetaminophen-oxycodone and rest. He has hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hypercholesterolemia. On admission, his medications were clopidogrel, levalbuterol, pravastatin, glipizide, metoprolol, lisinopril, and aspirin. The clopidogrel and aspirin were discontinued on hospital day 1. Five years ago, drug-eluting stents were placed in the left main coronary artery and circumflex artery for an anterior myocardial infarction. His current medications are nitroprusside, metoprolol, furosemide, acetaminophen-oxycodone, and levalbuterol. He has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 50 years. His temperature is 38.3 C (101 F), pulse is 110/min, and blood pressure is 180/106 mm Hg. Examination shows livedo reticularis over the dorsum of the left lower leg. There are petechiae over the dorsa of the feet and several small, purplish black lesions over the left first, second, and third toes and the right second and third toes. The toes are tender to palpation. There is lower abdominal tenderness; bowel sounds are increased. Laboratory studies show:</br>Leukocyte count               14,000/mm^3</br>Segmented neutrophils         80 percent</br>Eosinophils                   13 percent</br>Lymphocytes                   5 percent</br>Monocytes                     2 percent</br>Erythrocyte sedimentation rate   130 mm/h</br>Serum creatinine              2.4 mg/dL</br>Urine</br>  Protein                     2+</br>  RBC                         20–30/hpf</br>  Casts                       multiple RBC and granular</br>Maltese crosses are noted. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome</br>b. Bacterial endocarditis</br>c. Leukocytoclastic vasculitis</br>d. Multiple cholesterol emboli syndrome</br>e. Systemic necrotizing vasculitis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Multiple cholesterol emboli syndrome (athero-embolism)</p></br><p>────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>1. Timing/Setting<br /></br>   • Hospital day 5 after admission for a large descending thoracic aortic aneurysm with dissection → heavy aortic atherosclerotic burden; recent vascular manipulation and marked blood-pressure fluctuations predispose to plaque showering.  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Skin findings – classic “blue-toe/livedo” pattern<br /></br>   • Livedo reticularis over left leg<br /></br>   • Petechiae and painful purplish-black digits (“blue toes”) on both feet  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Kidney findings – abrupt sub-acute AKI with nephritic features<br /></br>   • Cr 2.4 mg/dL (previously normal)<br /></br>   • Hematuria + proteinuria, many RBC &amp; granular casts<br /></br>   • Maltese crosses = lipid-laden oval fat bodies (heavy glomerular injury)  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Systemic inflammatory response<br /></br>   • Fever 38.3 °C, WBC 14 000 with marked eosinophilia (13 %)<br /></br>   • ESR 130 mm/h<br /></br>   • Mild GI ischemia → lower abdominal pain, hyperactive bowel sounds  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Key triad for cholesterol emboli<br /></br>   • Skin ischemia + AKI + eosinophilia appearing days–weeks after aortic disease / cath / surgery.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Whenever you see 1) a recent vascular procedure or known severe atherosclerosis, 2) painful purple toes/livedo, 3) rising creatinine + eosinophilia/ESR → think cholesterol micro-embolization.</p></br><p>────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>a. Identify precipitating event (dissection/aneurysm = unstable plaque).<br /></br>b. Look for multi-organ small-vessel ischemia (skin, kidney, GI, retina).<br /></br>c. Note inflammatory markers—eosinophilia, high ESR, low-grade fever.<br /></br>d. Rule out infection or vasculitis → absence of murmurs, ANCA, immune-complex rashes; presence of eosinophilia &amp; “blue toes” strongly favors athero-embolism.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Cholesterol crystal showers cause occlusion &amp; intense eosinophil-rich end-arteritis, producing sub-acute organ ischemia days after aortic manipulation.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): Record skin findings, AKI trend, eosinophil count, discontinue anticoagulation where possible, discuss prognosis/renal follow-up.</p></br><p>────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Atherosclerotic plaque rupture releases needle-shaped cholesterol crystals.<br /></br>• Lodging in arterioles (&lt;200 µm) triggers complement &amp; eosinophil-rich inflammation → ischemia &amp; end-organ failure.<br /></br>• Kidneys (most common), skin, GI tract, retina, CNS are typical targets.</p></br><p>Risk factors: severe diffuse atherosclerosis, recent angiography, vascular surgery, thrombolysis, uncontrolled HTN.</p></br><p>────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Management</br>Diagnostics (confirmatory):<br /></br>• Skin or renal biopsy → biconvex, “cleft-like” empty spaces (dissolved cholesterol).<br /></br>• Fundoscopy: Hollenhorst plaques.  </p></br><p>Management: largely supportive<br /></br>1. Optimize BP/volume; discontinue inciting anticoagulants if possible.<br /></br>2. High-intensity statin (e.g., atorvastatin 40–80 mg) to stabilize plaques.<br /></br>3. Treat complications (dialysis for ESRD, PPI for GI ischemia).<br /></br>4. Prognosis: renal recovery limited; mortality high if multiorgan.</p></br><p>────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Memory Aids</br>“BLUE TOE” mnemonic:<br /></br>B – BP manipulation/procedure<br /></br>L – Livedo reticularis<br /></br>U – ↑ Urea/creatinine (AKI)<br /></br>E – Eosinophilia, ESR ↑<br /></br>T – Tender toes (purple)<br /></br>O – Oval fat bodies (Maltese crosses)<br /></br>E – Emboli of CholEsterol</p></br><p>────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>a. Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome<br /></br>   • Causes thrombosis &amp; pregnancy loss; no eosinophilia, ESR often normal; livedo may appear but AKI is from large-vessel thrombosis, not RBC casts/Maltese crosses.</p></br><p>b. Bacterial endocarditis<br /></br>   • Can produce emboli &amp; fever, but usually has murmur, positive blood cultures, Osler/Janeway lesions (not blue toes), and no eosinophilia. Renal disease is immune-complex GN (lower complement).</p></br><p>c. Leukocytoclastic (hypersensitivity) vasculitis<br /></br>   • Presents with palpable purpura on dependent areas; systemic organ involvement uncommon; UA usually bland; eosinophilia uncommon.</p></br><p>e. Systemic necrotizing vasculitis (e.g., PAN, GPA)<br /></br>   • Typically more protracted course, constitutional symptoms &gt; weeks; ANCA positivity (except PAN), pulmonary or neurologic manifestations. Eosinophilia rare (except EGPA). Blue-toe pattern and precipitating aortic event not characteristic.</p></br><p>────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line: Sudden livedo reticularis, painful blue toes, AKI with hematuria/proteinuria, eosinophilia, and high ESR a few days after an aortic event = multiple cholesterol (athero-) emboli syndrome.</p>
68. A 13-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his father because of a 1-day history of severe, left-sided throat pain. The pain is constant and increases when he swallows or opens his mouth. He also has had mild shortness of breath during this time. He had an episode of tonsillitis 5 years ago that resolved with amoxicillin therapy. He has no other history of serious illness and takes no medications. His temperature is 38.4 C (101.1 F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 110/65 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 96 percent. Examination shows edema of the left tonsil and deviation of the uvula with almost complete occlusion of the pharyngeal space. The tonsil is erythematous and fluctuant; there is a diffuse yellow exudate.  </br>Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Administration of amoxicillin–clavulanate  </br>b. Administration of hydrocortisone  </br>c. CT scan of the neck with contrast  </br>d. Needle aspiration of the peritonsillar space  </br>e. Tonsillectomy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Needle aspiration of the peritonsillar space  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that shout “peritonsillar abscess (PTA)”<br /></br>• Age/setting: adolescent with acute unilateral throat pain.<br /></br>• Severe odynophagia that worsens with swallowing or mouth opening (trismus).<br /></br>• Low-grade fever (38.4 °C).<br /></br>• Physical exam “buzz phrases”:<br /></br>  – Edema &amp; erythema of ONE tonsil.<br /></br>  – Fluctuant mass.<br /></br>  – Uvular deviation away from the involved side → pharyngeal airway nearly occluded.  </p></br><p>Pertinent negatives: Oxygen saturation is adequate (96 %), so no emergent airway maneuver (intubation) is required before drainage.  </p></br><p>Cognitive road-map for future questions<br /></br>1. Unilateral pharyngitis + “hot-potato” voice, uvular deviation, trismus → think PTA.<br /></br>2. Rule out deep-neck space infection (retropharyngeal, parapharyngeal) if there is neck stiffness, torticollis, or unsure exam—then you would image.<br /></br>3. If classic PTA and airway is stable → immediate drainage + IV antibiotics.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why needle aspiration NOW?<br /></br>• A true abscess must be physically drained—antibiotics alone penetrate poorly.<br /></br>• Needle aspiration (or incision-and-drain) provides:<br /></br>  – Rapid symptom relief &amp; ↓ airway obstruction.<br /></br>  – Pus for culture.<br /></br>• After drainage give IV ampicillin-sulbactam or clindamycin ± steroids to reduce edema.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Classic PTA with stable airway → drainage first, then antibiotics.  </p></br><p>Documentation points if this were a real case<br /></br>• Airway status (voice changes, stridor, O2 sat).<br /></br>• Detailed oropharyngeal findings (size, fluctuant center, uvular deviation).<br /></br>• Procedure note for aspiration: site, amount/character of pus, specimen sent for C&amp;S.<br /></br>• Empiric IV antibiotic started.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Etiology: complication of bacterial tonsillitis; polymicrobial—Group A Strep, S. aureus, anaerobes.  </li></br><li>Abscess forms between tonsillar capsule &amp; superior constrictor muscle.  </li></br><li>Untreated PTA may spread to parapharyngeal space → carotid sheath → mediastinum (descending necrotizing mediastinitis) or cause Lemierre syndrome (internal jugular septic thrombophlebitis).  </li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>Initial management sequence for PTA:<br /></br>1. Assess airway. If impending obstruction → secure airway first.<br /></br>2. Drain the abscess (needle aspiration or I&amp;D).<br /></br>3. IV empiric antibiotics:<br /></br>   • Ampicillin-sulbactam OR clindamycin (covers GAS, MSSA, anaerobes).<br /></br>4. Adjuncts: Dexamethasone for severe edema/pain, analgesia, hydration.<br /></br>5. Tonsillectomy (“quinsy tonsillectomy”) only if:<br /></br>   • Recurrent PTA, chronic tonsillitis, or drainage failure.  </p></br><p>Diagnostic imaging (CT with contrast) only if the physical exam is equivocal or concern for deeper neck space involvement.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Hot-potato voice + Uvula pushed away → Stick a needle today.”  </p></br><p>PTA 3 D’s mnemonic:<br /></br>1. Deviation of uvula<br /></br>2. Dysphagia (“hot-potato” voice)<br /></br>3. Drainage required  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Amoxicillin–clavulanate<br /></br>   • Appropriate for uncomplicated tonsillitis or peritonsillar cellulitis, but pus collection needs drainage first. Antibiotics alone are insufficient.  </p></br><p>b. Hydrocortisone<br /></br>   • Steroids may reduce edema but do not treat the underlying infection or remove the abscess. Adjunct only after drainage + antibiotics.  </p></br><p>c. CT scan of the neck with contrast<br /></br>   • Imaging delays definitive care. In classic PTA, physical findings alone establish the diagnosis. CT is reserved for atypical presentations or suspected retropharyngeal/parapharyngeal abscess.  </p></br><p>e. Tonsillectomy<br /></br>   • Not first-line in an acute setting. Consider after resolution for recurrent PTA or chronic tonsillitis, or if drainage fails.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Peritonsillar abscess presents with unilateral pharyngitis, uvular deviation, and muffled voice. Stable airway? → immediate needle aspiration/I&amp;D, followed by IV broad-spectrum antibiotics.</p>
69. A 47-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 3-month history of a nonproductive cough that is worse on weekdays when she is at work and improves during the weekend. She reports that the cough resolves completely by the end of the weekend. She has not had fever or weight loss. She does not smoke or use illicit drugs. Four months ago, she began working at a dry cleaning business. She is in no distress. Vital signs are within normal limits. An occasional end-expiratory wheeze is heard at both lung bases with mild hyperresonance to percussion. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Pulmonary function tests are most likely to show which of the following findings?</br>FEV₁:FVC Ratio   Peak Expiratory Flow Rate	Correct answer: A</br></br>a.   Decreased      decreased</br>b.   Decreased      increased</br>c.   Decreased      normal</br>d.   Increased      decreased</br>e.   Increased      increased</br>f.   Increased      normal</br>g.   Normal         decreased</br>h.   Normal         increased</br>i.   Normal         normal</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>a. FEV₁:FVC ratio – Decreased  Peak Expiratory Flow Rate – Decreased  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• “Cough worse on weekdays … resolves on weekends” → temporal link to workplace exposure = occupational lung disease.<br /></br>• New job “at a dry-cleaning business” → exposure to volatile organic solvents (eg, perchloroethylene) classically associated with occupational asthma.<br /></br>• Exam: “occasional end-expiratory wheeze” and “mild hyperresonance” → airflow obstruction.<br /></br>• No fever, weight loss, or systemic signs → argues against infection or malignancy.  </p></br><p>Approach when testing:<br /></br>1. Recognize exposure pattern (symptoms improve when away from work).<br /></br>2. Think “occupational asthma” → expect an obstructive PFT pattern.<br /></br>3. Match answer choice showing obstruction (↓FEV₁, ↓FEV₁/FVC, ↓flow rates).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Asthma = reversible obstruction. During an exacerbation or when exposed to trigger:<br /></br>• Airway smooth-muscle constriction &amp; mucosal edema ↓ airway caliber →<br /></br>  – FEV₁ falls a lot, FVC slightly or not as much → ↓FEV₁/FVC ratio (&lt;70%).<br /></br>  – All effort-dependent flow rates including Peak Expiratory Flow (PEF) decline.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Any obstructive disease (asthma, COPD) shows a decreased FEV₁/FVC ratio and reduced flow rates.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): onset, course relative to work, specific chemicals, spirometry results pre- &amp; post-shift, and action plan for avoidance or therapy.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Occupational asthma can be:<br /></br>• Immunologic (sensitizer-induced) – latency period, IgE or non-IgE mediated.<br /></br>• Non-immunologic (irritant-induced, “reactive airways dysfunction syndrome”) – usually immediate after high-level irritant exposure.  </p></br><p>Dry-cleaning solvents are low-molecular-weight chemicals that act as irritants leading to airway hyperresponsiveness.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>• Best initial diagnostic step: serial peak flow measurements at work vs away, or bronchoprovocation (methacholine) after a work-free period.<br /></br>• Management:<br /></br>  1. Eliminate/limit exposure (engineering controls or job change).<br /></br>  2. Pharmacologic same as non-occupational asthma – short-acting β₂-agonist PRN, inhaled corticosteroids; consider leukotriene modifiers.<br /></br>  3. Periodic PFT monitoring to document reversibility.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“AAO – Asthma = Airflow down (↓)”:<br /></br>• Airflow parameters (FEV₁, PEF) down → ratio down.<br /></br>• Restriction = “RVF Up” → Ratio Very high in Fibrosis (↑FEV₁/FVC).  </p></br><p>Mnemonic for occupational asthma clues: “WIPE”<br /></br>Weekday worse<br /></br>Improves on weekends<br /></br>Particular workplace exposure<br /></br>End-expiratory wheeze  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>b. Decreased ratio, ↑PEF – Impossible: obstruction always lowers PEF.<br /></br>c. Decreased ratio, normal PEF – PEF would fall with obstruction; not consistent.<br /></br>d–f. Increased ratio – Seen in restrictive lung diseases (pulmonary fibrosis, obesity hypoventilation) where PEF is normal or mildly ↓, not in this wheezy, exposure-related case.<br /></br>g. Normal ratio, ↓PEF – Suggests fixed upper-airway obstruction (eg, tracheal stenosis) rather than asthma (ratio would drop).<br /></br>h. Normal ratio, ↑PEF – No common pathologic correlate.<br /></br>i. Normal/normal – Would imply normal lungs, incompatible with exam findings and clear symptom pattern.  </p></br><p>Key discriminator: obstructive disease → ↓ratio AND ↓flow rates.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Occupational asthma from dry-cleaning solvent exposure produces a classic obstructive spirometric pattern with both FEV₁:FVC ratio and peak expiratory flow decreased (choice a).</p>
70. A 62-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 1-year history of increasing shortness of breath on exertion. He now has difficulty finishing yard work and climbing stairs in his home. He has not had cough, fever, or night sweats. He has hypertension treated with atenolol. He smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 30 years but quit 12 years ago. He worked in the auto parts department of a local car dealership until he had to retire last month because of his respiratory symptoms. His temperature is 36.2 C (97.2 F), pulse is 88/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 140/82 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 90 percent at rest and 82 percent after walking 20 feet. Fine inspiratory crackles are heard at both lung bases. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. A high-resolution CT scan of the chest shows a honeycomb appearance of the lungs. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Asbestosis</br>b. Bronchiolitis obliterans</br>c. Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis</br>d. Mesothelioma</br>e. Pulmonary embolism</br>f. Pulmonary Langerhans cell histiocytosis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF)  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Strategy<br /></br>1. Time course: “1-year history of increasing shortness of breath on exertion” → chronic, progressive.<br /></br>2. Exam: “Fine inspiratory crackles … at both lung bases” → classic “Velcro” crackles of interstitial fibrosis.<br /></br>3. Imaging: “High-resolution CT … honeycomb appearance” → hallmark of the usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP) pattern seen in IPF.<br /></br>4. Negatives: No systemic symptoms (fever, night sweats), no clear occupational exposure (e.g., asbestos), no acute pleuritic chest pain to suggest PE.<br /></br>Approach: Any middle-aged/older adult with progressive exertional dyspnea + basilar Velcro rales + HRCT honeycombing ⇒ think idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis unless a specific exposure or systemic disease is identified.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1: Recognize chronic restrictive lung disease presentation (progressive DOE, basilar crackles, ↓ resting &amp; exertional O2 sat).<br /></br>Step 2: Identify key imaging finding (honeycombing = cystic, fibrotic destruction of peripheral/subpleural lung).<br /></br>Step 3: Rule out known causes (asbestos, drug toxicity, connective-tissue disease) … none present → “idiopathic.”<br /></br>Core takeaway: UIP pattern on HRCT in a &gt;50-year-old male without another cause = IPF.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): duration and progression of dyspnea, smoking history, absence of occupational exposures, resting &amp; exertional O2 sat, HRCT report noting basilar/subpleural reticulation and honeycombing.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology High-Yield Review<br /></br>• Etiology: Repetitive alveolar epithelial injury in genetically susceptible individuals → aberrant wound healing → fibroblast proliferation &amp; collagen deposition.<br /></br>• Demographics: Males, age &gt;50, former or current smokers.<br /></br>• Anatomy: Patchy but predominantly peripheral &amp; basilar fibrosis → honeycomb cysts.<br /></br>• Physiology: Restrictive pattern (↓ TLC, ↓ FVC, ↑/normal FEV1/FVC), impaired gas exchange → exertional desaturation.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Management Snapshot<br /></br>1. Disease-modifying drugs:<br /></br>   • Pirfenidone (antifibrotic)<br /></br>   • Nintedanib (tyrosine-kinase inhibitor)<br /></br>2. Symptom control: O2, pulmonary rehab.<br /></br>3. Curative: Lung transplantation (selected patients).<br /></br><em>Steroids are NOT effective in classic IPF.</em></p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids<br /></br>“IPF = I’m Past Fifty with Fibrosis”:<br /></br>I – Inspiratory Velcro crackles<br /></br>P – Progressive dyspnea<br /></br>F – Fifty-plus, Fibrosis, Fine basilar crackles, Honeycomb</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Asbestosis – Needs history of shipyard, insulation, brake linings; HRCT: calcified pleural plaques ± ferruginous bodies, not honeycombing UIP pattern.<br /></br>b. Bronchiolitis obliterans – Post–lung-transplant, viral, or toxic inhalation; causes airflow obstruction, mosaic attenuation, not basal honeycomb fibrosis.<br /></br>d. Mesothelioma – Malignant pleural tumor; presents with unilateral pleural thickening/effusion, chest pain, weight loss, not diffuse bilateral honeycombing.<br /></br>e. Pulmonary embolism – Acute or subacute pleuritic chest pain, sudden dyspnea, normal lung parenchyma on CT angiography; not chronic fibrosis.<br /></br>f. Pulmonary Langerhans cell histiocytosis – Young adult smokers; HRCT shows nodules &amp; cysts in UPPER lobes, may cause spontaneous pneumothorax, not basal honeycomb pattern.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Key Exam Pearl<br /></br>“Basilar Velcro crackles + honeycomb lung on HRCT in an older smoker with no clear exposure = Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis.”</p>
71. A 12-month-old boy is brought to the physician 24 hours after the sudden onset of diarrhea. His parents report that he has had approximately 10 episodes of watery stools with no blood or mucus. He has vomited once. He has been drinking fluids and eating crackers but has not been as active as usual. He appears alert and is not in distress. His temperature is 38.3 C (101 F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 26/min, and blood pressure is 90/60 mm Hg. Physical examination shows moist mucous membranes. The oropharynx appears normal. The lungs are clear to auscultation. The abdomen is soft and nontender with no distention. Bowel sounds are normal. All extremities are warm and well perfused. Laboratory studies show:</br>Serum  </br>Na⁺ 139 mEq/L  </br>K⁺ 4.0 mEq/L  </br>Cl⁻ 110 mEq/L  </br>HCO₃⁻ 15 mEq/L  </br>Urea nitrogen 10 mg/dL  </br>Creatinine 0.5 mg/dL  </br>Fecal leukocytes none  </br>Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Oral rehydration  </br>b. Oral bismuth subsalicylate therapy  </br>c. Oral metoclopramide therapy  </br>d. Oral penicillin therapy  </br>e. Intravenous administration of 0.9 percent saline</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>a. Oral rehydration  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key words &amp; clues<br /></br>• “12-month-old” with “sudden onset of watery stools” → classic viral gastroenteritis (rotavirus, norovirus, adenovirus).<br /></br>• “No blood or mucus” and “fecal leukocytes none” → non-inflammatory diarrhea; argues against invasive bacterial pathogens (Shigella, Salmonella, Campylobacter, EHEC).<br /></br>• Vital signs: T 38.3 °C (mild fever); HR 120/min (upper-normal for age); BP 90/60 mm Hg (normal).<br /></br>• Exam: “moist mucous membranes,” “warm, well-perfused extremities,” no abdominal tenderness → at most minimal dehydration.<br /></br>• Labs: Na 139, K 4.0 (normal); HCO₃⁻ 15 with Cl⁻ 110 → non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis from bicarbonate loss in stool; urea nitrogen and creatinine normal → adequate renal perfusion.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>• Positive: watery diarrhea of short duration, mild fever.<br /></br>• Negative: no signs of moderate/severe dehydration (no sunken eyes/fontanelle, no delayed capillary refill, no tachypnea from metabolic acidosis), no dysentery, no leukocytes in stool.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>Step 1: assess hydration status; Step 2: decide between oral vs IV fluids; Step 3: consider specific therapy only if invasive/bacterial etiology suspected. Because child is alert and drinking, oral rehydration solution (ORS) is sufficient.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• Goal in acute pediatric diarrhea = prevent/treat dehydration &amp; electrolyte loss.<br /></br>• ORS (glucose-electrolyte solution) exploits Na-glucose cotransporter to enhance water absorption in the small intestine—first-line for mild (&lt;5% body weight loss) to moderate (5–10%) dehydration.<br /></br>• IV fluids reserved only if:<br /></br>  – Severe dehydration (≥10% weight loss), shock, or altered mental status.<br /></br>  – Intractable vomiting preventing ORS.<br /></br>• No evidence of bacterial invasion → antimicrobials not indicated; anti-diarrheal/anti-emetic agents add no benefit and may cause harm.</p></br><p>Take-home message: In a child with mild, non-bloody, viral-type diarrhea and minimal dehydration, give oral rehydration therapy—nothing more.</p></br><p>Documentation if real patient: hydration assessment, number of wet diapers, weight, ORS volume recommended, red-flag return precautions (persistent vomiting, bloody stools, signs of dehydration).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)  </p></br><p>• Viral gastroenteritis damages enterocytes → malabsorption &amp; secretory diarrhea; stool rich in HCO₃⁻ → non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis (↓HCO₃⁻, ↑Cl⁻).<br /></br>• Na⁺/glucose co-transport in small bowel remains intact → ORS rapidly corrects dehydration.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatments &amp; Diagnostics Review  </p></br><ol></br><li>Oral rehydration components:<br /></br>   – NaCl, trisodium citrate (or bicarbonate), KCl, glucose (WHO/CDC formula).  </li></br><li>Continue age-appropriate diet; avoid fruit juices, carbonated beverages (hypo-osmolar).  </li></br><li>No routine labs/imaging unless red flags (bloody stool, high fever, severe pain, immunocompromise).</li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“3 R’s of Viral Diarrhea in Kids: Rehydrate (oral), Resume diet, Re-assure.”<br /></br>Visualize a child sipping Pedialyte (ORS) from a straw shaped like a ‘Na-glucose cotransporter’—water follows the ride!</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Oral bismuth subsalicylate<br /></br>• Salicylate toxicity risk in young children (Reye syndrome). Minimal evidence of benefit in acute pediatric viral diarrhea.</p></br><p>c. Oral metoclopramide<br /></br>• Dopamine antagonist used for persistent vomiting/gastroparesis. Child vomited only once and is tolerating PO intake; drug unnecessary and carries risk of dystonia.</p></br><p>d. Oral penicillin therapy<br /></br>• Penicillins target Gram-positives; acute watery pediatric diarrhea is usually viral or, if bacterial, often Gram-negative enterics for which penicillin is ineffective. Antibiotics reserved for specific invasive or systemic bacterial illness.</p></br><p>e. Intravenous 0.9% saline<br /></br>• Indicated for moderate-to-severe dehydration, shock, or inability to take PO. Exam and labs show adequate perfusion and oral intake; IV route adds risk and unnecessary cost.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: Mild, non-bloody viral diarrhea in a hemodynamically stable infant is managed with oral rehydration solution—simple, evidence-based, and lifesaving.</p>
72. A 57-year-old man comes to the physician 3 days after undergoing colonoscopy screening that showed an ulcerated mass of invasive, poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma in the mid ascending colon. He feels well and has not had abdominal discomfort; black, tarry, or bloody stools; or weight loss. He has sciatica treated with ibuprofen as needed. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no abnormalities. Results of laboratory studies are within the reference ranges. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Abdominal ultrasonography</br>b. CT scan of the abdomen</br>c. Bone scan</br>d. PET scan</br>e. Surgical exploration</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. CT scan of the abdomen (‐ and, in real practice, CT of the abdomen AND pelvis ± chest for full staging)</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key words &amp; clues<br /></br>• “Ulcerated mass of invasive, poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma in the mid ascending colon” – biopsy-proven colon cancer.<br /></br>• “Feels well … no abdominal discomfort; black, tarry, or bloody stools; or weight loss … vitals and labs normal” – no alarm S/Sx of obstruction or perforation; he is clinically stable.<br /></br>• Question stem: “most appropriate next step in diagnosis” – i.e., after the tissue diagnosis, what is the very next diagnostic study?  </p></br><p>Cognitive strategy<br /></br>1.
