Recognize that once colon cancer is pathologically proven, the priority shifts from detection to staging.<br /></br>2. Ask: What is the standard first-line imaging modality for staging colon cancer? → Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen/pelvis (plus chest).<br /></br>3. Eliminate answer choices that either (a) do not stage adequately, (b) are only indicated when specific symptoms are present, or (c) are more advanced/expensive than needed.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. The biopsy (colonoscopy) already provided the tissue diagnosis.<br /></br>2. Staging determines resectability, need for neoadjuvant/adjunctive chemo, and prognosis (TNM system: depth of invasion, regional nodes, distant mets).<br /></br>3. Most common sites of spread from right-sided colon cancer = liver (via portal circulation) and lung. CT abdomen/pelvis (often with chest) is therefore first.<br /></br>4. Thus, CT abdomen (and pelvis) is the correct immediate next diagnostic step.  </p></br><p>Take-home message<br /></br>After histologic confirmation of colon cancer, obtain contrast CT of abdomen/pelvis (± chest) to stage the disease before any operative or oncologic intervention.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl<br /></br>“Plan: Order contrast CT abdomen/pelvis for staging; will review for evidence of regional LN involvement, liver metastases, peritoneal disease. Discussed rationale and next steps with patient.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• Right-sided (ascending) colon cancers often present insidiously (occult bleeding → iron-deficiency anemia) compared with left-sided lesions (obstruction, hematochezia).<br /></br>• Poorly differentiated histology is a worse prognostic factor.<br /></br>• Spread: hematogenous via portal vein to liver &gt; lung; lymphatic to mesenteric nodes.<br /></br>• TNM staging directs therapy:<br /></br>  – Stage I/II: surgical resection ± adjuvant chemo (high-risk II).<br /></br>  – Stage III: surgery + adjuvant FOLFOX (5-FU/leucovorin/oxaliplatin).<br /></br>  – Stage IV: systemic chemo; resect isolated liver mets if feasible.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Relevant Tests / Treatment Outline  </p></br><p>Diagnostic staging<br /></br>1. CT abdomen &amp; pelvis with IV and oral contrast → liver, peritoneum, nodes.<br /></br>2. CT chest or chest X-ray for pulmonary mets.<br /></br>3. CEA (carcinoembryonic antigen) level for baseline prognostication.<br /></br>4. Consider MRI liver or PET/CT if CT equivocal.  </p></br><p>Initial treatment (after staging)<br /></br>• Surgical right hemicolectomy with lymphadenectomy if resectable.<br /></br>• Adjuvant chemotherapy based on final pathologic stage.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Biopsy → CT” Mnemonic:<br /></br>B = Biopsy proves cancer<br /></br>C = CT Chest/abdomen/pelvis Comes next for Colon cancer staging  </p></br><p>Or think “Colon Cancer = CT before Cut (surgery).”  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Abdominal ultrasonography<br /></br>• Good for gallbladder or screening liver lesions but insensitive for small hepatic mets, nodal disease, or peritoneal spread → inadequate staging.  </p></br><p>C. Bone scan<br /></br>• Indicated only if bone pain, ↑alk phos, or known advanced disease. Colon cancer metastasizes to bone late; no skeletal symptoms here.  </p></br><p>D. PET scan<br /></br>• Higher cost, reserved for equivocal CT findings or assessing recurrence. Not first-line for initial staging of colorectal cancer.  </p></br><p>E. Surgical exploration<br /></br>• Premature. Operating without imaging may miss unresectable metastatic disease and change management. Modern protocols mandate pre-op staging imaging first.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: Jumping straight to surgery once a malignancy is found. Always stage first—imaging alters the operative plan in up to 25-30 % of colorectal cancers.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Summary  </p></br><p>Biopsy-proven colon adenocarcinoma ➜ next diagnostic step is contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen (and pelvis/chest) to stage disease, detect liver or nodal metastases, and guide surgical and oncologic management.</p>
73. An 84-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 1-week history of increasingly severe pain and swelling of the left side of her face. The pain worsens with eating. She has mild dementia, Alzheimer type, and hypertension. Her medications are donepezil and hydrochlorothiazide. Her temperature is 38.1 C (100.5 F), pulse is 86/min, and blood pressure is 144/90 mm Hg. Examination of the face shows left-sided edema that is tender to palpation. On palpation of the left parotid gland, purulent discharge is expressed from the orifice of the Stensen duct. Several tender nodes are palpated in the left anterior cervical chain. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely infectious agent?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Clostridium perfringens</br>b. Moraxella catarrhalis</br>c. Pseudomonas aeruginosa</br>d. Staphylococcus aureus</br>e. Streptococcus pneumoniae</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Staphylococcus aureus  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Key words &amp; clues<br /></br>   • 84-year-old, debilitated → ↓ salivary flow, poor oral hygiene = classic host for bacterial rather than viral parotitis<br /></br>   • 1-week “increasingly severe pain &amp; swelling,” tenderness, fever (38.1 °C) → acute, suppurative process<br /></br>   • Pain “worsens with eating” → salivary stimulation stretches inflamed parotid capsule<br /></br>   • Purulent material expressed from Stensen duct → pathognomonic for acute bacterial (suppurative) parotitis<br /></br>   • Unilateral involvement &amp; cervical adenopathy → typical bacterial pattern (viral mumps is usually bilateral, non-purulent)  </p></br><p>Approach tip:  When you see “elderly / dehydrated / postoperative + unilateral parotid swelling with pus from Stensen duct,” immediately think acute bacterial parotitis → Staph aureus until proven otherwise.  </p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-by-step:<br /></br>1. Identify syndrome = acute suppurative parotitis.<br /></br>2. Recall most common pathogen = Staphylococcus aureus (&gt;70 % of cases).<br /></br>3. Match to answer choice → D.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Acute bacterial parotitis in elderly, dehydrated, or postoperative patients is most often caused by S. aureus; management requires antistaphylococcal antibiotics plus supportive sialogogues/hydration.  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Salivary stasis (dehydration, anticholinergic drugs, poor oral intake) allows oral flora to ascend Stensen duct.<br /></br>• S. aureus, a gram-positive catalase-positive cocci, colonizes skin/oral cavity and invades the gland, producing purulent exudate.<br /></br>• Inflammation leads to gland enlargement, tenderness, fever, and cervical lymphadenitis.  </p></br><p>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>1. Lab: culture of duct drainage (but empiric therapy started immediately).<br /></br>2. Imaging (US or CT) only if abscess suspected or no response to therapy.<br /></br>3. Empiric IV anti-staphylococcal coverage:<br /></br>   • Nafcillin or oxacillin ± clindamycin; use vancomycin or linezolid if MRSA risk.<br /></br>4. Supportive: aggressive hydration, warm compresses, massage, sialogogues (lemon drops), oral hygiene.<br /></br>5. Abscess → surgical drainage.  </p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>• “Staph staffs the parotid.”<br /></br>• Picture a golden (aureus) staff stuck in Stensen’s duct dripping pus.<br /></br>• Elderly + unilateral + pus = Staph. Viral mumps = Young + bilateral + no pus.  </p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Clostridium perfringens – Causes gas gangrene/myonecrosis; would show crepitus, not localized parotid duct pus.<br /></br>b. Moraxella catarrhalis – Otitis media, sinusitis, COPD exacerbations; rarely infects salivary glands.<br /></br>c. Pseudomonas aeruginosa – Think malignant otitis externa (diabetics), ventilator pneumonia, burns; not typical for parotitis.<br /></br>e. Streptococcus pneumoniae – Leading cause of community-acquired pneumonia, otitis, meningitis; parotid infection uncommon and usually not purulent at duct opening.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: confusing suppurative parotitis (bacterial, unilateral, pus) with viral parotitis (mumps; bilateral, non-purulent, young, parotid/other salivary glands). Always look for the “purulent discharge from Stensen duct” clue.</p>
74. An 82-year-old woman, who resides in a skilled nursing care facility, is brought to the emergency department because of a 2-day history of diffuse, severe abdominal pain, which has worsened during the past day. She rates her pain as an 8 on a 10-point scale. Her last bowel movement was 3 days ago. She has severe diffuse arthritis, osteoporosis, hypertension, and scoliosis. Her medications are oxycodone, alendronate, hydrochlorothiazide, and calcium and vitamin D supplementation. Her blood pressure is 148/62 mm Hg. The abdomen is tympanic to percussion with marked distention and diffuse tenderness; there is no guarding or rigidity. High-pitched bowel sounds are heard. X-rays of the abdomen are most likely to show which of the following findings?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Dilation of the sigmoid colon</br>b. Inflammatory mass and fecalith in the cecum</br>c. Mucosal edema of the small intestine</br>d. Normal gas pattern in the large and small intestines</br>e. Presence of free air under the diaphragm</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Dilation of the sigmoid colon  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key words &amp; phrases<br /></br>   • 82-year-old nursing-home resident with chronic constipation (last BM 3 days ago)<br /></br>   • Bed-bound, debilitated, on opioids (oxycodone) → markedly ↓ colonic motility<br /></br>   • Progressive, diffuse abdominal pain + marked distention; abdomen tympanitic; high-pitched (“tinkling”) bowel sounds = mechanical obstruction rather than ileus.<br /></br>   • No guarding/rigidity → not yet perforated.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pattern recognition<br /></br>   Elderly, chronically constipated, institutionalized patients are classic for sigmoid volvulus. The torsion produces a closed-loop large-bowel obstruction, giving severe distention and pain but initially minimal peritoneal signs.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Cognitive approach for similar questions<br /></br>   Step 1: Decide “obstruction vs ileus vs perforation.” High-pitched sounds + distention → obstruction.<br /></br>   Step 2: Small vs large bowel? Marked distention + elderly/constipation → large bowel, especially sigmoid.<br /></br>   Step 3: Risk factors (age, neuro/psychiatric disease, opioid use) → volvulus.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• Sigmoid volvulus results when a redundant sigmoid loop with a long mesentery twists on its vascular pedicle.<br /></br>• X-ray hallmark = massively dilated, inverted-U / “coffee-bean” loop arising from the pelvis and pointing to RUQ or LUQ → “dilation of the sigmoid colon.”<br /></br>• Closed-loop obstruction explains high-pitched sounds; vascular compromise causes escalating pain.<br /></br>• Documentation in real life: abdominal exam, degree of distention, bowel sounds, serial abdominal girth, presence/absence of peritoneal signs, last BM/flatus, opioids, neuro status, plan for decompression vs surgery.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: In an elderly, constipated patient with an obstructive picture, think sigmoid volvulus → plain radiograph shows a massively dilated sigmoid.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Risk factors<br /></br>• Chronic constipation, megacolon, neuro disorders (Parkinson, dementia), psych meds, opioids.<br /></br>Mechanism<br /></br>• Axial twist obstructs lumen and mesenteric vessels → closed-loop obstruction ± ischemia → pain, distention, tympany.<br /></br>Clinical manifestations<br /></br>• Crampy pain, obstipation, abdominal distention; early high-pitched sounds, late silence if ischemia.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment  </p></br><p>Imaging<br /></br>• Plain upright abdominal film usually diagnostic (coffee-bean sign).<br /></br>• CT or contrast (water-soluble) enema if plain film equivocal.  </p></br><p>Management<br /></br>1. Initial: Endoscopic detorsion &amp; decompression with flexible sigmoidoscopy + rectal tube.<br /></br>2. Definitive: Elective sigmoid resection (because recurrence common).<br /></br>3. Emergent laparotomy if perforation or peritonitis.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aid  </p></br><p>“CAFFEINE in the Nursing Home”<br /></br>C – Coffee-bean sign<br /></br>A – Aged patient<br /></br>F – Foley? (often bedridden)<br /></br>F – Functional bowel disorders/constipation<br /></br>E – Endoscopic detorsion first<br /></br>I – Ischemia risk<br /></br>N – Needs surgical resection later  </p></br><p>Visualize a big coffee bean filling the pelvis of an elderly patient in a wheelchair.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Inflammatory mass and fecalith in the cecum<br /></br>   • Suggests appendicitis or cecal diverticulitis; would present with focal RLQ pain, fever, leukocytosis—not diffuse distention with high-pitched obstructive sounds.</p></br><p>c. Mucosal edema of the small intestine<br /></br>   • Indicates small-bowel ischemia (e.g., mesenteric ischemia); pain out of proportion but usually minimal distention and X-ray often normal or shows air-fluid levels in small bowel, not huge sigmoid dilation.</p></br><p>d. Normal gas pattern in the large and small intestines<br /></br>   • Incompatible with the marked distention, tympany, and high-pitched sounds given. Mechanical obstruction produces abnormal, not normal, gas pattern.</p></br><p>e. Presence of free air under the diaphragm<br /></br>   • Sign of perforated viscus; patient would have sudden severe pain, rigid abdomen, guarding, rebound tenderness—absent here.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Elderly, constipated, nursing-home patient + progressive abdominal distention and high-pitched bowel sounds → think sigmoid volvulus. X-ray shows massive dilation of the sigmoid colon (“coffee-bean” sign).</p>
75. A 57-year-old woman with diabetic nephropathy comes to the physician for a routine examination. She has chronic kidney disease, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hypertension. Her medications are glyburide, enalapril, and metoprolol. Her blood pressure is 128/90 mm Hg. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. There is trace pedal edema. A 24-hour urine collection shows a total protein concentration of 5000 mg and creatinine clearance of 40 mL/min. Measurement of which of the following is the most appropriate next step to prevent complications of this patient's kidney disease?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Hemoglobin concentration</br>b. Platelet count</br>c. Serum C-reactive protein concentration</br>d. Serum ferritin concentration</br>e. Serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Hemoglobin concentration  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – Long-standing diabetic nephropathy with creatinine clearance 40 mL/min (≈ CKD stage 3b).<br /></br>  – Nephrotic-range proteinuria (5 g/24 h) confirms significant chronic kidney damage.<br /></br>  – No current anemia evaluation is documented.<br /></br>• What the examiners want you to recognize<br /></br>  – Once glomerular filtration rate (GFR) falls below about 60 mL/min, the kidney’s production of erythropoietin (EPO) progressively decreases, leading to “anemia of chronic kidney disease (CKD).”<br /></br>  – Early identification of anemia allows timely treatment with iron and/or EPO-stimulating agents, which prevents downstream complications (fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance, left-ventricular hypertrophy, need for transfusions).<br /></br>Approach tip: In any CKD stem, always think “ABCDEF” monitoring:<br /></br>A – Anemia (check Hb/Hct)<br /></br>B – Blood pressure &amp; bone-mineral labs (Ca, PO4, PTH, vit D)<br /></br>C – Cardiovascular risk (lipids)<br /></br>D – Drugs (dose adjustment)<br /></br>E – Electrolytes<br /></br>F – Fluids (volume status)  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1  Recognize CKD: CrCl 40, proteinuria, diabetic history.<br /></br>Step 2  Recall most common, easily modifiable systemic complication = normocytic anemia from ↓EPO.<br /></br>Step 3  Screen with hemoglobin (or hematocrit). If ↓, then:<br /></br>   • Check iron studies (ferritin, TSAT),<br /></br>   • Replete iron if needed,<br /></br>   • Begin EPO-stimulating agent when Hb &lt;10 g/dL.<br /></br>Core takeaway: In CKD patients, the first laboratory step to prevent anemia-related morbidity is to measure hemoglobin/hematocrit.  </p></br><p>Required documentation (real life): “Screened for anemia of CKD—CBC ordered; will consider iron studies and ESA depending on results.”  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Healthy kidneys secrete EPO → stimulates red-cell production.<br /></br>• GFR &lt;60 mL/min ⇒ ↓ functional renal mass ⇒ ↓ EPO ⇒ normocytic, normochromic anemia.<br /></br>• Untreated anemia → myocardial strain, LVH, heart failure, decreased quality of life.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Framework<br /></br>1. Screen: Hemoglobin (or full CBC) at least annually once GFR &lt;60 mL/min.<br /></br>2. If Hb &lt;10 g/dL:<br /></br>   – Iron studies (serum ferritin, transferrin saturation).<br /></br>   – Replete iron (IV iron sucrose, ferric gluconate) if ferritin ≤500 ng/mL or TSAT ≤30 %.<br /></br>3. Add erythropoiesis-stimulating agent (epoetin alfa or darbepoetin) once adequate iron stores ensured.<br /></br>4. Target Hb 10–11.5 g/dL; avoid &gt;13 g/dL to prevent thrombosis/HTN.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“CKD = Low EPO = Check CBC.”<br /></br>Mnemonic: “CKD gets A-B-C labs every year: Anemia (CBC), Bone-mineral (Ca/PO4/PTH), Cholesterol.”  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>b. Platelet count – Uremia causes qualitative platelet dysfunction, not thrombocytopenia. A normal count would not rule out bleeding risk; measuring it doesn’t prevent CKD complications.  </p></br><p>c. Serum C-reactive protein – Non-specific inflammation marker; not part of routine CKD complication prevention.  </p></br><p>d. Serum ferritin concentration – Useful only AFTER anemia is documented to distinguish iron deficiency vs. anemia of CKD. You must first know the hemoglobin is low.  </p></br><p>e. Serum thyroid-stimulating hormone – Hypothyroidism can cause anemia, but diabetes-associated CKD patients are screened for anemia first; TSH isn’t routinely ordered to prevent CKD-specific complications.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: The earliest and most impactful screen for a modifiable CKD complication is a hemoglobin concentration to detect anemia due to reduced erythropoietin production.</p>
76. A previously healthy 42-year-old woman, gravida 3, para 3, comes to the physician because of a 3-month history of progressive urinary incontinence. She has loss of urine with coughing, sneezing, and exercising. Her last bowel movement was 3 days ago. She has no history of gynecologic illness. She has gastroesophageal reflux disease treated with omeprazole and hypertension treated with hydrochlorothiazide. Her children were born following uncomplicated vaginal deliveries. Her blood pressure is 120/70 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Pelvic examination shows bulging of the anterior vaginal wall. Her postvoid residual volume is 50 mL. Urinalysis shows no blood, WBCs, or nitrites. There is no loss of urine on cough stress test. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's loss of urine?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Bladder tumor</br>b. Constipation</br>c. Hydrochlorothiazide therapy</br>d. Overflow incontinence</br>e. Urethral hypermobility</br>f. Urinary tract infection</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Urethral hypermobility → stress urinary incontinence</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues (pertinent positives)</br>• “42-year-old woman, G3P3, progressive loss of urine with coughing, sneezing, exercising” = classic stress-incontinence story.<br /></br>• “Bulging of the anterior vaginal wall” = cystocele; physical marker of weakened pelvic floor → urethra loses its anatomic support.<br /></br>• Normal post-void residual (50 mL) rules-out overflow.<br /></br>• Normal urinalysis rules-out infection.<br /></br>• No urgency, frequency, dysuria → argues against urge incontinence.  </p></br><p>Pertinent negatives/traps<br /></br>• “No loss of urine on cough stress test” – the test has limited sensitivity if the bladder is not full or if the patient voluntarily contracts the pelvic floor. Do NOT let this single data point override the textbook pattern and the anatomic finding of a cystocele.  </p></br><p>Cognitive approach for future questions<br /></br>1. Classify the incontinence (stress, urge, overflow, functional).<br /></br>2. Use post-void residual and UA to exclude overflow and UTI.<br /></br>3. Look for anatomic findings (pelvic organ prolapse) that point to stress incontinence.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Stress incontinence = loss of urine with ↑intra-abdominal pressure (cough, laugh, sneeze, exercise).<br /></br>Mechanism: pelvic floor laxity → urethral hypermobility or intrinsic sphincter deficiency → bladder pressure briefly exceeds urethral pressure. Vaginal deliveries and multiparity are major risk factors.  </p></br><p>Why urethral hypermobility is correct here<br /></br>• Multiparity + cystocele = weakened endopelvic fascia &amp; pubocervical ligaments that normally keep the urethra closed.<br /></br>• Symptoms triggered exclusively by physical stress; no urgency.<br /></br>• Normal PVR confirms adequate emptying.  </p></br><p>Take-home: In a woman with a cystocele and purely stress-provoked leakage, urethral hypermobility is the cause.</p></br><p>Documentation (real patient)<br /></br>– Type and frequency of leakage, triggers, pad usage<br /></br>– Pelvic organ prolapse stage<br /></br>– Post-void residual<br /></br>– UA result<br /></br>– Impact on quality of life<br /></br>– Counseling about pelvic floor muscle therapy, pessary, surgical options  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Refresh  </p></br><p>Risk factors<br /></br>• Vaginal childbirth (stretching of the pubocervical fascia)<br /></br>• Menopause (estrogen deficiency → weaker connective tissue)<br /></br>• Obesity, chronic cough, heavy lifting  </p></br><p>Physiology<br /></br>When intra-abdominal pressure increases, the bladder and proximal urethra should be compressed equally within the pelvis. If the urethra descends below the pelvic floor (hypermobility), that pressure does not close the urethra → urine escapes.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics / Treatment Review  </p></br><p>Diagnostics (when needed)<br /></br>• Clinical diagnosis; urodynamic testing only if diagnosis unclear.  </p></br><p>First-line treatment<br /></br>1. Pelvic floor (Kegel) exercises – strengthen levator ani, improve urethral support.<br /></br>2. Intravaginal pessary – elevates bladder neck, esp. if prolapse present.  </p></br><p>Second-line / definitive<br /></br>• Mid-urethral sling (synthetic mesh or autologous fascia) – restores the “hammock” under the urethra.<br /></br>• Periurethral bulking agents for intrinsic sphincter deficiency.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Stress hits the TrUSS”:<br /></br>Trigger = physical STReSS<br /></br>Urethral hypermobility<br /></br>Sneeze/Squat/Skip → leak</p></br><p>Picture the urethra as a garden hose running across a trampoline (pelvic floor). If the trampoline sags (cystocele), every bounce (cough) makes water squirt out.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Rundown  </p></br><p>a. Bladder tumor<br /></br>• Presents with painless hematuria ± irritative voiding symptoms, not stress-triggered leakage; UA here is normal.  </p></br><p>b. Constipation<br /></br>• Can cause fecal impaction → overflow incontinence with high PVR, not stress leakage; PVR here is normal.  </p></br><p>c. Hydrochlorothiazide therapy<br /></br>• Causes polyuria, nocturia; leakage would be frequency/urgency-related, not solely with coughing. Removing the drug would not fix the anatomic prolapse.  </p></br><p>d. Overflow incontinence<br /></br>• Due to neurogenic bladder or obstruction; patients dribble continuously, have weak stream, and PVR &gt; 200 mL (not 50 mL).  </p></br><p>f. Urinary tract infection<br /></br>• Produces dysuria, urgency, frequency, positive leukocyte esterase/nitrites; UA here is negative.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>Stress incontinence in multiparous women with a cystocele is almost always due to urethral hypermobility from pelvic floor weakening; normal PVR and absence of urgency favor this diagnosis.</p>
77. A 32-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 1, at 38 weeks' gestation is admitted to the hospital in labor. Her pregnancy has been complicated by the diagnosis of HIV infection. Antiretroviral therapy with zidovudine (AZT), lamivudine (3TC), and nelfinavir was begun at 12 weeks' gestation. She has tolerated therapy well. Her most recent CD4+ T-lymphocyte count was 600/mm3 (Normal≥500), and plasma HIV viral load was 480 copies/mL. She wishes to attempt vaginal delivery. Which of the following is contraindicated with this patient's pregnancy?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Amnioinfusion</br>b. Bulb suctioning of the newborn</br>c. Determination of umbilical artery pH at delivery</br>d. Fetal scalp electrode placement</br>e. Intrauterine pressure catheter placement</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>d. Fetal scalp electrode placement  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – HIV-positive mother (vertical transmission is the issue).<br /></br>  – Undetectable/low viral load with ART → vaginal delivery is permissible.<br /></br>  – Question asks: “Which of the following is contraindicated with this patient’s pregnancy?” → look for an intrapartum maneuver that increases blood-to-blood contact between mother and fetus.  </p></br><p>• Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>  – Viral load &lt;1000 copies/mL → Cesarean not required solely for HIV (ACOG).<br /></br>  – Still must avoid intrapartum procedures that can abrade fetal skin or place fetal tissue in direct contact with maternal blood.  </p></br><p>Cognitive road map for similar questions<br /></br>1. Ask yourself: “Does this procedure break the fetal skin or prolong direct exposure to maternal blood?” If yes → contraindicated.<br /></br>2. Then screen the answer choices for the one (or more) that meet that criterion.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Fetal scalp electrodes (FSE) are applied by twisting a small cork-screw electrode into the fetal scalp to obtain continuous fetal ECG.<br /></br>• Mechanism of harm:<br /></br>  – Punctures fetal skin → creates a portal of entry for maternal blood/secretions.<br /></br>  – Significantly increases risk of vertical transmission of HIV, hepatitis B/C, and other blood-borne viruses.<br /></br>Core takeaway: In HIV-positive parturients, avoid any invasive fetal monitoring or diagnostic procedure that produces fetal abrasions—FSE and fetal scalp blood sampling are the classic NBME “never do” items.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl (real life): Record in the delivery note, “Invasive fetal monitoring (FSE/scalp sampling) avoided due to maternal HIV seropositivity.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Vertical (mother-to-child) HIV transmission occurs:<br /></br>• In utero (transplacental) – minor percentage.<br /></br>• Intrapartum – MAJORITY; fetal mucocutaneous exposure to maternal blood/vaginal secretions.<br /></br>• Post-partum – via breastfeeding.<br /></br>Risk is directly proportional to:<br /></br>1. Maternal viral load.<br /></br>2. Duration and intensity of exposure (prolonged ROM, traumatic delivery, invasive procedures).<br /></br>Skin breaks or mucosal breaches multiply the inoculum reaching the fetus/newborn.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatments (high-yield)<br /></br>Allowed/acceptable in well-treated, low-viral-load HIV pregnancies:<br /></br>• Spontaneous vaginal delivery.<br /></br>• Artificial rupture of membranes (only when obstetrically indicated and ideally close to delivery).<br /></br>• Intrauterine pressure catheter (IUPC) or amnioinfusion—because these do not abrade fetal skin.<br /></br>• Routine neonatal care (bulb suction, umbilical cord gases).<br /></br>Still required: intrapartum IV zidovudine if viral load unknown or &gt;1000; neonatal zidovudine for 4–6 weeks; no breastfeeding in the U.S.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“BREAK skin, RAISE risk” mnemonic:<br /></br>B – Blood sampling (scalp)<br /></br>R – Rotating screw electrode (FSE)<br /></br>E – Episiotomy/operative vacuum or forceps (if avoidable)<br /></br>A – Amniotomy early &amp; prolonged ROM<br /></br>K – Knife (cesarean) is actually protective if viral load high<br /></br>Any item that literally breaks the fetal skin (FSE, scalp sampling) is forbidden. IUPC and amnioinfusion slide past the skin, so they’re OK.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Amnioinfusion<br /></br>• Saline is infused through an IUPC to relieve cord compression. Catheter passes alongside fetus but does NOT penetrate fetal tissue. Acceptable.  </p></br><p>b. Bulb suctioning of the newborn<br /></br>• Performed after complete delivery; no added maternal blood exposure. Recommended to clear secretions.  </p></br><p>c. Determination of umbilical artery pH<br /></br>• Cord clamped and blood drawn after birth; no fetal skin breach. Safe.  </p></br><p>e. Intrauterine pressure catheter placement<br /></br>• Catheter sits between uterine wall and amniotic membranes; does not puncture fetal skin. Acceptable (though minimize manipulations).  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: thinking any internal catheter is automatically contraindicated. Only those that create fetal skin puncture (FSE, scalp blood sampling) clearly raise vertical transmission risk and are absolutely contraindicated.</p>
78. A 22-year-old primigravid woman at 38 weeks’ gestation comes to the physician as a new patient because she recently moved to this area. Pregnancy has been uncomplicated. Her only medication is a prenatal vitamin. Her father has factor VIII deficiency. The patient is concerned that her child may have hemophilia and bleeding complications at the time of delivery. Which of the following is the most appropriate physician recommendation?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Measurement of factor VIII concentration in the newborn at least 24 hours after delivery</br>b. Prophylactic maternal cryoprecipitate transfusion prior to delivery</br>c. Prophylactic maternal platelet transfusion prior to delivery</br>d. Scheduled cesarean delivery</br>e. Testing of cord blood for factor VIII concentration after delivery if the baby is a boy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>e. Testing of cord blood for factor VIII concentration after delivery if the baby is a boy</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – Father has factor VIII deficiency → classic X-linked recessive disorder (Hemophilia A).<br /></br>  – 22-y/o woman is a daughter of an affected male → she is an obligate carrier (XhX).<br /></br>  – Pregnancy currently at term with no complications and no maternal bleeding problems.</p></br><p>• Diagnostic reasoning<br /></br>  1. In X-linked recessive disease, affected fathers pass their mutant X to all daughters (carriers) and none to their sons.<br /></br>  2. A carrier mother will transmit the mutant X to 50 % of sons (affected) and 50 % of daughters (carriers).<br /></br>  3. The mother herself almost always has normal factor VIII levels, so the obstetric bleeding risk is chiefly for the neonate, not the mother.<br /></br>  4. The safest, least-invasive way to diagnose hemophilia immediately is to draw cord blood at delivery and assay factor VIII if the infant is male.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Anytime an X-linked recessive disorder appears, draw a quick Punnett square first → this tells you who is actually at risk and prevents over-treating the wrong patient.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-by-step approach:</br>1. Establish inheritance pattern (X-linked recessive).<br /></br>2. Decide who might be affected (male fetus).<br /></br>3. Consider timing and invasiveness of available tests.<br /></br>4. Choose the option that confirms disease in the neonate with no added maternal or fetal risk: cord-blood factor VIII assay in a male newborn.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: In pregnancies of known carriers for hemophilia A, routine obstetric care is unchanged; confirmatory testing is done on cord blood of male infants, and obstetric interventions (C-section, transfusions) are not indicated solely for hemophilia risk.</p></br><p>Documentation point: Delivery note should state, “Mother is obligate carrier of factor VIII deficiency; cord blood from male infant sent for factor VIII assay.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)</br>• Hemophilia A<br /></br>  – Defect: Factor VIII gene on X chromosome (Xq28).<br /></br>  – Inheritance: X-linked recessive.<br /></br>  – Clinical: Deep tissue bleeding, hemarthroses, intracranial hemorrhage in neonates.<br /></br>  – Labs: Prolonged aPTT, normal PT/bleeding time; ↓ factor VIII activity.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Highlights</br>1. Antenatal: Optional fetal sex determination (non-invasive cell-free DNA). No routine invasive procedures required.<br /></br>2. Delivery: Vaginal delivery is acceptable; avoid use of vacuum/forceps if possible.<br /></br>3. Neonate:<br /></br>   • Draw cord blood (already being clamped) → measure factor VIII.<br /></br>   • If deficient, give recombinant factor VIII to prevent bleeding.<br /></br>   • Avoid circumcision or IM injections until results known.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“X-tra risk only for X-tra Y (male)” → In X-linked recessive, only males are clinically affected; check the boy’s cord blood.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>a. Measurement of factor VIII ≥24 h after birth<br /></br>   • Delay misses potential early intracranial bleeding.<br /></br>   • Cord blood is readily available and reliable.  </p></br><p>b. Prophylactic maternal cryoprecipitate<br /></br>   • Cryoprecipitate supplies factor VIII but mother is not deficient; unnecessary exposure and cost.</p></br><p>c. Prophylactic maternal platelet transfusion<br /></br>   • Platelets provide vWF, not appreciable factor VIII. Maternal platelets are normal.</p></br><p>d. Scheduled cesarean delivery<br /></br>   • Elective C-section does NOT reduce hemorrhage risk in hemophilia compared with atraumatic vaginal delivery; adds maternal surgical risk.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>For a carrier mother, the standard of care is to obtain a cord-blood factor VIII level in a male newborn immediately after delivery. No maternal transfusions or automatic cesarean section are indicated purely for hemophilia risk.</p>
79. A 36-year-old woman comes to the clinic for a routine examination. She has hypertension treated with lisinopril. She has no other history of serious illness or operative procedures. She uses a copper IUD for contraception. She does not smoke cigarettes, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. Her maternal aunt was diagnosed with ovarian cancer at age 42 years. Her mother died in a motor vehicle collision at age 45 years. The patient’s vital signs are within normal limits. On examination, a 2-cm, fixed nontender mass is palpated in the upper outer quadrant of the right breast. Examination of the left breast shows no abnormalities. Findings on fine-needle biopsy of the mass are consistent with ductal carcinoma. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Bilateral mastectomy</br>b. Bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy</br>c. Genetic counseling for BRCA mutations</br>d. Pelvic ultrasonography and serum measurement of CA 125 concentration</br>e. Removal of the IUD</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Genetic counseling for BRCA mutations  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• Patient age 36 years with a new invasive ductal breast carcinoma → “early-onset” breast cancer (defined as &lt;50 y).<br /></br>• First-degree–equivalent relative (maternal aunt) with ovarian cancer at age 42 y → another “BRCA-associated” malignancy diagnosed at a young age.<br /></br>• No other risk-modifying history (eg, radiation, hormone therapy) that would fully explain the cancer.  </p></br><p>Pertinent positives = very young breast cancer + close relative with pre-menopausal ovarian cancer.<br /></br>Pertinent negatives = no smoking, no estrogen-containing contraception, no prior chest radiation, no known family history of BRCA testing → strengthens suspicion that a germ-line mutation may be present.  </p></br><p>Test-taking approach<br /></br>1. Identify whether the case meets NCCN/USPSTF criteria for hereditary breast-ovarian cancer (HBOC) evaluation.<br /></br>2. If criteria are met, the correct NEXT step is not prophylactic surgery but referral for genetic counseling and possible testing.<br /></br>3. Only after a pathogenic mutation is documented (and the patient is counseled) are risk-reducing surgeries considered.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why genetic counseling now?<br /></br>• HBOC due to BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutations accounts for ≈5–10 % of all breast cancers.<br /></br>• Indications for referral/testing:<br /></br>  – Breast cancer diagnosed &lt;50 y<br /></br>  – Any ovarian, fallopian tube, or primary peritoneal cancer in a patient or close relative<br /></br>  – Multiple HBOC-related cancers in the same patient or the family<br /></br>• Genetic counseling ensures informed consent, accurate interpretation of results, and discussion of implications for the patient and her relatives.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: When a patient’s personal or family history meets criteria for a hereditary breast/ovarian cancer syndrome, the FIRST management step is genetic counseling ± testing—not immediate prophylactic surgery.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls<br /></br>• Record personal/family cancer history, age at diagnosis, and degree of relatedness.<br /></br>• Note that counseling is recommended and that patient agrees to referral.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (BRCA-related HBOC)  </p></br><p>Etiology: Germ-line loss-of-function mutation in BRCA1 (chr17) or BRCA2 (chr13) tumor-suppressor genes → defective homologous DNA repair → ↑ accumulation of DNA damage.  </p></br><p>Lifetime cancer risks if BRCA1/2 positive<br /></br>• Breast: 45–70 %<br /></br>• Ovarian/fallopian tube: 10–45 % (higher in BRCA1)<br /></br>• Also prostate, pancreatic, melanoma (esp. BRCA2).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Framework After Counseling  </p></br><ol></br><li>Germ-line testing (multigene panel recommended).  </li></br><li>If mutation found:<br /></br>   • Breast: annual MRI starting age 25 and mammography starting 30 y OR prophylactic bilateral mastectomy.<br /></br>   • Ovary/Tubes: risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy ideally at 35–40 y (BRCA1) or 40–45 y (BRCA2).<br /></br>   • Consider chemoprevention (tamoxifen) if estrogen-receptor positive.  </li></br><li>Family cascade testing.  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“BROC-A before you cut-A”<br /></br>BROC = BRCA-Related Oncology Counseling → always comes BEFORE you CUT (surgery).  </p></br><p>Use the 50-50 rule: Breast CA &lt;50 or ovarian CA at any age in a 1st/2nd-degree relative → 50 % chance guidelines trigger genetic eval.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Bilateral mastectomy<br /></br>• May eventually be recommended if BRCA positive OR if patient chooses after counseling, but immediate contralateral prophylactic mastectomy without genetic confirmation is premature. Surgical morbidity without proven benefit yet.</p></br><p>b. Bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy<br /></br>• Same issue—risk-reducing BSO is standard for confirmed BRCA carriers after childbearing. Not the first step; no proven mutation and patient still 36 y.</p></br><p>d. Pelvic ultrasonography + CA-125<br /></br>• Screening for ovarian cancer with US/CA-125 has poor sensitivity/specificity and is NOT recommended even in high-risk women. Risk-reducing surgery is preferred after mutation confirmation.</p></br><p>e. Removal of the IUD<br /></br>• Copper IUD is non-hormonal and not linked to breast cancer risk; no indication for removal.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Young breast cancer PLUS close relative with early-onset ovarian cancer fulfills criteria for hereditary breast-ovarian cancer evaluation. The correct next step is referral for genetic counseling and BRCA testing—surgery or screening strategies follow only after the genetic risk is clarified.</p>
80. A 27-year-old woman is evaluated in the hospital 36 hours after undergoing cesarean delivery at term because of a prolonged first stage of labor and rupture of membranes. She currently reports abdominal pain and breast tenderness. Current medications are oxycodone and acetaminophen. Her temperature is 38.9 C (102 F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 110/60 mm Hg. Examination shows mild breast tenderness and induration. The incision site is clean and not erythematous. The uterus is diffusely tender to mild palpation. A urine sample shows multiple RBCs. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Observation only</br>b. Breast binding</br>c. Incentive spirometry</br>d. Endometrial culture</br>e. Antibiotic therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Antibiotic therapy (broad-spectrum IV coverage, classically clindamycin + gentamicin)</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Timing: 36 hr postpartum (early) after an intrapartum cesarean section performed for prolonged labor and ruptured membranes → very high risk for postpartum endometritis.<br /></br>• Fever 38.9 °C + uterine (fundal) tenderness = classic diagnostic dyad.<br /></br>• Incision clean → rules out wound infection.<br /></br>• Mild breast tenderness, but engorgement begins 2-3 days postpartum and rarely causes fever this high this early.<br /></br>• A few RBCs in urine are common after Foley catheterization and do not explain uterine tenderness.<br /></br>Key cognitive cue: “womb” (endometritis) is the single most common cause of fever within 24–72 h after cesarean delivery, especially when membranes were ruptured and labor was prolonged.</p></br><p>Approach for similar questions<br /></br>1. Draw up the postpartum fever differential (“5 W’s + Womb” or “7 B’s”: wind, water, wound, walk, wonder drugs, breasts, womb).<br /></br>2. Match each to the typical postoperative day it appears and the associated focal tenderness/ findings.<br /></br>3. When uterine tenderness is present within 2–3 d of cesarean delivery, jump to endometritis and treat empirically; cultures are not required.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>1. Does she truly have a postpartum fever? Yes (T &gt;38 °C on one reading postpartum).<br /></br>2. Localize the source: Breast? Incision? Urinary tract? Lungs? Uterus?<br /></br>   – Breasts: only mild engorgement; no erythema/fluctuance.<br /></br>   – Incision: “clean and not erythematous.”<br /></br>   – Lungs: vitals stable; no pulmonary signs.<br /></br>   – Urine: only RBCs, no dysuria or suprapubic pain.<br /></br>   – Uterus: “diffusely tender” → most compelling finding.<br /></br>3. Diagnose postpartum endometritis.<br /></br>4. Immediate empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics are indicated because infection is polymicrobial (aerobes, anaerobes, gram-negatives, group B Strep). Classic regimen: clindamycin (anaerobes + gram-pos) plus gentamicin (gram-neg).</p></br><p>Take-home message: Early postpartum fever + uterine tenderness after cesarean = treat right away with clindamycin/gentamicin; do NOT wait for cultures.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): Record fever curve, uterine fundal tenderness, lochia character, prior intrapartum risk factors, antibiotic regimen, patient counseling.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Ascending polymicrobial infection of the decidua and myometrium.<br /></br>• Risk factors: cesarean after labor, prolonged ROM (&gt;18 h), multiple vaginal exams, internal monitors, chorioamnionitis.<br /></br>• Clinical triad: high fever, uterine tenderness, foul-smelling lochia (may or may not be noted on exam).</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>• First-line: IV clindamycin + gentamicin until afebrile for 24 h.<br /></br>  – Alternatives if intolerant: ampicillin-sulbactam, piperacillin-tazobactam, or a 3rd-gen cephalosporin + metronidazole.<br /></br>• No imaging or cultures required prior to therapy; blood cultures optional if severe sepsis.<br /></br>• Add ampicillin if Enterococcus coverage needed (e.g., persistent fever &gt;48 h).<br /></br>• Prevention: single peri-incisional IV dose of a 1st-gen cephalosporin at skin incision (already standard of care).</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>Mnemonic “C-G to Save the Womb”:<br /></br>C = Clindamycin, G = Gentamicin → mainstay combo whenever the uterus (“womb”) is the source of a postpartum infection.<br /></br>Timeline mnemonic “1-2-3-W”: within 1–3 days postpartum think Womb.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Observation only<br /></br>• Fever + uterine tenderness mandates treatment; risk of septic shock if untreated.</p></br><p>B. Breast binding<br /></br>• Used for symptomatic relief of severe engorgement in women who choose not to breast-feed. Does not treat infection; engorgement alone rarely causes ≥39 °C fever and would not explain uterine tenderness.</p></br><p>C. Incentive spirometry<br /></br>• Atelectasis presents POD 1–2 with mild fever but no focal abdominal tenderness; lungs, not uterus, hurt on deep inspiration.</p></br><p>D. Endometrial culture<br /></br>• Endometritis is polymicrobial; cultures yield little additional management information and should not delay empiric therapy.</p></br><p>E. Antibiotic therapy (correct)<br /></br>• Directly addresses the most likely and potentially serious cause of this patient’s postpartum fever.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>Within 48 h of cesarean delivery, the leading cause of fever is polymicrobial postpartum endometritis. Fever + uterine tenderness = start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (clindamycin + gentamicin) immediately; cultures are unnecessary.</p>
81. A 51-year-old woman, gravida 1, para 1, comes to the office for an annual health maintenance examination. She says she has been generally healthy and feels well but is concerned about her risk for ovarian cancer because her friend of the same age recently was diagnosed with such cancer. Medical history is significant for an uncomplicated pregnancy and vaginal delivery. She breast-fed her child until he was age 6 months. Her only medications are a calcium supplement and daily multivitamin. She took an oral contraceptive for 15 years and discontinued the therapy 1 year ago; her menses did not resume after discontinuing the therapy. She has not taken hormone replacement therapy. She exercises regularly and does not smoke cigarettes. She has no known family history of cancer. All Pap smears in the past have shown no abnormalities. She is 173 cm (5 ft 8 in) tall and weighs 63 kg (139 lb); BMI is 21 kg/m^2. Vital signs are normal. Physical examination, including pelvic examination, discloses no abnormalities. Which of the following factors in the patient’s history is most strongly associated with a decreased risk for ovarian cancer?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Absence of family history of ovarian cancer</br>b. BMI</br>c. Breast-feeding history</br>d. Negative smoking history</br>e. Oral contraceptive use</br>f. Postmenopausal status</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Oral contraceptive use</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Major clue: “She took an oral contraceptive for 15 years.”<br /></br>2. Question stem: “Which factor … is MOST strongly associated with a decreased risk for ovarian cancer?” – The test-maker wants the single strongest protective factor present in her history.<br /></br>3. High-yield concept: Anything that suppresses ovulation (OCPs, pregnancy, breast-feeding, tubal ligation, salpingo-oophorectomy) lowers epithelial ovarian cancer risk. The longer the anovulatory state, the greater the protection.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Any time the NBME asks about ovarian-cancer risk, think “Ovulation count.” More ovulations = ↑ risk; fewer ovulations = ↓ risk. Then scan the choices for the item that best fits “fewer lifetime ovulations.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. Identify modifiable vs non-modifiable factors.<br /></br>2. Recall magnitude of effect: OCPs confer ≈35–50 % risk reduction after ≥5 years of use, and protection persists for up to 20 years after discontinuation.<br /></br>3. Compare that magnitude with other protective factors given.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Prolonged combined oral contraceptive pill (COCP) use is the single most powerful non-surgical protective factor for epithelial ovarian cancer because it halts monthly ovulation and therefore repetitive surface-epithelium disruption.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Most ovarian malignancies arise from surface (serous) epithelium.<br /></br>• Each ovulation → rupture &amp; repair of surface → DNA damage &amp; potential malignant transformation (“incessant ovulation” hypothesis).<br /></br>• COCPs provide exogenous estrogen + progestin that suppress the LH surge, preventing ovulation; fewer rupture-repair cycles → ↓ cumulative DNA damage → ↓ cancer risk.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls (context)<br /></br>• No effective screening test exists for average-risk women (CA-125 &amp; TVUS are NOT recommended).<br /></br>• Risk reduction strategies: prolonged OCP use, multiparity, breast-feeding, tubal ligation, risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy in high-risk (BRCA) women.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“O-C-P = Ovarian Cancer Protection” – Every 5 years of continuous COCP use cuts risk by roughly one-third.<br /></br>Think “Ovulation Calendar”:<br /></br>– Cross out ovulatory months (pregnancy, lactation, OCP). The more X’s, the safer the ovary.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Absence of family history of ovarian cancer<br /></br>• Not having BRCA or Lynch mutations lowers <em>baseline</em> risk, but it is not an <em>active</em> protective factor and its magnitude is far smaller than the 35–50 % reduction conferred by OCPs.</p></br><p>b. Low BMI (21 kg/m²)<br /></br>• Obesity is a mild risk factor, but normal BMI offers only a modest benefit, again less than OCP usage.</p></br><p>c. Breast-feeding history<br /></br>• Lactation suppresses ovulation and is protective, but 6 months of breast-feeding provides far less cumulative anovulatory time than 15 years of OCPs.</p></br><p>d. Negative smoking history<br /></br>• Smoking is inconsistently associated with mucinous ovarian tumors; its absence is not nearly as protective as OCP use.</p></br><p>f. Postmenopausal status<br /></br>• Menopause ends ovulation, but by definition it occurs after 45–55 years of cumulative cycles. It does not retroactively erase prior exposure; therefore, the protection gained is less significant than long-term OCP suppression during peak reproductive years.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Long-term oral contraceptive use is the strongest history-based protective factor against epithelial ovarian cancer because it markedly reduces the total number of lifetime ovulations.</p>
82. A 17-year-old boy is brought to the physician for an examination prior to participation in sports. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He was diagnosed with intellectual developmental disorder at the age of 5 years. He is 165 cm (5 ft 5 in) tall and weighs 50 kg (110 lb); BMI is 18 kg/m2. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows minimal facial hair. There is mild bilateral gynecomastia. The testes are Tanner stage 1. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's gynecomastia?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Congenital adrenal hyperplasia</br>b. Constitutional delay of puberty</br>c. Incomplete androgen insensitivity syndrome</br>d. Seminiferous tubule dysgenesis (Klinefelter) syndrome</br>e. Normal variant of puberty</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Seminiferous tubule dysgenesis (Klinefelter) syndrome  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that narrow the diagnosis:<br /></br>• 17-year-old male who has not entered puberty (Tanner stage 1 testes, minimal facial hair).<br /></br>• Mild bilateral gynecomastia.<br /></br>• Long-standing intellectual disability (occurs in ~30-40 % of Klinefelter patients).<br /></br>• No systemic illness or medications that could explain low androgen levels.  </p></br><p>Pertinent positives → primary hypogonadism pattern (small firm testes, eunuchoid habitus, gynecomastia).<br /></br>Pertinent negatives → no precocious puberty, no virilization, no ambiguous genitalia, no systemic disease.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Ask “Is estrogen high or androgen low?” Gynecomastia in an adolescent male with pre-pubertal testes = androgen deficiency.<br /></br>2. Differentiate central (constitutional delay) vs primary testicular failure (Klinefelter). Small testes in a 17-year-old → testicular failure.<br /></br>3. Match classic syndrome: intellectual disability + small testes + gynecomastia = 47,XXY.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Klinefelter syndrome = seminiferous tubule dysgenesis →<br /></br>• Degeneration/fibrosis of seminiferous tubules → ↓ inhibin B → ↑ FSH.<br /></br>• Leydig cell dysfunction → ↓ testosterone → ↑ LH → ↑ estrogen (via peripheral aromatization).<br /></br>• Result: Tall, long limbs; small, firm testes; sparse body/facial hair; gynecomastia; infertility.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: In a post-pubertal age male who never progressed beyond Tanner stage 1 and develops gynecomastia, think primary testicular failure—most commonly Klinefelter (47,XXY).  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Record testicular volume (&lt;4 mL), Tanner staging, and consider karyotype/LH-FSH-testosterone panel.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Genetics: 47,XXY (nondisjunction—meiotic error ↑ with maternal age)<br /></br>Endocrine profile:<br /></br>• ↓ Testosterone<br /></br>• ↓ Inhibin B<br /></br>• ↑ FSH &amp; LH → ↑ Estrogen/Testosterone ratio → gynecomastia<br /></br>Clinical spectrum: Learning disability → frank intellectual disability; infertility (azoospermia), cryptorchidism, osteoporosis risk.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><p>Diagnostics:<br /></br>• Serum testosterone, LH, FSH (primary hypogonadism pattern).<br /></br>• Karyotype confirmation (47,XXY).<br /></br>Management:<br /></br>• Lifelong testosterone replacement (improves virilization, muscle mass, bone density).<br /></br>• Psychological/educational support; fertility counseling (testicular sperm extraction + ICSI in some).<br /></br>• Breast cancer screening if significant gynecomastia.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“XXY gives an extra X-tra chest”: Extra X chromosome → estrogen excess → gynecomastia.<br /></br>“Small testes, tall man, learning plan = Klinefelter.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH): Causes androgen excess (virilization), not deficiency. Would see precocious puberty or ambiguous genitalia, not small testes with gynecomastia.  </p></br><p>b. Constitutional delay of puberty: Common, but testicular volume is prepubertal without gynecomastia (estrogen levels low). Normal eventual puberty; no intellectual disability.  </p></br><p>c. Incomplete androgen insensitivity syndrome: 46,XY with partial receptor defect → ambiguous or undervirilized external genitalia, often undescended testes; may have gynecomastia but genital ambiguity is expected, and testes are usually palpable in the inguinal canal, not uniformly small/fibrotic.  </p></br><p>e. Normal variant of puberty (physiologic adolescent gynecomastia): Occurs during Tanner stages 2-4 when testes have already enlarged; transient; patient is Tanner 1 at age 17, making a normal variant unlikely.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line:<br /></br>A 17-year-old male who never entered puberty yet develops gynecomastia has primary testicular failure—classic for Klinefelter syndrome (seminiferous tubule dysgenesis).</p>
83. A 16-year-old boy with type 1 diabetes mellitus is brought to the emergency department by his parents because of a 1-hour history of vomiting. The parents say he was at an overnight camp during the past week. When he returned 12 hours ago, he was not as active as usual and said he was tired. The patient told his parents that he had taken his insulin while at camp, but when they checked his bag, his supply of insulin indicated that he had not been taking it regularly. On arrival, he appears ill and drowsy but fully oriented. There is a sweet odor to his breath. His temperature is 37 C (98.6 F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 26/min and deep, and blood pressure is 115/60 mm Hg. Examination shows dry mucous membranes. No other abnormalities are noted. Laboratory studies show:</br>Fingerstick blood glucose    >600 mg/dL</br>Serum HCO3-                  10 mEq/L</br></br>Arterial blood gas analysis on room air shows:</br>pH    7.13</br>PCO2  32 mm Hg</br>PO2   95 mm Hg</br></br>Administration of which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Intramuscular promethazine</br>b. Intravenous bicarbonate</br>c. Intravenous insulin</br>d. Intravenous 0.9 percent saline bolus</br>e. Subcutaneous insulin</br>f. Sublingual ondansetron</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Intravenous 0.9 percent saline bolus</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Approach<br /></br>1. Key clues<br /></br>   • Type 1 diabetes + missed insulin at camp<br /></br>   • Fruity (acetone) breath, deep tachypneic respirations (Kussmaul)<br /></br>   • Glucose &gt; 600 mg/dL, HCO₃⁻ 10 mEq/L, pH 7.13 → high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis<br /></br>   • Tachycardia, dry mucous membranes → marked volume depletion from osmotic diuresis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Pattern recognition<br /></br>   “Sick-appearing T1DM + dehydration + acidemia + ketone breath” = classic diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA).</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Metacognitive tip<br /></br>   For any DKA/HHS stem, immediately run the “F I K” checklist in order:<br /></br>   Fluids → Insulin (IV) → K⁺ repletion (± other fixes).  Replace volume first; insulin always comes after initial isotonic crystalloid and confirmation that K⁺ &gt; 3.3 mEq/L.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Severe osmotic diuresis has depleted 3–6 L of water and up to 1000 mEq of sodium.<br /></br>• Restoring intravascular volume with isotonic crystalloid (0.9 % saline) improves tissue perfusion, lowers stress-hormone surge, and by itself reduces glucose 50–100 mg/dL/h.<br /></br>• Giving insulin before fluid resuscitation can precipitate vascular collapse and severe hypokalemia because insulin drives water and K⁺ into cells.<br /></br>Therefore, the very first order in the ED is a rapid IV bolus of normal saline (typically 10–20 mL/kg over the first hour).</p></br><p>Take-home: In DKA, “FLUIDS FIRST.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Snapshot<br /></br>• Absolute insulin deficiency → uninhibited hormone-sensitive lipase → ↑ free fatty acids → hepatic ketogenesis (β-hydroxybutyrate &amp; acetoacetate) → metabolic acidosis.<br /></br>• Hyperglycemia + ketonuria → osmotic diuresis → dehydration, electrolyte loss, and compensatory respiratory alkalosis (Kussmaul breathing).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Framework<br /></br>Immediate ED steps (in order):<br /></br>1. Isotonic saline bolus (0.9 % NaCl).<br /></br>2. Once serum K⁺ is verified:<br /></br>   • If K⁺ &lt; 3.3 → hold insulin, give K⁺.<br /></br>   • If K⁺ 3.3–5.2 → start IV regular insulin + K⁺ supplementation.<br /></br>   • If K⁺ &gt; 5.2 → start insulin, monitor K⁺ hourly.<br /></br>3. When glucose &lt; 200–250 mg/dL, switch to D5-½NS and continue insulin till anion gap closes.<br /></br>4. Give bicarbonate only if pH &lt; 6.9.<br /></br>5. Identify/ treat precipitating cause (infection, MI, etc.).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>Mnemonic “FIKR” (pronounced “FIK-er”):<br /></br>F – Fluids (NS)<br /></br>I – Insulin (IV)<br /></br>K – K⁺ repletion<br /></br>R – Resolution of precipitating factor / Reassess gap  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong<br /></br>a. IM promethazine – Symptomatic antiemetic; does nothing for life-threatening dehydration and acidosis.<br /></br>b. IV bicarbonate – Reserved for severe acidosis (pH &lt; 6.9). At pH 7.13 it offers no mortality benefit and may worsen intracellular acidosis.<br /></br>c. IV insulin – Essential, but not until after initial fluid resuscitation and K⁺ assessment.<br /></br>e. SC insulin – Absorption unreliable in hypoperfusion; IV route is standard of care.<br /></br>f. SL ondansetron – Like promethazine, treats nausea only; does not address hemodynamic instability.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Exam Pearl<br /></br>Any question on DKA/HHS: if you see profound dehydration → choose isotonic IV fluids first, unless the stem explicitly states fluids have already been given.</p>
84. A 32-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-week history of double vision and dry, red eyes. She also has had a 4.5-kg (10-lb) weight loss and increased fatigability during this time. Her only medication is oral contraceptive. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 50 kg (110 lb); BMI is 19 kg/m2. Her temperature is 37.2 C (99 F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 135/60 mm Hg. Examination shows prominent-appearing eyes and bilateral scleral injection. She has limited upward gaze, and she reports severe diplopia when she looks up; the eyes open wider on downward gaze. Visual acuity is 20/40 in the left eye and 20/30 in the right. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Schirmer test</br>b. Acetylcholine receptor antibody titers</br>c. Measurement of serum angiotensin-converting enzyme activity</br>d. Measurement of serum glucose concentration</br>e. Thyroid function testing</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Thyroid function testing  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• 2-week history of diplopia + dry, red “prominent-appearing” eyes → think orbital process.<br /></br>• Bilateral scleral injection, lid retraction (“eyes open wider on downward gaze”), limited up-gaze with diplopia → classic for Graves ophthalmopathy (extra-ocular muscle inflammation/fibrosis, especially inferior rectus).<br /></br>• Systemic hyperadrenergic signs: 4.5-kg weight loss, tachycardia (HR 100), fatigue, low BMI.<br /></br>• Female in her 30s (epidemiologic peak for Graves disease).  </p></br><p>Pertinent negatives that narrow the differential: no ptosis that worsens with fatigue (myasthenia); no painful ophthalmoplegia or acute nerve palsy (diabetes, sarcoid); no parotid enlargement or arthritis (Sjӧgren).  </p></br><p>Strategy for future questions:<br /></br>1. Identify the eye findings (exophthalmos, lid lag/retraction, restrictive gaze pattern).<br /></br>2. Scan for systemic hyperthyroid symptoms.<br /></br>3. Link them → Graves ophthalmopathy → order thyroid function tests first.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. Suspect Graves disease based on ocular findings + hyperthyroid symptoms.<br /></br>2. The initial diagnostic confirmation of hyperthyroidism is a serum thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) with free T4 ± total T3.<br /></br>3. If hyperthyroidism is present, reflex tests (thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulin, radioactive iodine uptake) and ocular staging follow later.<br /></br>Take-home message: New-onset exophthalmos with systemic hyperthyroid features → start with thyroid function testing.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): document visual acuity, motility restriction, degree of proptosis, systemic hyperthyroid symptoms, and plan for thyroid panel.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Graves ophthalmopathy results from autoimmune stimulation of orbital fibroblasts via T-cell–mediated reaction against TSH receptor → glycosaminoglycan deposition, edema, and fibrosis of extra-ocular muscles. Restrictive myopathy (esp. inferior rectus) produces vertical diplopia; proptosis causes exposure keratopathy (dry, red eyes).  </p></br><p>Risk factors: smoking, female sex, personal/family autoimmune disease.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><p>Initial tests: TSH (will be suppressed) + free T4 (elevated). Further: TSI antibody or radioactive iodine uptake scan (diffuse uptake).  </p></br><p>Management overview:<br /></br>• Control thyroid state (antithyroid drugs: methimazole or PTU; radioactive iodine; surgery).<br /></br>• Ocular measures: artificial tears, glucocorticoids for moderate–severe disease, orbital decompression in sight-threatening cases.<br /></br>• Smoking cessation (major prognostic factor).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“GET my EYES”: Graves = Exophthalmos &amp; lid lag, Tachycardia, weight loss → order Thyroid tests.  </p></br><p>Picture: a gravesite (Graves) with two big cartoon eyeballs popping out while a tachy metronome ticks—reminds you to check thyroid labs.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Schirmer test – evaluates tear production in suspected Sjögren syndrome. Sjögren presents with chronic dry eyes/mouth, arthritis, parotid enlargement—not acute diplopia or exophthalmos.  </p></br><p>b. Acetylcholine receptor antibody titers – for myasthenia gravis. Ocular MG causes fluctuating ptosis and diplopia that worsen with fatigue; there is no proptosis, conjunctival injection, or lid retraction.  </p></br><p>c. Serum ACE level – screens for sarcoidosis; ocular sarcoid typically causes uveitis, lacrimal gland enlargement, or optic nerve involvement, not exophthalmos + thyroid-like systemic symptoms.  </p></br><p>d. Serum glucose – diabetic ophthalmoplegia usually presents with isolated CN III palsy (ptosis, “down and out” eye, pupil spared) or diplopia from CN VI palsy, not bilateral exophthalmos or systemic weight loss.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core takeaway: Diplopia + exophthalmos + hyperthyroid symptoms in a young woman = Graves ophthalmopathy; first diagnostic step is thyroid function testing (TSH &amp; free T4).</p>
85. A 42-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after her husband found her unconscious in her bed. She has a 6-year history of systemic lupus erythematosus treated with prednisone. Her husband says she has been on a drinking binge for the past 7 days and has not been eating. Three days ago, she had the onset of rhinitis, conjunctivitis, and a nonproductuve cough. She has a long-standing history of alcoholism. She moans in response to painful stimuli. Her temperature is 38.9 C (102 F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 22/min, and palpable systolic blood pressure is 80 mm Hg. The lungs are clear to auscultation and percussion. Abdominal examination shows no abnormalities. Fluid resuscitation is begun. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Serum antinuclear antibody assay</br>b. Corticosteroid therapy</br>c. Cyclosporine therapy</br>d. Ganciclovir therapy</br>e. Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Corticosteroid therapy (IV “stress-dose” hydrocortisone)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that should immediately jump out:</p></br><ol></br><li>Chronic exogenous steroids (6-year history of prednisone for SLE) → chronic suppression of the hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal (HPA) axis.  </li></br><li>Acute physiologic stressors:<br /></br>   • Febrile viral syndrome for 3 days (now 38.9 °C)<br /></br>   • 7-day alcohol binge with little oral intake (hypoglycemia risk)<br /></br>   • Now profoundly hypotensive (SBP 80 mm Hg) and altered (moans only to pain).  </li></br><li>No focal cardiopulmonary or abdominal cause is identified; hypotension persists despite IV fluids just started.</li></br></ol></br><p>Pattern recognition: “Patient on long-term steroids + acute stress + refractory hypotension/AMS ⇒ think acute adrenal (Addisonian) crisis.”</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>• Any time you see chronic steroid use, ask: “Could this presentation be adrenal insufficiency?”<br /></br>• In emergency medicine questions: If adrenal crisis is plausible, immediate IV hydrocortisone is life-saving and comes before confirmatory tests.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why B is correct)</p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:</p></br><ol></br><li>Chronic prednisone → secondary (central) adrenal insufficiency; baseline cortisol production is suppressed.  </li></br><li>Acute illness/surgery/sepsis requires a 3- to 5-fold increase in cortisol output. This patient cannot mount that response.  </li></br><li>Manifestations: fever, vomiting or poor PO intake, hypotension unresponsive to fluids, altered mental status, possible hypoglycemia and hyponatremia (labs often delayed).  </li></br><li>First-line, empiric, life-saving therapy = IV stress-dose corticosteroids (e.g., 100 mg hydrocortisone IV bolus, then 50 mg q6 h) while continuing aggressive fluids ± dextrose.</li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home: In any chronically steroid-treated patient with shock of unclear etiology, administer IV steroids immediately; do not delay for testing.</p></br><p>Documentation if real patient: “Suspected adrenal crisis due to chronic steroid therapy; administered hydrocortisone 100 mg IV bolus, plan q6 h, monitor vitals, glucose, electrolytes.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</p></br><p>• Exogenous glucocorticoids suppress CRH and ACTH release → adrenal glands atrophy over months.<br /></br>• Abrupt cessation OR inadequate dose during stress → acute cortisol deficiency.<br /></br>• Cortisol maintains vascular tone (permissive for catecholamines) and gluconeogenesis. Deficiency → vasodilatory shock, hyponatremia, hypoglycemia, fever.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment Pearls</p></br><p>Diagnostics (after stabilization): serum cortisol, ACTH level, BMP, glucose. But never delay therapy.</p></br><p>Treatment algorithm:</br>1. ABCs + large-bore IV, isotonic fluids with dextrose.<br /></br>2. Empiric IV hydrocortisone (or dexamethasone if planning ACTH stim test).<br /></br>3. Broad-spectrum antibiotics if sepsis suspected (after cultures).<br /></br>4. Treat precipitating illness, electrolyte/glucose monitoring.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</p></br><p>“Stress? Give Steroids.”<br /></br>Mnemonic: “ACES” for adrenal crisis warning signs in steroid users:<br /></br>A – Altered mental status<br /></br>C – Collapse (hypotension)<br /></br>E – Electrolyte issues (↓Na⁺, ↑K⁺)<br /></br>S – Stress event (infection, surgery)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong</p></br><p>A. Serum antinuclear antibody assay<br /></br>• Diagnostic, not therapeutic; already has confirmed SLE; does nothing acutely for shock.</p></br><p>C. Cyclosporine therapy<br /></br>• Immunosuppressant used for transplant, nephrotic syndrome, some SLE cases; would worsen infection risk and not treat adrenal crisis.</p></br><p>D. Ganciclovir therapy<br /></br>• Antiviral for CMV; no evidence of CMV infection; does not address hypotension.</p></br><p>E. Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) therapy<br /></br>• Used for mild SLE flares or pain; contraindicated in hypotension/possible renal hypoperfusion and offers no benefit in adrenal crisis.</p></br><p>Common test-writing trap: students see “SLE flare” and reflexively pick immunosuppression or NSAIDs; but vital signs scream SHOCK – think cortisol first.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Core Takeaway</p></br><p>In any chronically steroid-dependent patient who becomes acutely ill and hypotensive, the most appropriate immediate management is IV stress-dose corticosteroids to treat presumed adrenal crisis; delay in administration can be fatal.</p>
86. A 3-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department after her mother found her in the kitchen next to an open bottle of drain cleaner that had been stored under the sink. The bottle was still almost full, but some of its contents were spilled on the patient's clothes and on the floor. On arrival, she is alert, crying, and drooling but in no respiratory distress. Her pulse is 120/min, respirations are 30/min, and blood pressure is 95/70 mm Hg. The mouth and oral mucosa is bright red, but there are no ulcers in the oropharynx. The lungs are clear to auscultation. Abdominal examination shows no abnormalities. The extremities are well perfused. An x-ray of the chest shows clear lung fields and normal cardiac silhouette and mediastinum. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Barium swallow</br>b. Administration of activated charcoal</br>c. Administration of ipecac</br>d. Esophagoscopy</br>e. Gastric lavage by nasogastric tube</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Esophagoscopy  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• 3-year-old + opened drain cleaner (strong alkali) → high-risk caustic ingestion<br /></br>• Drooling &amp; erythematous oral mucosa = symptomatic mucosal injury<br /></br>• No stridor, wheeze, or mediastinal widening → airway stable, no obvious perforation<br /></br>• Normal chest x-ray rules out free air/perforation for now  </p></br><p>NBME/USMLE pattern<br /></br>Step-wise approach to caustic ingestion:<br /></br>1. ABCs ± decontaminate skin/clothes (already done)<br /></br>2. CXR/AXR if severe chest/abd pain; here CXR is normal<br /></br>3.
