no treatment is needed other than reassurance and monitoring growth velocity in the chart.</p></br><p>Key takeaway: “Short child + delayed bone age + delayed puberty + otherwise healthy = constitutional growth delay.”</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Record growth percentiles, calculated bone age, Tanner staging, and parental heights; plan for follow-up growth charting and anticipatory guidance.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Etiology: Not pathologic; simply slower tempo of skeletal maturation and hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis activation.<br /></br>• Risk: Often familial—parents report their own late puberty spurts.<br /></br>• Outcome: Final adult height appropriate for genetics once puberty occurs; rapid catch-up during late adolescent growth spurt.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment / Diagnostic Tests</br>• No pharmacologic therapy required.<br /></br>• Reassurance + annual height/weight plotting.<br /></br>• Endocrine work-up only if growth velocity falls or pubertal delay persists beyond expected limits (boys &gt;14 y, girls &gt;13 y).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“CONSTITUTIONAL”:<br /></br>C – Catch-up later<br /></br>O – Otherwise healthy<br /></br>N – Normal labs<br /></br>S – Skeletal age delayed<br /></br>T – Tanner delay  </p></br><p>Or use the image of a “late-bloomer flower” that buds after the others but reaches the same height.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices are Wrong</br>B. Familial short stature<br /></br>   • Bone age equals chronological age; puberty on time. Parents usually short. Not the case here (average-height parents, delayed bone age).</p></br><p>C. Fetal growth restriction<br /></br>   • Starts prenatal; birth weight/length &lt;3rd %ile and tracking low since birth. Bone age ≈ chronologic. This vignette gives no prenatal small-for-gestational-age history.</p></br><p>D. Growth hormone deficiency<br /></br>   • Marked height &lt;3rd %ile, often ↑weight-for-height, cherubic facies, midline defects, low IGF-1, profoundly delayed bone age. Patient’s proportionate stature and athletic activity argue against.</p></br><p>E. Nutritional deficiency<br /></br>   • Would show weight falling off before or more than height, possible micronutrient signs (hair loss, pallor, glossitis). Here weight and BMI are normal, diet issues not suggested.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>A healthy, proportionately small 12-year-old with delayed bone age and delayed pubertal development nearly always represents constitutional growth delay—expect a late adolescent growth spurt and normal adult stature.</p>
102. A 10-year-old boy is brought to the office by his mother because of a 7-day history of fever and nasal congestion. He has allergic rhinitis. He has had multiple episodes of otitis media and sinusitis and two episodes of pneumonia. His only routine medication is cetirizine. He appears ill but is in no distress. He is at the 30th percentile for height, 40th percentile for weight, and 35th percentile for BMI. His temperature is 37.8 C (100.0 F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Examination discloses an erythematous posterior pharynx and green nasal secretions. The left tympanic membrane is bulging and immobile with insufflation. No other abnormalities are noted. This patient most likely has a deficiency of which of the following immunoglobulins (Ig)?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. IgA</br>b. IgD</br>c. IgE</br>d. IgG</br>e. IgM</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. IgA  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Clinical “Walk-Through”<br /></br>1. Recurrent mucosal infections<br /></br>   • Multiple episodes of otitis media, sinusitis, and pneumonia → think defects in first-line mucosal defense.<br /></br>2. Atopic history<br /></br>   • Allergic rhinitis is classic in selective IgA deficiency (many patients have asthma, eczema, food allergy).<br /></br>3. Otherwise normal growth and development; physical exam shows typical acute URI/OM but no lymphoid absence, no severe viral/fungal disease, no systemic opportunists.<br /></br>4. Age 10 with only moderate infection burden → argues against severe pan-hypogammaglobulinemias (e.g., X-linked agammaglobulinemia presents in infancy with much more severe disease).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>• USMLE questions that combine “recurrent sinopulmonary infections + atopy ± transfusion reactions” are nearly always testing selective IgA deficiency, the most common primary immunodeficiency.<br /></br>• Ask yourself: “Are infections at mucosal surfaces? Is everything else pretty normal? = IgA.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Why IgA Is Correct – Clinical Reasoning Steps<br /></br>1. IgA is the principal immunoglobulin in mucosal secretions (nasal, salivary, bronchial, GI).<br /></br>2. Deficiency → poor opsonization/neutralization at these sites → recurrent otitis media, sinusitis, bronchitis/pneumonia, chronic diarrhea (esp. Giardia).<br /></br>3. Additional associations: atopy, autoimmune disease (celiac, ITP, RA), and anaphylaxis to IgA-containing blood products.<br /></br>Core takeaway: “Selective IgA deficiency = Airway &amp; GI infections, Atopy, AnaphylAxis to blood.” (The 4 A’s).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Snapshot<br /></br>• Mechanism: Failure of B-cell class-switch recombination to IgA (exact defect often unknown).<br /></br>• Labs: Very low/undetectable serum &amp; secretory IgA; normal IgG, IgM, total B-cell count.<br /></br>• Epidemiology: 1:400–1:700 (most common primary immunodeficiency); many asymptomatic.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Management Pearls<br /></br>• Treat infections aggressively (amoxicillin, amox-clavulanate, azithro, etc. as indicated).<br /></br>• Consider prophylactic antibiotics if frequent.<br /></br>• Vaccinate (pneumococcal, influenza).<br /></br>• IVIG generally NOT useful (contains little IgA).<br /></br>• If transfusion required → washed or IgA-deficient blood products to avoid anaphylaxis.<br /></br>Documentation (real patient): Record infection frequency, vaccination status, history of transfusion reactions, and counsel family on future transfusion precautions.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aid<br /></br>4 A’s of IgA deficiency:<br /></br>Airway &amp; GI infections, Atopy, Autoimmune disease, Anaphylaxis to IgA-containing transfusions.<br /></br>Visual: Imagine a capital “A” guarding the nose, ears, and lungs (mucosal castles), but the guard is missing → invaders (bugs) walk right in.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>b. IgD – Rare isolated deficiency, no well-defined clinical syndrome; IgD mainly acts as naïve B-cell receptor, not a mucosal defender.<br /></br>c. IgE – Hyper-IgE (Job) syndrome shows extremely high IgE with eczema, “cold” abscesses, retained primary teeth, facial features; deficiency of IgE alone does not cause recurrent bacterial sinopulmonary infections.<br /></br>d. IgG – Low IgG (e.g., X-linked agammaglobulinemia, common variable immunodeficiency) causes severe, often systemic bacterial infections beginning at 3–6 mo of age; you would also see low/absent tonsils or lymph nodes and pan-hypogammaglobulinemia—none described here.<br /></br>e. IgM – Selective IgM deficiency is exceedingly rare; if present, patients get severe infections with encapsulated organisms and often opportunistic infections; again, not classic for atopy/anaphylaxis pattern and far less common for exam purposes.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Takeaway<br /></br>Selective IgA deficiency: look for recurrent otitis/sinusitis/pneumonia + atopy ± transfusion anaphylaxis, with otherwise normal immune work-up and growth.</p>
103. A 67-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her husband after having a generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home. Her husband reports that shortly after awakening this morning, she had involuntary twitching of the right side of her face and right arm, lost consciousness, stiffened, and had generalized shaking for 2 minutes. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. Four months ago, she received the diagnosis of adenocarcinoma of the breast, metastatic to the lungs and bone. Current medications include metformin, atenolol, and letrozole. She is drowsy but able to follow simple commands with all extremities. Her temperature is 37 C (98.6 F), pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 160/90 mm Hg. She has a right lower facial droop. There is no meningismus. The pupils are 3 mm and reactive to light. The fundi are normal. There is conjugate gaze deviation to the patient's left. Muscle strength is 4/5 in the right upper extremity. Deep tendon reflexes are 3+ in the right upper extremity and 2+ elsewhere. Babinski sign is absent bilaterally. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin    12 g/dL</br>Leukocyte count    3000/mm³</br>Platelet count    80,000/mm³</br>Serum:</br>  Na+    128 mEq/L</br>  Glucose    70 mg/dL</br>Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's seizure?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Brain metastasis</br>b. Hypoglycemia</br>c. Hyponatremia</br>d. Intracerebral hemorrhage</br>e. Paraneoplastic limbic encephalitis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Brain metastasis  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Key buzzwords &amp; clues<br /></br>   • New-onset generalized tonic–clonic seizure that began with twitching of the RIGHT face/arm → a focal (partial) motor seizure with secondary generalization.<br /></br>   • Persistent focal neurologic findings afterward:<br /></br>     – Right lower facial droop<br /></br>     – 4/5 strength &amp; hyperreflexia (3+) in the right UE<br /></br>     – Conjugate gaze deviation to the LEFT<br /></br>   • Known systemic malignancy (breast adenocarcinoma) already metastatic to lung &amp; bone.<br /></br>   • Normal vital signs except mild HTN; afebrile → infection/meningitis less likely.<br /></br>   • Labs show only mild hyponatremia (128 mEq/L) and mild hypoglycemia (70 mg/dL) – neither typically severe enough to provoke seizures.</p></br><ol></br><li>How those clues narrow the differential<br /></br>   • Focal onset + lateralizing post-ictal deficits ⇒ cortical structural lesion in the contralateral (LEFT) hemisphere, not a diffuse metabolic problem.<br /></br>   • Breast cancer is one of the top two solid tumors that seed the brain (lung &gt; breast).<br /></br>   • Mild metabolic derangements may lower seizure threshold but rarely cause a frank seizure with focal signs.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Approach for future questions:<br /></br>   a) Ask “focal or generalized onset?”—focal points to a structural lesion.<br /></br>   b) Scan the stem for a cancer history—think brain mets.<br /></br>   c) Check lab numbers—decide if they are in the seizure-producing range (&lt;60 glucose, &lt;115 Na⁺).  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (why choice A is correct)<br /></br>Step-by-step thought process:<br /></br>1. A seizure starting in the right face/arm originates in the LEFT frontal motor cortex.<br /></br>2. Persistent neurologic deficits postictally (Todd’s paralysis) and new gaze deviation further indicate left hemispheric irritation.<br /></br>3. The patient already has metastatic breast cancer; hematogenous spread to gray-white junctions of the cerebral cortex is common and classically presents with headaches, focal deficits, or seizures.<br /></br>Core takeaway: In any cancer patient, a new focal neurologic event (seizure or deficit) is brain metastasis until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>Documentation in a real chart would include: description of seizure, focal findings, cancer history, differential (mets vs ICH vs metabolic), and an urgent plan for brain MRI with contrast, steroids, and antiepileptics.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Breast cancer cells disseminate via blood, lodge at gray-white junction, induce vasogenic edema and irritate surrounding cortex → focal seizures.<br /></br>• Vasogenic edema causes mass effect and focal neurologic signs; steroids (dexamethasone) rapidly shrink edema.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Diagnostic Tests &amp; Treatment (high-yield)<br /></br>1. MRI brain with gadolinium = most sensitive for metastases (often multiple, ring-enhancing).<br /></br>2. Initial management:<br /></br>   • IV dexamethasone (↓ vasogenic edema)<br /></br>   • Levetiracetam (or another non-enzyme-inducing AED) for seizure prophylaxis<br /></br>3. Definitive tumor control:<br /></br>   • Single lesion in surgically accessible cortex: resection ± SRS (stereotactic radiosurgery).<br /></br>   • Multiple lesions: whole-brain radiotherapy or SRS; systemic therapy as appropriate.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Lung &amp; boob go to the lobe.”<br /></br>Visualize lung and breast tumors in a plane flying to the gray-white “airstrip” (junction) in the brain—landing there causes focal seizures.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>b. Hypoglycemia<br /></br>   • Seizures typically when glucose &lt;50 mg/dL. Her level is 70 mg/dL.<br /></br>   • Metabolic seizures are usually generalized without persistent focal lateralizing deficits.</p></br><p>c. Hyponatremia<br /></br>   • Seizure threshold usually &lt;115 mEq/L (sometimes &lt;120). 128 mEq/L is mild and unlikely to cause a seizure, let alone focal onset.</p></br><p>d. Intracerebral hemorrhage<br /></br>   • Would present with sudden, severe headache, vomiting, rapidly progressive deficits, often decreased consciousness.<br /></br>   • Seizure is less common as the presenting event in spontaneous ICH.<br /></br>   • Thrombocytopenia (80 k) increases ICH risk, but focal seizure + known metastatic cancer makes metastasis far more likely. CT/MRI would be needed to distinguish.</p></br><p>e. Paraneoplastic limbic encephalitis<br /></br>   • Classically from small-cell lung cancer or testicular germ cell tumors.<br /></br>   • Produces subacute mood changes, short-term memory loss, psychosis, and complex-partial seizures—NOT focal motor seizures with lateralizing deficits.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Take-Home Message<br /></br>In a patient with known systemic cancer, new focal neurologic signs or focal-onset seizures are most commonly due to brain metastases. Mild metabolic abnormalities rarely explain focal seizures with persistent deficits.</p>
104. A 52-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 2-year history of progressive hearing loss in his right ear. He says he initially only noticed gradual changes in his hearing but has had severe hearing loss during the past 6 months. He also has had ringing in his right ear for 12 months and episodes of light-headedness and numbness and tingling on the right side of his face during the past month. He has a history of mild hypertension. Current medications include hydrochlorothiazide and 81-mg aspirin. He has been employed operating heavy equipment on construction sites for 30 years. He is not in distress. Vital signs are within normal limits. The external ear canals and tympanic membranes appear normal. He cannot hear whispered voice in the right ear. Air conduction is greater than bone conduction on the right. A tuning fork held over the forehead at midline is heard better on the left. Results of laboratory studies are within the reference range. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma)</br>b. Benign positional vertigo</br>c. Drug toxicity</br>d. Meniere disease</br>e. Multiple sclerosis</br>f. Vertebrobasilar insufficiency</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Pattern of hearing loss<br /></br>   • “Cannot hear whispered voice in the right ear” + Rinne test: air conduction &gt; bone conduction (Rinne-positive) + Weber lateralizes to the left → unilateral sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL) in the right ear.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Associated otologic &amp; neurologic symptoms<br /></br>   • “Ringing (tinnitus) in his right ear” – common early symptom of VIII-nerve tumors.<br /></br>   • “Light-headedness” – vestibular dysfunction.<br /></br>   • “Numbness and tingling on the right side of his face” – compression of the trigeminal nerve root (CN V) at the cerebellopontine angle.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Tempo<br /></br>   • “2-year history … more rapid decline in the last 6 months” – slow, progressive course typical for a benign Schwann-cell tumor.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Normal otoscopic exam<br /></br>   • Rules out external or middle-ear disease; points toward retrocochlear pathology.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive pearl:<br /></br>When you see unilateral, progressive SNHL with ipsilateral tinnitus ± facial numbness, immediately think “retrocochlear mass” (most commonly vestibular Schwannoma) and order an MRI with gadolinium of the internal auditory canal/cerebellopontine angle on the real exam.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Identify type of hearing loss with Weber/Rinne → sensorineural.  </li></br><li>Decide unilateral vs bilateral → unilateral suggests focal lesion.  </li></br><li>Look for other cranial-nerve signs → facial numbness (CN V), vestibular symptoms (CN VIII).  </li></br><li>Candidate lesion at cerebellopontine angle → vestibular Schwannoma.</li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home concept: Unilateral progressive SNHL + tinnitus ± vestibular or facial nerve findings = vestibular Schwannoma until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>Documentation in the chart: “Progressive unilateral SNHL with tinnitus and trigeminal paresthesia – high suspicion for vestibular schwannoma; plan MRI IAC with contrast and ENT/neurosurgery referral.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Benign Schwann-cell tumor of the vestibular portion of CN VIII.<br /></br>• Located in the internal auditory canal → can enlarge into the cerebellopontine angle.<br /></br>• Compresses adjacent cranial nerves (VIII → VII → V) and cerebellar pathways causing:<br /></br>  – Unilateral SNHL, tinnitus<br /></br>  – Disequilibrium/vertigo<br /></br>  – Facial weakness or spasm (CN VII)<br /></br>  – Facial numbness (CN V)<br /></br>• Associated with NF2 when bilateral (due to merlin gene mutation on chromosome 22).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><p>First-line diagnostic study: MRI with gadolinium focusing on internal auditory canals.<br /></br>Audiogram: asymmetric high-frequency SNHL.<br /></br>Management:<br /></br>• Tumor &lt;2 cm &amp; minimal symptoms – observation with serial MRIs.<br /></br>• Symptomatic or ≥2–3 cm – stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife) or microsurgical resection.<br /></br>• Corticosteroids acutely if sudden hearing decline from tumor edema.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic “UNIlateral U8”:<br /></br>U – Unilateral<br /></br>N – Neuro signs (V, VII)<br /></br>I – Internal auditory canal tumor<br /></br>8 – CN VIII Schwannoma</p></br><p>Visual memory: Picture a SCHWAN with big HEADPHONES over one ear (unilateral hearing loss) bumping into the TRIGEMINAL “5” billboard.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Answers Are Wrong  </p></br><p>B. Benign positional vertigo (BPV)<br /></br>• Brief (&lt;1 min) vertigo triggered by head movements; NO hearing loss, NO tinnitus, NO cranial-nerve deficits. Dix-Hallpike positive, normal audiogram.</p></br><p>C. Drug toxicity<br /></br>• Aminoglycosides, loop diuretics, cisplatin cause bilateral—not unilateral—SNHL. No associated trigeminal symptoms.</p></br><p>D. Ménière disease<br /></br>• Triad: episodic vertigo, fluctuating SNHL, tinnitus/aural fullness. Hearing loss waxes/wanes, often low-frequency; no facial numbness.</p></br><p>E. Multiple sclerosis<br /></br>• Can cause vertigo or trigeminal neuralgia but typically presents with multifocal CNS signs (optic neuritis, internuclear ophthalmoplegia, sensory/motor deficits) and not isolated progressive unilateral SNHL.</p></br><p>F. Vertebrobasilar insufficiency<br /></br>• Transient, seconds-to-minutes episodes of vertigo, ataxia, diplopia, dysarthria; vascular risk factors. Normally no chronic unilateral tinnitus or isolated hearing loss.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Takeaway:<br /></br>Any patient with chronic, unilateral sensorineural hearing loss—especially if accompanied by tinnitus or other cranial-nerve deficits—must be evaluated for vestibular schwannoma with MRI.</p>
105. A 32-year-old man comes to the clinic because of a slow-healing lesion on his nose that has been present for the past year. The patient is a member of the US National Guard and says he first noticed the lesion 2 months after he returned home from deployment to Southwest Asia. The lesion initially appeared as two small pimples but gradually enlarged to form a single open sore, eventually developing a scaly crust. The lesion has not been painful or itchy. Aside from several insect bites and itching at various times during his deployment, he had no skin trauma or infections and did not have any significant contact with the local population or animals. He received all required vaccines, including smallpox, anthrax, and typhoid, prior to his deployment. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. A photograph of the lesion is shown. The lesion is not tender. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Anthrax</br>b. Leishmaniasis</br>c. Psoriasis</br>d. Squamous cell carcinoma</br>e. Tinea corporis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Leishmaniasis  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Deployment to Southwest Asia (Iraq, Afghanistan, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Kuwait, etc) ⇒ endemic region for cutaneous leishmaniasis transmitted by the female phlebotomine sandfly.<br /></br>• “Appeared as two small pimples … gradually enlarged to form a single open sore … scaly crust” ⇒ classic evolution of a painless papule → nodule → ulcer with raised indurated border and adherent crust.<br /></br>• Lesion is painless, not pruritic, and has persisted for ≈1 year (“slow-healing”) ⇒ hallmark of cutaneous leishmaniasis.<br /></br>• No animal/human exposure or vaccination issue implicated; insect (sandfly) exposure is the key clue.  </p></br><p>Cognitive approach for future questions<br /></br>1. Geographic/travel clue.<br /></br>2. Chronic, painless ulcerative lesion with heaped-up margins.<br /></br>3. Think: sandfly → Leishmania.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>1. Identify chronic painless ulcer → list infectious (leishmaniasis, cutaneous TB, sporotrichosis, anthrax) and non-infectious (SCC, BCC) causes.<br /></br>2. Overlay travel history → leishmaniasis rises to the top.<br /></br>3. Morphology (raised border, central crust, “volcano” appearance) consistent with leishmaniasis, not anthrax (black eschar) or SCC (keratotic, friable).  </p></br><p>Take-home message:<br /></br>Cutaneous leishmaniasis presents as a slowly enlarging, painless ulcer with indurated borders in travelers or military personnel returning from endemic regions (Middle East, Central/South America).  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real encounter):<br /></br>• Onset, evolution, symptoms (pain/pruritus absent)<br /></br>• Travel locations &amp; insect exposure<br /></br>• Photograph &amp; measurement of lesion<br /></br>• Plan for skin scraping/PCR for Leishmania spp.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: Protozoa (Leishmania major, L. tropica in Old World; L. mexicana, L. braziliensis in New World).<br /></br>• Vector: Female phlebotomine sandfly injects promastigotes → taken up by macrophages → convert to amastigotes, replicate intracellularly.<br /></br>• Host response forms granulomatous inflammation → ulcer with raised border.<br /></br>• Immunity usually limits disease to skin; some species (L. braziliensis) can later cause mucocutaneous disease.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment<br /></br>Diagnostics<br /></br>1. Skin scraping/biopsy with Giemsa stain: intracellular amastigotes (“Leishman-Donovan bodies”).<br /></br>2. PCR (highest sensitivity).<br /></br>3. Sometimes culture or Montenegro skin test.  </p></br><p>Treatment (cutaneous)<br /></br>• Often self-heals in 6-12 mo, but therapy shortens course/prevents scarring:<br /></br>  – Sodium stibogluconate (pentavalent antimonial) – traditional first-line.<br /></br>  – Alternatives: miltefosine (oral), liposomal amphotericin B, azoles (ketoconazole, fluconazole) for some species.<br /></br>  – Local modalities: cryotherapy or intralesional antimonial.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“ME-FLY sore”:<br /></br>Middle East + SandFLY → Year-long painless Sore ⇒ Leishmania.<br /></br>Visualize a soldier swatting a sandfly under a desert sun while a “volcano” lesion grows on his nose.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Anthrax (cutaneous)<br /></br>• Painless papule → vesicle → black eschar with surrounding edema within days-weeks, not months-year.<br /></br>• Requires exposure to animal hides, wool, or bioterror spores. Lesion center is necrotic black, not scaly crust.  </p></br><p>C. Psoriasis<br /></br>• Well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scale on extensor surfaces, scalp, sacrum. Pruritic; no chronic solitary ulcer.  </p></br><p>D. Squamous cell carcinoma<br /></br>• Non-healing, sometimes painful keratotic nodule/plaque; risk factors: chronic sun, immunosuppression, radiation, scars. Travel history and insect bites do not fit; evolution over a year without pain/bleeding is less typical. Histology would be needed.  </p></br><p>E. Tinea corporis<br /></br>• Annular pruritic plaque with central clearing (“ringworm”), not an ulcer. Responds to topical antifungals; heals within weeks, not persistent for a year.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core takeaway:<br /></br>Chronic, painless, slow-healing ulcer in a traveler from an endemic area with sandfly exposure = cutaneous leishmaniasis.</p>
106. A previously healthy nulligravid 18-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-day history of increasingly severe pain in her left wrist and left knee. She first had diffuse joint stiffness 5 days ago during a flu-like illness. Her symptoms, except for the joint pain, resolved spontaneously without treatment. She now has difficulty holding heavy items in her left hand and has pain with weight bearing on her left side. She is sexually active with multiple partners. Her only medication is an oral contraceptive. Her temperature is 37.4 C (99.3 F), pulse is 75/min, and blood pressure is 134/82 mm Hg. There is no rash. Pelvic examination shows mucoid cervical discharge but no other abnormalities. Examination of the left wrist shows tenderness and no effusions; active and passive range of motion is limited by pain. The left knee is warm and markedly tender with a large effusion. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Analysis of left knee aspirate shows a leukocyte count of 85,000/mm^3 with 85 percent neutrophils, 10 percent monocytes, and 5 percent lymphocytes; a Gram stain of the fluid is negative. Cultures of the aspirate are pending, as are blood, pharyngeal, cervical, and rectal cultures. The patient is admitted to the hospital, and intravenous ceftriaxone and oral azithromycin are begun. After 2 days, all cultures show no growth. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Continuation of current drug regimen</br>b. Determination of erythrocyte sedimentation rate</br>c. Serologic testing for parvovirus</br>d. Switching from ceftriaxone to naproxen therapy</br>e. Addition of linezolid to the medication regimen</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>a. Continuation of current drug regimen (IV ceftriaxone + oral azithromycin)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that seal the diagnosis of disseminated gonococcal infection (DGI):<br /></br>• Young, sexually-active woman with multiple partners → high N. gonorrhoeae risk.<br /></br>• Prodrome of “flu-like” illness followed by acute migratory/oligo-articular arthritis (wrist then knee).<br /></br>• One joint (knee) now has a purulent effusion; the other (wrist) has tenosynovitis-type pain without effusion → classic “septic arthritis” form of DGI.<br /></br>• Mucopurulent cervical discharge on pelvic exam.<br /></br>• Synovial fluid: very high WBC (&gt;50,000) with neutrophilic predominance = septic arthritis, but Gram stain is frequently negative in DGI (organism is fastidious; &lt;25 % of joint fluids are positive).<br /></br>• All cultures “no growth after 2 days” does NOT rule out DGI—pharyngeal/rectal/cervical cultures are positive in only ~50–70 %, and joint cultures even less.</p></br><p>Cognitive approach for exam day: ❶ Recognize the demographic + sexual history → think STI-related causes. ❷ Septic arthritis with negative Gram stain→ consider gonococcus or Lyme. ❸ Add tenosynovitis OR migratory pattern → points strongly to DGI. ❹ Know that culture negativity is common → continue empiric therapy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><ol></br><li>DGI is treated empirically with a 3rd-generation cephalosporin (ceftriaxone IV/IM) PLUS azithromycin (1-time oral dose) to cover possible co-infection with Chlamydia trachomatis and to slow cephalosporin resistance.  </li></br><li>Recommended duration: 7 days of parenteral ceftriaxone (may switch to oral once improving).  </li></br><li>Premature discontinuation risks persistent infection, post-infectious arthritis, and transmission.<br /></br>Therefore, with cultures still negative but strong clinical suspicion, the correct step is to keep the current regimen.</li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home: Negative cultures do NOT exclude DGI; continue empiric ceftriaxone + azithromycin for at least 7 days once clinical suspicion is high.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): sexual history, contraception, joint exam, synovial WBC count, empiric antibiotics started, partner notification/expedited partner therapy plan.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• N. gonorrhoeae (gram-negative diplococcus) can spread hematogenously.<br /></br>• Two classic DGI phenotypes:<br /></br>  – Triad: polyarthralgia + tenosynovitis + dermatitis (pustular lesions).<br /></br>  – Purulent mono/oligo-articular septic arthritis (no rash).<br /></br>• Complement deficiency (especially terminal pathway C5-C9) predisposes.<br /></br>• Synovial fluid Gram stain/culture sensitivity &lt;50 % due to low bacterial load &amp; fastidious growth requirements.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><p>First-line therapy (CDC/IDSA):<br /></br>• Ceftriaxone 1-2 g IV/IM daily × 7 days (longer if endocarditis or meningitis).<br /></br>• PLUS azithromycin 1 g PO once (or doxycycline 100 mg PO bid × 7 d).<br /></br>• Drainage of purulent joint fluid (arthrocentesis or arthroscopic lavage) until effusion resolves.<br /></br>No role for switching to oral NSAID monotherapy or adding MRSA drugs (linezolid).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Can’t see, can’t pee… can’t flee, can’t climb a tree” = Reiter’s, BUT<br /></br>For DGI remember “Tenosynovitis, Dermatitis, and arthriTIS” (“TD-TIS”) → think GoNocci on the run!<br /></br>Culture-Negative Septic joint in a sexually active teen/young adult → “Go Negative = Go Noccio” (gonorrhea).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. ESR measurement – ESR would likely be elevated but is nonspecific and would not change immediate management.  </p></br><p>c. Parvovirus B19 serology – Parvovirus causes symmetric small-joint arthropathy (often in school teachers/day-care exposure) WITHOUT purulent effusion or high synovial WBC; not consistent here.  </p></br><p>d. Switch to naproxen – NSAIDs alone cannot eradicate septic arthritis; would allow joint destruction and systemic spread.  </p></br><p>e. Add linezolid – Reserved for resistant gram-positive organisms (MRSA, VRE). Gonorrhea is gram-negative; no indication.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>Disseminated gonococcal infection often produces Gram-stain/culture-negative septic arthritis; continue empiric ceftriaxone plus azithromycin for at least 7 days despite initially negative cultures.</p>
107. A 42-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 1-day history of severe pain, swelling, and warmth in her left knee. She notes that she awoke with the pain 1 day after she did an aerobic kickboxing workout. Her temperature is 37.2 C (99 F), pulse is 86/min, and blood pressure is 120/70 mm Hg. The upper aspect of the left knee is erythematous and warm to the touch with an effusion. Range of motion is limited by pain. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Arthrocentesis is performed. Joint fluid analysis shows a leukocyte count of 17,000/mm3 with 90 percent segmented neutrophils and intracellular calcium pyrophosphate crystals. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy at this time?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Oral acetaminophen</br>b. Oral doxycycline</br>c. Oral hydroxychloroquine</br>d. Oral ibuprofen</br>e. Intravenous nafcillin</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Oral ibuprofen (an NSAID)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • “Severe pain, swelling, and warmth in her left knee … effusion … limited ROM by pain” → acute mono-articular arthritis<br /></br>   • “Leukocyte count 17,000/mm³ with 90% neutrophils” → inflammatory fluid (not non-inflammatory &lt;2 000, not septic &gt;50 000–100 000).<br /></br>   • “Intracellular calcium pyrophosphate crystals” → pathognomonic for calcium-pyrophosphate crystal deposition disease (CPPD, ‘pseudogout’).</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • Afebrile (37.2 °C is normal) and WBC count of fluid &lt;50 000/mm³ → argues against septic arthritis.<br /></br>   • No rash, no migratory pattern, no chronic polyarthritis → less likely RA, Lyme, SLE, etc.<br /></br>   • No mention of trauma severe enough for hemarthrosis.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>   • Step 1: Is it crystal, septic, or osteoarthritis? Always look at fluid WBC and crystal report.<br /></br>   • Step 2: Identify the crystal. Monosodium urate (negatively birefringent, needle-shaped) = gout. Calcium pyrophosphate (positively birefringent, rhomboid) = pseudogout.<br /></br>   • Step 3: Acute pseudogout treatment = NSAID first-line (or colchicine, or intra-articular glucocorticoid).  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>CPPD causes sudden mono-articular inflammation that is clinically indistinguishable from gout or septic arthritis until fluid is analyzed. Once calcium pyrophosphate crystals are identified and no signs of infection are present, the goal is rapid anti-inflammatory control. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen, indomethacin, or naproxen are the standard first-line therapy for acute attacks.</p></br><p>Take-home message:<br /></br>Acute CPPD = treat like gout → NSAID first, colchicine second, steroids if needed. Always rule out infection with fluid analysis first.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real-life):<br /></br>• Joint location, onset, swelling, erythema<br /></br>• Synovial fluid cell count, Gram stain, culture, crystal analysis<br /></br>• Initiation of NSAID and GI/renal risk assessment  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Etiology<br /></br>• Deposition of calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate crystals in cartilage and synovium.<br /></br>• Risk factors: aging, hemochromatosis, hyperparathyroidism, hypothyroidism, hypomagnesemia, joint trauma/osteoarthritis.</p></br><p>Mechanism<br /></br>Crystals incite neutrophilic inflammation within synovial fluid → release of cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α) → pain, swelling, warmth.</p></br><p>Clinical manifestations<br /></br>• Acute mono-arthritis (most commonly knee, wrist, ankle)<br /></br>• Can also cause chronic degenerative arthropathy that mimics OA (“chondrocalcinosis” on x-ray).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics Review  </p></br><p>Acute attack<br /></br>1. NSAID (e.g., ibuprofen 600–800 mg PO TID with meals)<br /></br>2. Colchicine 1.2 mg → 0.6 mg 1 hr later if NSAIDs contraindicated<br /></br>3. Intra-articular glucocorticoid (triamcinolone) or short oral steroid taper if polyarticular or refractory.</p></br><p>Chronic / prophylaxis<br /></br>• Low-dose colchicine 0.6 mg once or twice daily.<br /></br>• Address underlying metabolic disorders; optimize Mg²⁺, treat hyperparathyroidism, etc.</p></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>• Synovial fluid analysis: rhomboid, weakly positively birefringent crystals under polarized light.<br /></br>• X-ray: chondrocalcinosis (calcification of articular cartilage).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Pseudo-gout = Positive (birefringent) Pyrophosphate” → 3 P’s.<br /></br>“Gout Needle points Negative” (negative birefringence, needle-shaped).<br /></br>Acute CPPD Rx mnemonic: “NSAID, Next is Steroid, or And Colchicine” (N-S-C).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Oral acetaminophen<br /></br>• Provides analgesia but virtually no anti-inflammatory action at therapeutic doses; inadequate for acute CPPD inflammation.</p></br><p>B. Oral doxycycline<br /></br>• Used for Lyme arthritis or atypical infections, not for sterile crystal-induced arthritis. Synovial fluid would show spirochetes PCR/specific antibodies, not crystals.</p></br><p>C. Oral hydroxychloroquine<br /></br>• Disease-modifying agent for chronic autoimmune arthritis (RA, SLE). Works over months, not for abrupt inflammatory crystal arthritis.</p></br><p>E. Intravenous nafcillin<br /></br>• Empiric therapy for MSSA septic arthritis, indicated if synovial WBC &gt;50 000/mm³ with purulent appearance or positive Gram stain/culture. This patient’s lower WBC count, afebrile status, and presence of crystals make infection unlikely.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>By recognizing the synovial fluid profile and crystal type, you swiftly narrow to CPPD and select an NSAID—ibuprofen—as the most appropriate immediate therapy.</p>
108. A 66-year-old man comes to the office because of worsening pain in his right shoulder that began 1 day after installing overhead tile in his house 9 months ago. Initially, the pain only affected his muscle strength but during the past month he has been unable to raise his arm higher than 90 degrees, even when the pain is mild. The pain is worse at night and improves moderately an hour or two after he wakes up in the morning. Medical history is noncontributory; the patient takes ibuprofen for the shoulder pain. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination of the right shoulder shows mild muscular atrophy with no tenderness to palpation. Passive range of motion in the shoulder is full, except that the patient reports a "twinge" of discomfort during testing. Drop-arm testing is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Adhesive capsulitis</br>b. Biceps tendinitis</br>c. Cervical nerve impingement</br>d. Labral tear</br>e. Rotator cuff tear</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Rotator cuff tear  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• 66-year-old → degenerative shoulder pathology common (rotator cuff &gt; tendinitis)<br /></br>• Onset “1 day after installing overhead tile” → overhead activity = classic mechanism for supraspinatus injury.<br /></br>• Progressive course over 9 mo with night pain, weakness, visible atrophy → chronic full-thickness tear.<br /></br>• Cannot actively abduct &gt;90° but passive range is essentially full → loss of strength, not capsular restriction.<br /></br>• Positive drop-arm test → highly suggestive of supraspinatus tear.<br /></br>• Minimal tenderness → argues against inflammatory tendinitis or bursitis.  </p></br><p>Cognitive strategy<br /></br>1. Separate “pain + weakness” entities (rotator cuff tear) from “pain + stiffness” (adhesive capsulitis).<br /></br>2. Compare active vs passive ROM; mismatch (active ↓, passive normal) = muscle/tendon tear or neurologic weakness.<br /></br>3. Plug in a special test (drop-arm) that points directly to cuff tear.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why E is right)  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Elderly + overhead activity → consider degenerative cuff pathology.<br /></br>2. Passive ROM preserved rules out adhesive capsulitis.<br /></br>3. Strength loss and atrophy = structural failure of contractile unit.<br /></br>4. Drop-arm test specificity ≈ 90 % for full-thickness supraspinatus tear.  </p></br><p>Take-home concept<br /></br>Full-thickness rotator cuff tear = painful, nocturnal shoulder with preserved passive motion and objective weakness (positive cuff tests).  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls<br /></br>• Active vs passive ROM findings<br /></br>• Special-test results (drop-arm, empty can)<br /></br>• Presence of atrophy<br /></br>• Neurovascular status  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Most tears involve the supraspinatus tendon (part of SITS: Supraspinatus, Infraspinatus, Teres minor, Subscapularis).<br /></br>• Degenerative microtrauma + poor vascular supply → tendon frays → full-thickness defect → humeral head depressor power lost.<br /></br>• Night pain: humeral head subluxates superiorly when lying on affected side.  </p></br><p>Risk factors: age &gt;60, repetitive overhead work/sports, smoking, hypercholesterolemia.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic Tests &amp; Treatment  </p></br><p>Diagnosis<br /></br>• MRI shoulder or high-resolution US confirms tear size; plain x-ray may show superior humeral head migration.  </p></br><p>Management<br /></br>1. Initial: NSAIDs, activity modification, dedicated PT (rotator cuff–strengthening, scapular stabilizers).<br /></br>2. Full-thickness with persistent functional limitation in active adults → arthroscopic or open surgical repair.<br /></br>3. Subacromial corticosteroid injection for temporary pain relief.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“DROP” the arm, think “SUPRA is torn.”<br /></br>D = Degenerative/Overhead work<br /></br>R = ROM passive normal<br /></br>O = Older (&gt;60) with Over-night pain<br /></br>P = Positive Drop-arm  </p></br><p>Mnemonic for cuff muscles: “SITS keeps the shoulder in its SITS.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Adhesive capsulitis (“frozen shoulder”)<br /></br>• Global fibrosis of the glenohumeral capsule.<br /></br>• BOTH active and passive ROM markedly limited; no significant weakness until end-stage.<br /></br>• Pain usually diffuse; drop-arm test negative.  </p></br><p>b. Biceps tendinitis<br /></br>• Anterior shoulder pain, point tenderness at bicipital groove, aggravated by resisted forearm supination (Yergason) or flexion (Speed).<br /></br>• Strength intact; drop-arm negative; atrophy uncommon.  </p></br><p>c. Cervical nerve impingement (cervical radiculopathy)<br /></br>• Neck pain radiating past the shoulder, paresthesias, positive Spurling.<br /></br>• Passive shoulder motion normal, but there is also neck-dependent pain. No drop-arm sign.  </p></br><p>d. Labral tear (e.g., SLAP lesion)<br /></br>• Typically younger patients or acute traction injury.<br /></br>• Mechanical symptoms (clicking, catching), positive O’Brien or crank test; strength preserved.<br /></br>• Passive ROM usually normal; no atrophy months later unless pain-limited.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Preserved passive motion + objective weakness/positive drop-arm in an older patient with chronic overhead use → think rotator cuff tear, most commonly the supraspinatus.</p>
109. A 23-year-old man comes to the emergency department 45 minutes after the sudden onset of palpitations and mild shortness of breath that began while he was drinking beer with friends. He has not had chest pain or loss of consciousness. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. On arrival, he appears anxious. He is alert and fully oriented. His pulse is 138/min and regular, and blood pressure is 100/65 mm Hg; other vital signs are within normal limits. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 99 percent. The patient’s tachycardia ends abruptly during the examination. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Atrial fibrillation</br>b. Atrioventricular nodal reentrant tachycardia</br>c. Multifocal atrial tachycardia</br>d. Sinus tachycardia</br>e. Ventricular tachycardia</br>f. Wandering atrial pacemaker</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Atrioventricular nodal re-entrant tachycardia (AVNRT)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key words: “23-year-old … sudden onset … palpitations … mild shortness of breath … no chest pain or syncope … pulse 138/min and regular … tachycardia ends abruptly.”<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: young healthy patient, regular narrow-complex tachycardia, paroxysmal (abrupt start and stop), provoked by stimulants (beer/EtOH, sympathetic tone).<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: no structural heart disease, no irregularly irregular pulse, no COPD history, no gradual onset/offset, no wide QRS.<br /></br>Pattern recognition: Paroxysmal, regular SVT in a young person → think AVNRT or AVRT; AVNRT is by far the most common.</p></br><p>Cognitive/Metacognitive Pearl<br /></br>When confronted with tachycardia:<br /></br>1. Regular vs irregular?<br /></br>2. Narrow vs wide QRS?<br /></br>3. Sudden vs gradual onset/offset?<br /></br>Regular + narrow + abrupt → AVNRT (classic), AVRT (WPW) less common unless pre-excitation clues are given.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>1. Regular rhythm at 138/min excludes atrial fibrillation (irregular) and multifocal atrial tachycardia (irregular).<br /></br>2. Abrupt termination excludes sinus tachycardia (gradual).<br /></br>3. Young patient with no structural heart disease and no mention of wide QRS complexes makes ventricular tachycardia unlikely.<br /></br>4. Wandering atrial pacemaker shows heart rate &lt;100/min with at least three P-wave morphologies—doesn’t fit.<br /></br>Take-home: The hallmark of AVNRT is paroxysmal episodes of rapid, REGULAR tachycardia that begin and end suddenly.</p></br><p>Documentation (real clinical encounter)<br /></br>• Onset/offset characteristics, provoking factors, vitals, absence of syncope/chest pain.<br /></br>• Rhythm strip if captured, response to vagal maneuvers or adenosine if attempted.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Dual pathways within the AV node (fast &amp; slow). A premature atrial beat finds the fast pathway refractory, travels down the slow pathway, then retrograde up the fast pathway, creating a re-entrant circuit → rapid atrial and ventricular activation (usually 150–250 bpm).<br /></br>• Sudden onset/termination because the re-entrant loop either catches or is broken abruptly.</p></br><p>Risk factors/Triggers<br /></br>↑Sympathetic tone, caffeine, alcohol, stress, hyperthyroidism.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment (High-Yield)</br>Acute termination:<br /></br>1. Vagal maneuvers (Valsalva, carotid massage).<br /></br>2. IV adenosine (drug of choice for stable AVNRT).  </p></br><p>Chronic/preventive:<br /></br>• β-blockers (metoprolol), nondihydropyridine CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil).<br /></br>• Catheter radiofrequency ablation of slow pathway is definitive in recurrent cases.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“AVNRT = A Very (abrupt) Nodally-Revolving Tachycardia.”<br /></br>Think “On/Off switch” to picture its sudden start and stop.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>A. Atrial fibrillation – Irregularly irregular pulse; no abrupt termination.<br /></br>C. Multifocal atrial tachycardia – Irregular rhythm, &gt;3 P-wave morphologies, usually in COPD; HR often 100–150.<br /></br>D. Sinus tachycardia – Gradual rise &amp; fall, clear sinus P waves, etiologic stressor (pain, fever, anemia).<br /></br>E. Ventricular tachycardia – Wide QRS, often &gt;150 bpm, typically in structural heart disease; hemodynamic instability more common.<br /></br>F. Wandering atrial pacemaker – HR &lt;100, multiple P-wave morphologies, benign baseline rhythm, not paroxysmal.</p></br><p>Core memory cue: Sudden, regular, narrow-complex tachy in a young healthy patient → think AVNRT first.</p>
110. A previously healthy 32-year-old man comes to the office because of a 6-month history of palpitations, fatigue, and shortness of breath on exertion. He also has had occasional chest pain after eating large meals. He appears healthy and is not in acute distress. His pulse is 88/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 138/86 mm Hg. Carotid pulses are forceful. Cardiac examination shows a left ventricular lift. An S4 gallop and grade 3/6, harsh, systolic crescendo-descrescendo murmur are heard best at the lower left sternal border and apex. Results of Valsalva maneuver and ECG are shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Aortic sclerosis</br>b. Aortic stenosis</br>c. Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy</br>d. Mitral regurgitation</br>e. Mitral stenosis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key phrases that immediately narrow the differential<br /></br>   • 32-year-old, previously healthy → congenital / inherited causes more likely than degenerative valvular disease<br /></br>   • Palpitations, exertional fatigue, dyspnea, “occasionally chest pain after large meals” (↑ after preload reduction) → outflow obstruction physiology<br /></br>   • Forceful carotid upstroke (bisferiens pulse), left-ventricular lift, S4 → stiff, hypertrophied ventricle<br /></br>   • Harsh, systolic crescendo–decrescendo murmur best at lower LSB and apex → LVOT (left-ventricular outflow-tract) murmur, classic for HOCM when heard away from the aortic area<br /></br>   • Murmur behavior with Valsalva (graph provided) → intensity ↑ when preload ↓, a hallmark of dynamic obstruction (HOCM)<br /></br>   • ECG strip (not shown to you) in exams typically displays tall R in aVL, deep S in V3, or “dagger” Q waves in inferior/lateral leads → asymmetric septal hypertrophy  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives &amp; negatives  </p></br></li></br><li>Young age, S4, dynamic murmur ↑ with Valsalva<br /></br>   – No radiation to carotids (rules against classic aortic stenosis)<br /></br>   – No opening snap or diastolic rumble (rules out mitral stenosis)<br /></br>   – No holosystolic “blowing” quality or radiation to axilla (rules out MR)<br /></br>   – No ejection sound or carotid plateau pulse (rules out fixed AS due to sclerosis)  </li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: In NBME/Step questions, ALWAYS think “dynamic vs fixed” murmur. Maneuvers that DECREASE preload (Valsalva, abrupt standing) INTENSIFY a dynamic HOCM murmur but soften virtually every other systolic murmur.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (stepwise)</p></br><p>Step 1 – Localize the murmur: harsh, midsystolic, best at LSB/apex ⇒ LV outflow tract but not at right upper sternal border (which would favor AS).<br /></br>Step 2 – Determine if dynamic: Valsalva ↑ intensity ⇒ dynamic obstruction ⇒ HOCM.<br /></br>Step 3 – Corroborate with age &amp; exam: young adult with S4 and forceful, bifid carotid pulse further supports hypertrophied, non-compliant LV.<br /></br>Take-home: A crescendo–decrescendo systolic murmur that gets LOUDER with valsalva/standing = HOCM until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): document intensity change with maneuvers, presence of S4, family history of sudden cardiac death, plan for echo.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review</p></br><p>• Autosomal-dominant mutations in β-myosin heavy chain or myosin-binding protein-C.<br /></br>• Asymmetric septal hypertrophy + systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the mitral valve → dynamic LVOT narrowing.<br /></br>• ↓ Preload or ↓ afterload (Valsalva, standing, nitrates) brings septum &amp; anterior mitral leaflet closer → ↑ obstruction &amp; murmur.<br /></br>• ↑ Preload or ↑ afterload (squatting, hand-grip) ↓ murmur.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment (exam-relevant)</p></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>• Transthoracic echocardiogram: asymmetric septal thickness ≥15 mm, SAM, LVOT gradient.<br /></br>• ECG: LVH, deep septal Q waves.  </p></br><p>Management (first-line)<br /></br>1. β-blockers (metoprolol) → ↑ diastolic filling time, ↓ contractility.<br /></br>2. Non-dihydropyridine CCBs (verapamil) if β-blockers intolerant.<br /></br>3. Disopyramide for additional negative inotropy.<br /></br>4. Refractory: septal myectomy or alcohol septal ablation.<br /></br>5. Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) for high-risk sudden death.</p></br><p>Patient counseling: avoid dehydration, extreme exertion, and vasodilators (nitrates) that drop preload.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips</p></br><p>“HOCM gets LOUDER when the Heart is Hollow.”<br /></br>(Hollow = less preload = Valsalva/standing.)  </p></br><p>“AS radiates to the cArotids, HOCM radiates to the Heart apex.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</p></br><p>a. Aortic sclerosis<br /></br>   • Elderly; benign systolic ejection murmur at RUSB, no obstruction gradient, no S4. Maneuvers have minimal effect. Not compatible with 32-year-old.</p></br><p>b. Aortic stenosis<br /></br>   • Harsh crescendo–decrescendo, but best at RUSB, radiates to carotids, pulsus parvus et tardus (delayed, weak pulse), murmur QUIETS with Valsalva. Age typically &gt;60 unless bicuspid valve.</p></br><p>d. Mitral regurgitation<br /></br>   • Holosystolic “blowing” murmur loudest at apex, radiates to axilla, unchanged or ↑ with hand-grip, not crescendo–decrescendo and not affected by preload reduction in the same way.</p></br><p>e. Mitral stenosis<br /></br>   • Diastolic rumble with opening snap at apex, best heard in left lateral decubitus; often history of rheumatic fever; murmur intensity UNrelated to Valsalva the same way.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway</p></br><p>Dynamic LVOT obstruction that intensifies with Valsalva or standing in a young patient = Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy.</p>
111. A 57-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 1-day history of constant sharp chest pain and shortness of breath. The pain is located to the left of her sternum, increases when she lies on her back, and decreases when she sits and leans forward. She has had no cough, sputum production, recent trauma, or recent respiratory tract infection. She has hypertension and reactive airway disease. Medications are inhaled tiotropium, inhaled salmeterol-fluticasone, montelukast, amlodipine, and 81-mg aspirin. Her temperature is 37.6 C (99.6 F), pulse is 62/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 87/55 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 88 percent. Jugular venous pressure is 9 cm H2O. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Her hematocrit is 39 percent, leukocyte count is 15,800/mm3, and platelet count is 252,000/mm3. Serum electrolyte concentrations are within the reference ranges. An ECG shows diffuse ST-segment elevation. A CT scan of the chest with contrast is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Measurement of peak expiratory flow rate</br>b. Measurement of serum brain natriuretic peptide concentration</br>c. Measurement of serum cortisol concentration</br>d. Serum D-dimer assay</br>e. Blood cultures</br>f. Echocardiography</br>g. Pulmonary angiography</br></br></br><p>Correct answer provided by user: f. Echocardiography  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>VIGNETTE ANALYSIS<br /></br>1. Key “buzz-phrases” &amp; pertinent positives<br /></br>• Sharp, constant chest pain that ➚ when supine and ➘ when sitting up/leaning forward → classic for acute pericarditis.<br /></br>• Diffuse ST-segment elevation on ECG → reinforces pericarditis (versus localized ST-elevation of MI).<br /></br>• Hypotension (BP 87/55 mm Hg) + tachypnea + O2 sat 88 % → suggests hemodynamic compromise.<br /></br>• Mildly elevated JVP (9 cm H2O) → possible early tamponade.<br /></br>• CT chest with contrast obtained in ED (image not shown here) → could show a pericardial fluid stripe but CT is not the quickest bedside tool to determine if the effusion is causing tamponade physiology.</p></br><ol></br><li>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>• No trauma → rules out traumatic hemopericardium.<br /></br>• No cough/sputum or infection symptoms → makes pneumonia/sepsis less likely.<br /></br>• No RV strain pattern or PE risk factors given → PE less likely.</li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>Step 1: Identify pain character/ECG → acute pericarditis.<br /></br>Step 2: Note vitals → unstable (SBP &lt; 90 mm Hg).<br /></br>Step 3: When a pericarditis patient is hypotensive, immediately think about pericardial effusion → tamponade.<br /></br>Step 4: Best rapid, non-invasive test to confirm effusion &amp; tamponade physiology at bedside = transthoracic echocardiography (TTE).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>CLINICAL CRITICAL REASONING<br /></br>• Core takeaway: In any patient with pericarditis PLUS signs of shock, the priority is to look for pericardial tamponade with an emergent bedside echo.<br /></br>• TTE will show effusion size, right-atrial/ventricular diastolic collapse, and assess stroke volume. Findings guide urgent pericardiocentesis.</p></br><p>Documentation you would make: “Patient with suspected pericardial tamponade; emergent bedside transthoracic echocardiogram ordered to evaluate for effusion and chamber collapse.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>PATHOPHYSIOLOGY REVIEW<br /></br>Acute pericarditis → inflammation of pericardium → may lead to fluid accumulation in pericardial sac. If fluid accumulates faster than the pericardium can stretch, intrapericardial pressure rises → impaired diastolic filling → ↓ stroke volume → hypotension and shock (cardiac tamponade). Hallmarks:<br /></br>• Beck triad – hypotension, elevated JVP, muffled heart sounds (not always all three).<br /></br>• Pulsus paradoxus (&gt;10 mm Hg drop in SBP during inspiration).<br /></br>Echo is gold-standard bedside test to evaluate effusion and tamponade physiology.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>TREATMENT &amp; DIAGNOSTIC TESTS REVIEW<br /></br>Diagnostic:<br /></br>• STAT transthoracic echocardiography (portable in ED) – look for: large effusion, RA or RV diastolic collapse, IVC plethora, exaggerated respiratory variation in trans-mitral flow.<br /></br>Treatment:<br /></br>• If tamponade confirmed → urgent pericardiocentesis (US-guided) ± surgical drainage.<br /></br>• For uncomplicated pericarditis without tamponade → NSAIDs (e.g., high-dose ibuprofen) + colchicine.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>MEMORIZATION TIPS<br /></br>“Pericarditis Position Pain Pattern” mnemonic:<br /></br>P – Positional (worse supine, better forward)<br /></br>E – EKG diffuse ST elevation<br /></br>R – Rub (pericardial friction)<br /></br>I – Inflamed sac (pericardial effusion possible)<br /></br>C-A-R = Cardiac Arrest Risk → if hypotensive, think tamponade → Echo ASAP!</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>INCORRECT OPTION ANALYSIS<br /></br>a. Peak expiratory flow rate – Used for asthma/COPD exacerbation. Chest pain pattern and diffuse ST-elevation do not fit. No wheezing or typical asthma trigger.  </p></br><p>b. Serum BNP – Helps diagnose decompensated heart failure. HF pain is usually pressure-like, not positional sharp pain; CXR/echo would be first anyway.  </p></br><p>c. Serum cortisol – Evaluates adrenal insufficiency (e.g., hypotension, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia). Vital signs could resemble adrenal crisis, but positional chest pain &amp; diffuse ST-elevation are not explained by cortisol deficiency. Electrolytes are normal.  </p></br><p>d. Serum D-dimer – Screen for pulmonary embolism. CT angiography (already done) is definitive; positional pain and diffuse ST-elevation argue against PE.  </p></br><p>e. Blood cultures – For infectious pericarditis/endocarditis. Cultures do not address emergent hemodynamic compromise; imaging to detect tamponade takes precedence.  </p></br><p>g. Pulmonary angiography – Invasive test for PE when CT with contrast is inconclusive or contraindicated. CT already done; findings point toward pericardial pathology, not PE.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>BOTTOM LINE<br /></br>In a patient with pericarditis features who becomes hypotensive, always rule out cardiac tamponade with an emergent echocardiogram—the fastest and most informative next diagnostic step.</p>
112. A 25-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 2-day history of right calf pain that began after he played in a soccer match. The pain is worse with walking and resolves with rest or acetaminophen therapy. He is otherwise healthy and takes no other medications. There is no family history of serious illness. The patient traveled outside the USA by plane 4 months ago. He drinks 48 oz of beer weekly. He does not smoke cigarettes or use illicit drugs. His pulse is 85/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 98 percent. Skin examination shows ecchymoses over the anterior and lateral aspects of the right calf. There are no cords. The calves are symmetric in size, and there is no warmth. The right lower extremity is diffusely tender to deep palpation below the knee. Cardiopulmonary and neurologic examinations show no abnormalities. Gait is normal. Serum D-dimer concentration is less than 0.2 µg/mL (N<0.5) by ELISA. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Compression ultrasonography of the right lower extremity</br>b. CT pulmonary angiography</br>c. Measurement of prothrombin and partial thromboplastin times</br>d. MRI of the right lower extremity</br>e. Venography of the right lower extremity</br>f. No further testing is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>f. No further testing is indicated  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis – How to read this stem like the test-writer<br /></br>1. Temporal relationship: “Pain began after he played in a soccer match” → musculoskeletal mechanism likely.<br /></br>2. Physical exam: ecchymoses (bruising), no warmth, cords, or asymmetric swelling → classic for contusion/strain, not deep-vein thrombosis (DVT).<br /></br>3. Risk profile: 25-year-old, no immobilization, surgery, cancer, prior DVT, or OCPs. Only long plane ride 4 mo ago (remote). → Wells pre-test probability is LOW (≤1).<br /></br>4. Objective test supplied: high-sensitivity ELISA D-dimer 0.2 µg/mL (normal &lt;0.5). In a low-risk patient, a negative D-dimer virtually rules out acute DVT (&gt;99% NPV).<br /></br>Cognitive approach:<br /></br>• Step 1 – Assign Wells score. If ≤1 (low), go straight to sensitive D-dimer.<br /></br>• Step 2 – Negative D-dimer? You’re DONE. No imaging needed.<br /></br>Metacognitive tip: Any time the question already gives you a normal D-dimer in a clearly low-risk patient, they are testing whether you know to STOP (“Wells low + D-dimer low → you can go”).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Calculate Wells criteria quickly:<br /></br>   – Active cancer? 0<br /></br>   – Paralysis, paresis, recent cast? 0<br /></br>   – Recently bedridden ≥3 d or major surgery? 0<br /></br>   – Localized tenderness along deep system? 0 (diffuse tenderness ≠ Wells point)<br /></br>   – Entire leg swollen? 0<br /></br>   – Calf swelling ≥3 cm vs other leg? 0<br /></br>   – Pitting edema in symptomatic leg only? 0<br /></br>   – Collateral superficial veins? 0<br /></br>   – Alternative diagnosis at least as likely as DVT? +1 (contusion most likely)<br /></br>   Total = 1 → Low probability.<br /></br>2. In low probability:<br /></br>   – Negative D-dimer → rule out DVT, no further imaging (US or venography) and no anticoagulation needed.<br /></br>Key takeaway: Use structured clinical prediction (Wells) + D-dimer to avoid unnecessary imaging.  </p></br><p>What you would document: “Low Wells score (1). High-sensitivity D-dimer negative. DVT effectively excluded. Likely calf contusion; treat with RICE and NSAIDs.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• D-dimer = fibrin degradation product. Highly sensitive (&gt;95%) but non-specific.<br /></br>• Negative result in LOW pre-test probability population has high negative predictive value.<br /></br>• Contusion/strain: traumatic micro-tears of muscle fibers → ecchymosis, focal tenderness without deep venous involvement.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatments (concise)<br /></br>• No further DVT work-up.<br /></br>• Supportive care for muscle contusion: rest, ice, compression, elevation, NSAIDs/acetaminophen.<br /></br>• Return precautions: progressive swelling, redness, warmth, fever, or dyspnea (PE signs).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>“Low Wells, D-dimer Dwells below 0.5 → Don’t Delve further.”<br /></br>OR visualize a stop sign after a negative D-dimer in a slim green soccer player.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Compression ultrasonography – First-line imaging when (1) Wells ≥2 (moderate/high) OR (2) D-dimer positive. Not indicated when both pre-test probability and D-dimer are low.<br /></br>b. CT pulmonary angiography – Evaluates pulmonary embolism, not isolated calf pain; no respiratory symptoms, normal O2 sat.<br /></br>c. PT/PTT measurement – Useful for unexplained bleeding/clotting disorders, not acute DVT diagnosis. Calf contusion needs no coagulation study.<br /></br>d. MRI of the leg – Could delineate muscular injury but adds cost and is unnecessary; clinical exam suffices for simple contusion.<br /></br>e. Venography – Historical gold standard; invasive, contrast load. Reserved only when non-invasive tests are non-diagnostic but suspicion remains high.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Low clinical suspicion + negative high-sensitivity D-dimer rules out DVT. Do not chase additional tests—treat the obvious musculoskeletal injury and reassure the patient.</p>
113. Five days after discharge from the hospital following treatment of ischemic left hemispheric cerebral infarction, an 82-year-old man is brought to the physician because of repeated coughing after drinking liquids. Recovery in the hospital was complicated by right lower lobe pneumonia that resolved after treatment with antibiotics. The patient has lived in a skilled nursing care facility since discharge. He has no known allergies. His medications are lisinopril and aspirin. He currently is not in respiratory distress. Vital signs are within normal limits. Muscle strength is 3/5 in the right extremities and 5/5 in the left extremities. On mental status examination, he is alert and fully oriented. His speech is dysarthric, and he has occasional difficulty finding words. Which of the following is most likely to decrease this patient's risk for pulmonary complications?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Addition of metoclopramide with meals</br>b. Addition of a thickening agent to all liquids</br>c. Direct supervision when drinking liquids</br>d. Tracheostomy</br>e. Use of a straw when drinking liquids</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Addition of a thickening agent to all liquids  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues in the stem<br /></br>• 5-day-old hemispheric stroke → classic cause of oropharyngeal dysphagia.<br /></br>• Coughing “after drinking liquids” (thin fluids) → hallmark of aspiration of thin, low-viscosity substances.<br /></br>• Recent right-lower-lobe pneumonia → strongly suggests prior aspiration event.<br /></br>• Dysarthria and word-finding difficulty but intact alertness → cortical stroke with bulbar muscle incoordination, not global dementia.<br /></br>• Muscle weakness confined to right side (left MCA stroke) → no generalized neuromuscular disease.  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition<br /></br>Stroke + coughing with thin liquids + aspiration pneumonia history = oropharyngeal dysphagia → next step is to modify the diet (speech/swallow therapy) with thickened liquids to reduce aspiration.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach for future questions<br /></br>1. When a post-stroke patient coughs immediately after swallowing, think oropharyngeal dysphagia.<br /></br>2. First-line prevention of aspiration = dietary texture modification, upright positioning, swallow therapy—NOT prokinetics, straws, or invasive airways.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why thickened liquids work<br /></br>• Higher viscosity moves more slowly → gives impaired pharyngeal muscles extra time to coordinate epiglottic closure.<br /></br>• Bolus is easier to sense and control, decreasing spillover into the laryngeal vestibule.<br /></br>• Evidence-based: reduces incidence of aspiration pneumonia in post-stroke patients.  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real life)<br /></br>• Bedside or formal videofluoroscopic swallow evaluation results.<br /></br>• Diet order: “NPO thin liquids, nectar-thick liquids only; refer to speech pathology.”<br /></br>• Education of nursing staff on aspiration precautions.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>The safest, simplest, first-line way to lower aspiration risk in neurogenic oropharyngeal dysphagia is to increase liquid viscosity.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Swallowing has oral, pharyngeal, and esophageal phases.<br /></br>• Left MCA stroke often damages cortical areas controlling the pharyngeal phase → delayed or absent swallowing reflex, incomplete laryngeal elevation.<br /></br>• Thin liquids slip rapidly through the pharynx, reaching the larynx before the airway can be protected → cough, aspiration, pneumonia.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls  </p></br><ol></br><li>Immediate measures<br /></br>   – NPO for thin liquids; thickened liquids/soft solids.<br /></br>   – Sit upright ≥45° while eating.<br /></br>   – Supervised feeding by trained staff if needed.  </li></br><li>Further work-up<br /></br>   – Bedside swallow evaluation → videofluoroscopic modified barium swallow (gold standard).  </li></br><li>Long-term options if severe<br /></br>   – Enteral feeding (PEG) if recurrent aspiration despite therapy.  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Tips  </p></br><p>“Cough on the clear—make it thick to keep it near.”<br /></br>• Thin/clear = high aspiration risk.<br /></br>• Thick/nectar = safer post-stroke.  </p></br><p>Picture a milkshake (thick) versus water (thin). A shaky epiglottis can corral the milkshake but water runs straight past it.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Metoclopramide: A dopamine antagonist that enhances gastric emptying, useful for gastroparesis. Oropharyngeal dysphagia is not due to delayed gastric motility.  </p></br><p>C. Direct supervision alone: Watching the patient does not change the impaired swallow physiology; aspirated material is still thin liquid. Supervision is adjunctive, not definitive.  </p></br><p>D. Tracheostomy: Provides an alternate airway but does NOT prevent aspiration; oropharyngeal secretions can still enter the trachea above the stoma. Reserved for prolonged mechanical ventilation or severe airway obstruction, not routine post-stroke dysphagia.  </p></br><p>E. Straw use: Increases flow rate of liquid into the oropharynx and actually can worsen aspiration risk in dysphagic patients.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Post-stroke coughing with liquids signals neurogenic oropharyngeal dysphagia. Thickening all liquids is the simplest, evidence-based intervention to cut aspiration pneumonia risk.</p>
114. A 32-year-old woman with Crohn disease is being placed on total parenteral nutritional support. During insertion of a central venous catheter into the left subclavian vein, she has the sudden onset of severe chest pain, dyspnea, and agitation. She is supine, and a sterile drape is placed over her. Her medications are infliximab and prednisone. She appears malnourished and anxious. She is 157 cm (5 ft 2 in) tall and weighs 45 kg (100 lb); BMI is 18 kg/m2. Examination shows diaphoresis.	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Leave the patient supine and reassure her that the procedure is going well</br>b. Remove the sterile drape and seat the patient in the upright position</br>c. Place the patient into Trendelenburg and left lateral decubitus position</br>d. Attempt to place the catheter in the right subclavian vein</br>e. Administer intravenous lorazepam and perform pericardiocentesis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>C. Place the patient into Trendelenburg and left-lateral-decubitus position (Durant maneuver)</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• “Central venous catheter … sudden onset of severe chest pain, dyspnea, and agitation” immediately after opening of a large central vein → classic setup for venous air embolism.<br /></br>• Patient is supine with a large sterile drape: air entering the line can rapidly travel to the right heart / pulmonary circulation.<br /></br>• No mention of unilateral breath-sound loss, tracheal deviation, or JVD to suggest tension pneumothorax; no hypotension/muffled heart sounds to suggest tamponade.<br /></br>Pattern: Acute cardiopulmonary decompensation seconds–minutes after manipulation of central venous lines = think air embolus.</p></br><p>Cognitive approach for future questions:<br /></br>1. Ask, “What can go wrong immediately during/after a CVC?” → air embolus, tension PTX, arterial puncture.<br /></br>2. Look for timing (seconds), symptoms (air hunger, sudden chest pain, agitation), and absence of lung findings = air embolus.<br /></br>3. Recall immediate bedside maneuver (Durant) + 100 % O₂.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Goal: prevent air from entering pulmonary artery or cerebral circulation and buy time for definitive therapy (aspiration via the catheter or hyperbaric O₂).<br /></br>• Trendelenburg (head-down) + left-lateral decubitus traps the air bubble in the apex of the right atrium and right ventricle, away from the RV outflow tract, reducing risk of “air-lock” and pulmonary circulation obstruction.<br /></br>• Also decreases risk of paradoxical embolization across a PFO to the brain.<br /></br>Core takeaway: In any suspected venous air embolism, immediately place the patient in Durant position and give 100 % oxygen.</p></br><p>Documentation points if real patient: time of onset, vital signs, positioning maneuver performed, amount of air aspirated (if any), O₂ delivery, hemodynamic response, plan for hyperbaric therapy.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Venous air embolism = entrainment of ≥0.5–1 mL/kg of air into central veins.<br /></br>• Negative intrathoracic pressure during spontaneous inspiration or when catheter hub is open → air sucked into vein.<br /></br>• Air collects in RA/RV → obstructs RV outflow (air-lock) → acute pulmonary vascular obstruction, hypoxia, and decreased preload → chest pain, dyspnea, agitation, cardiovascular collapse.<br /></br>Risk factors: CVC placement/removal, neurosurgery in sitting position, trauma to large neck veins, hemodialysis lines.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>Immediate:<br /></br>1. Durant maneuver (answer choice C).<br /></br>2. 100 % O₂ (helps shrink N₂ bubble via diffusion).<br /></br>3.
