If possible, aspirate air through the central line.<br /></br>Definitive: hyperbaric oxygen therapy for severe cases.</p></br><p>Evaluation: bedside echo may show “snowstorm” appearance of air in right heart; but positioning is done first—time-critical.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>“CVC AIR”<br /></br>C – Central line manipulation<br /></br>V – Venous air enters<br /></br>C – Chest pain / dyspnea suddenly<br /></br>A – Action = Air left-side down (left lateral)<br /></br>I – In Trendelenburg<br /></br>R – Respirate with 100 % O₂</p></br><p>Picture tilting a soda bottle: tilt it upside-down so the bubbles rise to the bottom (apex) and stay away from the outlet.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Incorrect  </p></br><p>A. Leave the patient supine and reassure her<br /></br>• Does nothing to prevent further air movement; no therapeutic action—patients can deteriorate rapidly.</p></br><p>B. Remove drape and seat upright<br /></br>• Upright position makes the air rise toward pulmonary outflow/cerebral circulation, worsening obstruction or causing stroke.</p></br><p>D. Attempt right-sided catheter placement<br /></br>• Ignores the acute complication already in progress; adds risk of repeating the event or causing additional injury.</p></br><p>E. IV lorazepam + pericardiocentesis<br /></br>• Pericardiocentesis treats tamponade (Beck triad: hypotension, muffled heart sounds, JVD), not present here. Lorazepam addresses anxiety but not the life-threatening cause.</p></br><p>Common pitfall: confusing venous air embolism with tension pneumothorax (managed by needle decompression) or tamponade. Key discriminator is the immediate temporal relation to opening the venous system and lack of unilateral breath-sound findings.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Take-Home Message<br /></br>During or immediately after central line manipulation, sudden dyspnea and chest pain = suspect venous air embolism. First step: Durant maneuver (Trendelenburg + left-lateral decubitus) and 100 % oxygen to trap and shrink the air bubble before definitive management.</p>
115. An 82-year-old man comes to the physician for a follow-up examination 1 week after discharge from the hospital, where he received treatment for streptococcal pneumonia. He received a 7-day course of intravenous ceftriaxone in the hospital, then completed a 4-day course of oral doxycycline at home. His fever, shortness of breath, cough, and chills resolved by the time of hospital discharge. He has hypertension and hyperlipidemia. His current medications are metoprolol, lisinopril, and atorvastatin. He smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 50 years but quit 10 years ago. His blood pressure is 128/84 mm Hg. Breath sounds are decreased at the right lung base; no crackles, rhonchi, or rubs are heard. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:  </br>Hemoglobin 13.8 g/dL  </br>Hematocrit 41 percent  </br>Leukocyte count 5200/mm3  </br></br>A sputum culture shows no leukocytes or organisms. A chest x-ray is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. CT scan of the chest  </br>b. Lung biopsy  </br>c. Pleural biopsy  </br>d. Repeat sputum culture  </br>e. No further testing is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. No further testing is indicated  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key time line clue – Only 1 week has elapsed since hospital discharge and &lt;2 weeks since completion of all antibiotics.<br /></br>• Clinical course – Fever, cough, dyspnea, and leukocytosis have completely resolved; the patient feels well.<br /></br>• Examination – Mildly decreased breath sounds at the right base are typical of residual atelectasis or a small resolving effusion after lobar pneumonia. There are no crackles, egophony, or signs of volume loss that would suggest an unresolving process.<br /></br>• Laboratory data – Normal leukocyte count; sputum with no leukocytes or organisms (a sterile specimen).<br /></br>• Imaging (not shown) – In elderly patients, a residual opacity, blunting of the costophrenic angle, or streaky infiltrate often persists for weeks after clinical cure of pneumonia.  </p></br><p>Cognitive approach for test-takers<br /></br>1. Ask: “Has the patient clinically recovered?” Yes → think benign, post-infectious changes.<br /></br>2. Ask: “How long has it been?” &lt;4–6 weeks → radiographic clearing lags behind symptoms, especially in the elderly.<br /></br>3. If both answers are reassuring, additional testing now is unnecessary.  </p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Radiographic infiltrates from community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) typically resolve in:<br /></br>• ~4 weeks in healthy adults<br /></br>• 6–8+ weeks in elderly or those with comorbidities  </p></br><p>Because this patient is asymptomatic and afebrile with a normal WBC count, the residual chest-x-ray abnormality is almost certainly post-infectious. Current guidelines recommend repeating a chest radiograph at 6–8 weeks (outpatient) in patients &gt;50 years or with significant smoking history to ensure complete clearing, but no immediate imaging or invasive test is warranted.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Clinical resolution trumps early radiographic persistence; do not chase a still-opaque film in an otherwise recovered patient. Document in the note: “Symptoms resolved, vitals stable, residual radiographic changes expected—will obtain follow-up CXR in 6 weeks.”  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• During CAP, alveoli fill with exudate.<br /></br>• After bacterial eradication, macrophages slowly resorb debris, and lymphatics clear fluid—thus radiographic clearing is delayed.<br /></br>• Elderly patients have diminished mucociliary clearance and slower macrophage activity, so infiltrates persist longer.  </p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. CT scan of the chest – Indicated if symptoms persist, there are recurrent pneumonias in the same lobe, suspicion of lung cancer, abscess, or empyema. None apply here; CT would only add radiation and cost.<br /></br>b. Lung biopsy – Reserved for diagnosing interstitial lung disease or unexplained pulmonary nodules/masses. No indication in a clinically resolved pneumonia.<br /></br>c. Pleural biopsy – Used for undiagnosed exudative pleural effusions (e.g., TB, malignancy). Patient lacks a significant effusion or suggestive symptoms.<br /></br>d. Repeat sputum culture – Appropriate when infection has not clinically improved or multidrug resistance is suspected. Patient is already cured.  </p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>• “Symptoms clear first, X-rays lag behind” – think: Symptom vs. Shadow lag (“S-S lag”).<br /></br>• Rule of 6’s: In elders, expect ~6 weeks for a CXR to clear; schedule the follow-up then, not sooner.</p>
116. A 49-year-old man is admitted to the hospital because of a 2-year history of progressive, oily, foul-smelling diarrhea and an unintentional 18-kg (40-lb) weight loss. He has not had nausea, vomiting, melena, or rectal bleeding. He also has a 5-year history of intermittent joint pain and swelling of the wrists, hips, and knees. His wife reports that his memory has declined during the past month. He has no other history of serious illness and takes no medications. He is a farmer. He is 178 cm (5 ft 10 in) tall and weighs 57 kg (125 lb); BMI is 18 kg/m2. His temperature is 36.3  C (97.3  F), pulse is 88/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 124/82 mm Hg. Examination shows cervical, axillary, and inguinal lymphadenopathy. The abdomen is nontender to palpation; no masses are palpated. There is no hepatosplenomegaly. Bowel sounds are normal. Test of the stool for occult blood is positive. Endoscopic biopsy specimen of the small intestine shows numerous periodic acid–Schiff–positive macrophages in the lamina propria. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Ceftriaxone therapy followed by trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole therapy</br>b. Omeprazole, amoxicillin, and clarithromycin therapy</br>c. Pancreatic enzyme therapy</br>d. Prednisone therapy</br>e. Recommendation for gluten-free diet</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A.  Ceftriaxone therapy followed by trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole therapy</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • Chronic oily, foul-smelling diarrhea + 18-kg weight loss → fat malabsorption<br /></br>   • 5-yr history of migratory large-joint arthralgias (wrists, hips, knees)<br /></br>   • New cognitive decline (CNS involvement)<br /></br>   • Generalized lymphadenopathy<br /></br>   • Small-bowel biopsy: “numerous periodic-acid–Schiff (PAS)-positive macrophages in lamina propria”  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>How these clues narrow the differential<br /></br>   • Fat malabsorption + PAS-positive macrophages is classic for Whipple disease (Tropheryma whipplei).<br /></br>   • Extra-GI manifestations (arthralgias, CNS changes, LAD) are also hallmarks.<br /></br>   • PAS positivity rules out celiac sprue (villous atrophy, not foamy macrophages) and pancreatic insufficiency (normal mucosa).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No abdominal pain, pancreatitis history, alcohol use → argues against chronic pancreatitis.<br /></br>   • No dermatitis herpetiformis → argues against celiac sprue.<br /></br>   • Normal vitals; no IBD signs of bloody diarrhea.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Test-taking tip: When you see malabsorption + PAS-positive foamy macrophages, think “Whipple” immediately. The buzz phrase is nearly pathognomonic.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Identify Whipple disease from biopsy and systemic symptoms.<br /></br>2. Recall standard therapy: an initial parenteral antibiotic that penetrates the blood–brain barrier (ceftriaxone or penicillin G) for 2–4 weeks, followed by prolonged oral therapy (commonly TMP-SMX for 1 year) to eradicate organisms and prevent relapse, especially of CNS disease.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Whipple disease requires long-course, CNS-penetrating antibiotics because the organism is intracellular and relapses in the brain are common if therapy is stopped early.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Record extra-intestinal manifestations (arthralgias, neurologic symptoms, lymphadenopathy) and justify prolonged antibiotic plan with CNS coverage.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Etiology: Tropheryma whipplei, a Gram-positive actinomycete.<br /></br>• Pathogenesis: Bacteria accumulate within macrophages → foamy, PAS-positive cytoplasm (glycoprotein-rich cell wall). Macrophage-laden villi block chylomicron flow → fat malabsorption → steatorrhea &amp; weight loss.<br /></br>• Classic triad: 1) Steatorrhea, 2) Weight loss, 3) Migratory arthralgias. Added findings: LAD, hyperpigmentation, endocarditis, CNS (dementia, myoclonus, ophthalmoplegia).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><ol></br><li>Confirm diagnosis: small-bowel biopsy (already done); PCR for T. whipplei or electron microscopy can support.  </li></br><li>Treatment<br /></br>   • First-line: IV ceftriaxone (or penicillin G) for 2–4 weeks.<br /></br>   • Follow with oral TMP-SMX (double-strength) for ≥1 year.<br /></br>   • Monitor with periodic CSF or stool PCR if available; relapse can occur.  </li></br></ol></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic: “FOAMY WHIPPED cream in a CAN”<br /></br>FOAMY macrophages<br /></br>WHIPPed (Whipple)<br /></br>Cardiac, Arthralgias, Neurologic involvement  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>B. Omeprazole + amoxicillin + clarithromycin (triple therapy for Helicobacter pylori)<br /></br>   • Treats peptic ulcer disease, not small-bowel malabsorption. No gastric biopsy or ulcer symptoms in vignette.  </p></br><p>C. Pancreatic enzyme therapy<br /></br>   • Used for exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (e.g., chronic pancreatitis, cystic fibrosis). Would show low fecal elastase and normal intestinal biopsy, not PAS-positive macrophages.  </p></br><p>D. Prednisone therapy<br /></br>   • Steroids would transiently improve arthralgias but worsen underlying infection and delay diagnosis. In Whipple disease, immunosuppression can lead to fulminant progression.  </p></br><p>E. Gluten-free diet<br /></br>   • Treats celiac sprue. Celiac biopsy shows villous atrophy with increased intraepithelial lymphocytes, not PAS-positive macrophages. Arthralgias and CNS disease are uncommon in celiac.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>PAS-positive macrophages + malabsorption + systemic symptoms = Whipple disease. Treat with CNS-penetrating IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone) followed by prolonged oral TMP-SMX.</p>
117. A 47-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 1-year history of difficulty swallowing liquids and solids. She says she often feels food and drink stick in the middle of her chest. For 8 weeks, she has been awakening at night after regurgitating partially digested food. After meals, she has mild heartburn that is occasionally associated with a mild sensation of fullness and discomfort in her chest. The heartburn is not relieved by over-the-counter antacids. She has had a 3.2-kg (7-lb) weight loss during the past 6 months. She has no history of serious illness. She does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcohol. She takes a daily multivitamin. Examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a.  Achalasia</br>b.  Esophageal carcinoma</br>c.  Esophageal spasm</br>d.  Gastroesophageal reflux disease</br>e.  Pharyngoesophageal (Zenker) diverticulum</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A.  Achalasia  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Dysphagia pattern<br /></br>   • “Difficulty swallowing liquids and solids” from the beginning → simultaneous dysphagia to both points to a motility disorder (achalasia, diffuse esophageal spasm). Mechanical obstruction (eg, cancer, stricture) classically begins with solids only and later progresses to liquids.  </li></br><li>Regurgitation of UNDIGESTED food, especially when lying flat → typical for achalasia (food pools above a non-relaxing LES and passively flows back).  </li></br><li>Night-time awakening with regurgitation/aspiration → again fits achalasia.  </li></br><li>Heartburn not relieved by antacids → the burning is from food stasis/fermentation, not acid reflux.  </li></br><li>Mild weight loss (7 lb over 6 mo) but otherwise healthy 47-y/o, no smoking/alcohol → less supportive of malignancy.  </li></br><li>Normal physical exam → oropharyngeal &amp; neurologic causes less likely.</li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive road-map for similar questions:<br /></br>Step 1 – Decide if the dysphagia is oropharyngeal (trouble initiating swallow, coughing, nasal regurg) vs esophageal (food “sticks” in chest).<br /></br>Step 2 – In esophageal dysphagia, ask: “solids then liquids” (obstruction) or “liquids &amp; solids together” (motility).<br /></br>Step 3 – Match ancillary clues (regurg of undigested food, nocturnal cough, response to antacids, risk factors) to the most common motility disorders.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why Achalasia)  </p></br><p>Achalasia = failure of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) to relax due to degeneration of inhibitory ganglion cells (NO/VIP) in the myenteric (Auerbach) plexus →<br /></br>• Aperistalsis of distal esophagus<br /></br>• High LES resting pressure<br /></br>• Functional obstruction → retained food → progressive dysphagia, regurgitation, fermentative heartburn, mild weight loss.</p></br><p>Documentation points if this were a real patient: chronic progressive esophageal dysphagia (liquids = solids), nocturnal regurgitation, no risk factors for malignancy, suspect achalasia; plan barium swallow + esophageal manometry.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Etiology: idiopathic degeneration (most common in USA), Chagas disease (Trypanosoma cruzi), paraneoplastic.<br /></br>Key mechanisms: loss of inhibitory (NO, VIP) neurons → LES fails to relax (&gt;45 mm Hg) and absent peristaltic waves.  </p></br><p>Clinical manifestations:<br /></br>• Progressive dysphagia to both solids &amp; liquids<br /></br>• Regurgitation of saliva/undigested food, especially when supine<br /></br>• Chest discomfort/heartburn not helped by antacids<br /></br>• Weight loss, risk of aspiration pneumonia<br /></br>• ↑ risk of esophageal squamous cell carcinoma over time.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment  </p></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>1. Barium swallow – “bird-beak” tapering of distal esophagus.<br /></br>2. Esophageal manometry (gold standard) – ↑ LES tone, incomplete relaxation, aperistalsis.<br /></br>3. Endoscopy – done to rule out pseudoachalasia from obstructing cancer.  </p></br><p>Treatment hierarchy<br /></br>• Pneumatic balloon dilation of LES<br /></br>• Heller surgical myotomy ± partial fundoplication<br /></br>• Peroral endoscopic myotomy (POEM)<br /></br>• Temporary/poor surgical candidates: botulinum toxin injection, nitrates, calcium-channel blockers.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>• “A-chalasia = A bsent peristalsis.”<br /></br>• Picture a bird beak squeezing the esophagus shut → barium swallow finding.<br /></br>• Solids + Liquids together → think mOtility (both contain the letter “O”).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>B. Esophageal carcinoma<br /></br>– Age usually &gt;55 with risk factors (smoking, alcohol, Barrett)<br /></br>– Dysphagia classically starts with solids only, then progresses to liquids; often rapid, marked weight loss.<br /></br>– Regurg of partially digested food is uncommon.  </p></br><p>C. Diffuse esophageal spasm<br /></br>– Causes intermittent, not progressive, dysphagia and severe chest pain precipitated by hot/cold liquids; barium shows corkscrew esophagus. Night-time regurg of large volumes is unusual.  </p></br><p>D. Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD)<br /></br>– Heartburn relieved by antacids/PPI is typical; significant dysphagia implies a stricture or Barrett’s complication, which would first affect solids. Regurg is acidic, not undigested; antacids would help.  </p></br><p>E. Zenker (pharyngoesophageal) diverticulum<br /></br>– Oropharyngeal dysphagia (immediately after initiating swallow), halitosis, coughing, gurgling in the neck; seen in elderly (&gt;60 y/o). Food gets stuck in the throat/neck, not mid-chest.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>Simultaneous dysphagia to liquids and solids + nocturnal regurgitation of undigested food that is NOT relieved by antacids is classic for achalasia, a primary esophageal motility disorder due to failed LES relaxation.</p>
118. A 35-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 4-month history of progressive fatigue, generalized itching, and dry eyes and mouth. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Her pulse is 80/min, and blood pressure is 123/77 mm Hg. Examination shows excoriations over the extremities. The liver span is 20 cm. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:</br>Serum</br>Glucose 88 mg/dL</br>Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL</br>Total bilirubin 1.5 mg/dL</br>Alkaline phosphatase 300 U/L</br>AST 30 U/L</br>ALT 35 U/L</br>Antimitochondrial antibodies positive</br>Liver biopsy shows lymphocytic infiltrates in portal tracts with loss of medium-sized interlobar bile ducts. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Ursodeoxycholic acid</br>b. Azathioprine</br>c. Colchicine</br>d. Methotrexate</br>e. Prednisone</br>f. Propranolol</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>f. Propranolol  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Key “buzz phrases”<br /></br>   • Progressive fatigue + generalized pruritus + markedly ↑alkaline phosphatase + positive antimitochondrial antibody (AMA) → classic pattern for primary biliary cholangitis/cirrhosis (PBC).<br /></br>   • Liver span 20 cm (hepatomegaly) suggests the disease is already producing architectural distortion that can progress to portal-hypertension complications.<br /></br>   • Biopsy: “loss of medium-sized interlobar ducts with lymphocytic infiltrates” → confirms PBC.  </li></br><li>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • No mention of alcohol use, viral hepatitis serologies, or medication exposure → rules out many other causes of cholestasis.<br /></br>   • Normal AST/ALT argues against predominant hepatocellular injury (e.g., autoimmune hepatitis).  </li></br><li>Test-taking strategy (metacognitive)<br /></br>   • USMLE questions on chronic cholestatic liver disease often test two distinct management issues:<br /></br>     a) Disease-modifying therapy (slows PBC progression).<br /></br>     b) Prevention of fatal complications of cirrhosis (eg, variceal hemorrhage).<br /></br>   • Read the stem carefully for clues that the examiner is asking about the latter rather than the former—liver size, chronicity, and lack of other findings may point to portal-hypertension prophylaxis.  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step 1 – Diagnose the underlying condition: PBC.<br /></br>Step 2 – Anticipate its major morbidity/mortality risk: cirrhosis → portal hypertension → esophageal variceal bleeding.<br /></br>Step 3 – Decide on the ONE intervention from the list that most directly addresses that life-threatening complication: a non-selective β-blocker (propranolol or nadolol) lowers portal venous inflow and is first-line primary prophylaxis against variceal hemorrhage in cirrhotics.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: In any patient with established cirrhosis—regardless of etiology—primary prophylaxis against variceal bleeding with a non-selective β-blocker is essential and lifesaving.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: “Initiated non-selective β-blocker therapy (propranolol 20 mg orally BID) for primary prevention of esophageal varices/variceal hemorrhage in the setting of presumed cirrhosis due to primary biliary cholangitis.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• PBC: Autoimmune-mediated, T-cell destruction of small/medium intrahepatic bile ducts → cholestasis → fibrosis → micronodular cirrhosis → portal hypertension.<br /></br>• Portal hypertension elevates pressure in collateral veins (left gastric &amp; esophageal) → variceal formation &amp; bleeding.<br /></br>• Non-selective β-blockers (β1 + β2 blockade) reduce:<br /></br>  – β1: cardiac output → ↓splanchnic arterial inflow.<br /></br>  – β2: unopposed α-adrenergic vasoconstriction in splanchnic venous bed → ↓portal flow.<br /></br>Result = ∼15–20 % reduction in portal pressure &amp; ∼45 % reduction in first variceal bleed.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><ol></br><li>Disease-modifying therapy for PBC<br /></br>   • Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) – slows progression, improves transplant-free survival.<br /></br>   • Obeticholic acid – second-line if inadequate UDCA response.  </li></br><li>Portal-hypertension management<br /></br>   • Screening EGD at diagnosis of cirrhosis.<br /></br>   • If varices present (or if unable to perform EGD promptly), start non-selective β-blocker (propranolol/nadolol) or endoscopic variceal ligation.<br /></br>   • Large varices or red-wale signs → mandatory β-blocker.  </li></br><li>Symptomatic therapies<br /></br>   • Cholestyramine or rifampin for pruritus.<br /></br>   • Artificial tears/pilocarpine for Sicca symptoms.  </li></br><li>Definitive cure for end-stage disease: liver transplantation.  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“BBC”:<br /></br>• B-blocker for Bleeding (variceal) Control in any Cirrhosis.<br /></br>• As soon as a patient crosses the “C” (cirrhosis) threshold, think β-Blocker.  </p></br><p>“Urso SLOWS, Beta BLOWS”:<br /></br>• Urso “slows” disease progression;<br /></br>• β-blocker “blows” (reduces) portal pressure to prevent a blow-out bleed.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Ursodeoxycholic acid – Indicated for all PBC patients to slow progression, but it does NOT directly lower portal pressure or immediately prevent hemorrhage. Question focuses on life-threatening complication management → propranolol preferred.  </p></br><p>b. Azathioprine – Immunosuppressant for autoimmune hepatitis/inflammatory bowel disorders; no proven benefit in PBC.  </p></br><p>c. Colchicine – Occasionally studied for cirrhosis but no clear mortality benefit and not used for PBC/portal-HTN prophylaxis.  </p></br><p>d. Methotrexate – Used for psoriasis, rheumatoid arthritis; can actually cause hepatotoxicity. Not appropriate.  </p></br><p>e. Prednisone – Mainstay for autoimmune hepatitis; steroids do not help and may worsen cholestatic liver disease (PBC).  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Once PBC has advanced toward cirrhosis, the most urgent pharmacologic intervention to prevent catastrophic mortality is non-selective β-blockade (propranolol) for primary prophylaxis of variceal bleeding; disease-modifying agents such as UDCA should also be started but are secondary to life-saving bleed prevention in this context.</p>
119. A 72-year-old woman is hospitalized because of a 3-day history of increasingly severe central abdominal pain and bilious vomiting. Her only medications are hydrochlorothiazide and atorvastatin for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, respectively. She does not smoke cigarettes and drinks one glass of wine daily. Temperature is 38.2 C (100.8 F), pulse is 95/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 120/75 mm Hg. Abdominal examination shows moderate epigastric and periumbilical tenderness without rebound. Results of laboratory studies are shown:</br>Total bilirubin       1.1 mg/dL</br>Alkaline phosphatase  180 U/L</br>AST                   40 U/L</br>ALT                   30 U/L</br>Amylase               350 U/L</br>Lipase                500 U/L (N=14–280)</br>Which of the following is the most likely cause of these findings?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Hyperlipidemia</br>b. Infection</br>c. Lithogenic bile</br>d. Medication adverse effect</br>e. Substance abuse</br></br></br><p>Correct answer<br /></br>c. Lithogenic bile (gallstone-induced pancreatitis)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test–Taking Approach</br>• Key words/phrases: “72-year-old woman,” “central/epigastric abdominal pain,” “bilious vomiting,” temperature 38.2 °C, marked elevations of amylase (350 U/L) and lipase (500 U/L).<br /></br>• LFT pattern: normal bilirubin, mildly elevated AST/ALT, but alkaline phosphatase is disproportionately high (180 U/L) → cholestatic picture suggesting obstruction of the common bile duct.<br /></br>• Epidemiology: Elderly female = classic demographic for gallstone disease.<br /></br>Metacognitive pearl: In any pancreatitis question, immediately ask, “Which of the ‘GET SMASHED’ causes fits best?” then use (1) history, (2) lab pattern, (3) risk factors to rule in/out each possibility.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step 1 — Diagnose acute pancreatitis: Epigastric pain radiating centrally + vomiting + lipase &gt;3× normal fulfills Atlanta criteria.<br /></br>Step 2 — Identify etiology: The two most common causes are gallstones (∼40–70 %) and chronic alcohol (∼25–35 %). This patient drinks minimal alcohol and has a cholestatic LFT pattern → gallstone migration into the ampulla → transient blockage of pancreatic duct → pancreatic enzyme activation → inflammation.<br /></br>Core takeaway: In older women with pancreatitis and cholestatic labs, gallstone (lithogenic bile) etiology should be your first thought.</p></br><p>What would be documented in a real note: suspected gallstone pancreatitis, Ranson’s criteria on admission, order RUQ ultrasound, NPO, aggressive IV fluids, analgesia, consider ERCP if cholangitis/obstruction persists.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology High-Yield Review</br>• Lithogenic bile = supersaturated with cholesterol → gallstone formation in gallbladder.<br /></br>• A small stone can pass into the common bile duct and lodge transiently at the ampulla of Vater, obstructing both the pancreatic duct and CBD.<br /></br>• Stasis + pressure → premature trypsin activation → autodigestion, edema, inflammation of pancreas.<br /></br>• Alkaline phosphatase and sometimes bilirubin rise because of biliary obstruction; AST/ALT only mildly elevated.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Work-Up</br>1. Initial management of ANY pancreatitis:<br /></br>   • Aggressive isotonic IV fluids (e.g., LR), pain control (IV opioids), NPO.<br /></br>2. Etiology-specific:<br /></br>   • RUQ ultrasound → look for gallstones/CBD dilation.<br /></br>   • If stone suspected in CBD or cholangitis (fever, jaundice), perform ERCP with sphincterotomy ± stone extraction.<br /></br>   • Cholecystectomy during same hospitalization once inflammation cools to prevent recurrence.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>Mnemonic “GET SMASHED” for pancreatitis causes:<br /></br>G-Gallstones, E-Ethanol, T-Trauma, S-Steroids, M-Mumps/Malignancy, A-Autoimmune, S-Scorpion, H-Hypercalcemia/Hypertriglyceridemia, E-ERCP, D-Drugs (sulfa, azathioprine, thiazides, valproate).<br /></br>Visual aid: Picture a small pebble blocking the common channel of a plumbing system labeled “bile” and “pancreas” to recall pathophysiology.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>a. Hyperlipidemia (hypertriglyceridemia): Would show triglycerides &gt;1000 mg/dL, often in younger or diabetic patients; no cholestatic LFT pattern.<br /></br>b. Infection: Viral (mumps, coxsackie) pancreatitis more common in children; there is no prodromal URI or parotitis; alk phos usually normal.<br /></br>d. Medication adverse effect: Hydrochlorothiazide can rarely cause pancreatitis, but drug-induced cases generally lack the cholestatic LFT elevation and stone-prone demographic; plus she’s been on it chronically without issue.<br /></br>e. Substance abuse (ethanol): Requires heavy, chronic intake (≥4–5 drinks/day). One glass of wine daily is far below that threshold; alcoholic pancreatitis also typically shows AST &gt; ALT and no alk-phos bump.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Key Take-Home Message</br>Gallstone (lithogenic bile) pancreatitis presents with acute epigastric pain, elevated amylase/lipase, and cholestatic liver enzymes—especially in older women with minimal alcohol use. Always order an RUQ ultrasound and plan for cholecystectomy after stabilization.</p>
120. A 16-day-old girl is brought to the physician for a well-child examination. She has a 1-week history of increasingly yellow skin. She was born at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery. Her birth weight was 3005 g (6 lb 10 oz). Newborn screening showed no abnormalities. She receives no medications. She is breast-feeding well. Today, she weighs 3062 g (6 lb 12 oz). Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows generalized jaundice and scleral icterus. Laboratory studies show a serum bilirubin concentration of 15 mg/dL, with a direct component of 10 mg/dL and an indirect component of 5 mg/dL. Urinalysis shows no reducing substances or por. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Weekly monitoring of serum bilirubin concentration</br>b. Abdominal ultrasonography</br>c. Upper gastrointestinal series</br>d. Oral administration of bile salts</br>e. Oral neomycin therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Abdominal ultrasonography  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-taking Strategy<br /></br>1. Age &amp; Timing: 16-day-old (≈ 2 weeks) with jaundice that is becoming more pronounced rather than resolving.<br /></br>2. Lab pattern: Total bilirubin 15 mg/dL, DIRECT (conjugated) 10 mg/dL, indirect 5 mg/dL → direct fraction &gt; 20 % of total → pathologic conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.<br /></br>3. Well appearing, breast-feeding adequately, no hemolysis, no meds → physiologic or breast-milk jaundice (both unconjugated) ruled out.<br /></br>4. Neonatal cholestasis must be evaluated quickly because some causes (especially biliary atresia) are surgically correctable only if treated early (&lt; 6-8 wk).<br /></br>Metacognitive tip: For any infant &gt; 14 days with direct bilirubin ≥ 2 mg/dL or &gt; 20 % of total, jump out of the “wait and see” mindset and start a cholestasis work-up; imaging comes first.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why ultrasound is first)<br /></br>Step 1: Confirm conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.<br /></br>Step 2: Look for obstructive vs. metabolic etiologies.<br /></br>• Non-invasive, no radiation, and quickly shows gallbladder size/absence, common bile duct dilation, choledochal cysts, or other anatomic obstruction = abdominal US.<br /></br>Step 3: If US suggests biliary atresia (small or absent GB, absent CBD) → hepatobiliary iminodiacetic acid (HIDA) scan with phenobarbital priming &amp; surgical referral for Kasai portoenterostomy.<br /></br>Bottom line: Abdominal ultrasonography is the mandated first imaging test in the algorithm for neonatal cholestasis.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Bilirubin conjugation occurs in hepatocytes via UDP-glucuronyl transferase.<br /></br>• Conjugated (direct) bilirubin is water-soluble; buildup reflects impaired excretion (biliary atresia, choledochal cyst, Alagille, inspissated bile) or intrahepatic cholestasis (alpha-1-antitrypsin deficiency, infections, metabolic disorders).<br /></br>• Biliary atresia = progressive fibro-obliteration of extrahepatic bile ducts beginning after birth; presents 2-6 wk with jaundice, acholic stools, dark urine, hepatomegaly. Surgical drainage (Kasai) before 2 months dramatically improves outcomes; hence urgent evaluation.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Work-up Snapshot<br /></br>1. Initial tests: Fractionated bilirubin, AST/ALT, GGT, PT/INR → already know bilirubin pattern.<br /></br>2. First-line imaging: Abdominal US.<br /></br>3. If obstructive picture or US equivocal → HIDA scan (after 5 days phenobarbital to ↑ bile flow).<br /></br>4. Confirmatory cholangiogram ± intra-op liver biopsy.<br /></br>5. Management directed at cause (Kasai vs. medical therapy vs. transplant).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“D-CONJ at TWO weeks → Do an UltraSound.”<br /></br>D = Direct, CONJ = Conjugated, TWO = &gt; 2 wk or &gt; 20 % direct, US = Ultrasound.<br /></br>Picture the yellow baby holding a “US” flag at exactly 2 weeks as a visual cue.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Weekly monitoring of bilirubin<br /></br>• Acceptable only for physiologic or breast-milk (unconjugated) jaundice. Pathologic conjugated hyperbilirubinemia demands immediate work-up, not watchful waiting.</p></br><p>c. Upper GI series<br /></br>• Evaluates malrotation/volvulus, pyloric stenosis, GER; does not assess the biliary tree.</p></br><p>d. Oral administration of bile salts<br /></br>• May be used for symptomatic relief of pruritus in chronic cholestasis, but treating symptoms before diagnosing etiology delays life-saving surgery in biliary atresia.</p></br><p>e. Oral neomycin therapy<br /></br>• Non-absorbable antibiotic used to lower ammonia in hepatic encephalopathy, not for neonatal cholestasis work-up.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Key Take-home Message<br /></br>Any infant older than 2 weeks with a DIRECT bilirubin ≥ 2 mg/dL or &gt; 20 % of total has pathologic cholestasis. The first, time-sensitive step is abdominal ultrasonography to look for obstructive lesions, particularly biliary atresia, so that curative surgery is not missed.</p>
121. A previously healthy 27-year-old woman comes to the physician because of frequent urination and dysuria for 2 days. She has had no fever, chills, or back pain. She has had two similar episodes over the past 12 months treated with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. Examination shows suprapubic tenderness to palpation. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Urinalysis shows 20–30 WBC/hpf and is positive for nitrites. Which of the following is the most appropriate measure to prevent recurrent symptoms?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Emptying the bladder one-half hour before vaginal intercourse  </br>b. Ingestion of sodium bicarbonate in one-half glass of water 1 hour before vaginal intercourse  </br>c. Amoxicillin therapy for the first 3 days of each month  </br>d. Postcoital oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole therapy  </br>e. Propantheline therapy three times daily at the onset of symptoms</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Post-coital oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole therapy  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Strategy<br /></br>1. Key clues<br /></br>   • 27-year-old woman → pre-menopausal, anatomically normal urinary tract<br /></br>   • Frequency + dysuria, no fever/flank pain → uncomplicated acute cystitis<br /></br>   • Two prior culture-proven episodes in the past 12 mo → “recurrent UTI” (≥2 in 6 mo or ≥3 in 12 mo)<br /></br>   • No other risk factors mentioned; in young women, intercourse is the most common precipitant.  </p></br><ol></br><li>“Step-wise” approach USMLE loves<br /></br>   a. Diagnose acute uncomplicated cystitis.<br /></br>   b. Recognize the pattern of recurrence.<br /></br>   c. Decide on a prophylactic strategy tailored to the trigger (sexual activity).<br /></br>   d. Choose the agent with proven efficacy (TMP-SMX single dose).  </li></br></ol></br><p>Take-away: For sexually related recurrent cystitis, a single post-coital dose of an appropriate antibiotic is first-line prophylaxis.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• ­Recurrent uncomplicated UTIs are most often caused by peri-urethral colonization with E. coli that is mechanically introduced during intercourse.<br /></br>• Evidence-based prophylaxis options:<br /></br>  – Patient-initiated post-coital chemoprophylaxis (preferred if episodes correlate with intercourse)<br /></br>  – Continuous low-dose nightly prophylaxis for 6 months (if not intercourse-related or if post-coital fails)<br /></br>• TMP-SMX (single DS tablet 160/800 mg) is highly effective, inexpensive, and familiar to the patient (previously successful treatment).  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real life): frequency of infections, culture results, temporal relation to intercourse, counselling on prophylaxis options, risks/benefits of chronic antibiotics, plan for follow-up.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)<br /></br>• Ascending infection: peri-urethral colonization → urethra → bladder.<br /></br>• Intercourse: friction + transient urethral reflux of bacteria.<br /></br>• Recurrent UTI ≠ underlying structural abnormality in most young women; instead, behavioral/colonization factors dominate.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics Snapshot<br /></br>• Acute episode: 3-day oral TMP-SMX, nitrofurantoin 5 days, or fosfomycin 1 dose (culture if atypical).<br /></br>• Prophylaxis:<br /></br>  – Single dose post-coital (TMP-SMX 160/800 mg, nitrofurantoin 100 mg, or cephalexin 250 mg)<br /></br>  – Continuous nightly low-dose x 6 mo (same agents)<br /></br>  – Topical estrogen in post-menopausal women<br /></br>• Non-antibiotic measures (voiding after intercourse, increased fluids) can be suggested but are not reliably preventive when used alone.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Mnemonic / Memory Aid<br /></br>“Post-coital POST = Prophylaxis One Single Tablet.”<br /></br>Think of “Sulfa-sex” → after sex, take a sulfa pill (TMP-SMX).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Emptying the bladder one-half hour BEFORE intercourse<br /></br>   • Data support voiding AFTER intercourse; pre-voiding has no proven benefit and certainly not enough for someone with documented recurrences.</p></br><p>b. Sodium bicarbonate before intercourse<br /></br>   • Urine alkalinization does not prevent bacterial adhesion or growth in vivo and is not guideline-supported.</p></br><p>c. Amoxicillin for the first 3 days of every month<br /></br>   • Not timed to the trigger; continuous “pulsed” regimens are inferior to nightly or post-coital dosing.<br /></br>   • E. coli resistance to amoxicillin is common.</p></br><p>e. Propantheline TID at symptom onset<br /></br>   • Anticholinergic used for overactive bladder; has no antibacterial activity and would worsen urinary retention, potentially increasing UTI risk.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Rapid Review Pearl<br /></br>≥2 culture-proven cystitis in 6 mo (or ≥3 in a year)<br /></br> → Evaluate for triggers<br /></br> → If intercourse-related and patient is young &amp; healthy<br /></br>  → give single-dose post-coital TMP-SMX prophylaxis.</p>
122. An 18-year-old man who is a college student comes to the office for a follow-up examination 3 days after routine dipstick urinalysis during a sports physical examination showed proteinuria. Repeat dipstick urinalysis 2 days later was negative for protein. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Today, vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no abnormalities. Microscopic examination of urine sediment shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management regarding the change in urine findings?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Bone marrow biopsy</br>b. 24-Hour protein collection</br>c. Kidney biopsy</br>d. Urine protein immunoelectrophoresis</br>e. No management is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>E. No management is indicated  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues:<br /></br>  – Young (18-year-old) healthy male with a “sports physical” → low pre-test probability for chronic kidney disease.<br /></br>  – Initial dipstick: protein-positive. Repeat dipstick 48 h later: protein-negative.<br /></br>  – Normal vitals, benign physical exam, bland urine sediment.<br /></br>• Pertinent positives/negatives: absence of edema, hypertension, hematuria, casts, or systemic illness rules out glomerulonephritis or nephrotic syndrome.<br /></br>• Pattern: isolated, short-lived proteinuria in a teenager strongly suggests transient or orthostatic proteinuria, both of which resolve spontaneously.  </p></br><p>Cognitive road map for similar questions:<br /></br>1. Any protein on a single dipstick? → Repeat to confirm.<br /></br>2. If ≥1+ protein on two separate occasions → quantify (spot protein/Cr or 24-h).<br /></br>3. If repeat is negative → stop; it was transient.  </p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>The core concept: Transient proteinuria (d/t fever, exercise, stress, dehydration, orthostatic changes) is common in adolescents and requires no work-up once subsequent urinalysis is normal. Pursuing extensive tests would waste resources and expose the patient to unnecessary risks.</p></br><p>What would be documented: “Repeat UA negative; likely transient/orthostatic proteinuria. No further evaluation indicated. Will re-screen at next routine visit.”</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Transient proteinuria:<br /></br>• Etiology – hemodynamic changes cause mild, reversible increases in glomerular basement membrane permeability.<br /></br>• Orthostatic variant – protein is present after upright posture, absent after recumbency; peaks in teens/young adults, benign prognosis.  </p></br><p>Treatments and Diagnostic Tests Review<br /></br>• First-line: repeat dipstick after rest or first-morning void.<br /></br>• If negative → no further testing.<br /></br>• If persistent → quantify protein (spot protein/Cr or 24-h), consider renal function tests, possible nephrology referral.  </p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Teen + Temporary Trace” → If protein disappears on the repeat test, let it rest.<br /></br>OR<br /></br>“1-2-3 Rule”: 1 dipstick positive → 2nd dipstick; if 2nd is clear → 3 words: “Stop right here.”</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Bone marrow biopsy – Indicated for plasma-cell dyscrasia or leukemia suspicion (anemia, hypercalcemia, lytic bone lesions, Bence-Jones protein). None present.<br /></br>b. 24-hour protein collection – Appropriate only if proteinuria persists on repeat testing or is ≥1 g/day; unnecessary after a negative follow-up dipstick.<br /></br>c. Kidney biopsy – Reserved for unexplained persistent ≥1 g/day proteinuria, nephrotic syndrome, active urinary sediment, declining GFR. Not warranted here.<br /></br>d. Urine protein immunoelectrophoresis – Screens for monoclonal light chains in suspected multiple myeloma or amyloidosis, which typically affects adults &gt;40 y and presents with other systemic findings.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Always confirm isolated dipstick abnormalities. Transient/orthostatic proteinuria in a healthy adolescent resolves without intervention; no further management is needed.</p>
123. A 37-year-old woman, gravida 4, para 4, has continued to have vaginal bleeding 2 hours after forceps delivery of a 4300-g (9-lb 8-oz) newborn at term. Pregnancy was complicated by gestational diabetes. During delivery, shoulder dystocia required hyperflexion of the maternal hips, suprapubic pressure, and manual rotation of the fetal shoulders. The patient's pulse is 115/min, and blood pressure is 90/55 mm Hg. The uterine fundus is firm and palpable at the umbilicus, and the placenta appears to have been delivered intact. Which of the following is the most likely cause of the vaginal bleeding?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Genital tract laceration</br>b. Myometrial atony</br>c. Uterine inversion</br>d. Uterine rupture</br>e. Normal lochia</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Genital tract laceration  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Thought Process<br /></br>1. Persistent bleeding 2 h after delivery = primary postpartum hemorrhage (PPH).<br /></br>2. Uterine fundus is FIRM at the umbilicus → rules out uterine atony (“boggy” uterus).<br /></br>3. Placenta appears intact → rules out retained placental tissue.<br /></br>4. Delivery details: forceps, shoulder dystocia maneuvers, macrosomic infant. These are classic risk factors for TRAUMA to the cervix, vagina, or perineum.<br /></br>5. Vital signs (HR 115, BP 90/55) = symptomatic blood loss, so bleeding is more than normal lochia.  </p></br><p>When the uterus is well-contracted yet hemorrhage continues, always think “second T” of the 4 T’s of PPH: Trauma → genital tract laceration.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On exam day, use the 4 T’s algorithm (Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin). Once Tone is excluded by a firm uterus, immediately inspect for Trauma.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Reasoning (Why the correct answer fits)<br /></br>• Operative vaginal delivery (forceps/vacuum) + macrosomic baby + shoulder dystocia create shear forces that tear the cervix, vagina, or periurethral/perineal tissues.<br /></br>• A firm uterus cannot tamponade bleeding originating below the uterine cavity, so brisk bleeding persists despite uterine massage or oxytocin.<br /></br>• Next clinical step: prompt speculum examination of cervix/vagina and direct repair (sutures, pressure). Large or expanding hematomas may need drainage or interventional radiology for uterine/iliac artery embolization. Document estimated blood loss, site/extent of laceration, and repair performed.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Primary PPH (&lt;24 h postpartum) mnemonic = 4 T’s<br /></br>1. Tone – uterine atony (≈80% of cases)<br /></br>2. Trauma – lacerations/hematomas (operative delivery, precipitous labor, big baby)<br /></br>3. Tissue – retained placenta/membranes<br /></br>4. Thrombin – coagulation disorder (e.g., DIC, severe pre-eclampsia)  </p></br><p>Genital tract lacerations bleed because vessels in the cervix and vaginal sidewalls are torn. Uterine contraction does not affect these vessels, so bleeding continues until repaired.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics Snapshot<br /></br>• Initial: fundal massage, IV oxytocin for all PPH while source sought.<br /></br>• Firm uterus + continued bleeding → visual inspection with good lighting &amp; speculum.<br /></br>• Repair with absorbable sutures; consider uterine/iliac artery ligation or embolization if bleeding persists.<br /></br>• Monitor CBC, coagulation profile, transfuse PRBCs as needed.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids<br /></br>“Firm but Flowing = Think Laceration.”<br /></br>4 T’s order of prevalence: Tone (most), then Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin.<br /></br>Operative instruments = “Forceps = Tears.” Visualize metal blades nicking soft tissue.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>b. Myometrial (uterine) atony – Would present with a soft, boggy, enlarged uterus; bleeding decreases with massage/uterotonics. Not the case here.<br /></br>c. Uterine inversion – Fundus absent on abdominal exam; often a dimple at cervix; profound shock out of proportion to blood loss. Fundus is palpable here.<br /></br>d. Uterine rupture – Typically intrapartum with sudden abdominal pain, loss of fetal station, fetal distress; risk factors are prior uterine surgery. After delivery uterus is usually tender, not firm/normal. Rare cause of postpartum bleeding.<br /></br>e. Normal lochia – Physiologic discharge is scant to moderate, does NOT cause hypotension/tachycardia.</p></br><p>Key Take-Home: In postpartum hemorrhage, a firm uterus + ongoing bleeding = look for lower-genital-tract lacerations due to obstetric trauma.</p>
124. A previously healthy 27-year-old woman, gravida 1, para 1, comes to the physician because of a 2-week history of anxiety, irritability, and difficulty sleeping. During this time, she has noticed a mild tremor of her hands. Two months ago, she gave birth to a healthy male newborn at term. Delivery was uncomplicated. She is not breast-feeding. She initially attributed her symptoms to giving birth. Her only medication is a prenatal vitamin. She appears slightly anxious. Her temperature is 37.1 C (98.8 F), pulse is 108/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 122/74 mm Hg. Examination shows a normal-sized thyroid that is slightly tender to palpation. There is a fine tremor of the outstretched hands. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin            11.5 g/dL</br>Hematocrit            35 percent</br>Mean corpuscular volume       82 μm³</br>Leukocyte count       9800/mm³</br>Platelet count        410,000/mm³</br>Serum</br>Thyroid-stimulating hormone   0.3 μU/mL</br>Thyroxine (T4)        12 μg/dL</br>Antithyroid peroxidase antibody   negative</br>Thyroid ¹²³I scintigraphy shows decreased uptake of radioactive iodine. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Metoprolol therapy</br>b. Prednisone therapy</br>c. Propylthiouracil therapy</br>d. ¹³¹I ablation</br>e. No further management is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct answer: a. Metoprolol therapy  </p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – 2-week history of anxiety, irritability, insomnia, fine hand tremor, and sinus tachycardia → hyperadrenergic state typical of thyrotoxicosis.<br /></br>  – Occurs 2 months after an uncomplicated delivery (post-partum period).<br /></br>  – Thyroid is normal-sized but slightly tender → suggests a thyroiditis picture rather than Graves disease (Graves is usually nontender, often with goiter/eye findings).<br /></br>  – Labs: suppressed TSH (0.3 µU/mL) with high-normal T4 (12 µg/dL) → biochemical thyrotoxicosis.<br /></br>  – Antithyroid peroxidase Ab negative (argues against chronic lymphocytic/postpartum autoimmune thyroiditis, which is usually painless and often Ab-positive).<br /></br>  – Radioiodine uptake scan: diffusely decreased uptake → thyroid is not synthesizing new hormone; it is “dumping” pre-formed hormone, a hallmark of destructive thyroiditis (e.g., subacute granulomatous/de Quervain thyroiditis).  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition for exams<br /></br>1. Hyperthyroid symptoms (+ low TSH).<br /></br>2. Low radioiodine uptake = thyroiditis vs exogenous hormone ingestion.<br /></br>3. Tender thyroid → subacute granulomatous (de Quervain).<br /></br>4. Management of thyroiditis‐induced thyrotoxicosis is symptomatic β-blockade ± NSAIDs for pain. Antithyroid drugs and radioactive iodine do not work because hormone synthesis is already shut off.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach: Always pair “low uptake” with “do NOT give thionamides/RAI”; treat symptoms only, unless severe neck pain warrants steroids.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-by-step link to answer:<br /></br>1. Identify etiology: Tender thyroid + low uptake → subacute (granulomatous) thyroiditis.<br /></br>2. Understand pathophysiology: Viral/post-viral inflammatory destruction releases pre-formed T4/T3; the gland is temporarily hyperthyroid then often becomes hypothyroid before recovery. There is no ongoing overproduction.<br /></br>3. Decide treatment goals: Control adrenergic symptoms (tachycardia, tremor) while inflammation runs its course (weeks-to-months).<br /></br>4. β-blocker (e.g., metoprolol or propranolol) relieves tremor, palpitations, anxiety. NSAID or, if severe pain, glucocorticoid for neck tenderness.  </p></br><p>Take-home: Thyrotoxicosis with low RAIU = treat supportively with β-blocker; antithyroid drugs/RAI are ineffective.</p></br><p>Documentation points if real patient: vitals, thyroid exam findings, degree of pain, neurological/eye findings, plan for β-blocker, follow-up TSH/T4 in 4-6 weeks.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Subacute granulomatous (de Quervain) thyroiditis<br /></br>• Often follows viral URI; peak incidence in young/middle-aged women.<br /></br>• Painful tender thyroid, malaise, low-grade fever.<br /></br>• Triphasic course: transient hyperthyroid → euthyroid → transient hypothyroid → recovery (90% within 6-12 mo).<br /></br>• Inflammation damages follicles → release of stored hormone → low TSH, ↑T4/T3.<br /></br>• Lab: markedly elevated ESR, CRP; thyroid antibodies usually absent.<br /></br>• RAIU: very low (&lt;5%) because TSH suppressed and gland cannot trap iodine when damaged.  </p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics Review<br /></br>1. Symptom control<br /></br>   – β-blocker: Metoprolol 25–50 mg PO BID (titrate to HR &lt;90).<br /></br>2. Pain/inflammation control<br /></br>   – NSAIDs (e.g., high-dose ibuprofen).<br /></br>   – Prednisone 40 mg PO daily tapered over 2–4 weeks if severe pain or refractory to NSAIDs.<br /></br>3. Monitor thyroid function every 4–6 weeks; start levothyroxine only if prolonged hypothyroid phase with symptoms.<br /></br>Further imaging/labs generally not required once diagnosis secure.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“de QUERVAIN = QUivering, tEnder, Viral”<br /></br>• QUivering → hyperadrenergic symptoms needing β-blocker.<br /></br>• tEnder → painful gland (NSAID ± steroid).<br /></br>• Viral → post-viral granulomatous inflammation.<br /></br>Low uptake? Think “blocked pump”—antithyroid meds can’t fix a pump already off.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>b. Prednisone therapy<br /></br>   – Glucocorticoids reserved for severe neck pain or thyroid storm. Patient’s main complaint is mild tremor/anxiety; no significant pain reported. NSAID/β-blocker first.  </p></br><p>c. Propylthiouracil therapy<br /></br>   – Inhibits thyroid hormone synthesis; useless when hyperthyroidism is due to passive release of pre-formed hormone (low uptake). Would not relieve symptoms faster than a β-blocker.  </p></br><p>d. ¹³¹I ablation<br /></br>   – Employed for Graves disease or toxic nodular goiter with HIGH uptake; contraindicated in thyroiditis (low uptake) and in recent postpartum if breastfeeding (though she isn’t).  </p></br><p>e. No further management<br /></br>   – She is symptomatic (tachycardia 108, tremor, anxiety); β-blocker improves quality of life and prevents complications (e.g., arrhythmias). Doing nothing misses standard of care.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Core takeaway:<br /></br>Low radioiodine uptake thyrotoxicosis from subacute (painful) thyroiditis is treated with symptomatic β-blockade (± NSAIDs). Antithyroid drugs and ablation are ineffective because the gland is inflamed, not overactive.</p>
125. A 42-year-old woman comes to the office 2 months after noticing a mass in her left breast. There is no personal or family history of serious illness. Her last Pap smear 2 years ago and mammography 1 year ago showed no abnormalities. Menses occur at regular 28-day intervals and last 3 days. Her last menstrual period was 2 weeks ago. She is sexually active with one male partner and uses an oral contraceptive. She drinks four cups of coffee daily. Examination shows a 3-cm, firm, irregular mass in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast. Examination of the right breast shows no abnormalities. In addition to mammography, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Discontinue oral contraceptive and reexamine in 1 month</br>b. Perform breast biopsy</br>c. Perform breast MRI</br>d. Recommend discontinuing caffeine use</br>e. Reexamine in 1 month</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Perform breast biopsy (core-needle or excisional)</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis – How to read this stem</br>1. Patient profile: 42-year-old pre-menopausal woman (breast cancer risk begins to rise &gt;40 y).<br /></br>2. Chief concern: “mass in her left breast” present × 2 mo → persistent.<br /></br>3. Lesion characteristics: 3 cm, firm, irregular, upper-outer quadrant. Those three adjectives = classic “red flag” morphology for malignancy (hard, non-mobile, irregular borders).<br /></br>4. No cyclic variation is mentioned and it persisted through one full menstrual cycle → unlikely fibrocystic change.<br /></br>5. Pertinent negatives: no trauma, no infection signs. A normal mammogram one year ago does NOT rule out cancer (interval cancers can arise within 12 mo).<br /></br>6. Key NBME clue: The stem already tells you “in addition to mammography” – therefore imaging will be done, but the question is what to do NEXT to establish the diagnosis. For any suspicious solid mass in a woman &gt;30 y, the next step is tissue diagnosis.</p></br><p>Cognitive approach for test day<br /></br>Ask yourself: “Is this mass suspicious?” If yes → “Imaging + TISSUE.” Never delay with observation or lifestyle tweaks when cancer is on the table.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)</br>1. Suspicious clinical exam → proceed to diagnostic mammogram ± ultrasound (already ordered per stem).<br /></br>2. The definitive diagnosis of breast cancer requires histologic confirmation. Core-needle biopsy of the mass is the least invasive way to obtain tissue and is recommended for any dominant, suspicious mass in women ≥30 y, regardless of menstrual status or OCP use.<br /></br>3. Document in the chart: size, location, skin/nipple changes, lymph-node exam, BI-RADS score once imaging returns, and that biopsy consent was obtained.</p></br><p>Take-home message<br /></br>Hard, irregular, fixed, or enlarging breast masses in women over 30 demand a biopsy—do NOT “watch-and-wait.”</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)</br>• Breast carcinoma (most commonly invasive ductal) arises from epithelial cells lining terminal ducts/lobules.<br /></br>• Risk factors: age, prolonged estrogen exposure, first-degree relative w/ breast CA, BRCA mutations, obesity, radiation. Caffeine and OCPs play minimal/no causal role.<br /></br>• Upper-outer quadrant has greatest volume of breast tissue → most common cancer location.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Pearls</br>Diagnostic sequence:</br>   1. Diagnostic (not screening) mammography ± ultrasound to further characterize mass.<br /></br>   2. Core-needle biopsy (14-gauge) → histology, ER/PR/HER2 status.<br /></br>   3. Staging work-up after pathology (sentinel LN biopsy, imaging if indicated).</p></br><p>Treatment (once cancer confirmed):</br>   • Early stage: lumpectomy + sentinel node biopsy + radiation OR mastectomy.<br /></br>   • Adjuvant systemic therapy tailored to receptor status:<br /></br>        – ER/PR⁺: tamoxifen (premenopause) or aromatase inhibitor (postmenopause)<br /></br>        – HER2⁺: trastuzumab<br /></br>        – Triple-negative: chemotherapy (e.g., anthracycline + taxane).</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────</br>Memory Tip</br>“Firm + Irregular + ≥40 y = ‘FIRm biopsy’ → FIrst Response = Biopsy.”</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong</br>a. Discontinue oral contraceptive and reexamine in 1 month<br /></br>   • OCPs rarely cause discrete dominant masses; delaying evaluation violates cancer work-up guidelines.</p></br><p>c. Perform breast MRI<br /></br>   • MRI is adjunctive (dense breasts, staging, BRCA screening) but not first-line for an obvious palpable mass. You still need tissue.</p></br><p>d. Recommend discontinuing caffeine use<br /></br>   • Caffeine reduction may help cyclic mastalgia, not a firm 3-cm irregular mass.</p></br><p>e. Reexamine in 1 month<br /></br>   • Acceptable only if the mass clearly fluctuates with cycle and imaging is benign (BI-RADS 2). Here the clinical exam is worrisome; delaying biopsy risks stage progression.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Any dominant, suspicious breast mass in a woman &gt;30 y mandates prompt core-needle biopsy after imaging—no therapeutic trials, no observation.</p>
126. A 47-year-old woman comes to the physician because of heavy menstrual flow for 7 days. Menses usually occur at regular 28-day intervals and last 4 to 5 days. She has not had intermenstrual or postcoital bleeding. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Bimanual examination shows a uterus consistent in size with a 20-week gestation. A Pap smear shows no abnormalities. Ultrasonography of the pelvis shows an enlarged uterus with three solid, well-circumscribed masses within the myometrium that are homogeneous in appearance. The largest mass is 7 cm in diameter. Results of an endometrial biopsy specimen show proliferative endometrium. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Adenocarcinoma</br>b. Adenomyosis</br>c. Endometrial hyperplasia</br>d. Endometritis</br>e. Leiomyomata uteri</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Leiomyomata uteri (uterine fibroids)  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Strategy<br /></br>1. Heavy, regular menses (menorrhagia) → common presenting symptom of fibroids.<br /></br>2. Uterus “consistent with a 20-week gestation” → focal, often markedly enlarged uterus points to a space-occupying lesion rather than diffuse thickening.<br /></br>3. Pelvic US: “solid, well-circumscribed, homogeneous masses within the myometrium” → classic sonographic description of leiomyomas (firm, whorled, smooth-muscle tumors).<br /></br>4. Endometrial biopsy: proliferative (normal) endometrium → rules out hyperplasia or carcinoma.<br /></br>Metacognitive pearl: Anytime you see a markedly enlarged but irregularly contoured uterus with discrete masses on US in a woman with otherwise normal cycles, think fibroids first.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why E is correct)<br /></br>Step 1: SYMPTOMS – Menorrhagia with otherwise normal 28-day cycles.<br /></br>Step 2: PHYSICAL – Uterus enlarged to ~20-wk size (suggests multiple intramural lesions).<br /></br>Step 3: IMAGING – Discrete, solid, homogeneous myometrial masses = leiomyomas.<br /></br>Step 4: BIOPSY – Normal proliferative endometrium; bleeding source is mechanical distortion of endometrium, not neoplastic change.<br /></br>Take-home: Intramural or submucosal fibroids increase endometrial surface area → heavy but cyclic bleeding.  </p></br><p>Documentation points if this were a real encounter: character of bleeding, effect on hemoglobin/quality of life, size/location of myomas, counseling on medical (OCPs, tranexamic acid, GnRH agonist) vs surgical (myomectomy vs hysterectomy) options.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology High-Yield Review<br /></br>• Benign monoclonal proliferation of smooth muscle cells; estrogen dependent (grow during reproductive years, pregnancy; shrink after menopause).<br /></br>• Types: submucosal, intramural, subserosal; intramural most often cause menorrhagia.<br /></br>• Complications: anemia, infertility, recurrent pregnancy loss, bulk symptoms (pressure, urinary frequency), pain with degeneration.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Diagnosis &amp; Management Snapshot<br /></br>Diagnosis: Transvaginal US (first-line); MRI if unclear.<br /></br>Medical Tx:<br /></br>– NSAIDs for dysmenorrhea &amp; blood loss<br /></br>– Combined OCPs or levonorgestrel IUD<br /></br>– Tranexamic acid for acute heavy bleeding<br /></br>– GnRH agonists (leuprolide) temporarily shrink fibroids (premenopausal bridge to surgery).<br /></br>Surgical Tx: hysteroscopic myomectomy (submucosal, fertility desired), abdominal myomectomy, uterine artery embolization, definitive hysterectomy.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids<br /></br>“FIBROID” mnemonic:<br /></br>F – Firm, focal masses<br /></br>I – Increase with estrogen<br /></br>B – Bleeding (menorrhagia)<br /></br>R – Reproductive age, Regression after menopause<br /></br>O – Obstructive/bulk sx<br /></br>I – Intramural most common<br /></br>D – Distinctly circumscribed on US  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>A. Adenocarcinoma (Endometrial cancer)<br /></br>• Usually postmenopausal, presents with irregular or postmenopausal bleeding.<br /></br>• Biopsy would show malignant glands, not normal proliferative endometrium.<br /></br>• Ultrasound shows thickened endometrium, not discrete intramural masses.  </p></br><p>B. Adenomyosis<br /></br>• Endometrial glands within myometrium → diffuse, symmetric, “globular” boggy uterus, not multiple well-circumscribed masses.<br /></br>• Often associated with severe secondary dysmenorrhea.<br /></br>• US/MRI shows heterogenous myometrium with no distinct nodules.  </p></br><p>C. Endometrial hyperplasia<br /></br>• Caused by unopposed estrogen (obesity, anovulation, PCOS). Presents with irregular, inter-menstrual bleeding.<br /></br>• Biopsy would show hyperplastic (crowded) glands ± atypia.<br /></br>• Uterus usually normal size; ultrasound shows thickened endometrium.  </p></br><p>D. Endometritis<br /></br>• Infection (postpartum or post-procedure). Features: fever, uterine tenderness, foul lochia.<br /></br>• No discrete masses; biopsy unnecessary for diagnosis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Key Takeaway<br /></br>Regular heavy menses + enlarged, irregular uterus with well-defined intramural masses on ultrasound = leiomyomata uteri. Remember: discrete, estrogen-responsive smooth-muscle tumors → menorrhagia, mass effect, infertility risk, normal biopsy.</p>
127. A 37-year-old nulligravid woman comes to the physician because she has been unable to conceive for 6 months. She has been married for 18 years and discontinued her oral contraceptive 6 months ago. She is an attorney, and she and her husband have sexual intercourse twice monthly. Menses occur at regular 28-day intervals. She feels well and has no history of serious illness. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Marital therapy to increase frequency of sexual intercourse</br>b. Use of an ovulation predictor kit to time sexual intercourse</br>c. Measurement of serum testosterone concentrations of the patient and her husband</br>d. Sildenafil therapy for the patient's husband</br>e. Intrauterine insemination during ovulation</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Use of an ovulation predictor kit to time sexual intercourse  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• 37-year-old, nulligravid, regular 28-day cycles → very likely ovulating normally.<br /></br>• Trying to conceive for 6 months (meets definition of “infertility work-up” for women &gt; 35 y).<br /></br>• Intercourse only twice monthly → probability that intercourse coincides with the 3- to 4-day fertile window is low.<br /></br>• No pelvic pain, abnormal bleeding, systemic illness, or findings on exam → no immediate red flags for tubal or uterine pathology.</p></br><p>Cognitive approach<br /></br>1. Ask: “Is the couple actually hitting the fertile window?”<br /></br>2. If not, correct that simple modifiable factor before ordering expensive tests or invasive procedures.<br /></br>3. In women &gt;35, a formal infertility evaluation is reasonable once timed intercourse is optimized.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Always check frequency and timing of intercourse first; it is the cheapest, highest-yield “test.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic<br /></br>1. Regular cycles ⇒ ovulation likely; no need to prove it yet.<br /></br>2. The barrier to conception appears behavioral (poor timing), not biologic.<br /></br>3. LH surge urine kits pinpoint ovulation 24–36 h in advance, allowing the couple to have intercourse on the peak-fertile days.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Before embarking on laboratory or invasive fertility testing, ensure intercourse is timed with ovulation, especially when menses are regular.  </p></br><p>Documentation point: “Counseled patient on fertile window; recommended home LH surge kit and intercourse every 1–2 days starting 2 days before expected ovulation.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• Ovulation occurs ≈14 days before the next menses in women with regular cycles.<br /></br>• The LH surge precedes ovulation by 24–36 h; home kits detect this surge.<br /></br>• Fertile window: begins ~5 days before ovulation, peaks the 2 days before and the day of ovulation (highest conception rates when intercourse occurs ≤2 days before ovulation).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment Ladder  </p></br><ol></br><li>Optimize timing (ovulation predictor, cervical mucus, basal body temp).  </li></br><li>If still not pregnant after 3–6 months, proceed to:<br /></br>   • Semen analysis (first male test).<br /></br>   • Ovarian reserve (AMH, day-3 FSH/estradiol), anovulation labs if cycles irregular.<br /></br>   • Tubal/uterine evaluation (hysterosalpingogram, TVUS, hysteroscopy).  </li></br><li>Interventions: clomiphene/letrozole ovulation induction, intrauterine insemination (IUI), IVF, etc.  </li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“TIME before TESTS”<br /></br>T – Timing of intercourse<br /></br>I – Intercourse every other day around day 14<br /></br>M – Monitor LH surge (predictor kit)<br /></br>E – Evaluate only after optimal timing or after 6–12 mo (6 mo if &gt;35 y)  </p></br><p>Visualize a traffic light: green light (ovulation kit) must turn on before you drive down the fertility work-up road.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Marital therapy to increase frequency<br /></br>• Frequency is low but the key issue is timing, not relationship dysfunction. Direct, simple physiologic solution (ovulation kit) is superior and more targeted.  </p></br><p>c. Measure serum testosterone in both partners<br /></br>• Male infertility initial test = semen analysis, not serum testosterone. Patient has regular menses → unlikely androgen excess or anovulation.  </p></br><p>d. Sildenafil for husband<br /></br>• No evidence of erectile dysfunction; intercourse is occurring but poorly timed.  </p></br><p>e. Intrauterine insemination during ovulation<br /></br>• An assisted-reproduction technique reserved for unexplained infertility, mild male factor, or after failure of simpler measures. Too invasive/expensive as first step.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>For a couple with regular menstrual cycles and infrequent, poorly timed intercourse, the first and simplest intervention is to use an LH-surge ovulation predictor kit and time intercourse during the fertile window.</p>
128. A 32-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 2, comes to the physician for a routine health maintenance examination. At her last examination 1 year ago, a Pap smear and screening test results for sexually transmitted diseases were negative. Her two children, ages 6 and 4 years, were delivered by cesarean section. Her first child weighed 4394 g (9 lb 11 oz) at birth, and her second child weighed 4593 g (10 lb 2 oz) at birth. The patient is sexually active with her husband and uses an oral contraceptive. She is 157 cm (5 ft 2 in) tall and weighs 72 kg (158 lb); BMI is 29 kg/m2. Her temperature is 37 C (98.6 F), pulse is 84/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 130/75 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no other abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate screening test at this visit?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Measurement of fasting serum glucose concentration</br>b. Measurement of serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration</br>c. Urinalysis</br>d. Mammography</br>e. Transvaginal ultrasonography for endometrial thickness</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Measurement of fasting serum glucose concentration  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Pertinent positives<br /></br>   • Two prior infants weighed 4394 g (9 lb 11 oz) and 4593 g (10 lb 2 oz) → neonatal macrosomia.<br /></br>   • Current BMI 29 kg/m² (overweight).  </li></br><li>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No thyroid, breast, urinary, or gynecologic complaints.<br /></br>   • Age only 32 y (below routine mammography threshold).  </li></br><li>Key phrase to lock on: “history of two macrosomic infants.” For the exam, always translate “&gt;4000 g (≈9 lb)” to “think maternal diabetes (gestational or overt).”  </li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When a stem mentions birth weights &gt;9 lb, immediately put “screen for diabetes” on your short-list—no other single detail carries as much predictive weight.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step 1 – Recognize the risk factor.<br /></br>   Macrosomic delivery is a surrogate marker for gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM), which confers a 7- to 10-fold lifetime risk of type 2 diabetes in the mother.</p></br><p>Step 2 – Apply guidelines.<br /></br>   • ADA &amp; USPSTF: Any woman with prior GDM OR a history of delivering a macrosomic infant should receive a postpartum 75-g OGTT at 4–12 wk and, if normal, be rescreened at least every 3 y (fasting plasma glucose, 2-hr OGTT, or HbA1c).<br /></br>   • Overweight (BMI ≥25 kg/m²) is an independent indication to screen beginning at age 35, but the macrosomia history trumps age and calls for earlier testing.</p></br><p>Step 3 – Choose the correct screening test.<br /></br>   Fasting plasma glucose (or HbA1c/OGTT) satisfies the recommendation; the vignette offers fasting glucose as a choice.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: History of macrosomic infant(s) = lifelong periodic diabetes screening, starting now.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): “Due to prior macrosomic deliveries and current BMI 29, ordered fasting plasma glucose to screen for type 2 diabetes per ADA guidelines.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• Gestational diabetes = placental hormones → insulin resistance → maternal hyperglycemia → fetal hyperinsulinemia → fetal overgrowth (macrosomia).<br /></br>• β-cell stress during pregnancy unmasks an already limited pancreatic reserve; approximately 50% of women with GDM develop overt type 2 DM within 10 years.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Management Snapshot  </p></br><p>Screening options (any one acceptable):<br /></br>1. Fasting plasma glucose (≥126 mg/dL = diabetes).<br /></br>2. 2-hr 75-g OGTT (≥200 mg/dL).<br /></br>3. HbA1c (≥6.5%).  </p></br><p>If abnormal: confirm on repeat testing → lifestyle modification, metformin, or insulin as per diabetes guidelines.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Big Baby → Back for Blood Sugar.”<br /></br>Visualize a gigantic newborn holding a glucose meter to mom: cues you to order a diabetes screen.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong  </p></br><p>b. Serum TSH: Routine screening only in pregnancy or when symptomatic; 32-y-old asymptomatic nonpregnant woman = not indicated.  </p></br><p>c. Urinalysis: No urinary complaints, not pregnant, no CKD risk factors; UA is no longer part of routine health maintenance in asymptomatic adults.  </p></br><p>d. Mammography: USPSTF and ACOG begin at 40–50 y (depending on organization) for average-risk women; she is 32.  </p></br><p>e.
