Transvaginal ultrasound for endometrial thickness: Not a screening test for average-risk women; reserved for abnormal uterine bleeding or very high-risk genetics (e.g., Lynch syndrome).  </p></br><p>Common trap: Students sometimes pick mammography because it’s the “classic” screening test in women, but age matters. Always match the guideline age window.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>A history of delivering a macrosomic infant is a red-flag risk factor for future type 2 diabetes. The single most appropriate screening test for this 32-year-old woman is a fasting serum glucose measurement.</p>
129. A 47-year-old woman comes to the physician for a routine examination. Two years ago, she underwent total thyroidectomy for medullary cancer; her postoperative course was uncomplicated, but she did not keep her appointments for follow-up examinations. She says she feels well. She has never had radiation therapy. There is no family history of thyroid disease. Her only current medication is levothyroxine. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination of the neck shows a well-healed surgical scar; there are no masses or cervical lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following serum measurements is most appropriate to order to determine if this patient's cancer is in remission?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. CA 19-9</br>b. Calcitonin</br>c. Chromogranin A</br>d. Thyroglobulin</br>e. Thyroid-stimulating hormone</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Calcitonin  </p></br><p>───────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• “Total thyroidectomy for medullary cancer” → the primary lesion was medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC).<br /></br>• “Feels well… no masses… wants routine follow-up” → question is asking for the optimal tumor-surveillance study, not a diagnostic test for a new problem.<br /></br>• No family history, no radiation exposure → rules out papillary cancer and anaplastic radiation-associated cancers as dominant concerns.  </p></br><p>Test‐taking mindset<br /></br>1. Recognize what cell type gives rise to the tumor.<br /></br>2. Recall the peptide(s) secreted by that cell type.<br /></br>3. Choose the serum marker that directly reflects residual or recurrent tumor burden.  </p></br><p>───────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Stepwise thought process<br /></br>1. Medullary thyroid carcinoma originates from parafollicular C cells.<br /></br>2. C cells synthesize and secrete calcitonin.<br /></br>3. After total thyroidectomy, calcitonin levels should fall to undetectable levels within weeks.<br /></br>4. Persistent or rising calcitonin means residual, recurrent, or metastatic MTC.<br /></br>Thus, the correct monitoring test is serum calcitonin.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>Medullary thyroid carcinoma → C cells → Calcitonin is both a diagnostic and surveillance biomarker.  </p></br><p>Documentation Pearl<br /></br>“In remission if postoperative calcitonin undetectable; order serum calcitonin level today to assess disease status.”  </p></br><p>───────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Cell of origin: Parafollicular (C) cells derived from neural crest.<br /></br>• Mutation associations: Sporadic (RET proto-oncogene somatic) or familial (MEN2A/2B, germline RET).<br /></br>• Hormonal products: Calcitonin (classic), occasionally CEA (carcinoembryonic antigen).<br /></br>• Post-op expectations: Calcitonin half-life ≈ 1–2 hrs → undetectable &lt; 3 days; durable nadir by 2–3 months.  </p></br><p>───────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Summary  </p></br><p>Surveillance schedule (ATA guidelines):<br /></br>• Serum calcitonin ± CEA: 2–3 months post-op, then q6–12 months.<br /></br>• High calcitonin (&gt;150 pg/mL) → consider imaging (neck ultrasound, CT chest/abdomen, PET/CT) for metastases.<br /></br>• TSH should be kept in the normal range (levothyroxine is replacement only—TSH suppression does NOT influence C-cell tumors).  </p></br><p>───────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Medullary = C‐cells = Calcium control → Calcitonin.”<br /></br>Visual: Think of the letter “C” embedded in the word “MeduCllary” to remind you of C-cells and Calcitonin.  </p></br><p>───────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. CA 19-9 – Tumor marker for pancreatic adenocarcinoma and some biliary/GI tumors; unrelated to thyroid neoplasms.  </p></br><p>C. Chromogranin A – Elevated in carcinoid tumors, pheochromocytoma, other neuroendocrine tumors; not specific or sensitive for MTC surveillance.  </p></br><p>D. Thyroglobulin – Secreted by follicular thyroid cells; used to monitor papillary and follicular (well-differentiated) thyroid cancers after total thyroidectomy/RAI. C-cells do not produce thyroglobulin, so levels can be zero even with extensive medullary disease. Classic distractor.  </p></br><p>E. Thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) – Guides levothyroxine dosing and recurrence risk in papillary/follicular cancers (TSH suppression therapy). However, TSH does not stimulate C-cells, and its level provides no information about MTC recurrence.  </p></br><p>Common Pitfall<br /></br>Students often default to thyroglobulin for “any” thyroid cancer. Always match the tumor marker to the cell type:<br /></br>• Follicular cells → Thyroglobulin<br /></br>• C-cells → Calcitonin (± CEA)  </p></br><p>───────────────</p>
130. A 54-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 1-day history of intermittent episodes of severe, sharp pain in the right side of her back that occur every 30 minutes. The episodes are associated with nausea; the patient has had two episodes of nonbloody vomiting since her symptoms began. During the past month she has had fatigue, generalized aches, and difficulty concentrating on simple tasks. She has had painful urination during the past 2 days. She has osteoarthritis treated with ibuprofen as needed. Her temperature is 37 C (98.6 F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 146/90 mm Hg. The patient is in moderate distress and shifts around on the examination table. No murmurs are heard. Bowel sounds are normal. Abdominal examination shows tenderness to percussion at the right flank. There is no hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin               12 g/dL</br>Hematocrit               38 percent</br>Leukocyte count          5100/mm³</br>Platelet count           204,000/mm³</br>Serum</br>Na⁺                      142 mEq/L</br>K⁺                       5 mEq/L</br>Cl⁻                      100 mEq/L</br>HCO₃⁻                    25 mEq/L</br>Ca²⁺                     12 mg/dL</br>Urea nitrogen            14 mg/dL</br>Glucose                  92 mg/dL</br>Creatinine               0.9 mg/dL</br>Urine</br>Color                     clear yellow</br>pH                        7.0 (N=4.5-7.8)</br>Specific gravity          1.018 (N=1.003-1.029)</br>Blood                     grossly positive</br>Glucose                   negative</br>Bilirubin                 negative</br>Protein                   trace</br>Ketones                   negative</br>Nitrites                  negative</br>Leukocyte esterase        negative</br>Urobilinogen              0.2 mg/2 hr (N=0.1-1.1)</br>X-ray of the abdomen shows a 4-mm calcified calculus in the right ureteropelvic junction. There is no hydronephrosis. Which of the following is the most appropriate additional study at this time?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. CT scan of the abdomen</br>b. Measurement of serum intact parathyroid hormone concentration</br>c. MRIs of the brain and abdomen</br>d. Urine culture</br>e. No further testing is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>b. Measurement of serum intact parathyroid hormone (PTH) concentration</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key clues</br>  – Renal colic: “severe, sharp pain in the right side of her back,” 4-mm ureteral calculus on abdominal x-ray.<br /></br>  – Hypercalcemia: serum Ca2+ 12 mg/dL (normal ≈ 8.5-10.5).<br /></br>  – Systemic “hyper-Ca” symptoms for 1 month: fatigue, generalized aches, difficulty concentrating.<br /></br>  – Urinalysis: large blood, no leukocyte esterase or nitrite → hematuria from stone, not infection.<br /></br>  – No hydronephrosis and stone &lt;5 mm → likely to pass spontaneously; imaging has already identified it.</p></br><p>Approach for similar questions<br /></br>1. Separate acute issue (stone) from chronic “why did she form it?” question.<br /></br>2. Hypercalcemia + calcium-containing stone → always evaluate for hyperparathyroidism.<br /></br>3. First, check intact PTH; it dictates the rest of the work-up (PTH-dependent vs PTH-independent causes).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>• Core takeaway: Calcium kidney stones mandate an etiologic work-up. In an otherwise stable patient, the very next step after confirming hypercalcemia is to measure serum intact PTH.<br /></br>• Why PTH?<br /></br>  – Primary hyperparathyroidism is the most common outpatient cause of hypercalcemia (“stones, bones, groans, psychiatric overtones”).<br /></br>  – PTH result directs management:<br /></br>     ▸ High / inappropriately normal → primary or tertiary hyperparathyroidism → neck ultrasound, sestamibi scan, surgery.<br /></br>     ▸ Suppressed → consider malignancy, vitamin D intoxication, sarcoid, thyrotoxicosis, etc.</p></br><p>Documentation note: record serum calcium level, plan for PTH testing, patient education about stone passage precautions (hydration, alpha-blocker if needed), NSAID use, and return precautions if fever or inability to pass urine develops.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>Primary hyperparathyroidism<br /></br>• Etiology: solitary adenoma (85%), hyperplasia, carcinoma.<br /></br>• Excess PTH → ↑ bone resorption, ↑ renal tubular Ca2+ reabsorption, ↑ activation of vitamin D → hypercalcemia &amp; hypercalciuria → calcium oxalate/phosphate stones.<br /></br>• Symptoms: nephrolithiasis, mild neurocognitive complaints, constipation, bone pain; often asymptomatic and discovered on labs.<br /></br>• Classic labs: ↑ Ca2+, ↓/low-normal phosphate, ↑ or inappropriately normal PTH, ↑ alkaline phosphatase, ↑ urinary cAMP.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment Pearls</br>• Hypercalcemia work-up (memorize order): 1) repeat Ca to confirm, 2) measure intact PTH.<br /></br>• Kidney stone &lt;5 mm, no obstruction: hydration + NSAID; add tamsulosin if needed; strain urine for stone analysis.<br /></br>• Hyperparathyroidism definitive Rx: parathyroidectomy when symptomatic or when meeting guideline criteria (Ca ≥1 mg/dL above normal, age &lt;50, osteoporosis, GFR &lt;60, nephrolithiasis/nephrocalcinosis).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“Ca2+ STONES” mnemonic – When you see Ca2+ kidney STONES:<br /></br>S – Screen Ca2+ again (confirm)<br /></br>T – Then check intact PTH<br /></br>O – Overproduction of PTH most common<br /></br>N – Next steps guided by PTH result<br /></br>E – Evaluate bones (DEXA) later<br /></br>S – Surgery if criteria met</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>a. CT scan of the abdomen<br /></br>   – Non-contrast CT is gold standard to find stones, but the stone is already visualized on x-ray, size is known, and there is no obstruction. Adds radiation without altering management.</p></br><p>c. MRIs of the brain and abdomen<br /></br>   – Not part of initial hypercalcemia or nephrolithiasis evaluation; no neurologic findings to warrant brain MRI.</p></br><p>d. Urine culture<br /></br>   – UA lacks pyuria, nitrites, or leukocyte esterase; afebrile. Dysuria is from ureteral irritation. Culture would be low yield.</p></br><p>e. No further testing is indicated<br /></br>   – Ignores unexplained hypercalcemia, risking missed primary hyperparathyroidism.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Take-Home Message</br>In any adult with calcium kidney stones and an elevated serum calcium level, the single most important next study is an intact PTH level to rule in/out primary hyperparathyroidism—the leading treatable cause of recurrent nephrolithiasis.</p>
131. A 67-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her daughter because of a 2-day history of progressive fatigue, confusion, shortness of breath with exertion, and difficulty breathing when she lies flat. At night, she has had the sudden need to run to an open window for air. She had a myocardial infarction 2 years ago but has had no recent chest pain. Her medications are metoprolol, lisinopril, spironolactone, and furosemide. The daughter says that she does not know if her mother has taken her medications during the past 2 days. On arrival, the patient appears ill and in mild respiratory distress; she is ashen. Her temperature is 36.4 C (97.6 F), pulse is 102/min and regular, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 82/46 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 89 percent. Examination shows jugular venous distention. Crackles are heard throughout the lower lung fields. On cardiac examination, an S3 gallop is heard. There is 2+ pitting edema at the ankles. Intravenous administration of which of the following is the most appropriate next step in pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Ceftriaxone</br>b. Dopamine</br>c. Esmolol</br>d. Heparin</br>e. Nitroprusside</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Dopamine (intravenous infusion)</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key phrases identifying acute decompensated heart failure (ADHF)<br /></br>   • “Progressive fatigue, confusion, shortness of breath with exertion” – evidence of low‐output state and pulmonary congestion.<br /></br>   • “Difficulty breathing when she lies flat” (orthopnea) and “sudden need to run to an open window for air at night” (paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea).<br /></br>   • Physical findings: jugular venous distention, S3 gallop, diffuse crackles, ankle edema → volume overload.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Evidence the patient is in cardiogenic shock (“cold &amp; wet” profile)<br /></br>   • Hypotension: 82/46 mm Hg.<br /></br>   • Tachycardia, cool/ashen appearance, altered mentation.<br /></br>   • Low O2 saturation despite spontaneous breathing.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Metacognitive approach for similar NBME/Step 2 questions<br /></br>   • Classify ADHF into “warm vs. cold” (perfusion) and “dry vs. wet” (congestion).<br /></br>   • “Cold &amp; wet” requires inotrope ± vasopressor first; diuretics and afterload reduction come only after perfusion is restored.<br /></br>   • Always check blood pressure before giving vasodilators or additional β-blockade.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic to the correct therapy:<br /></br>1. She has poor forward flow (low BP) + volume overload (pulmonary edema).<br /></br>2. The immediate, life-threatening issue is end-organ hypoperfusion.<br /></br>3. Choice of drug: an agent that (a) increases contractility, and (b) raises systemic vascular resistance enough to support blood pressure.<br /></br>4. Dopamine (dose-dependent β1 inotropy and α1 vasoconstriction) accomplishes both, making it the classic first-line IV pressor/inotrope for hypotensive cardiogenic shock.  </p></br><p>Take-home pearl:<br /></br>“Cold &amp; wet” decompensated HF → start an IV inotrope with vasopressor qualities (dopamine or high-dose norepinephrine). Once perfusion improves, add IV loop diuretic and, if BP tolerates, vasodilators.</p></br><p>Documentation must note: BP, evidence of pulmonary edema, mental status, urine output, rationale for inotrope initiation, and continuous hemodynamic monitoring plan.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• After an old MI, LV systolic dysfunction lowers stroke volume → ↑ LVEDP → pulmonary congestion (“wet”).<br /></br>• Compensatory sympathetic activation is now exhausted → systemic hypotension (“cold”).<br /></br>• Dopamine at 5-15 μg/kg/min → β1 (↑ inotropy/chronotropy) + some α1 (↑ SVR) → ↑ MAP and cardiac output.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls  </p></br><p>Immediate (“ABC”) measures<br /></br>• Oxygen, possible non-invasive ventilation.<br /></br>• IV access + telemetry.<br /></br>• Dopamine infusion titrated to MAP &gt;65 mm Hg.</p></br><p>Subsequent steps (once BP &gt;90 mm Hg)<br /></br>• IV furosemide for diuresis.<br /></br>• Consider IV nitroglycerin or nitroprusside for afterload reduction if still hypertensive and congested.<br /></br>• Optimize chronic HF meds (ACE-I/ARB/ARNI, β-blocker, MRA) after stabilization.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Cold &amp; wet? Press and pep!”<br /></br>• Press = pressor (dopamine/norepinephrine).<br /></br>• Pep = pep up the heart (inotropy).<br /></br>Mnemonic: D-O-P-A:<br /></br>D – Drops in BP<br /></br>O – Orthopnea/paroxysmal dyspnea<br /></br>P – Pulmonary crackles<br /></br>A – Administer DopAmine.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Ceftriaxone – Appropriate for community-acquired pneumonia, but no fever, focal infiltrate, or leukocytosis. Primary problem is cardiogenic shock.  </p></br><p>c. Esmolol – Short-acting β-blocker would further depress contractility and worsen hypotension. β-blockers are held during acute decompensation.  </p></br><p>d. Heparin – Used for pulmonary embolism or acute coronary syndromes. No pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, or new ECG changes. Does not address low cardiac output.  </p></br><p>e. Nitroprusside – Potent arterial/venous vasodilator beneficial in hypertensive pulmonary edema, but contraindicated in MAP &lt;60–65 mm Hg because it can precipitate cardiovascular collapse in already hypotensive patients.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>A hypotensive, volume-overloaded patient with systolic heart failure (“cold &amp; wet”) needs an IV inotrope/vasopressor first—dopamine is the classic choice. Once perfusion is restored, diuretics and vasodilators can safely be added.</p>
132. A study is conducted to assess the incidence of mortality in children with idiopathic seizure disorder and children without seizure disorder. A total of 1283 age-matched patients without significant comorbidities are enrolled in the study and observed for 20 years. Results show that the mortality rate among the 692 children with seizure disorder for the 20-year period following diagnosis is 6.2 percent, whereas the mortality rate among the 591 children without seizure disorder for the same 20-year period is 0.8 percent. Which of the following is the most accurate conclusion based on this study population?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Children with idiopathic seizure disorder have a five times greater risk of mortality than children without seizure disorder</br>b. Children with idiopathic seizure disorder have more than a seven times greater risk of mortality than children without seizure disorder</br>c. Mortality risk cannot be determined with the data provided</br>d. The overall risk of mortality in children during a 20-year period is 5.4 percent</br>e. The overall risk of mortality in children during a 20-year period is 7 percent</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>B. Children with idiopathic seizure disorder have more than a seven-times greater risk of mortality than children without seizure disorder</p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key data points:<br /></br>  – 20-year follow-up, prospective cohort style (exposed vs unexposed).<br /></br>  – Mortality in seizure cohort = 6.2 %.<br /></br>  – Mortality in control cohort = 0.8 %.<br /></br>• Task: interpret the risk difference between the two groups → calculate RELATIVE RISK (RR).<br /></br>• Approach to similar questions:<br /></br>  1. Identify whether the study supplies incidence (risk) in two groups.<br /></br>  2. Divide risk in exposed by risk in unexposed → RR.<br /></br>  3. Translate the numeric RR into a plain-language conclusion that matches one of the answer choices.</p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Risk in exposed (idiopathic seizures) = 6.2 %<br /></br>Risk in unexposed (no seizures) = 0.8 %<br /></br>Relative Risk (RR) = 6.2 / 0.8 ≈ 7.75.<br /></br>Therefore, mortality risk is “more than seven times greater,” matching answer choice B.<br /></br>Core takeaway: When both numerator and denominator are person-based risks, use RR to compare; wording such as “times greater risk” describes RR.</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield pearl)<br /></br>Idiopathic epilepsy carries an elevated long-term mortality mostly from:<br /></br>• SUDEP (sudden unexpected death in epilepsy)<br /></br>• Status epilepticus complications<br /></br>• Injuries associated with seizures<br /></br>While interesting clinically, the question focuses on epidemiologic calculation, not mechanism.</p></br><p>Treatments / Diagnostics<br /></br>Not tested here; no management decision required.</p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>RR formula: “Risk in Row 1 divided by Risk in Row 2.”<br /></br>Mnemonic: “R over R” (Risk of the exposed Over Risk of the reference) → Relative Risk.</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. “Five times greater risk” – Would require RR ≈ 5. Actual RR ≈ 7.8.<br /></br>C. “Mortality risk cannot be determined” – False; incidence proportions are directly provided.<br /></br>D. “Overall risk 5.4 %” – Total deaths ≈ 43 (seizure) + 5 (control) = 48. 48 / 1283 ≈ 3.7 %, not 5.4 %.<br /></br>E. “Overall risk 7 %” – Same miscalculation; inflates overall mortality.  </p></br><p>Avoid the trap: Distinguish between overall (pooled) risk and group-specific risk; and between relative risk and absolute risk difference.</p>
133. A study is being conducted to investigate the association between childhood exposure to secondhand cigarette smoke and risk for developing lung disease as an adult in individuals who have never smoked. A total of 500 adults with lung disease and 500 adults with no history of lung disease are enrolled in the study; participants in both groups are interviewed about exposure to secondhand cigarette smoke during childhood. Which of the following is the most appropriate measure of association for the investigators to report?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Absolute risk</br>b. Attributable risk</br>c. Hazard ratio</br>d. Odds ratio</br>e. Relative risk</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Odds ratio (OR)</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Study design clues<br /></br>   • “500 adults with lung disease (cases) and 500 adults with no history of lung disease (controls)”<br /></br>   • “Participants … are interviewed about exposure … during childhood”  </li></br></ol></br><p>➜ Investigators start with outcome status (disease vs no disease) and look backward for a prior exposure. That is the textbook definition of a case-control study.</p></br><ol></br><li>Key test-taking pattern<br /></br>   • Case-control → cannot calculate incidence because you artificially set the number of cases and controls.<br /></br>   • Without incidence, you cannot compute risk-based measures (absolute risk, relative risk, attributable risk) or time-to-event measures (hazard ratio).<br /></br>   • What you can compute is the odds of exposure among cases vs controls → Odds Ratio.</li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive/meta approach<br /></br>   “Outcome first, look back for exposure? Think CASE-CONTROL → Odds Ratio.”<br /></br>   A quick mental flowchart:<br /></br>   • Cohort (start with exposure) → Risk Ratio; can also get AR.<br /></br>   • RCT → Risk Ratio, AR, HR.<br /></br>   • Case-control → Odds Ratio.<br /></br>   • Cross-sectional → Prevalence Ratio/OR.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>1. Identify design = case-control.<br /></br>2. Recognize incidence cannot be determined.<br /></br>3. Select measure that compares exposure odds = Odds Ratio.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway:<br /></br>In any case-control study, the appropriate association measure is the odds ratio.</p></br><p>Documentation (real life):<br /></br>Would record detailed exposure history (secondhand smoke), disease confirmation, and matching variables to control for confounding.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (concise)  </p></br><p>• Secondhand smoke is a mix of mainstream and sidestream smoke containing carcinogens and irritants → chronic airway inflammation, oxidative DNA damage → ↑ risk COPD, lung cancer even in never-smokers.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Pearls (FYI)  </p></br><p>• No tests here; but epidemiologic findings often prompt public-health measures such as smoking bans, parental counseling, and early COPD screening in exposed populations.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“Case means Came with Condition first → Odds only.”<br /></br>OR = “Outcome-Retro” (start with outcome, look retro).</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Absolute risk (incidence of disease in exposed group)<br /></br>   • Requires following exposed vs unexposed over time to COUNT new cases. Not possible when you fix the number of cases/controls at enrollment.</p></br><p>b. Attributable risk (Riskexposed − Riskunexposed)<br /></br>   • Same limitation as absolute risk; incidence data unavailable in case-control design.</p></br><p>c. Hazard ratio<br /></br>   • Time-to-event (survival) analysis from cohort or RCT with variable follow-up. The present study does not track time until disease onset.</p></br><p>e. Relative risk (Risk Ratio)<br /></br>   • Needs incidence in exposed vs unexposed populations; cannot be derived when outcome groups are predetermined.</p></br><p>Common pitfall: Seeing equal numbers of people in two groups (500/500) may trick students into thinking “cohort,” but the direction (outcome → exposure) defines it as case-control.</p></br><p>───────────────────────</p>
134. A 2608-g (5-lb 12-oz) newborn is delivered at term to a 32-year-old primigravid woman who received no prenatal care. Examination of the newborn shows generalized hypertonia. Meconium testing for cocaine metabolites is positive. A review of the literature shows that this screening test has a sensitivity of 100 percent and a true-negative rate of 90 percent. Which of the following is necessary to calculate the positive predictive value of the test?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Incidence of no prenatal care</br>b. Negative predictive value of the test</br>c. Prevalence of cocaine use by pregnant women in the community</br>d. Rate of newborn testing for cocaine</br>e. Specificity of the test</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Prevalence of cocaine use by pregnant women in the community  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues: term newborn, generalized hyper­tonia, mother with no prenatal care, positive meconium screen for cocaine.<br /></br>• Test facts the question explicitly gives: sensitivity = 100 %, “true-negative rate” = 90 % (i.e., specificity = 90 %).<br /></br>• Task: “Which of the following is necessary to calculate the positive predictive value (PPV)?”<br /></br>Meta-approach: The exam writers want to see if you know how predictive values depend on disease prevalence, not just on sensitivity/specificity. Always recall the 2×2 table:<br /></br>             Disease + Disease −<br /></br>  Test +  TP    FP<br /></br>  Test −  FN    TN<br /></br>PPV = TP / (TP + FP). To fill in TP and FP you must know how many people in your sample actually have the disease (prevalence).</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. PPV links a test result (“positive”) to the probability of true disease.<br /></br>2. We are given operating characteristics (sensitivity, specificity) but we still need the <em>baseline probability</em> that a pregnant woman in this community uses cocaine. That baseline is the prevalence.<br /></br>3. Once prevalence is known, you can assign absolute numbers (e.g., 1000 pregnancies) and compute TP and FP, then PPV.</p></br><p>Take-home: Predictive values move with prevalence; sensitivity &amp; specificity do not.</p></br><p>Documentation piece (real life): When ordering or interpreting meconium toxicology, document maternal risk factors and community prevalence data to interpret a positive result’s likelihood of true exposure.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (quick)<br /></br>• Maternal cocaine use → fetal vasoconstriction, possible intrauterine growth restriction, neurobehavioral issues (e.g., hypertonia).<br /></br>• Meconium testing detects metabolites accumulated during the 2nd &amp; 3rd trimesters, useful when prenatal history is lacking.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Tips<br /></br>“PRevalence determines Predictive values” – both start with “PR”.<br /></br>Alternatively: “S and S (Sensitivity &amp; Specificity) are Stable with prevalence; PPV and NPV Paddle up and down with Prevalence.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Incidence of no prenatal care<br /></br> – Irrelevant to the 2×2 table for cocaine use; affects screening decisions, not PPV calculation.  </p></br><p>b. Negative predictive value of the test<br /></br> – NPV is an <em>output</em> like PPV, not an input. You can’t derive PPV from NPV without prevalence.  </p></br><p>d. Rate of newborn testing for cocaine<br /></br> – That tells how often the test is ordered, not how common cocaine exposure actually is nor the intrinsic test properties.  </p></br><p>e. Specificity of the test<br /></br> – Already provided (true-negative rate 90 %). Knowing it again doesn’t add missing information; prevalence is still needed to find FP count.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>To convert sensitivity and specificity into a predictive value, you must plug in the disease’s prevalence. Hence the prevalence of maternal cocaine use is required to calculate the positive predictive value of the meconium screen.</p>
135. A randomized placebo-controlled trial is initiated to investigate the use of a new medication for the treatment of hypertension. The trial enrolled 1000 men and women ages 40 years and older; these patients are equally randomized to either the new medication or a placebo. The primary outcome was established as cardiovascular death or morbidity. After 1 year, 4.2 percent of patients taking the new medication experienced the primary outcome as determined by hospital records, compared with 3.4 percent of patients taking placebo (P=.52). The investigators then reanalyze the outcomes for only African American study patients. Of the 250 African American patients in the study, 2.4 percent of those taking the new medication experienced the primary outcome, compared with 6.0 percent in the placebo group (P=.03). The investigators conclude that the medication is effective only for African American patients. Which of the following is the primary threat to the validity of this conclusion?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Inadequate sample size</br>b. Post hoc analysis</br>c. Recall bias</br>d. Sampling bias</br>e. Volunteer bias</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Post hoc analysis  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Testing Strategy<br /></br>1. Primary analysis (all 1000 patients) → NO statistical difference (4.2 % vs 3.4 %, P = 0.52).<br /></br>2. Investigators then “re-analyze” a subgroup (African-American, n = 250) → now find a statistically significant difference (2.4 % vs 6.0 %, P = 0.03).<br /></br>   • Key clue: this subgroup comparison was not prespecified in the study design or statistical plan.<br /></br>   • When a primary end point is negative and investigators start slicing data into many subgroups, the probability of finding at least one “significant” result by chance alone rises rapidly (inflated type I error).<br /></br>3. They conclude the drug “works only in African Americans.” That conclusion rests entirely on an unplanned, after-the-fact analysis—classic post hoc (data-dredging) error.</p></br><p>Metacognitive pearl: When you see “investigators reanalyzed” or “looked only at women/men/left-handed truck drivers, etc.” after a null primary result, immediately think of post hoc subgroup analysis as the validity threat.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Stepwise logic the exam wants you to follow:<br /></br>• Was the analysis prespecified?  — No.<br /></br>• Was the primary outcome significant?  — No.<br /></br>• Are they trying to salvage a positive finding by examining multiple subgroups? — Yes.<br /></br>⇒ Therefore the main threat is post hoc analysis (increased risk of spurious association).</p></br><p>Take-home: Subgroup findings are only credible when (a) prespecified, (b) biologically plausible, and (c) interaction tests confirm the treatment effect differs between subgroups. Otherwise regard as hypothesis-generating, not practice-changing.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Statistics / Pathophysiology Refresher<br /></br>• Type I error (α): probability of concluding there is a difference when none exists.<br /></br>• Multiple comparisons without adjustment (Bonferroni, Holm, FDR, etc.) inflate α.<br /></br>• Post hoc analyses exploit this inflation; the more slices, the greater the false-positive rate.<br /></br>No pathophysiology of hypertension is needed here; this is purely study-design methodology.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Rundown<br /></br>A. Inadequate sample size—Threatens power (type II error) and makes it hard to find real differences. Here they DID find a difference; the problem is false-positives, not false-negatives.<br /></br>C. Recall bias—Occurs in retrospective self-report studies (e.g., case–control dietary questionnaires). Outcomes here came from hospital records, not memories.<br /></br>D. Sampling bias—Would mean the enrolled subjects don’t represent the target population (e.g., only wealthy insured patients). Randomized allocation inside the trial handles sampling; issue voiced is about subgroup re-analysis.<br /></br>E. Volunteer bias—Bias from who chooses to participate in a trial vs who doesn’t. Possible in any RCT but not the primary flaw undermining the African-American conclusion.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids<br /></br>• “If you torture the data long enough, it will confess” = Post hoc analysis.<br /></br>• PRE - SPECIFY or let it DIE (no retrospective fishing).<br /></br>• Think “S.T.A.T.” for subgroup credibility:<br /></br>  S = Stated in protocol,<br /></br>  T = Tested interaction,<br /></br>  A = Adequate power,<br /></br>  T = Theoretically plausible.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Documentation Note (real-world)<br /></br>In the study report you would need to label the African-American analysis as exploratory, adjust for multiple comparisons, and explicitly state it should not guide clinical practice without confirmatory trials.</p>
136. A 37-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 1-week history of pain with swallowing. He received the diagnosis of AIDS 4 years ago, but he has not been able to tolerate highly active antiretroviral therapy. He currently takes trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows a few white plaques over the pharynx. No other abnormalities are noted. Laboratory studies show a CD4+ T-lymphocyte count of 50/mm3 (Normal≥500) and a plasma HIV viral load of 50,000 copies/mL. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Acyclovir</br>b. Amphotericin B</br>c. Ciprofloxacin</br>d. Fluconazole</br>e. Foscarnet</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>d. Fluconazole  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Key clues<br /></br>   • “Pain with swallowing” = odynophagia → think infectious esophagitis in an AIDS patient.<br /></br>   • CD4 count 50/mm³ (severe immunosuppression).<br /></br>   • “A few white plaques over the pharynx.” Oral thrush strongly points to Candida.<br /></br>   • No fever, no vesicles, no large linear ulcerations described.  </li></br><li>Pattern recognition for HIV-related esophagitis<br /></br>   • Candida: oral thrush ± esophageal involvement, CD4 &lt;200; plaques/patches.<br /></br>   • HSV: small, well-circumscribed “volcano” or punched-out ulcers, ± perioral lesions.<br /></br>   • CMV: large linear or longitudinal ulcers, usually at CD4 &lt;50.  </li></br><li>Test-taking strategy<br /></br>   • In an HIV patient with odynophagia + thrush, NBME/USMLE expects you to treat empirically for Candida with systemic fluconazole without additional testing (EGD reserved for non-responders).  </li></br></ol></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)  </p></br><p>Step 1: Identify syndrome → infectious esophagitis in AIDS.<br /></br>Step 2: Look for bedside clue to pathogen → oral thrush = Candida.<br /></br>Step 3: Choose first-line therapy → systemic azole (fluconazole) because esophageal disease requires penetration beyond mucosal surface; topical nystatin is inadequate.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Odynophagia + thrush in AIDS = start oral fluconazole empirically.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): “Oral thrush with probable Candida esophagitis; plan: start fluconazole 200 mg PO loading then 100 mg PO daily ×14 days, monitor LFTs.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Candida albicans is part of normal oropharyngeal flora; cell-mediated immunity (Th1) keeps it in check.<br /></br>• With CD4 &lt;200, overgrowth → pseudomembranous plaques; extension to esophagus common when CD4 &lt;100.<br /></br>• Azoles (fluconazole) inhibit fungal 14-α-demethylase → ↓ ergosterol synthesis → defective cell membrane → fungal death.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Notes  </p></br><p>First-line:<br /></br>• Fluconazole 200 mg PO once, then 100–200 mg PO daily for 14 days.  </p></br><p>Second-line / refractory:<br /></br>• Echinocandin (caspofungin, micafungin) or IV amphotericin B.  </p></br><p>Diagnostics:<br /></br>• Endoscopy only if no response in 3–5 days, atypical presentation, or severe pain/bleeding.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“C in Candida = C for Creamy plaques → treat with F-luconazole (F = First-line).”<br /></br>Quick grid for HIV esophagitis (remember the mnemonic “Can Have Candied Gum”):<br /></br>C – Candida → plaques → Fluconazole<br /></br>H – HSV → small volcano ulcers → Acyclovir<br /></br>C – CMV (“Candied” = sugar = long LINEar) → large LINEar ulcers → GanCiclovir (or FosCArnet if resistant)  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Acyclovir<br /></br>   • Indicated for HSV esophagitis (small discrete ulcers ± herpetic labial lesions). No white plaques present.  </p></br><p>b. Amphotericin B<br /></br>   • Reserved for severe, refractory, or systemic candidemia. Too toxic for first-line esophageal candidiasis when fluconazole is effective.  </p></br><p>c. Ciprofloxacin<br /></br>   • Fluoroquinolone antibiotic for gram-negative/atypical bacteria; no role in fungal or viral esophagitis.  </p></br><p>e. Foscarnet<br /></br>   • Second-line for ganciclovir-resistant CMV or acyclovir-resistant HSV. Used when CMV causes large linear ulcers or drug resistance; unnecessary here.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: Jumping to acyclovir or ganciclovir solely because CD4 is &lt;50; always match endoscopic/oral findings to pathogen.</p>
137. A 20-year-old man is brought to the physician by his parents because of auditory hallucinations and bizarre behavior over the past year. He recently dropped out of college and moved back home with his parents. He has not attempted to find a job. He says that he has been feeling strange, "like being in a dream," and talks to his great-great-grandfather who died 50 years ago. He was diagnosed with hepatitis A 2 years ago after an episode of jaundice, and he has been treated with thyroxine for hypothyroidism for the past 6 months. His pulse is 68/min, respirations are 10/min, and blood pressure is 100/70 mm Hg. Physical examination shows hyperreflexia of the lower extremities and mild resting tremor of the upper extremities. On mental status examination, his voice is monotonous, his face is immobile, and he seems very anxious. He stares at the physician and barely answers questions. Serum studies show:</br>Total bilirubin 1 mg/dL</br>Thyroid-stimulating hormone 1 μU/mL</br>AST 21 U/L</br>ALT 20 U/L</br>Urine toxicology screening is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: G</br></br>a. Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis</br>b. Bipolar disorder</br>c. Hepatic encephalopathy</br>d. Major depressive disorder with psychotic features</br>e. Parkinson disease</br>f. Psychotic disorder due to a general medical condition</br>g. Schizophrenia</br>h. Substance-induced psychotic disorder</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>g. Schizophrenia  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Time course: “over the past year.”  Psychotic symptoms ≥ 6 mo automatically puts schizophrenia at the top of the differential (1–6 mo = schizophreniform; &lt; 1 mo = brief psychotic disorder).  </li></br><li>Core psychotic features:<br /></br>   • Auditory hallucinations (“talks to his great-great-grandfather”).<br /></br>   • Bizarre behavior / disorganized life (dropped out of college, no job).  </li></br><li>Negative symptoms: monotone voice, immobile face, poor eye contact, limited speech (“barely answers”), social withdrawal—classic “5 A’s” of negative symptoms (Affect flattening, Alogia, Avolition, Anhedonia, Attention deficit).  </li></br><li>Functional decline: loss of academic/occupational functioning is required by DSM-5.  </li></br><li>Rule-outs:<br /></br>   • No substance use (“urine tox negative”).<br /></br>   • No metabolic derangement—normal LFTs/Bili, normal TSH (so hypothyroidism treated).<br /></br>   • No prominent mood episodes to suggest bipolar or major depressive w/ psychosis.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Approach tip: On exams, once you see (1) ≥ 6 months of psychosis, (2) social/functional decline, and (3) no better explanation, lock in schizophrenia.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Identify psychotic Sx → hallucinations, disorganized behavior.  </li></br><li>Determine duration → 1 year (meets ≥6 mo criterion).  </li></br><li>Assess functional impairment → dropped out, no job.  </li></br><li>Exclude medical/substance causes → labs &amp; tox normal.  </li></br><li>Screen for mood disorder → no manic or major depressive episodes described.<br /></br>Conclusion → DSM-5 criteria for schizophrenia satisfied.</li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home: Duration + psychotic symptoms + decline in function in absence of mood/substance/medical etiology → schizophrenia.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Always record onset, duration, types of psychotic symptoms, level of functioning, substance screen, and medical work-up.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (High-yield)  </p></br><p>• Multifactorial: dopaminergic hyperactivity (mesolimbic → positive symptoms) &amp; hypoactivity (mesocortical → negative symptoms), glutamate &amp; GABA dysregulation, strong genetic component (≈1% lifetime prevalence, ↑↑ with first-degree relative).<br /></br>• Typical onset: late teens–early 30s, earlier in men.<br /></br>• Course: chronic with exacerbations; negative symptoms often predominate over time.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Work-up  </p></br><ol></br><li>Antipsychotics:<br /></br>   • First-line: second-generation (risperidone, aripiprazole, olanzapine, quetiapine, ziprasidone).<br /></br>   • Clozapine for treatment-resistant disease or suicidality.  </li></br><li>Psychosocial interventions: CBT, social skills training, family therapy, vocational rehabilitation.  </li></br><li>Baseline studies before starting antipsychotics: CBC (if clozapine), fasting lipids/glucose, weight/BMI, BP, ECG (QTc).  </li></br><li>Monitor for extrapyramidal symptoms, metabolic syndrome, tardive dyskinesia.</li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“6 letters in S-C-H-I-Z-O = ≥ 6 months” – remember the duration threshold.<br /></br>5 A’s of negative symptoms: Affect flat, Avolition, Alogia, Anhedonia, Attention.<br /></br>SchizoPHRENIA ⇒ Positive (hallucinations) + negative (flat affect) + Reality distortion + ≥6 mo + Impaired function + Absence of mood/SUD.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis – Pure motor neuron disease; no psychosis.<br /></br>b. Bipolar disorder – Needs distinct manic/hypomanic ± depressive episodes; none described.<br /></br>c. Hepatic encephalopathy – Acute fluctuating confusion, asterixis, elevated ammonia, abnormal LFTs; patient’s liver profile is normal and symptoms chronic.<br /></br>d. Major depressive disorder w/ psychotic features – Requires a major depressive episode; vignette lacks pervasive depressed mood, anhedonia, vegetative signs.<br /></br>e. Parkinson disease – Resting tremor &amp; rigidity occur in older adults and motor symptoms dominate; psychosis is late or medication-induced.<br /></br>f. Psychotic disorder due to a general medical condition – Must demonstrate a direct physiologic link (e.g., lupus cerebritis, hyperthyroidism). Normal labs argue against.<br /></br>h. Substance-induced psychotic disorder – Urine tox is negative; symptoms persist far beyond usual intoxication/withdrawal windows.</p></br><p>Key differentiator: Only schizophrenia meets BOTH the duration criterion and the exclusion criteria.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: A 20-year-old man with ≥ 12 months of auditory hallucinations, negative symptoms, and functional decline without mood, substance, or medical causes fulfills DSM-5 criteria for schizophrenia.</p>
138. A 62-year-old man comes to the physician because of sadness and difficulty sleeping since his wife of 35 years died of breast cancer 7 months ago. He began grieving for her before she died, but the grief has become more intense since her death. During this period, he has had loss of appetite and is no longer interested in golfing and having dinner with friends. He has started to believe that he will never feel better and sees himself as worthless. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Mental status examination shows a depressed mood and sad affect. He says that life is no longer tolerable, but he has not thought about killing himself. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Acute stress disorder</br>b. Adjustment disorder with depressed mood</br>c. Dysthymic disorder</br>d. Major depressive disorder</br>e. Normal bereavement</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Major depressive disorder (MDD)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Temporal clues<br /></br>   • Wife died 7 months ago → symptoms have persisted well beyond the first few weeks after the death.<br /></br>   • Adjustment-disorder window is ≤ 6 mo after the stressor ends; acute stress disorder is ≤ 1 mo after trauma.</p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Symptom inventory (SIG-E-CAPS)<br /></br>   • Sleep disturbance – “difficulty sleeping”<br /></br>   • Interest ↓ – “no longer interested in golfing or dinner with friends”<br /></br>   • Guilt/Worthlessness – “sees himself as worthless”<br /></br>   • Energy – (not explicitly stated)<br /></br>   • Concentration – (not stated)<br /></br>   • Appetite/weight – “loss of appetite”<br /></br>   • Psychomotor – (not stated)<br /></br>   • Suicidal ideation – denies active thoughts but says “life is no longer tolerable” (hopelessness)<br /></br>   He meets ≥ 5 symptoms (bolded above), including the core criteria of depressed mood or anhedonia, for ≥ 2 weeks → satisfies DSM-5 major depressive episode.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Bereavement vs MDD “red flags”<br /></br>   • Pervasive worthlessness &amp; hopelessness<br /></br>   • Marked functional impairment (stopped all hobbies/socializing)<br /></br>   • Symptoms not confined to thoughts of the deceased; they are global.<br /></br>   • DSM-5 no longer has a bereavement exclusion—if full MDE criteria are met, diagnose MDD.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive strategy: Anytime grief is accompanied by SIG-E-CAPS features (especially worthlessness, suicidal thoughts, psychosis, severe functional decline), think MDD rather than “normal bereavement.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)<br /></br>1. Identify the stressor (wife’s death) → consider bereavement spectrum and adjustment disorders.<br /></br>2. Check symptom count/duration against MDE criteria → ≥ 5 for ≥ 2 wk → yes.<br /></br>3. Evaluate timeframe → &gt; 6 mo from stressor and persistent → rules out adjustment disorder.<br /></br>4. Look for complicated-grief vs MDD differentiators → worthlessness/hopelessness points to MDD.<br /></br>Key takeaway: Bereavement does not protect a patient from MDD; diagnose MDD whenever full criteria are met.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): record SIG-E-CAPS screen, suicidal-ideation assessment, functional impairment, and offer treatment plan (psychotherapy ± SSRI).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>MDD is linked to dysregulation of monoamine neurotransmitters (↓ serotonin, norepinephrine, dopamine), HPA-axis hyperactivity, and neuroplastic changes (e.g., ↓ hippocampal volume). Genetic predisposition + psychosocial stressors (spousal death) trigger an episode.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>1. First-line:<br /></br>   • Pharmacotherapy – SSRI (e.g., sertraline, escitalopram).<br /></br>   • Psychotherapy – cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) or interpersonal therapy.<br /></br>2. Combined therapy often superior to either alone.<br /></br>3. Screen for suicidality at every visit; consider emergency management if active intent/plan emerges.<br /></br>4. If no response → switch or augment (SNRI, bupropion, mirtazapine).<br /></br>5. Severe/refractory with suicidality or psychosis → electroconvulsive therapy (ECT).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>• SIG-E-CAPS = Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal thoughts.<br /></br>• “Worthless, hopeless, helpless” → think MDD, not normal grief.<br /></br>• Adjustment disorder = “3-6-9 rule”: starts within 3 mo, resolves by 6 mo after stressor, symptoms sub-MDD.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Acute stress disorder<br /></br>   – Requires exposure to a traumatic event with intrusive memories, dissociation, avoidance, arousal for 3 days–1 mo. Wife’s death is a loss (not typically classified as the type of trauma for ASD definition) and duration is 7 mo → wrong.</p></br><p>b. Adjustment disorder with depressed mood<br /></br>   – Onset within 3 mo of stressor and symptoms do NOT meet criteria for MDD; must resolve ≤ 6 mo after stressor ends. Here, symptoms meet full MDE and persist &gt; 6 mo → wrong.</p></br><p>c. Dysthymic disorder (persistent depressive disorder)<br /></br>   – Chronic, low-grade depression most days for ≥ 2 years with no symptom-free period &gt; 2 mo. This patient has an episode &lt; 1 year with more severe (not “low-grade”) symptoms → wrong.</p></br><p>e. Normal bereavement<br /></br>   – Grief waves tied to memories, preserves self-esteem, lacks pervasive worthlessness or suicidal ideation except thoughts of joining the deceased. This patient has global anhedonia and worthlessness → abnormal → wrong.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>The presence of a bereavement stressor does not exclude Major Depressive Disorder. When full SIG-E-CAPS criteria are met for ≥ 2 weeks with pervasive impairment, diagnose and treat MDD.</p>
139. A 27-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by police 1 hour after they found him lying in the street and proclaiming himself to be invulnerable to death because he is 'the Antichrist.' He is not able to give a coherent history, but imipramine and thioridazine bottles are found in his pocket. He appears somnolent. His pulse is 88/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Physical examination shows red, dry skin and dilated pupils. Serum electrolyte concentrations are within the reference ranges. An ECG shows a prolonged QT interval. The patient is admitted to the intensive care unit. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Measurement of blood alcohol concentration</br>b. Measurement of serum imipramine concentration</br>c. Measurement of urine porphobilinogen concentration</br>d. MRI of the brain</br>e. Urine culture</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Measurement of serum imipramine concentration  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Strategy<br /></br>1. What jumps out?<br /></br>   • Empty bottles in his pocket: imipramine (a tricyclic antidepressant, TCA) and thioridazine (low-potency typical antipsychotic).<br /></br>   • Anticholinergic toxidrome: “red, dry skin,” dilated pupils, somnolence (“hot, dry, blind, mad, red”).<br /></br>   • Cardiac conduction abnormality: prolonged QT on ECG.<br /></br>2. What does that pattern equal? Classic TCA (± low-potency antipsychotic) overdose, which can be fatal from arrhythmias and seizures.<br /></br>3. Key Step for similar questions: Whenever the stem gives you a drug container + matching toxidrome + an ECG abnormality, think “quantify the drug level.”  </p></br><p>Metacognitive pearl: The exam often asks for either (a) the lifesaving treatment (IV sodium bicarbonate for TCA-induced QRS widening) or (b) the confirmatory test/level that guides prognosis—here they want the latter.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Why measure a serum imipramine level?<br /></br>• TCAs have a narrow therapeutic window; serum concentrations correlate with risk of ventricular arrhythmias and seizures.<br /></br>• Labs are readily obtainable and guide ICU monitoring and need for serial ECGs / NaHCO₃ therapy.<br /></br>• Thioridazine can prolong QT, but it is rarely measured and is less lethal than TCA overdose. Imipramine level therefore has the highest diagnostic and prognostic yield.  </p></br><p>Documentation if real patient: “Suspected tricyclic antidepressant overdose with anticholinergic signs and QT prolongation; serum TCA level ordered; continuous ECG monitoring; consider sodium bicarbonate if QRS &gt; 100 msec.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology High-Yield Review<br /></br>Tricyclic antidepressants<br /></br>• MOA: Block NE &amp; 5-HT reuptake; additionally block fast Na⁺ channels (cardiotoxic), muscarinic, α₁, and H₁ receptors.<br /></br>• Toxicity triad (“Tri-C”): Convulsions, Coma, Cardiotoxicity (wide QRS/QT, VT/VF).<br /></br>• Anticholinergic effects produce the red/dry skin, mydriasis, urinary retention, hyperthermia.<br /></br>• Mortality mechanism: ventricular arrhythmias from Na⁺-channel blockade → treat with IV sodium bicarbonate which alkalinizes serum and provides Na⁺ load to overcome blockade.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Pitfalls<br /></br>a. Blood alcohol level – No smell of alcohol, no intoxication pattern, no metabolic acidosis. Would not explain anticholinergic signs or QT prolongation.<br /></br>c. Urine porphobilinogen – For acute intermittent porphyria (colicky abdominal pain, neuropathy, psychosis) but QT prolongation and anticholinergic signs are not features.<br /></br>d. MRI brain – Structural imaging is unnecessary; presentation clearly suggests toxic/metabolic etiology, rapid onset, and known ingestions.<br /></br>e. Urine culture – No fever, dysuria, or leukocytosis; UTI does not cause anticholinergic toxidrome or QT prolongation.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Flow (exam memory aid)<br /></br>“ABC + ECG + TCA level” for suspected TCA overdose:<br /></br>A – Airway/oxygen<br /></br>B – Bolus fluids, BP support<br /></br>C – Cardiac monitor (look for QRS &gt; 100 msec, QT prolongation)<br /></br>ECG – obtain immediately and serially<br /></br>TCA level – imipramine / amitriptyline / nortriptyline concentration guides prognosis<br /></br>If QRS &gt; 100 msec or ventricular arrhythmia → IV sodium bicarbonate. Benzodiazepines for seizures.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tip<br /></br>Picture a red, dry “antichrist” (patient’s delusion) standing on railroad tracks shaped like a long QT interval. The railroad cars are labeled “IMI-PRAMINE.” To keep him from being hit, you first measure how fast the train is coming (get the imipramine level) before deciding on the big white buffer (sodium bicarbonate).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>The vignette screams TCA overdose. The single most useful immediate diagnostic test is a serum imipramine (TCA) concentration, which both confirms the diagnosis and stratifies risk for life-threatening arrhythmias.</p>
140. A 32-year-old woman comes to the physician because of weakness in her right leg that began 1 week ago and has resolved over the past 24 hours. She is currently asymptomatic. Two years ago, she had tingling in both hands and an "electric-like" sensation down her spine when she flexed her neck; her symptoms resolved after 3 weeks. Six months ago, she had visual loss in her right eye and pain with eye movement. Examination at that time showed a right central scotoma that resolved after 2 weeks. She has a history of mitral valve prolapse. She takes no medications. Her pulse is 64/min and regular, and blood pressure is 100/60 mm Hg. Cardiac examination shows a systolic click. Funduscopic examination shows mild pallor of the right optic disc; there is no afferent pupillary defect. Muscle strength is normal. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ in the upper extremities and 3+ in the lower extremities. Babinski sign is present on the right. An MRI of the brain is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment for this patient?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Stress management</br>b. Amitriptyline therapy</br>c. Aspirin therapy</br>d. Dexamethasone therapy</br>e. Interferon beta therapy</br>f. Propranolol therapy</br>g. Warfarin therapy</br>h. Radiation therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Interferon-β therapy  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Relapsing neurologic episodes separated in time:<br /></br>   • 2 y ago – distal paresthesias + Lhermitte sign (“electric shock” down spine with neck flexion → cervical cord lesion).<br /></br>   • 6 mo ago – unilateral painful visual loss with central scotoma → optic neuritis.<br /></br>   • 1 wk ago – unilateral leg weakness with brisk reflexes &amp; Babinski → corticospinal tract lesion.<br /></br>   Each attack resolved, fulfilling dissemination in time.</p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Different neuro-anatomic areas involved (optic nerve, cervical cord, corticospinal tract) → dissemination in space.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Supporting exam/MRI: optic disc pallor (prior optic neuritis); hyperreflexia; Babinski; MRI (not shown here) classically demonstrates ovoid, periventricular, T2/FLAIR hyperintense “Dawson finger” lesions.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Classic picture = relapsing–remitting multiple sclerosis (MS).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach for future questions:<br /></br>• Any young woman with episodic CNS deficits that resolve → think MS.<br /></br>• Ask: Is the patient currently in an acute flare (needs steroids) or between flares (needs disease-modifying therapy, DMT)?  </p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• She is now asymptomatic; current neuro exam shows only residual signs (hyperreflexia, optic pallor) ⇒ NOT an acute relapse.<br /></br>• Goal: prevent new relapses and slow disability progression. First-line DMTs for relapsing-remitting MS include interferon-β, glatiramer acetate, dimethyl fumarate; escalation agents: natalizumab, fingolimod, ocrelizumab, etc.<br /></br>• Therefore interferon-β therapy is most appropriate.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: In a patient with established relapsing-remitting MS who is not in an acute attack, start disease-modifying therapy such as interferon-β to reduce relapse rate and delay progression.</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Autoimmune, T-cell–mediated demyelination of CNS with loss of oligodendrocytes.<br /></br>• Lesions disseminated in space/time → episodic neurologic deficits.<br /></br>• Risk factors: women, 20–40 y, HLA-DRB1, northern latitude, low vitamin D.<br /></br>• Immunomodulators (interferon-β) decrease inflammatory cytokines, up-regulate anti-inflammatory pathways, and maintain blood-brain barrier integrity.</p></br><p>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>Acute relapse: high-dose IV methylprednisolone ± oral taper.<br /></br>Chronic DMT (initiate soon after diagnosis):<br /></br>  – Interferon-β (first-line)<br /></br>  – Glatiramer acetate<br /></br>  – Dimethyl fumarate<br /></br>  – Second-line/for aggressive disease: natalizumab, fingolimod, ocrelizumab, alemtuzumab, cladribine.<br /></br>Symptom control: baclofen (spasticity), amantadine (fatigue), oxybutynin (urge incontinence), etc.<br /></br>Diagnostics: MRI brain/spine with gadolinium (active lesions enhance), CSF (oligoclonal IgG bands, ↑myelin basic protein).</p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Multiple Sclerosis has Multiple Spots and Multiple Solutions:”<br /></br>• Spots = periventricular plaques on MRI.<br /></br>• Solutions = Steroids for flares, beta-Interferon Between flares.<br /></br>Mnemonic: “IFN-β for the Brain’s Blotches.”</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Stress management – Stress can trigger flares but is not disease-modifying therapy.<br /></br>b. Amitriptyline – Used for neuropathic pain/depression; does not reduce relapse frequency.<br /></br>c. Aspirin – Prevents thrombotic events; MS deficits are demyelinating, not ischemic.<br /></br>d. Dexamethasone – Glucocorticoids are for acute relapses; patient is currently stable.<br /></br>f. Propranolol – For essential tremor, migraine prophylaxis, thyrotoxicosis, etc.; no role in MS disease modification.<br /></br>g. Warfarin – Anticoagulation for thromboembolism prevention (e.g., atrial fibrillation, mechanical valves); unrelated to MS pathogenesis.<br /></br>h. Radiation therapy – Used for neoplasms; MS is autoimmune, not neoplastic.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Key Exam Pearl<br /></br>When a young woman presents with neurologic deficits that “come and go,” immediately think MS. Decide first: flare (give steroids) or remission (start DMT such as interferon-β).</p>
141. A 77-year-old man with hypertension comes to the physician for a routine examination. His medications are metoprolol and simvastatin. His father died of a cerebral infarction. The patient smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 25 years but quit 10 years ago. He walks 2 miles daily. He is 180 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and weighs 82 kg (180 lb); BMI is 25 kg/m2. His pulse is 70/min, and blood pressure is 140/90 mm Hg. Examination shows no abnormalities. Serum studies show a cholesterol concentration of 210 mg/dL. Results of a recent cardiac stress test are negative. Which of the following is the strongest predisposing factor for cerebral infarction in this patient during the next 5 years?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Age</br>b. Family history</br>c. Hyperlipidemia</br>d. Lack of antiplatelet therapy</br>e. Smoking history</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Age  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• 77-year-old man → places him in the “older than 75” age bracket.<br /></br>• Well-controlled hypertension (140/90 on β-blocker), ex-smoker, LDL only mildly elevated (total cholesterol 210 mg/dL).<br /></br>• No neurologic symptoms now; question is asking about future risk of cerebral infarction (ischemic stroke) over the next 5 years.<br /></br>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>1. List all classic stroke risk factors (age, HTN, diabetes, AFib, prior TIA, smoking, hyperlipidemia, FHx).<br /></br>2. Determine which of the answer choices are present.<br /></br>3. Recall epidemiologic data: the single strongest independent predictor of stroke is advancing age; risk roughly doubles with each decade after age 55.<br /></br>4. Select “Age” because none of the other listed factors confers as large an incremental risk.  </p></br><p>Take-home pattern: When age ≥75 is an answer choice for cerebrovascular disease risk, it trumps family history, former smoking, or modest lipid elevation unless another option is uncontrolled hypertension or atrial fibrillation.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why age?<br /></br>• Non-modifiable, but most powerful: incidence of ischemic stroke rises exponentially with aging vasculature due to cumulative endothelial injury, atherosclerosis, arterial stiffness, and increased prevalence of atrial fibrillation and small-vessel lipohyalinosis.<br /></br>• Population studies (Framingham, ARIC) show age ≥75 carries &gt;7-fold higher stroke risk vs age 55–64, independent of other risk factors.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient):<br /></br>– Note age category in cerebrovascular risk assessment.<br /></br>– Record BP control, statin use, smoking status, consideration of antiplatelet therapy.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><ol></br><li>Atherosclerotic plaque formation accelerates over decades.  </li></br><li>Aging arteries lose compliance, develop intimal thickening → predisposes to large-artery thrombosis and small-vessel (lacunar) disease.  </li></br><li>Cumulative oxidative stress + endothelial dysfunction → higher likelihood of plaque rupture or thromboembolism.  </li></br></ol></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Prevention Snapshot  </p></br><p>Modifiable first-line measures (in descending effect size):<br /></br>1. Tight BP control (thiazide, ACE-I/ARB, CCB preferred).<br /></br>2. Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 81 mg daily) if ≥10% 10-yr ASCVD risk.<br /></br>3. High-intensity statin if age 40-75 with LDL ≥70 and ASCVD risk ≥7.5%.<br /></br>4. Smoking cessation (already achieved).<br /></br>Non-modifiable: age, sex, genetics—recognize but cannot alter.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“OLD age Overpowers Others for stroke.”<br /></br>OR “Strokes LOVE A-GE”:<br /></br>Lipid, Obesity, Vices (smoking), Elevated BP all matter, but Age gets capital letters.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>B. Family history<br /></br>• Parental stroke &lt;65 yrs mildly increases risk (≤2-fold). Still far less than the &gt;7-fold jump seen between ages 55 and 75.  </p></br><p>C. Hyperlipidemia<br /></br>• Elevates ischemic stroke risk but effect size is modest once LDL is &lt;190 mg/dL. Statin therapy further blunts this risk.  </p></br><p>D. Lack of antiplatelet therapy<br /></br>• Primary prevention benefit is small in a 77-year-old with controlled BP and no diabetes; aspirin’s NNT for stroke prevention is higher than the risk conferred by age itself.  </p></br><p>E. Smoking history<br /></br>• Current smoking doubles stroke risk. After 10 years of cessation, risk falls substantially and approaches that of never-smokers, so it is now lower than the age effect.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: Many students instinctively pick “hyperlipidemia” or “smoking” because they are modifiable, forgetting the magnitude of epidemiologic risk associated with advanced age.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>Advancing age is the single strongest independent predictor of ischemic stroke; once a patient crosses 75 years old, age alone outweighs other individual risk factors such as former smoking, modest hyperlipidemia, or family history.</p>
142. A 57-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 10-year history of an intermittent rash on his face. The rash worsens with cold weather and stress. Examination shows a scaly, erythematous rash in several areas around the nose and eyebrows. There is dandruff from the scalp. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Acne rosacea</br>b. Acne vulgaris</br>c. Herpesvirus infection</br>d. Psoriasis</br>e. Seborrheic dermatitis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Seborrheic dermatitis  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key phrases: “10-year history,” “intermittent rash,” “worsens with cold weather and stress,” “scaly, erythematous rash around the nose and eyebrows,” and “dandruff from the scalp.”<br /></br>• Distribution: nasolabial folds, glabella/eyebrows, scalp (classic sebaceous‐gland–rich areas).<br /></br>• Chronic, relapsing course that flares in winter and during emotional stress = hallmark of seborrheic dermatitis (SD).<br /></br>Approach tip: When you see “scaly erythema” in oily facial zones + scalp flaking, think Malassezia-driven SD first unless another specific clue is present.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Identify lesion morphology (erythematous plaques covered by greasy scales).<br /></br>2. Note anatomic pattern (scalp, central face, eyebrows, beard, presternal area possible).<br /></br>3. Recognize aggravating factors (cold/dry climate, stress, HIV, Parkinson disease).<br /></br>4. Compare to differentials that can affect the face but do NOT combine all the above (see Incorrect Answer Analysis).<br /></br>Take-home: Scalp dandruff + facial “greasy scale” = seborrheic dermatitis until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: Inflammatory reaction to Malassezia (Pityrosporum) yeast that thrives in sebaceous regions.<br /></br>• Risk factors: Male sex, Parkinson disease, HIV/AIDS, mood disorders, stress, cold/dry weather.<br /></br>• Mechanism: Lipase activity of Malassezia converts sebaceous triglycerides into irritating free fatty acids → epidermal hyperproliferation &amp; inflammation.<br /></br>• Clinical manifestations:<br /></br>  – Infants: cradle cap.<br /></br>  – Adults: dandruff, eyebrows/glabella scale, moustache/beard, external ear canals, sternum.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>First line:<br /></br>• Topical antifungals—ketoconazole 2% shampoo/cream, ciclopirox.<br /></br>• Mild low-potency topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone 1%) for short courses to reduce inflammation.<br /></br>Adjuncts: selenium sulfide, zinc pyrithione shampoos; topical calcineurin inhibitors (pimecrolimus, tacrolimus) for steroid-sparing use on face.<br /></br>Labs/imaging: None needed for straightforward presentation. Consider HIV testing if unusually severe/refractory.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Seborrheic = SEBO-rich areas”: Scalp, Eyebrows, Beard, Oleaginous (oily) skin.<br /></br>Picture a “Santa-Claus” face—red, flaky eyebrows &amp; beard that worsen every winter.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Acne rosacea<br /></br>• Presents with centrofacial erythema, telangiectasias, papules/pustules; triggers alcohol, heat, spicy foods.<br /></br>• Lacks greasy scales and scalp dandruff. No comedones (vs acne vulgaris).  </p></br><p>b. Acne vulgaris<br /></br>• Comedones, papules, pustules, nodules on face/back; peaks in adolescence/young adulthood.<br /></br>• Not typically scaly; does not preferentially involve nasolabial folds/eyebrows; scalp dandruff not part of picture.  </p></br><p>c. Herpesvirus infection<br /></br>• Grouped vesicles on erythematous base, painful; recurrent but short-lived lesions in same location (perioral).<br /></br>• No diffuse scaling, no relationship to cold weather except triggering, and no scalp involvement.  </p></br><p>d. Psoriasis<br /></br>• Well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scale, often on extensor surfaces, scalp can be involved.<br /></br>• Facial involvement uncommon; would expect nail pitting or Auspitz sign; course tends to be persistent rather than waxing/waning purely with weather stress.  </p></br><p>e. Seborrheic dermatitis—matches every vignette clue (distribution, morphology, triggers, chronic waxing/waning).</p>
143. A 67-year-old man comes to the physician 1 hour after a 5-minute episode of moderate left-sided chest pain that began while he was unloading groceries. The pain radiated to the left jaw. During the past 3 months, he also has had progressive shortness of breath when lying down. He has a 1-year history of decreasing stamina; he now often has to stop and rest while taking out the trash. He has well-controlled hypertension, well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus, and chronic bronchitis. His medications are hydrochlorothiazide, metoprolol, glipizide, and a nebulized albuterol inhaler as needed. He smoked one and one-half packs of cigarettes daily for 40 years but quit 10 years ago. His temperature is 37.2 C (99 F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 100/58 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 90 percent. Examination shows jugular venous distention. Crackles are heard at both lung bases. On cardiac examination, there is an S3 and a grade 2/6 harsh, late-peaking, systolic murmur heard best at the upper right sternal border. There is 2+ pitting edema of the lower legs. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Coronary angiography</br>b. Echocardiography</br>c. Exercise stress test</br>d. MR aortography</br>e. Spiral CT scan of the chest</br></br></br><p>Correct answer: B. Echocardiography  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues:<br /></br>  – 67-y/o man with exertional, crescendo–decrescendo chest pain radiating to jaw (classic angina pattern).<br /></br>  – Progressive exertional limitation + orthopnea, JVD, bibasilar crackles, S3, bilateral leg edema → decompensated left-sided heart failure.<br /></br>  – NEW cardiac finding: “grade 2/6 harsh, late-peaking, systolic murmur heard best at the upper right sternal border” → classic description of aortic stenosis (AS). A late-peaking murmur in an older patient essentially equals severe AS until proven otherwise.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: no tearing chest pain, unequal pulses (dissection less likely); no pleuritic pain, hemoptysis, tachypnea (PE less likely).<br /></br>• Metacognitive tip: When a question presents HF symptoms + a classic murmur, FIRST confirm the structural lesion with imaging before jumping to ischemia or stress testing.  </p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Identify decompensated heart failure.<br /></br>2. Note physical-exam pattern diagnostic of AS.<br /></br>3. Ask: “What test definitively confirms severity of AS and provides LV systolic function information?” → Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE).<br /></br>Core takeaway: In any patient with suspected valvular heart disease (especially AS) causing HF or angina, the next diagnostic step is an echocardiogram.  </p></br><p>Documentation points if real patient: murmur description, HF class, vitals, O2 sat, risk factors, and urgent order for stat echo.  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)<br /></br>• Calcific (“senile”) AS: degeneration and calcification of the aortic valve in patients &gt;60 y/o.<br /></br>• AS produces LV outflow obstruction → ↑LV pressure → concentric hypertrophy → diastolic then systolic failure, S3, pulmonary congestion, and low-output symptoms (angina, syncope, HF).<br /></br>• Late-peaking murmur correlates with more severe obstruction because peak pressure gradient occurs later in systole.  </p></br><p>Treatment / Diagnostic Tests Review<br /></br>Echocardiogram yields:<br /></br>– Valve area, peak/mean gradient, severity (mild, moderate, severe).<br /></br>– LV ejection fraction, wall thickness, diastolic function.<br /></br>– Pulmonary pressures, other valvular lesions.<br /></br>Management after echo:<br /></br>– Severe symptomatic AS → surgical aortic valve replacement (SAVR) or transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR).<br /></br>– Guideline-directed HF therapy tailored to EF; cautious use of vasodilators/diuretics in critical AS.  </p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“OLD CASH”: Older patient with Late-peaking, Diamond-shaped (crescendo-decrescendo), harsh Systolic murmur at High (right 2nd ICS) → think aortic Stenosis → Echo first.  </p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Coronary angiography – Appropriate for unstable angina/NSTEMI or pre-valve surgery, but valve severity must be defined with echo first.<br /></br>c. Exercise stress test – Contraindicated in severe, unconfirmed AS due to risk of sudden death; never stress-test until valve disease is ruled out.<br /></br>d. MR aortography – Used for aortic dissection/aneurysm evaluation, not for valvular lesions causing HF.<br /></br>e. Spiral CT chest – Evaluates pulmonary embolism; patient’s presentation (HF signs + classic AS murmur) doesn’t fit PE picture.  </p></br><p>Bottom line: In a patient with symptoms and signs suggestive of aortic stenosis–induced heart failure, echocardiography is the essential first diagnostic study.</p>
144. A 27-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 50 minutes after he sustained a gunshot wound to his right leg. He was injured while attempting to flee a crime scene.
