Paramedics report significant bright red bleeding that was controlled with pressure dressings at the scene. On arrival, the patient is conscious and responsive. His pulse is 100/min and regular, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 100/85 mm Hg. Examination shows a single gunshot entry and exit wound in the right thigh with moderate oozing of bright red and dark blood but no pulsatile bleeding. The mid thigh is edematous. The right femoral pulse is palpable; no popliteal or pedal pulses are palpable. The right ankle brachial index is 0.5 (N>1) at the dorsalis pedis. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. In addition to intravenous fluid administration, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Arterial ligation</br>b. CT scan of the right lower extremity</br>c. Endovascular stent placement</br>d. Intraoperative angiography</br>e. Intravenous administration of heparin</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>D. Intraoperative angiography</p></br><p>───────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Mechanism: penetrating (gunshot) trauma to the mid-thigh ➜ high suspicion for superficial femoral or popliteal artery injury.<br /></br>• Key hard signs of vascular injury<br /></br>  – Absent distal pulses (no popliteal or pedal)<br /></br>  – Ankle–brachial index (ABI) 0.5 (&lt;0.9 is abnormal; &lt;0.5 = severe ischemia)<br /></br>• Other findings: continued oozing, expanding edema. Systolic BP 100 mm Hg suggests marginal perfusion.</p></br><p>Hard signs = time-critical limb‐threatening ischemia. The algorithm is:</br>1. Stabilize (IV fluids, pressure dressings).<br /></br>2. Straight to the operating room for exploration ± intra-operative angiography to define the exact lesion and guide repair. Imaging that requires moving to CT delays reperfusion and risks limb loss.</p></br><p>Test-taking pearl: “No distal pulses after penetrating extremity trauma” → skip CT, go to OR.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise thought process:</br>1. Determine presence of hard vs soft vascular signs.<br /></br>   • Hard = pulsatile bleeding, expanding hematoma, bruit/thrill, distal ischemia, absent pulses/ABI &lt;0.9.<br /></br>2. Hard sign present → emergent surgical exploration.<br /></br>3. Because the anatomic level of injury is uncertain (femoral pulse present but popliteal absent), intraoperative angiography helps localize the lesion quickly without delaying repair.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: In limb-threatening penetrating vascular injuries, definitive operative management (often aided by intraoperative angiography) supersedes pre-operative imaging.</p></br><p>Documentation points (real patient): time of injury, neurovascular exam (pulses, motor, sensory), ABI, response to resuscitation, consent for emergent exploration and possible bypass/graft.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)</br>• Penetrating trauma can produce transection, intimal flap, thrombosis, or spasm.<br /></br>• Loss of distal pulses/low ABI reflects critical reduction in arterial flow. Ischemia &gt;6 h risks irreversible muscle-nerve damage → amputation.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls</br>First-line: Emergent OR exploration.<br /></br>• Intra-op angiography: catheter inserted proximally, contrast injected under C-arm to map injury; repair with primary repair, interposition graft (often saphenous vein), or bypass.<br /></br>• Systemic heparin usually given after proximal and distal control is obtained to prevent thrombosis (but not the very first step).<br /></br>• CT angiography (CTA) or duplex US is reserved for soft-sign cases (normal pulses, ABI ≥0.9).</p></br><p>───────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“SHOT” mnemonic for hard vascular signs:<br /></br>S – Shot/Sharp bleeding (pulsatile)<br /></br>H – Hematoma expanding<br /></br>O – Occluded flow (absent pulse/low ABI)<br /></br>T – Thrill/bruit<br /></br>Find any → “SHOT to OR.”</p></br><p>───────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong</br>A. Arterial ligation<br /></br>• Acceptable only for small distal branches or uncontrollable hemorrhage in a non-salvageable limb. Ligation of the superficial femoral/popliteal artery in a viable limb would cause limb loss.</p></br><p>B. CT scan of the right lower extremity<br /></br>• CTA is excellent in stable patients with soft signs. Here it wastes precious ischemia time.</p></br><p>C. Endovascular stent placement<br /></br>• May be considered after definitive angiographic definition of lesion; still requires imaging first and generally not initial management in the setting of hard signs and possible transection.</p></br><p>E. Intravenous administration of heparin<br /></br>• Heparinization is useful once arterial control is obtained, but giving it now before knowing the exact injury risks worsening bleeding and does not restore flow.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>Absent distal pulses after penetrating extremity trauma = hard vascular sign → go straight to the OR with intraoperative angiography to diagnose and repair the arterial injury without delay.</p>
145. A 33-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 1, at 35 weeks’ gestation is admitted to the hospital because of a 1-hour history of moderate, bright red vaginal bleeding. During this time, she has saturated two sanitary pads. She has not felt contractions or loss of amniotic fluid and reports good fetal movement. She has received no prenatal care. Her first pregnancy ended in cesarean delivery at term because of fetal bradycardia. She is unemployed and lives in a local homeless shelter. She has a history of cocaine use but has not used cocaine during the past 2 months. Her temperature is 37 C (98.6 F), pulse is 100/min and regular, and blood pressure is 90/60 mm Hg. The abdomen is nontender and consistent in size with a 35-week gestation. External fetal monitoring shows a heart rate of 150/min with moderate variability, several spontaneous accelerations, and occasional variable decelerations. An external tocometer shows irregular uterine contractions. Examination shows a small amount of bright red blood at the perineum. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Apt test</br>b. Contraction stress test</br>c. Digital cervical examination</br>d. Immediate cesarean delivery</br>e. Pelvic ultrasonography</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>E. Pelvic ultrasonography  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key phrases that jump out<br /></br>   • “35 weeks’ gestation … moderate, bright-red vaginal bleeding”<br /></br>   • “No contractions or abdominal pain; abdomen nontender” → painless bleeding<br /></br>   • Prior cesarean delivery (scar on uterus)<br /></br>   • Risk factors: no prenatal care, homelessness, cocaine history<br /></br>   • Fetal tracing: baseline 150/min, moderate variability, occasional variable decels → currently reassuring<br /></br>   • Maternal BP 90/60 mm Hg (mildly low), but she is alert and not in extremis  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pattern recognition<br /></br>   • Painless third-trimester bleeding + previous C-section = think placenta previa until proven otherwise.<br /></br>   • A key test-taking rule: NEVER place fingers (digital exam) into the cervix when placenta previa is on the differential; first localize the placenta by ultrasound.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>   • Step 1: Is mom unstable? No (BP a little low but mentating; fetal status OK).<br /></br>   • Step 2: Painless vs painful bleeding? Painless → placenta previa or vasa previa → localize placenta.<br /></br>   • Step 3: Imaging before any vaginal exam or labor-stimulation test.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why ultrasound first?<br /></br>• Placenta previa = placental tissue covering (or within 2 cm of) the internal cervical os. Digital or speculum manipulation can shear placental vessels → catastrophic hemorrhage.<br /></br>• Transabdominal ultrasound (and, if needed, transvaginal) localizes placenta quickly, is non-invasive, and guides whether expectant management or delivery is required.<br /></br>• Because fetal tracing is currently reassuring and hemorrhage volume is modest (2 pads), immediate C-section can be deferred until diagnosis is confirmed.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real-world)<br /></br>• Amount/color of bleeding, maternal vitals, fetal heart tracing category, abdominal tenderness, uterine tone, plan to obtain STAT bedside ultrasound, IV access &amp; type+screen initiated.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>“First painless bleed at ≥20 wk + risk factors = order ultrasound to rule out placenta previa; no digital exams until placenta location is known.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review – Placenta Previa  </p></br><p>Etiology: Implantation over or near the cervical os.<br /></br>Risk factors: Prior C-section (scar acts as nidus), multiparity, multiple gestation, maternal age &gt;35, smoking, cocaine use.<br /></br>Clinical picture: Sudden painless vaginal bleeding after 20 weeks; uterus soft, nontender; fetal parts usually palpable.<br /></br>Complications: Maternal hemorrhage, preterm birth, abnormal placental invasion (accreta spectrum).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Snapshot  </p></br><p>Diagnostic test of choice:<br /></br>• Transabdominal, followed (if equivocal) by transvaginal ultrasound (safe and more accurate).  </p></br><p>Management once previa confirmed:<br /></br>• Expectant (pelvic rest, no intercourse/digital exams) if bleeding stops and both patients stable.<br /></br>• Corticosteroids if &lt;37 wk for lung maturity.<br /></br>• Planned C-section at 36–37 wk or earlier for heavy/recurrent bleeding or non-reassuring fetal status.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic:<br /></br>“PreVia = Painless, Very red, Image before Fingers.”<br /></br>Visual: Picture a “STOP” sign over the cervix—do not cross (no fingers) until ultrasound clears the path.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Apt test<br /></br>• Detects fetal vs maternal blood (alkali denaturation) in suspected vasa previa or neonatal GI bleeding. Does not identify placenta location; delays care.  </p></br><p>B. Contraction stress test<br /></br>• Contraindicated in ANY unexplained antepartum bleeding—can provoke labor and worsen hemorrhage.  </p></br><p>C. Digital cervical examination<br /></br>• High-yield NBME trap. If placenta previa is possible, this maneuver can rupture placental vessels → massive bleeding. Always rule out previa first.  </p></br><p>D. Immediate cesarean delivery<br /></br>• Indicated if bleeding is hemodynamically significant or fetal tracing non-reassuring. Current scenario shows moderate bleed, stable vitals, reassuring FHR → diagnostic clarification first.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line:<br /></br>Painless third-trimester bleeding + prior uterine scar = suspect placenta previa. The safest, first next step is pelvic ultrasonography to localize the placenta before any vaginal manipulation or decision to deliver.</p>
146. A 3-year-old girl is admitted to the hospital because of a 7-day history of fever and irritability. Treatment with acetaminophen has not resolved her symptoms. During the past 2 days, she has had a decreased appetite but a normal fluid intake. She appears irritable. Her temperature is 38.7 C (101.7 F). Examination shows nontender, bilateral, cervical lymphadenopathy. The sclerae are injected without exudates. There is an erythematous papular rash over the perineum. Examination of the hands and feet shows erythema and tenderness. Her hemoglobin concentration is 10.2 g/dL, and platelet count is 285,000/mm³. Urinalysis shows 50 WBC/hpf and no bacteria. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Oral ibuprofen</br>b. Oral warfarin</br>c. Intramuscular penicillin</br>d. Intravenous immune globulin</br>e. Intravenous prednisone</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Intravenous immune globulin (IVIG)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Fever ≥ 5 days that is refractory to antipyretics (7-day history, temp 38.7 °C).  </li></br><li>Four of the five classic Kawasaki criteria are present:<br /></br>   • Bilateral non-exudative conjunctival injection<br /></br>   • Polymorphous rash (erythematous papular rash over the perineum)<br /></br>   • Changes in extremities (erythema/tenderness of hands and feet)<br /></br>   • Cervical lymphadenopathy (bilateral, nontender)<br /></br>   Oral mucosal changes are not described, but only 4/5 plus fever are required.  </li></br><li>Supportive “Kawasaki clues”: sterile pyuria (50 WBC/hpf, no bacteria) and anemia.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: For any child &lt;5 y with ≥ 5 days of fever, scan quickly for “CRASH and burn” (Conjunctivitis, Rash, Adenopathy, Strawberry tongue/mucosal changes, Hand/foot changes + fever).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• Diagnosis: Kawasaki disease (mucocutaneous lymph-node syndrome).<br /></br>• Core concern: coronary artery aneurysms due to a medium-vessel necrotizing vasculitis.<br /></br>• Evidence-based first-line therapy: single high-dose infusion of IVIG (2 g/kg) given within the first 10 days of illness, plus high-dose aspirin (80–100 mg/kg/day) until afebrile, then low-dose aspirin for 6–8 wk.<br /></br>• Documentation-worthy items: date/time of IVIG, dose, baseline echo, aspirin dosing, counseling on Reye risk, follow-up echo at 2 and 6 weeks.</p></br><p>Take-home: Early IVIG dramatically lowers the incidence of coronary aneurysms from ≈25% to &lt;5%.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Etiology: Auto-immune activation of vascular endothelium after an infectious trigger in genetically susceptible children (peak age 18–24 mo).<br /></br>Key manifestations: Fever + CRASH + lab anomalies (↑WBC, ↑ESR/CRP, thrombocytosis after day 7, sterile pyuria, anemia).<br /></br>Complications: Coronary aneurysm, MI, myocarditis, arrhythmia.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><ol></br><li>IVIG + aspirin = standard of care.  </li></br><li>Baseline and serial transthoracic echocardiogram (initial, 2 wk, 6–8 wk).  </li></br><li>Corticosteroids reserved only for IVIG-refractory cases or high-risk patients.  </li></br><li>Anticoagulation (warfarin, LMWH) only if giant coronary aneurysm develops.</li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“CRASH and burn”:<br /></br>C – Conjunctivitis (bilateral, non-purulent)<br /></br>R – Rash (polymorphous)<br /></br>A – Adenopathy (cervical, &gt;1.5 cm)<br /></br>S – Strawberry tongue / mucosal changes<br /></br>H – Hand/foot edema or erythema<br /></br>Burn – ≥5 days Fever  </p></br><p>Treatment mnemonic: “IV IG to save young heart” – IVIG (and aspirin) prevents infarction in kids.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Oral ibuprofen<br /></br>   • NSAIDs relieve fever/pain but do not reduce coronary aneurysm risk; aspirin at anti-inflammatory dose is specifically indicated, not ibuprofen.</p></br><p>B. Oral warfarin<br /></br>   • Anticoagulation is used only for giant coronary aneurysms (&gt;8 mm) or documented thrombosis, not as initial therapy.</p></br><p>C. Intramuscular penicillin<br /></br>   • Would target group A strep (scarlet fever). Scarlet fever presents with exudative pharyngitis, sandpaper rash, Pastia lines—none seen here; conjunctivitis and hand/foot changes argue against it.</p></br><p>E. Intravenous prednisone<br /></br>   • Steroids may help IVIG-resistant Kawasaki but are NOT first-line and, used alone, do not lower aneurysm risk.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: In Kawasaki disease, initiate IVIG within 10 days of fever onset (plus high-dose aspirin) to prevent coronary artery aneurysms.</p>
147. An 87-year-old woman with metastatic ovarian cancer has poorly controlled pain. Her cancer has been unresponsive to chemotherapy. Current medications include long-acting oral morphine, short-acting morphine as needed, and docusate. Her family is having difficulty caring for her at home. The patient's oldest daughter calls the physician to discuss her mother's care. She says, "We want my mother to receive hospice care at home, but no one wants her to die at home. Can she still have hospice services?" Which of the following is the most appropriate response?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. "No, but we can try to arrange for some other type of home service."</br>b. "No. It is important that hospice patients die at home."</br>c. "No. The patient cannot be enrolled in the hospice program if the patient or family prefer that death not take place at home."</br>d. "Yes, but we would have to transfer her to a nursing home to enroll her in the hospice program."</br>e. "Yes. Hospice can provide home-based care and attempt to transfer the patient to another site before death."</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. “Yes. Hospice can provide home-based care and attempt to transfer the patient to another site before death.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key phrases: “87-year-old woman with metastatic ovarian cancer … unresponsive to chemotherapy … poorly controlled pain … family difficulty caring for her at home … ‘We want my mother to receive hospice care at home, but no one wants her to die at home.’”<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: terminal illness with life-expectancy &lt; 6 mo, desire for comfort-focused care = meets Medicare hospice eligibility.<br /></br>• Pertinent negative: no continuing disease-directed therapy planned → fits hospice model.<br /></br>• Testing angle: common misconception that hospice = must die at home. Question probes knowledge of hospice flexibility and services.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When you see “hospice” questions, immediately ask 1) Does the patient have a ≤6-month prognosis? 2) Is the goal palliative, not curative? 3) Where can services be delivered? Remember: setting is flexible.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. Confirm eligibility (metastatic, refractory cancer; focus on palliation).<br /></br>2. Identify family concern (location of death).<br /></br>3. Recall hospice structure: interdisciplinary team can serve patients in multiple settings—home, nursing facility, dedicated inpatient hospice unit, or hospital “general inpatient” level of care for uncontrolled symptoms.<br /></br>4. Therefore, offer home hospice now and arrange transfer later if the family still prefers.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Enrollment in hospice does NOT obligate death in the home; it provides flexible, goal-directed care across settings.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls: prognosis discussion, patient/family goals, informed consent for hospice, plan for potential inpatient transfer.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (concise)<br /></br>• End-stage ovarian cancer → progressive peritoneal carcinomatosis, bowel obstruction, cachexia, pain.<br /></br>• Focus shifts from disease modification to symptom control (opioids, antiemetics, laxatives, psychosocial support).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Test Review<br /></br>Hospice services include:<br /></br>• Interdisciplinary team (physician, nurse, social worker, chaplain, volunteers).<br /></br>• Home visits for medication titration (e.g., long-acting and breakthrough morphine), nursing care, durable medical equipment.<br /></br>• Continuous care or inpatient hospice when symptoms (pain crisis, dyspnea, delirium) require 24-h management.<br /></br>• Respite care to relieve caregiver burden.<br /></br>No additional diagnostic tests are required for enrollment; prognosis certification by two physicians is needed.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“H-O-M-E-S” for hospice flexibility:<br /></br>H – Hospice team<br /></br>O – Options for setting (home, nursing home, inpatient, hospital)<br /></br>M – Medical equipment &amp; meds covered<br /></br>E – Emphasis on comfort, not extension of life<br /></br>S – Six-month prognosis (usual-course)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. “No, but we can try other home service.”<br /></br>• Falsely excludes hospice; home hospice is precisely the appropriate program.</p></br><p>B. “No. It is important that hospice patients die at home.”<br /></br>• Myth. Hospice philosophy does not mandate place of death; goal is comfort anywhere.</p></br><p>C. “No. The patient cannot be enrolled … if … prefer that death not take place at home.”<br /></br>• Incorrect—location preference does not affect eligibility.</p></br><p>D. “Yes, but we would have to transfer her to a nursing home to enroll …”<br /></br>• Hospice enrollment does NOT require nursing-home placement; can start at home and move later if desired.</p></br><p>E (Correct). Accurately states hospice can begin in the home and facilitate transfer before death if the family wishes.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Hospice is a flexible, patient-centered benefit that provides palliative care in multiple settings. Patients may enroll at home and later transition to inpatient hospice, hospital, or nursing facility; dying at home is not obligatory.</p>
148. Two days before undergoing removal of four molar teeth, a 27-year-old man comes to the physician for therapy to prevent excessive bleeding. He has a lifelong history of mild mucosal bleeding. His sister has a history of similar bleeding. Examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:</br>Platelet count                225,000/mm3</br>Bleeding time                 11 min</br>Prothrombin time              11 sec (INR=1)</br>Partial thromboplastin time   47 sec</br>Factor VIII                   15 percent of normal (reference range: 50 percent–150 percent of normal)</br>Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these findings?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome</br>b. Hemophilia A</br>c. Inhibitor to factor VIII</br>d. Lupus anticoagulant</br>e. von Willebrand disease</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. von Willebrand disease (vWD)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• “Lifelong history of mild mucosal bleeding” – vWD classically causes easy epistaxis, gingival bleeding, menorrhagia, and prolonged bleeding from minor cuts; hemophilia usually causes deep tissue or joint bleeds.<br /></br>• “Sister has a history of similar bleeding” – vWD is most often autosomal-dominant and therefore affects males and females; hemophilia A is X-linked recessive and would rarely manifest in a female sibling.<br /></br>• Normal platelet count but slightly prolonged bleeding time (11 min) – platelet number is normal; defective platelet adhesion due to ↓vWF prolongs bleeding time.<br /></br>• Prolonged aPTT (47 sec) with normal PT – vWF stabilizes factor VIII; ↓vWF → secondary ↓factor VIII → intrinsic‐pathway prolongation (aPTT).<br /></br>• Factor VIII level 15 % of normal – fits with vWF deficiency (vWF protects VIII from degradation).  </p></br><p>Test-taking strategy:<br /></br>1. Separate “platelet-type” bleeding (mucosal, superficial) from “coagulation-factor” bleeding (deep tissue).<br /></br>2. Note inheritance clues.<br /></br>3. Match lab pattern: normal PT, prolonged aPTT + prolonged bleeding time points almost uniquely to vWD.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1: Identify bleeding phenotype → superficial/mucosal = platelet/vWF problem.<br /></br>Step 2: Look at labs → prolonged aPTT implies intrinsic factor deficiency; prolonged bleeding time implies platelet function issue. Only vWF links the two (platelet adhesion + factor VIII carrier).<br /></br>Core takeaway: vWD presents with a combined platelet-function defect and intrinsic-pathway prolongation because vWF serves both roles.</p></br><p>Documentation points (if real patient): history of mucosal bleeding, family history, planned invasive procedure, labs showing ↑BT and ↑aPTT, plan for desmopressin (DDAVP) prophylaxis.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• vWF is produced by endothelial cells &amp; megakaryocytes.<br /></br>• Functions: (1) binds exposed collagen and GP1b on platelets to form initial plug, (2) carries &amp; stabilizes factor VIII in plasma.<br /></br>• Quantitative (type 1) or qualitative (type 2) deficiencies → ↓platelet adhesion + accelerated degradation of factor VIII → mild mucosal bleeding + prolonged aPTT.<br /></br>• Inheritance usually autosomal dominant → affects both sexes, family clusters.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>• First-line peri-procedural prophylaxis: Desmopressin (DDAVP) – releases vWF and factor VIII from endothelial stores.<br /></br>• Severe cases or type 3: vWF-containing factor concentrates.<br /></br>• Lab confirmation: ↓vWF antigen or activity (ristocetin cofactor assay), ± ↓factor VIII.<br /></br>• Mixing study: corrects aPTT (helps rule out inhibitors).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“vWF = vWF (von Willebrand) is For both (Factor VIII + platelets).”<br /></br>or<br /></br>“vWF = ‘v’ for vessels (endothelium), ‘W’ for Women (AD, both sexes), ‘F’ for Factor VIII.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome – Prolonged aPTT but causes thrombosis, miscarriages; bleeding time normal; no ↓factor VIII.<br /></br>b. Hemophilia A – X-linked; usually severe deep bleeding in males; normal bleeding time; female sibling rarely affected; isolated ↓factor VIII without platelet defect.<br /></br>c. Inhibitor to factor VIII – Acquired; no family history; mixing study would NOT correct aPTT; bleeding time normal.<br /></br>d. Lupus anticoagulant – A subtype of antiphospholipid syndrome; prolonged aPTT but hypercoagulable, not bleeding; normal bleeding time and factor VIII.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Take-Home<br /></br>Combined finding of prolonged bleeding time + prolonged aPTT with mucosal bleeding in both sexes = think von Willebrand disease; treat with DDAVP.</p>
149. A 23-year-old woman comes to the physician for a follow-up examination. Six months ago, she underwent splenectomy because of injuries sustained in a motor vehicle collision. Her postoperative course has been uncomplicated. She has a headache once monthly and takes acetaminophen as needed. Immunizations are up-to-date. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no abnormalities except for a well-healed scar. She asks if any precautions are required before her dental appointment in 1 week. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Postponement of dental work for 6 months</br>b. Measurement of serum complement concentrations</br>c. Influenza virus vaccine</br>d. Penicillin therapy prior to teeth cleaning</br>e. No precautions are necessary</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. No precautions are necessary  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – “23-year-old woman” with “splenectomy 6 months ago” after trauma.<br /></br>  – “Postoperative course uncomplicated,” “immunizations are up-to-date,” and she is otherwise healthy.<br /></br>  – Patient is specifically asking about a “dental appointment” (routine cleaning).<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives<br /></br>  – No prosthetic heart valve, prior infective endocarditis, congenital heart disease, or immunocompromising condition other than asplenia.<br /></br>  – No mention of active infection or fever.  </p></br><p>Approach: When you see an asplenic patient, think of (1) vaccines, (2) daily or standby antibiotics for children or fever &gt;38.5 °C, and (3) wearing a medic-alert tag. Routine procedures (e.g., dental cleaning) do NOT require antibiotic prophylaxis unless endocarditis criteria are met.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Every dental-prophylaxis NBME question boils down to “Does the patient meet the AHA endocarditis-prophylaxis list?”—if not, the answer is “No antibiotics.” Asplenia is NOT on that list.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Asplenia increases risk of overwhelming postsplenectomy infection (OPSI) from encapsulated bacteria (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae type b, Neisseria meningitidis).<br /></br>2. Protection is achieved with:<br /></br>   • Vaccines (pneumococcal PCV13 → PPSV23, Hib, MenACWY, MenB, plus routine influenza).<br /></br>   • Empiric antibiotics only when febrile or, in young children, daily penicillin until age 5.<br /></br>3. Routine dental work does NOT seed the bloodstream with these organisms in quantities that justify prophylaxis, nor does it predispose to OPSI.<br /></br>4. Endocarditis prophylaxis (amoxicillin 2 g 1 h before procedure) is reserved for specific cardiac conditions, none of which are present.<br /></br>Therefore, “No precautions are necessary” is correct.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Asplenia ≠ automatic antibiotic prophylaxis for dental procedures.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• The spleen removes opsonized encapsulated bacteria via splenic macrophages and produces IgM.<br /></br>• Absence → impaired opsonization and complement activation → risk of fulminant sepsis by SHiN organisms.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment (big-picture)<br /></br>1. Vaccination schedule (all given ≥2 wk before or ≥2 wk after splenectomy):<br /></br>   – PCV13 (then PPSV23 ≥8 wk later, booster at 5 yr)<br /></br>   – Hib (one dose if not already immune)<br /></br>   – MenACWY + MenB (per CDC timetable)<br /></br>   – Yearly inactivated influenza vaccine  </p></br><ol></br><li>Antibiotics<br /></br>   – Children &lt;5 yr: daily oral penicillin VK 125–250 mg BID.<br /></br>   – Adults: NO routine prophylaxis; give empiric amoxicillin-clavulanate or fluoroquinolone if febrile and can’t get to care fast.  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“NO SPELeN prophylaxis for Dental”:<br /></br>N – No<br /></br>O – OPSI organisms (SHiN) → focus on vaccines, not dental<br /></br>S – Spleen<br /></br>P – Prophylaxis only for<br /></br>E – Endocarditis indications<br /></br>L – Little kids daily penicillin<br /></br>eN – Not needed for routine cleaning  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Postponement of dental work for 6 months<br /></br>   – No physiologic basis; wound has healed, immune risk is chronic, not time-limited.  </p></br><p>B. Measurement of serum complement concentrations<br /></br>   – Low complement predisposes to Neisseria infections, but complement levels are not altered by splenectomy and would not change management.  </p></br><p>C. Influenza virus vaccine<br /></br>   – Good idea annually for everyone, but not specifically required “before dental work.” The vignette states “immunizations are up-to-date,” so it’s not the immediate recommendation.  </p></br><p>D. Penicillin therapy prior to teeth cleaning<br /></br>   – Would be indicated for infective endocarditis prophylaxis (prosthetic valve, previous IE, certain congenital lesions, transplant patients with valvulopathy). Asplenia is NOT an indication, and the target organisms differ (viridans streptococci vs SHiN).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>An asplenic adult with current vaccines and no high-risk cardiac condition needs no special antibiotics or delays for routine dental procedures.</p>
150. A 67-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 2-month history of progressive shortness of breath on exertion, muscle pain, fatigue, generalized weakness, and constipation. He also has had a decreased appetite, resulting in a 7-kg (15-lb) weight loss during this period. His current medications are hydrochlorothiazide for hypertension and laxatives. He smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 40 years but quit 5 years ago. His temperature is 37.5  C (99.5  F), pulse is 85/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 164/92 mm Hg. There is no jugular venous distention. Breath sounds are decreased, and scattered rhonchi are heard over the right upper lung field. Cardiac examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin 14.2 g/dL  </br>Leukocyte count 11,500/mm^3 with a normal differential  </br>Serum  </br>Na+ 140 mEq/L  </br>K+ 3.5 mEq/L  </br>Cl– 100 mEq/L  </br>HCO3– 25 mEq/L  </br>Ca2+ 13.3 mg/dL  </br>Urea nitrogen 22 mg/dL  </br>Glucose 72 mg/dL  </br>Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL  </br>Alkaline phosphatase 100 U/L  </br>A chest x-ray shows a mass in the upper lobe of the right lung that extends into the hilum. Which of the following is the most likely cause of these laboratory findings?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Adverse effect of hydrochlorothiazide  </br>b. Bone metastases from lung cancer  </br>c. Paraneoplastic syndrome  </br>d. Parathyroid carcinoma  </br>e. Primary hyperparathyroidism</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Paraneoplastic syndrome  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues: long-time smoker; hilar/upper-lobe lung mass; hypercalcemia (Ca²⁺ 13.3 mg/dL) with normal alkaline phosphatase; systemic “stones, bones, groans” symptoms (constipation, muscle pain, weakness, fatigue, anorexia, weight loss).<br /></br>• Pattern recognition: Central lung mass + significant hypercalcemia in a smoker → think squamous cell carcinoma of the lung secreting PTH-related peptide (PTHrP).<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: No bone pain or pathologic fractures; alkaline phosphatase not elevated (argues against extensive osteolytic bone metastases); taking HCTZ for years without prior hypercalcemia; no neck mass or history suggesting parathyroid disease.  </p></br><p>Approach for future questions:<br /></br>1. Identify the organ system driving the abnormal lab.<br /></br>2. Ask, “Is this primary disease of that organ or ectopic hormone production?”<br /></br>3. In smokers with a lung mass, always consider paraneoplastic syndromes (hypercalcemia, SIADH, ACTH, Lambert-Eaton, etc.).  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Lung mass in long-term smoker → malignancy likely (especially squamous cell if central).<br /></br>2. Squamous cell carcinoma is classically associated with PTHrP secretion.<br /></br>3. PTHrP mimics PTH, raising serum calcium via bone resorption and renal Ca²⁺ reabsorption but without raising alkaline phosphatase as much as true bone metastases.<br /></br>4. Therefore, hypercalcemia here is most consistent with a paraneoplastic syndrome rather than medication effect, bone mets, or primary parathyroid pathology.  </p></br><p>Key takeaway: Hypercalcemia + central lung mass in a smoker → think PTHrP-mediated paraneoplastic hypercalcemia of malignancy.  </p></br><p>Documentation in the chart: “Patient with suspected squamous cell carcinoma of the right upper lobe complicated by PTHrP-mediated hypercalcemia; plan—IV fluids, calcitonin, bisphosphonate, oncology referral for staging and treatment of primary tumor.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Squamous cell carcinoma → ectopic production of PTHrP → binds PTH1 receptor →<br /></br>• ↑ osteoclastic bone resorption<br /></br>• ↑ distal renal tubular Ca²⁺ reabsorption<br /></br>• ↓ phosphate reabsorption<br /></br>Endogenous PTH is suppressed (distinguishes from primary hyperparathyroidism).  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Pearls<br /></br>Diagnostics:<br /></br>• Serum PTH: low/undetectable.<br /></br>• PTHrP: elevated.<br /></br>• Phosphate: low-normal.<br /></br>• Bone scan often negative for metastases.  </p></br><p>Acute treatment of severe hypercalcemia:<br /></br>1. Vigorous isotonic saline hydration.<br /></br>2. Calcitonin (rapid, short-acting).<br /></br>3. IV bisphosphonate (eg, zoledronic acid or pamidronate) for sustained control.<br /></br>4. Treat underlying malignancy (definitive).  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Squamous cell makes PTHrP and causes Stones.”<br /></br>• S = Squamous<br /></br>• P = PTHrP<br /></br>• S = Stones (hypercalcemia manifestations)  </p></br><p>Alternatively: “CENTRAL Ca²⁺” – Central lung cancer (squamous) → Calcium elevation.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Options Explained<br /></br>a. Adverse effect of hydrochlorothiazide<br /></br>• Thiazides cause mild hypercalcemia (usually &lt;11.5 mg/dL) by ↓ urinary calcium excretion.<br /></br>• The drug effect alone rarely produces symptomatic or marked elevations like 13.3 mg/dL, and patient likely has taken HCTZ chronically without prior issues.  </p></br><p>b. Bone metastases from lung cancer<br /></br>• Osteolytic mets release calcium but usually elevate alkaline phosphatase and produce bone pain/pathologic fractures. Normal ALP and no skeletal complaints point away.  </p></br><p>d. Parathyroid carcinoma<br /></br>• Would raise both calcium and PTH; no lung mass expected. Neck mass often present. Very rare compared with paraneoplastic hypercalcemia in lung cancer.  </p></br><p>e. Primary hyperparathyroidism<br /></br>• Most common outpatient cause of hypercalcemia, but would show elevated PTH; again, does not explain lung mass.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: In a smoker with a central lung mass and significant hypercalcemia, think PTHrP-mediated paraneoplastic syndrome from squamous cell carcinoma.</p>
151. An 87-year-old man comes to the physician because of problems with his memory over the past 6 months. During this period, he has occasionally misplaced his house keys and forgotten to buy certain grocery items unless he has a list. He still enjoys following the stock market daily and visiting his grandchildren monthly. He has a history of benign prostatic hyperplasia and cataracts. His wife has breast cancer, and he is worried that she will suffer. His temperature is 37 C (98.6 F), pulse is 70/min, and blood pressure is 155/90 mm Hg. Physical examination shows an enlarged prostate; no masses are palpated. Neurologic examination shows no focal findings; his speech is fluent, and there is no aphasia. On mental status examination, he is alert and oriented to person, place, and time. His eye contact is good. Range of affect is full. He recalls two of three objects after 5 minutes. Long-term memory is intact. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these findings?	Correct answer: G</br></br>a. Capgras syndrome</br>b. Dementia, Alzheimer type</br>c. Lewy body dementia</br>d. Major depressive disorder</br>e. Metastatic prostate cancer</br>f. Multi-infarct (vascular) dementia</br>g. Normal aging</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>g. Normal aging  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Strategy<br /></br>1. Time course: Only 6 months of mild forgetfulness.<br /></br>2. Nature of deficits: • Occasionally misplaces keys • Needs a shopping list.<br /></br>3. Preserved function: Manages finances (follows the stock market), drives/visits grandchildren, independent ADLs/IADLs.<br /></br>4. Mental status exam: Alert, fully oriented, fluent speech, intact long-term memory, recalls 2/3 objects (slight drop that can be normal in the 80s). No aphasia or focal neuro findings.<br /></br>5. Affect/mood: Normal (“range of affect is full”), no signs of depression or psychosis.  </p></br><p>High-yield pattern: A very mild decline in short-term memory without impairment in daily functioning = age-associated memory change (sometimes called “benign senescent forgetfulness”).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive pearl: On NBME/Step questions, the single biggest discriminator between normal aging vs. dementia is loss of FUNCTION. Always ask yourself, “Can the patient still handle money, medications, cooking, and transportation?” If yes → not dementia.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Normal cognitive aging presents with:<br /></br>• Slower retrieval and processing speed<br /></br>• Occasional word-finding difficulty<br /></br>• Mild decline in new learning, but recognition cues help (lists, calendars)<br /></br>• No impairment of social or occupational function<br /></br>Therefore the mild 2/3 recall, preserved orientation, and independent living point squarely to normal aging rather than a pathologic process.</p></br><p>Take-home concept: Dementia = memory impairment + ≥1 other cognitive domain + FUNCTIONAL IMPAIRMENT. Absent functional loss → normal aging or (if clearly progressive) mild cognitive impairment (MCI).</p></br><p>Documentation (real encounter): “Mild age-appropriate forgetfulness, no evidence of dementia. Educated on cognitive health, will monitor.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Normal aging: Gradual loss of synaptic density, slightly reduced hippocampal volume, slower frontal-executive processing. Compensatory mechanisms preserve day-to-day function. Progression is extremely slow, non-disabling, and non-progressive to dementia in most older adults.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Management<br /></br>No imaging or labs are required when:<br /></br>• No red-flag neurologic signs<br /></br>• Cognition essentially intact<br /></br>• No suspicion of reversible causes (B12 deficiency, thyroid disease, etc.)<br /></br>Management: Reassure, encourage cognitive/physical activity, control vascular risk factors, schedule follow-up to monitor.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Three Fs for Normal Aging”<br /></br>1. Forget a name/keys,<br /></br>2. Find it later,<br /></br>3. Function fine.</p></br><p>Or use “REAL”: Retains skills, Episodic slips, Activities intact, Lives independently.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Capgras syndrome – Delusional belief familiar people are impostors; not present here.  </p></br><p>b. Dementia, Alzheimer type – Would show progressive memory loss interfering with IADLs (e.g., paying bills, cooking) plus word-finding difficulty, apraxia, or visuospatial deficits. Not seen.  </p></br><p>c. Lewy body dementia – Triad: fluctuating cognition, vivid visual hallucinations, parkinsonism ± REM sleep behavior disorder. None present.  </p></br><p>d. Major depressive disorder (“pseudodementia”) – Would expect depressed mood, anhedonia, sleep/appetite changes (SIGECAPS). Affect here is full, eye contact good, still enjoys activities.  </p></br><p>e. Metastatic prostate cancer – Could cause bone pain, anemia, or cord compression, not isolated mild forgetfulness.  </p></br><p>f. Multi-infarct (vascular) dementia – Stepwise decline, focal neuro deficits (hemiparesis, gait disturbance), executive dysfunction. Neurologic exam is completely normal.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Key Takeaway<br /></br>Mild memory lapses without functional decline in an elderly patient = normal aging. Dementia requires memory impairment + loss of independent function.</p>
152. A 2-month-old boy is admitted to the hospital because of a 1-day history of coughing, rapid breathing, and poor feeding. He had a temperature of 39 C (102.2 F) 4 hours ago. He has not had a runny nose. One month ago, he was admitted to the hospital for 10 days for treatment of orbital cellulitis. He was born at term after an uncomplicated pregnancy and spontaneous vaginal delivery. Results of maternal prenatal screening, including HIV serologic studies, were negative. On admission, the patient is alert and has grunting respirations and mild intercostal retractions. His temperature is 37.9 C (100.2 F), pulse is 170/min, respirations are 86/min, and blood pressure is 74/48 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on 40 percent oxygen via nasal cannula shows an oxygen saturation of 92 percent. Physical examination shows a supple neck with no adenopathy. Breath sounds are decreased bilaterally. Diffuse crackles are heard in both lungs. Cardiac examination shows a regular rhythm. S1 and S2 are normal. A grade 1/6, systolic ejection murmur is heard at the upper left sternal border. The abdomen is soft and nontender. The liver edge is firm and palpated 4 cm below the right costal margin, and the spleen tip is palpated 2 cm below the left costal margin. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL</br>Hematocrit 29 percent</br>Leukocyte count 9000/mm^3</br>Segmented neutrophils 70 percent</br>Bands 4 percent</br>Eosinophils 3 percent</br>Lymphocytes 20 percent</br>Monocytes 3 percent</br>Platelet count 220,000/mm^3</br>CD4+ T-lymphocyte count 150/mm^3 (Normal≥1750)</br>Serum</br>IgA <10 mg/dL</br>IgG 10 mg/dL</br>IgM 10 mg/dL</br></br>A chest x-ray shows diffusely hazy lung fields, no cardiomegaly, and a normal mediastinal silhouette. Which of the following is the most likely underlying diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Chédiak-Higashi syndrome</br>b. Chronic granulomatous disease</br>c. HIV infection</br>d. Severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID)</br>e. Thymic-parathyroid dysplasia</br>f. X-linked agammaglobulinemia</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID)</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• Age 2 months + recurrent/severe infections (orbital cellulitis at 1 mo, now diffuse interstitial pneumonia) → points to congenital immunodeficiency.<br /></br>• Labs: CD4⁺ T-cell count 150/mm³ (normal ≥ 1750) → profound T-cell lymphopenia.<br /></br>• Serum IgA, IgG, IgM all extremely low (&lt; 10 mg/dL each) → global failure of B-cell antibody production.<br /></br>• Chest X-ray: “normal mediastinal silhouette” (thymic shadow still present) rules out complete thymic aplasia (DiGeorge).<br /></br>• Early presentation, failure to thrive (poor feeding), hepatosplenomegaly = immune dysregulation from uncontrolled infections.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. In any infant &lt; 3 mo with severe infections ask, “Are both cellular and humoral arms low?”<br /></br>2. Combine the data: low T cells (CD4) + pan-hypogammaglobulinemia = combined defect → think SCID.<br /></br>3. Rapidly eliminate single-arm defects (pure B-cell, phagocyte, complement, etc.).</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-by-step:<br /></br>1. Severe infection very early in life → primary immunodeficiency.<br /></br>2. Quantify each immune compartment:<br /></br>   – T cells: CD4 only 150/mm³ (profoundly low).<br /></br>   – B-cell function: all immunoglobulins essentially absent.<br /></br>3. Combined deficiency narrows to SCID vs DiGeorge.<br /></br>4. DiGeorge usually has: absent thymic shadow, hypocalcemic tetany, conotruncal heart lesions. None are present; thymic silhouette is normal, Ca²⁺ status not flagged, no heart murmur beyond benign 1/6.<br /></br>5. Therefore SCID is the only choice that matches both the laboratory and radiographic findings.</p></br><p>Core takeaway:  “Profoundly decreased T cells + absent immunoglobulins in an infant = SCID until proven otherwise.”</p></br><p>Documentation point (real patient): note lymphopenia, hypogammaglobulinemia, need for immediate broad-spectrum antimicrobials, airborne isolation, and HLA-typing for hematopoietic stem-cell transplant (curative therapy).</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>SCID = heterogeneous group of genetic defects causing failure of both T-cell development and subsequent B-cell activation.<br /></br>• Common etiologies:<br /></br>  – X-linked IL-2R γ-chain mutation (most common)<br /></br>  – ADA deficiency, JAK3, RAG1/2, etc.<br /></br>• Result: absent/very low T cells → no help for B cells → profoundly low IgA, IgG, IgM.<br /></br>• Clinical manifestations: recurrent bacterial, viral, fungal, protozoal infections starting in first months; chronic diarrhea, FTT; opportunists (Pneumocystis jirovecii).  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment Review  </p></br><p>Diagnostics:<br /></br>• Absolute lymphocyte count (&lt; 3000/mm³ in infants), specific T-cell subsets, quantitative immunoglobulins, newborn T-cell receptor excision circles (TREC) screen.<br /></br>• CXR may show absent/equivocal thymic shadow but can be normal early on.</p></br><p>Management (must know for exams):<br /></br>1. Urgent broad-spectrum antibiotics + TMP-SMX prophylaxis (Pneumocystis).<br /></br>2. IVIG replacement until transplant.<br /></br>3. Live vaccines CONTRAINDICATED (e.g., rotavirus, MMR, varicella).<br /></br>4. Definitive: allogeneic hematopoietic stem-cell transplant ASAP; gene therapy for ADA deficiency.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“SCID = Six-Combined Immune Defects” mnemonic:<br /></br>S = Severe infections early<br /></br>C = Combined T + B failure (↓CD3, ↓Ig)<br /></br>I = Infections of every type (bacterial, viral, fungal, protozoal)<br /></br>D = Donor marrow cure (stem-cell transplant)</p></br><p>Visual: Picture a baby in a “bubble” (David Vetter) holding a locked box labeled “T-cells” and an empty Ig bottle.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Chédiak-Higashi syndrome<br /></br>• Giant lysosomal granules, partial albinism, neuropathy, Staph &amp; Strep infections.<br /></br>• Normal Ig levels; normal/only mildly reduced T cells. Not pan-hypogammaglobulinemia.</p></br><p>B. Chronic granulomatous disease (CGD)<br /></br>• Defective NADPH oxidase → impaired neutrophil oxidative burst, recurrent abscesses with catalase-positive organisms.<br /></br>• T &amp; B cell numbers and Ig levels normal. Presents later (infancy/childhood), not with profound lymphopenia.</p></br><p>C. HIV infection (perinatal)<br /></br>• Can present early, but maternal HIV screen was negative; Ig values often elevated (polyclonal hypergammaglobulinemia), not absent. CD4 low but Ig high — opposite of vignette.</p></br><p>E. Thymic-parathyroid dysplasia (DiGeorge)<br /></br>• Absent thymic shadow, hypocalcemia, conotruncal heart defects (e.g., truncus arteriosus).<br /></br>• Here, thymic silhouette normal, Ca²⁺ and cardiac exam unremarkable.</p></br><p>F. X-linked agammaglobulinemia (Bruton)<br /></br>• Absent B cells → markedly low Ig, BUT normal T-cell counts (CD3, CD4).<br /></br>• In vignette CD4 count is profoundly low, so not isolated B-cell defect.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line:<br /></br>An infant with both profound T-cell lymphopenia and near-zero immunoglobulins has severe combined immunodeficiency. Immediate protective isolation, prophylaxis, IVIG, and stem-cell transplant are life-saving.</p>
153. A 55-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 6-week history of easy bruising. Ten years ago, she underwent local resection and chemotherapy for cancer of the right breast. She takes no medications. Examination shows multiple cutaneous ecchymoses over the body, primarily over all the extremities. The spleen is not palpable. Her hemoglobin concentration is 12 g/dL, hematocrit is 34 percent, and platelet count is 34,000/mm^3. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Acute myelogenous leukemia</br>b. Aplastic anemia</br>c. Immune thrombocytopenic purpura</br>d. Metastatic breast cancer</br>e. Secondary hypersplenism</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP)</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key positives<br /></br>  – 6-week history of easy bruising → bleeding tendency<br /></br>  – Platelet count 34 000/mm³ (severe thrombocytopenia)<br /></br>  – Hemoglobin 12 g/dL, hematocrit 34 % → essentially normal red-cell mass<br /></br>  – No splenomegaly, no drugs, chemotherapy was 10 yrs ago<br /></br>  – No information suggesting leukocyte abnormalities  </p></br><p>• Key negatives<br /></br>  – No fever, weight loss, bone pain (makes leukemia or marrow infiltration less likely)<br /></br>  – No hepatosplenomegaly or portal hypertension (argues against hypersplenism)<br /></br>  – No concurrent anemia or leukopenia (rules out aplastic anemia, marrow metastasis)  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition: Isolated thrombocytopenia + otherwise normal CBC + absence of splenomegaly = classic board pattern for ITP.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Anytime you see a single low cell line on the CBC, think “peripheral destruction or sequestration,” not “marrow failure,” which gives pancytopenia.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)<br /></br>1. Confirm thrombocytopenia is isolated. ✔<br /></br>2. Ask: Is there splenomegaly? No → eliminates hypersplenism.<br /></br>3. Ask: Is marrow likely suppressed/infiltrated? No anemia or leukopenia → unlikely.<br /></br>4. Remaining high-yield cause of isolated platelet destruction = ITP → choose C.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: ITP presents with isolated thrombocytopenia and mucocutaneous bleeding signs; other cell lines and spleen size are normal.</p></br><p>Documentation Pearl: Note extent of bruising, platelet count, absence of hepatosplenomegaly, and plan for further work-up (peripheral smear, antiplatelet antibodies) or empiric steroids.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Autoimmune (IgG) antibodies form against platelet membrane glycoproteins (IIb/IIIa or Ib-IX).<br /></br>• Opsonized platelets are removed by splenic macrophages → isolated thrombocytopenia.<br /></br>• May be primary or secondary (e.g., HIV, HCV, SLE).  </p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Management Snapshot<br /></br>1. Adult with platelets &lt;30 000 or bleeding: high-dose corticosteroids.<br /></br>2. If refractory/relapse: IVIG, anti-D immunoglobulin, rituximab.<br /></br>3. Long-term cure: splenectomy (removes destruction site &amp; antibody source).<br /></br>4. Thrombopoietin receptor agonists (eltrombopag, romiplostim) if chronic.  </p></br><p>Diagnostic adjuncts: Peripheral smear (to exclude pseudothrombocytopenia or MAHA), HIV/HCV serology, antiplatelet antibody testing (not required for diagnosis).</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Breakdown<br /></br>a. Acute myelogenous leukemia – Would show anemia, thrombocytopenia, and abnormal WBCs/blasts; often presents with fatigue, infections, splenomegaly. Isolated thrombocytopenia without leukocyte abnormalities is atypical.</p></br><p>b. Aplastic anemia – Pancytopenia due to marrow failure; RBCs and neutrophils would also be low; physical exam often unremarkable but not an isolated platelet drop.</p></br><p>d. Metastatic breast cancer – Marrow infiltration causes leuko-erythroblastosis and pancytopenia; 10-year cancer-free interval and normal other cell lines make this unlikely.</p></br><p>e. Secondary hypersplenism – Splenomegaly mandatory; platelets (and often WBCs/RBCs) low due to sequestration; absent spleen enlargement here.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“ITP = Isolated Thrombocytopenia Problem.” If only the platelet line is down and the spleen is normal, think ITP.<br /></br>Mnemonic for ITP treatment hierarchy: “SIReS”<br /></br>S – Steroids<br /></br>I – IVIG/anti-D<br /></br>R – Rituximab<br /></br>e<br /></br>S – Splenectomy  </p></br><p>Visual cue: Picture platelets wearing “IgG bullseyes,” being eaten by a cartoon spleen—helps remember immune-mediated destruction.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>An otherwise healthy adult with new bruising and isolated severe thrombocytopenia, normal spleen size, and intact other blood counts = Immune thrombocytopenic purpura.</p>
154. A previously healthy 27-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 2-day history of fever, sore throat, and chills. Eight days ago, she received the diagnosis of Escherichia coli urinary tract infection and began a course of trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. During the past 6 years, she has had a total of four urinary tract infections. Her last menstrual period was 14 days ago. She is sexually active with one male partner and uses an oral contraceptive. She exercises regularly. Her temperature is 38.7 C (101.6 F), pulse is 96/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 122/78 mm Hg. The sinuses are nontender. There is slight erythema of the oropharynx but no exudates. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:</br>Leukocyte count 2200/mm3</br>Segmented neutrophils 4 percent</br>Lymphocytes 96 percent</br>Serum</br>Na+ 140 mEq/L</br>K+ 4.6 mEq/L</br>Cl– 105 mEq/L</br>HCO3– 24 mEq/L</br>Urea nitrogen 18 mg/dL</br>Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL</br>AST 34 U/L</br>ALT 37 U/L</br>β-hCG negative</br>Urine</br>RBC 0/hpf</br>WBC 3–4/hpf</br>An x-ray of the chest shows no abnormalities. Results of blood and urine cultures are pending. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's neutropenia?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Cytomegalovirus infection</br>b. Drug-induced agranulocytosis</br>c. Epstein-Barr virus infection</br>d. Parvovirus infection</br>e. Sepsis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Drug-induced agranulocytosis  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – “8 days ago … trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole” (sulfa antibiotic).<br /></br>  – Acute onset of high fever, sore throat → classic first manifestations of agranulocytosis (inability to fight normal oropharyngeal flora).<br /></br>  – WBC 2 200/µL with only 4 % neutrophils → ANC = 2 200 × 0.04 ≈ 90/µL (≤ 500 = severe, ≤ 100 = life-threatening).<br /></br>  – Otherwise healthy young woman; no organ system signs of overwhelming infection.  </p></br><p>Approach for similar questions<br /></br>1. As soon as you see fever + mucosal symptoms + profound neutropenia, calculate ANC.<br /></br>2. Scan the vignette for a new medication started within the last few weeks; recall “C-S-T MaKe Bone marrow fail” (Clozapine, Sulfonamides/Sulfasalazine, Thionamides, Methimazole, Carbamazepine, etc.).<br /></br>3. Rule out viral etiologies → usually lymphocytosis with atypical cells, not isolated neutrophil drop.<br /></br>4. Rule out sepsis → would expect hemodynamic instability ± neutrophilia/bandemia initially.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• TMP-SMX is a sulfonamide. Sulfonamides are on the short USMLE list of drugs that can cause idiosyncratic agranulocytosis.<br /></br>• Time course: 1–3 weeks after exposure.<br /></br>• Physical findings: fever, sore throat, sometimes oral ulcers; exam otherwise often benign.<br /></br>• Labs: Markedly low neutrophil count with relative lymphocytosis because lymphocytes are spared.  </p></br><p>Take-home message:<br /></br>When a patient on a sulfa, antithyroid drug, clozapine, or certain anticonvulsants develops sudden fever/sore throat and ANC &lt; 500 → think drug-induced agranulocytosis, stop the agent, give broad-spectrum antibiotics ± G-CSF.  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real patient)<br /></br>• Medication list with start dates.<br /></br>• ANC calculation.<br /></br>• Immediate discontinuation of offending drug.<br /></br>• Empiric IV antibiotics ordered.<br /></br>• Consider hematology consult/G-CSF.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Two mechanisms:<br /></br>1. Immune-mediated peripheral destruction of mature neutrophils.<br /></br>2. Direct toxic effect on myeloid precursors in the bone marrow.<br /></br>Reaction is idiosyncratic, not dose-related, and recurs on re-exposure.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Management Snapshot<br /></br>1. STOP TMP-SMX immediately.<br /></br>2. Febrile neutropenia protocol: empiric IV anti-pseudomonal agent (e.g., cefepime, piperacillin-tazobactam) pending cultures.<br /></br>3. Filgrastim (G-CSF) if ANC &lt; 100 or high-risk features.<br /></br>4. Monitor counts daily; most recover in 7–10 days once drug is discontinued.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>Mnemonic “Cant Stop The Madness, Bone cells Collapse”:<br /></br>Clozapine, Sulfonamides (incl. TMP-SMX), Thionamides (PTU, methimazole), Methotrexate, Carbamazepine → Agranulocytosis.<br /></br>Visualize neutrophils falling off a cliff labeled with these drug names.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Cytomegalovirus infection – Causes mononucleosis-like syndrome with atypical lymphocytosis, not isolated agranulocytosis this profound; usually seen in immunocompromised hosts.<br /></br>c. Epstein-Barr virus infection – Classic infectious mono has leukocytosis with atypical lymphocytes, sore throat with exudate, posterior cervical LAD, splenomegaly. ANC is not &lt; 500.<br /></br>d. Parvovirus B19 – Selectively halts erythroid precursors → transient aplastic crisis (severe anemia/low reticulocytes), not marked neutropenia.<br /></br>e. Sepsis – Early phase often neutrophilia with bandemia; late “burn-out” neutropenia can occur but patient would look toxic/hypotensive. This patient’s vitals and exam do not fit severe sepsis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>A new sulfonamide started 8 days ago + fever/sore throat + ANC ~90/µL = drug-induced agranulocytosis.</p>
155. A 47-year-old woman comes to the physician because of difficulty sleeping for 2 months. During this period, she has been awakening at 3 AM and remaining awake worrying about work, even though she is tired. She has unstable angina pectoris and required placement of one coronary artery stent 6 months ago and a second stent 4 months ago. Her chest pain started to recur 1 month ago, and she now has it almost daily. Evaluation shows no organic cause for her recurring chest pain. She works as an attorney for a large firm and reports that she has been given more responsibility during the past year. For the past month, she has not enjoyed her work as much as she previously did. She forces herself to go to work and has difficulty paying attention while she is there. She is a gourmet cook but no longer prepares meals because she does not enjoy the taste of the food. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she has a sad and worried mood and a reactive affect. She is alert and oriented to person, place, and time. She states that she frequently becomes despondent, especially when she thinks about her heart disease. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Adjustment disorder</br>b. Dysthymic disorder</br>c. Generalized anxiety disorder</br>d. Major depressive disorder</br>e. Primary insomnia</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Major depressive disorder (MDD)</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that point straight to MDD:<br /></br>• Depressed mood: “sad … despondent”<br /></br>• Anhedonia: no longer enjoys her once-loved gourmet cooking, no longer enjoys work<br /></br>• Insomnia with classic “early-morning awakening” (a biologic-clock sign of MDD)<br /></br>• Decreased concentration / attention at work<br /></br>• Duration ≥ 2 weeks (actually 2 months)<br /></br>• Functional impairment (forces herself to go to work, performance falling)<br /></br>These fulfill ≥ 5 SIGECAPS criteria (Sleep ↓, Interest ↓, Guilt/worthlessness, Energy ↓/fatigue, Concentration ↓). No evidence of mania, psychosis, bereavement, or substance/medical cause. Therefore DSM-5 criteria for a current major depressive episode are met.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>When you see sadness + loss of interest, immediately run the “SIGECAPS” checklist and time-stamp it (&gt; 2 weeks → MDD if ≥ 5 met). If criteria are met, other diagnoses (e.g., adjustment disorder, GAD, insomnia) are ruled out—even if stressors or medical illness are present.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><ol></br><li>Identify mood symptoms (sadness + anhedonia).  </li></br><li>Count additional neurovegetative/cognitive symptoms (sleep, concentration, energy).  </li></br><li>Confirm duration ≥ 2 weeks.  </li></br><li>Exclude mania, hypomania, grief-only picture, substances, or medical etiology.  </li></br><li>Diagnose MDD.</li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home: Always default to the syndromic diagnosis that fully meets criteria; do not “down-code” to adjustment or anxiety if depression criteria are satisfied.</p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): mood, anhedonia, SIGECAPS inventory, suicide screen, impairment level, medical comorbidities, current meds, plan for therapy ± pharmacotherapy.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• ↓ monoaminergic neurotransmission (serotonin, NE, dopamine).<br /></br>• HPA-axis hyperactivity → ↑ cortisol; circadian rhythm shift → early a.m. awakening.<br /></br>• Risk factors: female sex, chronic medical disease (CAD), high-stress job, prior depressive episodes, lack of social support.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><p>First-line:<br /></br>• SSRI (e.g., sertraline, escitalopram) or SNRI (e.g., venlafaxine) + evidence-based psychotherapy (CBT, interpersonal therapy).<br /></br>• Screen for suicide risk before starting.<br /></br>CAD note: Sertraline has good cardiac safety data; avoid TCAs due to QT prolongation and arrhythmia risk.<br /></br>Adjuncts if needed: bupropion, mirtazapine (watch weight/sedation), ECT for severe/refractory or psychotic depression.<br /></br>Follow-up: PHQ-9 monitoring, assess adherence, side effects, and response at 4–6 weeks.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Mnemonic / Memory Aid  </p></br><p>SIG-E-CAPS<br /></br>Sleep ↓/↑<br /></br>Interest ↓<br /></br>Guilt/worthlessness<br /></br>Energy ↓<br /></br>Concentration ↓<br /></br>Appetite ↓/↑ or weight change<br /></br>Psychomotor retardation/agitation<br /></br>Suicidal ideation<br /></br>≥ 5 for ≥ 2 weeks → MDD</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Adjustment disorder – Requires onset within 3 months of an identifiable stressor and DOES NOT meet full criteria for another disorder. Here she meets full MDD criteria, so AD is superseded.</p></br><p>B. Dysthymic disorder (Persistent depressive disorder) – Chronic, milder depression lasting ≥ 2 years without symptom-free interval &gt; 2 months. Her symptoms are only 2 months in duration.</p></br><p>C. Generalized anxiety disorder – Excessive worry ≥ 6 months plus restlessness, fatigue, etc. She has worry, but strong depressive signs/anhedonia dominate and duration is &lt; 6 months. Meets MDD criteria.</p></br><p>E. Primary insomnia – Sleep difficulty ≥ 3 nights/week for ≥ 3 months without other mental/medical cause. Her insomnia is explained by depression; plus multiple mood symptoms present.</p></br><p>Common pitfall: Attributing insomnia or worry to anxiety/insomnia alone—always screen for full depressive syndrome first.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Summary  </p></br><p>The patient’s 2-month history of sad mood, anhedonia, early-morning awakening, impaired concentration, and functional decline meets DSM-5 criteria for major depressive disorder.</p>
156. A 43-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after the onset of tonic-clonic movements of all extremities at home. Her son says she has a 10-year history of chronic back pain, for which she takes oxycodone and diazepam. She previously worked as a nurse but has not worked during the past 5 years because of the pain. She lives with her two adult sons. On arrival, the patient is observed speaking clearly while the tonic-clonic movements continue. The episode ends 20 minutes after arrival; following the episode, the patient is alert and fully oriented with intact memory. Vital signs are within normal limits. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 99 percent. Examination shows no evidence of incontinence or tongue biting during the episode. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Addition of phenytoin to the medication regimen</br>b. EEG</br>c. Increasing the dosage of diazepam</br>d. Lumbar puncture for examination of cerebrospinal fluid</br>e. Reassurance that she did not have a neurologic event</br></br></br><p>Correct answer</br>E. Reassurance that she did not have a neurologic event</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</p></br><p>Key clues</br>• “Tonic-clonic movements of all extremities” yet the patient “is observed speaking clearly while the movements continue.” – True epileptic generalized seizures produce loss of consciousness; speech during the event rules them out.<br /></br>• Episode lasts 30 min at home + 20 min in the ED, yet afterward she is “alert and fully oriented with intact memory” and has no post-ictal confusion.<br /></br>• No tongue biting, no urinary incontinence – common physical sequelae of generalized tonic-clonic seizures are absent.<br /></br>• Chronic pain, narcotic and benzodiazepine use, former health-care worker, on disability – psychosocial risk factors for a conversion disorder/psychogenic nonepileptic seizure (PNES).  </p></br><p>Pattern: Apparent seizure activity with preserved awareness, prolonged duration, and lack of post-ictal state → think PNES (a subtype of conversion disorder). Once organic causes are excluded by history and exam, the immediate ED management is reassurance rather than neurologic work-up or antiepileptic therapy.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: In any “seizure” question, first ask: 1) Was consciousness lost? 2) Any post-ictal period? 3) Physical stigmata (tongue bite, incontinence)? 4) Are there psychiatric/stress clues? If the answers don’t fit an epileptic pattern, consider PNES.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</p></br><p>Step-by-step thought process</br>1. Suspect a true generalized tonic-clonic seizure → look for loss of consciousness and post-ictal confusion. Not present.<br /></br>2. Consider focal seizure with awareness → would not be bilateral tonic-clonic from onset and would still create some impairment.<br /></br>3. Rule out status epilepticus → patient able to talk, oxygen saturation normal, no autonomic instability.<br /></br>4. Long duration, variable motor activity, and intact cognition → PNES.<br /></br>5. PNES is a manifestation of conversion disorder; acute management is reassurance and later outpatient psych follow-up. No emergency drugs or investigations needed in a clinically stable patient who returns to baseline.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Preserved awareness during bilateral motor activity + absence of post-ictal state = psychogenic nonepileptic seizure → reassure, do not give antiepileptics.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): Describe witnessed motor activity, mental status during and after event, absence of physical injury, note psychosocial factors, and provide patient education/reassurance.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</p></br><p>Psychogenic nonepileptic seizures (PNES)<br /></br>• Category: Somatic symptom &amp; related disorders; specifically, conversion disorder (functional neurologic symptom disorder).<br /></br>• Mechanism: Unconscious psychological conflict manifests as neurologic‐appearing events without cortical epileptiform discharges.<br /></br>• Risk factors: Females, prior trauma, chronic pain syndromes, health-care workers, secondary gain (disability), psychiatric comorbidity.<br /></br>• Clinical hallmarks: Prolonged/asynchronous movements, pelvic thrusting, side-to-side head movement, eyes closed, retained awareness, no post-ictal confusion, normal vitals, often occurs in presence of others.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics</p></br><p>Immediate ED management</br>• If vital signs stable and exam indicates PNES → reassurance and safe discharge.<br /></br>• No antiepileptic drugs, no urgent imaging, no lumbar puncture required.<br /></br>Follow-up<br /></br>• Outpatient neurology or psychiatry referral for video EEG confirmation and therapy (CBT, treatment of comorbid depression/anxiety, physical therapy for functional symptoms).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</p></br><p>“PSEUDO” for PNES red flags<br /></br>P – Prolonged (&gt;2-3 min)<br /></br>S – Side-to-side movements / Speaking during event<br /></br>E – Eyes closed/resistance to opening<br /></br>U – Unconsciousness absent (awareness retained)<br /></br>D – Duration variable / Distraction stops event<br /></br>O – Obtundation post-event absent</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</p></br><p>a. Addition of phenytoin – Appropriate only for confirmed epileptic seizures or status epilepticus. Antiepileptics do not help PNES and expose patient to adverse effects (ataxia, teratogenicity).  </p></br><p>b. EEG – A video-EEG is the gold standard to confirm PNES, but acutely in the ED, after a clearly non-epileptic event with full recovery, it is not the next best step. Outpatient EEG can be arranged later.  </p></br><p>c. Increasing the dosage of diazepam – Benzodiazepines abort active epileptic seizures. Here, the episode has resolved and was non-epileptic; more diazepam adds risk of respiratory depression, especially given chronic benzo/opioid use.  </p></br><p>d. Lumbar puncture – Indicated when CNS infection or subarachnoid hemorrhage is suspected (fever, stiff neck, severe headache, focal deficits). None are present.  </p></br><p>e. Reassurance – Correct, as explained.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom line: Apparent seizure with preserved consciousness, no post-ictal state, and psychosocial risk factors signals a psychogenic nonepileptic event. Provide reassurance and arrange outpatient follow-up; do not start antiepileptics or pursue invasive testing acutely.</p>
157. A 52-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department from a homeless shelter 1 hour after she awakened with confusion and agitation. On arrival, she is oriented to person but not to place or time. She is unable to provide any medical history. She had no medications with her at the shelter. She is tremulous and diaphoretic. Her temperature is 38.2 C (100.8 F), pulse is 124/min, and blood pressure is 170/106 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no other abnormalities. Urine toxicology screening is positive for Δ9-tetrahydrocannabinol and tricyclic antidepressants. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's current symptoms?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Alcohol intoxication</br>b. Alcohol withdrawal</br>c. Cannabis intoxication</br>d. Cannabis withdrawal</br>e. Tricyclic overdose</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>B. Alcohol withdrawal</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Demographic/Risk profile – 52-year-old, homeless shelter resident ⇒ very high prevalence of heavy alcohol use.<br /></br>• Timing – “awakened with confusion and agitation” only 1 hour ago; shelters frequently close alcohol access overnight, so the patient may have gone 8-12 h without drinking.<br /></br>• Autonomic hyperactivity – tremulous, diaphoretic, HR 124, BP 170/106, T 38.2 °C → classic early withdrawal signs.<br /></br>• Mental status – disoriented, fluctuating confusion → early delirium (can progress to delirium tremens).<br /></br>• Toxicology – +THC and tricyclics do NOT explain these sympathetic findings; an alcohol level is not part of a routine urine screen, so a negative screen does not rule out alcohol.<br /></br>Pertinent negatives – no seizure, no anticholinergic findings, no ataxia or slurred speech (would suggest intoxication rather than withdrawal).</p></br><p>Test-taking pearl: On NBME/Step 2, tremor + diaphoresis + tachy/HTN in a patient with possible alcoholism = think alcohol withdrawal, even if alcohol is not mentioned or lab is negative.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. Recognize autonomic hyperactivity (tremor, sweating, ↑HR, ↑BP).<br /></br>2. Associate with delirium/confusion → delirium tremens spectrum.<br /></br>3. Identify risk factors for chronic alcohol use (homelessness).<br /></br>4. Rule out other intoxications/withdrawals (look at specific toxidrome features).<br /></br>Key takeaway: Alcohol withdrawal begins 6–24 h after last drink (tremor, anxiety), progresses to seizures (12-48 h) and delirium tremens (48-96 h). Early detection is crucial.</p></br><p>Documentation: note last known drink, C-IWA-Ar score, need for benzodiazepine protocol, vitals, mental status, IV fluids, thiamine/folate.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>Chronic ethanol: up-regulates excitatory NMDA receptors and down-regulates inhibitory GABA-A receptors. Sudden cessation → unchecked CNS excitation → tremor, agitation, seizures, hallucinations, sympathetic surge. Delirium tremens has a 5–15 % mortality untreated.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics</br>1. Benzodiazepines (e.g., IV diazepam or lorazepam) titrated to symptom control.<br /></br>2. Thiamine before glucose, dextrose + IV fluids.<br /></br>3. Electrolyte correction (Mg²⁺, K⁺, PO₄³⁻).<br /></br>4.
