Continuous vitals, consider ICU for severe cases.<br /></br>Diagnostics: CMP, CBC, Mg, serum EtOH level, ECG; CT head only if focal neuro signs or trauma.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“AWET BED” (Alcohol Withdrawal Every Time → sequence)<br /></br>A – Anxiety, tremor (6–8 h)<br /></br>W – Withdrawal seizures (12–48 h)<br /></br>E – “E”thyl hallucinosis (visual/auditory, 12–48 h)<br /></br>T – Tremens (delirium tremens, 48–96 h)<br /></br>BED – think of patient thrashing in bed with tremens.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>A. Alcohol intoxication – Produces CNS depression: ataxia, slurred speech, low BP/HR, hypoglycaemia—not tremor + HTN/tachycardia.<br /></br>C. Cannabis intoxication – Causes conjunctival injection, ↑appetite, euphoria, sometimes mild tachycardia, but not marked autonomic storm or tremor.<br /></br>D. Cannabis withdrawal – Mild: irritability, insomnia, ↓appetite, rarely noticeable autonomic instability.<br /></br>E. Tricyclic overdose – Anticholinergic toxidrome: hyperthermia, dry skin, mydriasis, urinary retention, hypotension, widened QRS, seizures. Our patient is diaphoretic, hypertensive, and has no anticholinergic findings.</p></br><p>Common pitfall: assuming a positive urine tox screen explains the presentation; always correlate with the physiologic toxidrome.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Key Takeaway</br>In any agitated, tremulous, diaphoretic patient with risk factors for alcoholism and no alcohol level provided, prioritize alcohol withdrawal and begin benzodiazepine therapy promptly.</p>
158. A 27-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 1-year history of daily headaches involving her entire head. The headaches are worse in the afternoon, sometimes throbbing, and are associated with nausea and sensitivity to light. The headaches are worse during menses. She takes four to six tablets of acetaminophen with caffeine daily, which provides partial relief. She takes no other medications. She has smoked one-half pack of cigarettes daily for 10 years and drinks four beers weekly. She works the night shift at an industrial plant. Vital signs are within normal limits. Neurologic examination shows no focal findings. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment for this patient's headaches?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Switching to daytime shift work</br>b. Cessation of alcohol consumption</br>c. Cessation of smoking</br>d. Discontinuation of acetaminophen with caffeine</br>e. Oral contraceptive therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Discontinuation of acetaminophen with caffeine  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key time course: “1-year history of daily headaches.”<br /></br>• Headache description: holocephalic, often throbbing, ± nausea/photophobia, worse in the afternoon. This sounds like transformed (chronic) migraine.<br /></br>• Analgesic history: takes acetaminophen-caffeine “four to six tablets daily.” That is ≥4–6 × 30 days ≈ 120–180 doses/month—well over the 10- to 15-day/month cutoff for medication-overuse headache (MOH).<br /></br>• Normal neurologic exam → primary, not secondary, headache.  </p></br><p>Cognitive approach:<br /></br>1. When a patient reports “daily or near-daily headaches,” immediately ask, “Are they taking acute pain meds more than 10–15 days/month?”<br /></br>2. If yes → think MOH (rebound) regardless of the initial headache type.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Medication-overuse headache is the most common cause of chronic daily headaches. Any short-acting analgesic (acetaminophen, NSAID, caffeine combo, triptan, opioid, butalbital) used &gt;10–15 days/month can transform episodic migraine or tension headache into a daily, refractory pattern.<br /></br>• Core takeaway: The first step in management is to withdraw the offending agent; otherwise prophylactic or lifestyle changes rarely help.<br /></br>• Documentation points: frequency/dose of each acute medication, plan for discontinuation, bridge therapy offered, counseling on expected transient worsening (“rebound”) lasting 1–2 weeks.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Chronic exposure to short-acting analgesics or caffeine induces:</br>• Up-regulation of pain pathways (central sensitization).<br /></br>• Down-regulation of serotonin receptors → lower threshold for trigeminovascular activation.<br /></br>• Rebound vasodilation as drug levels fall → afternoon “return” of headache.  </p></br><p>Diagnostic Criteria (ICHD-3):<br /></br>1. Headache on ≥15 days/month.<br /></br>2. Regular overuse of ≥1 acute/symptomatic headache drug for &gt;3 months.<br /></br>3. Headache not better accounted for by another diagnosis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>1. Discontinue the overused medication (cold turkey for simple analgesics/caffeine; tapered for opioids or barbiturates).<br /></br>2. Bridge: long-acting NSAID (naproxen), prednisone burst, or dihydroergotamine infusion for severe rebound phase.<br /></br>3. Begin migraine prophylaxis after withdrawal:<br /></br>   • First line: topiramate, propranolol, or amitriptyline.<br /></br>4. Educate: limit future acute meds to ≤2 days/week.<br /></br>No imaging is needed given a normal neuro exam and typical MOH story.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Too much SAP (Simple Analgesic/Acetaminophen, ASA, APAP) saps the brain.” → Daily Short-Acting Pain relievers SAP → S ensitization A nalgesic P ain (med-overuse)<br /></br>Rule of 15’s: &gt;15 days/month of headache OR &gt;15 doses/month of simple analgesic → MOH.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Switching to daytime shift work<br /></br>   • Circadian disruption can worsen primary headaches, but not the major driver here. Would not resolve daily headaches created by drug rebound.  </p></br><p>b. Cessation of alcohol consumption<br /></br>   • Alcohol triggers cluster headaches and some migraines but she drinks only 4 beers/week and headaches are daily; pattern inconsistent.  </p></br><p>c. Cessation of smoking<br /></br>   • Smoking is associated with cluster headaches and overall vascular risk, yet it does not produce daily rebound headaches. Quitting is healthy but not the key treatment.  </p></br><p>e. Oral contraceptive therapy<br /></br>   • Combined OCPs may help pure menstrual migraine but can also worsen or even contraindicate migraine with aura. They do not address medication overuse.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Daily or near-daily use of short-acting analgesics or caffeine is classic for medication-overuse headache. Definitive management is complete discontinuation of the offending medication, followed by introduction of appropriate migraine prophylaxis.</p>
159. A 25-year-old woman, gravida 1, para 1, delivers a 2268-g (5-lb) male newborn at 38 weeks’ gestation. The mother received no prenatal care. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. During pregnancy, she drank four to six glasses of wine daily and used cocaine weekly. A photograph of the newborn is shown. Which of the following would have significantly decreased the risk of this fetal outcome?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Abstinence from alcohol</br>b. Abstinence from cocaine</br>c. Copper supplementation</br>d. Folic acid supplementation</br>e. Vitamin B1 (thiamine) supplementation</br>f. Zinc supplementation</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Folic acid supplementation  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues in the stem<br /></br>• “No prenatal care” → likely missed standard prenatal supplements.<br /></br>• 38-wk infant with birth weight 2268 g (small-for-gestational-age but term).<br /></br>• Photograph (not shown on the exam screen) – on NBME forms this picture is a classic spinal lesion consistent with myelomeningocele (lumbosacral sac with exposed meninges and neural tissue).<br /></br>• Question stem asks: “What would have significantly decreased the risk of this fetal outcome?” → looking for a preventive measure, not a teratogen to avoid.  </p></br><p>Cognitive/Metacognitive approach<br /></br>1. When you see “photo of newborn” + preventive question, first identify the lesion in the photo. Common NBME images: anencephaly, meningomyelocele, cleft lip, FAS facies, limb defects from thalidomide.<br /></br>2. Ask which maternal factor prevents that lesion. Neural-tube defects (NTDs) → folate. Cleft lip → no proven supplement, but maternal smoking/antiepileptics, etc. FAS → alcohol abstinence.<br /></br>3. Eliminate distractors that do not correlate with the pictured defect.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-by-Step)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Neural tube closure occurs days 22-28 post-conception.  </li></br><li>Adequate maternal folate stores are essential before this window.  </li></br><li>Daily folic acid 0.4 mg for low-risk women (4 mg for high-risk) started ≥1 mo prior to conception and continued through the first trimester cuts NTD risk by ≈70 %.  </li></br><li>Therefore, lack of folate supplementation is the modifiable factor most strongly and specifically linked to myelomeningocele in this clinical scenario.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home message: Maternal folic acid supplementation before conception and during early gestation is the single most effective intervention to prevent neural-tube defects.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient)<br /></br>• Pre-pregnancy counseling note: “Started 0.4 mg folic acid daily.”<br /></br>• OB Hx: “Discussed NTD prevention; patient verbalized understanding.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Folate (vitamin B9) is required for DNA synthesis and methylation reactions.<br /></br>• Deficiency during early embryogenesis → failure of neural-fold fusion → NTDs (spina bifida occulta, meningocele, myelomeningocele, anencephaly).<br /></br>• Risk factors: Folate deficiency, maternal diabetes, valproate/carbamazepine, hyperthermia, anti-folate drugs (methotrexate, TMP-SMX).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Notes  </p></br><p>Prenatal:<br /></br>• Universal recommendation: folic acid supplementation as above.<br /></br>• High-risk mothers (prior NTD pregnancy) → 4 mg/day.  </p></br><p>Postnatal:<br /></br>• Myelomeningocele → surgical closure within 48 h, VP shunt if hydrocephalus, bladder/bowel program, PT/OT.<br /></br>• Screen with postnatal ultrasound of head (Arnold-Chiari II, hydrocephalus).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“FOLe up the neural tube” – FOLate keeps the tube closed.<br /></br>“4 for Folate” – 0.4 mg for normal pregnancies, 4 mg for high-risk.<br /></br>Visualization: Picture a “folded” tube stapled shut by a green folate tablet.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Abstinence from alcohol<br /></br>• Prevents fetal alcohol spectrum disorders: smooth philtrum, thin upper lip, small palpebral fissures, microcephaly, developmental delay—not NTDs.<br /></br>• Does not significantly alter NTD incidence.  </p></br><p>B. Abstinence from cocaine<br /></br>• Decreases placental abruption, preterm birth, IUGR, neonatal abstinence.<br /></br>• No proven effect on neural-tube closure.  </p></br><p>C. Copper supplementation<br /></br>• Copper deficiency causes anemia, neutropenia, bone abnormalities, Menkes-like symptoms, but not NTDs.  </p></br><p>E. Vitamin B1 (thiamine) supplementation<br /></br>• Prevents Wernicke-Korsakoff in alcoholics; not related to fetal neural-tube formation.  </p></br><p>F. Zinc supplementation<br /></br>• Zinc is important for growth and immune function; deficiency → growth retardation, dermatitis, alopecia. No strong link to NTD prevention.  </p></br><p>Common Pitfall<br /></br>Students often fixate on the obvious teratogens mentioned (alcohol, cocaine). Always match the preventive measure to the specific congenital anomaly shown or described.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>Neural-tube defects are largely preventable. The single most effective intervention is maternal folic acid supplementation started before conception and continued through the first trimester.</p>
160. An 11-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department because of a 1-hour history of severe pain and blurred vision in his right eye that began when he was hit in the eye with a tennis ball. He says his eye was closed when he was hit. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He cries and appears anxious but gradually calms down. Vital signs are within normal limits. Ophthalmologic examination of the right eye shows mild periorbital edema; pooling blood in the anterior chamber obscures the lower third of the iris and pupil. Blinking does not increase pain. Gaze is conjugate and symmetric. The right pupil is slightly larger and less reactive to light compared to the left pupil. There are no foreign bodies or visual field defects. The remainder of the neurologic examination shows no focal findings. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Concussion</br>b. Corneal abrasion</br>c. Hyphema</br>d. Orbital fracture</br>e. Retro-orbital bleeding</br>f. Subconjunctival hemorrhage</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>C – Hyphema (traumatic hemorrhage into the anterior chamber)</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Mechanism: blunt trauma from a fast-moving tennis ball while the lids were closed → classic set-up for shearing of the iris and ciliary body vessels.<br /></br>• Key physical finding: “Pooling blood in the anterior chamber obscures the lower third of the iris and pupil.”  Nothing else in ophthalmology is described as a meniscus-like layer of blood anterior to the iris.<br /></br>• Pain improves once the initial shock subsides; blinking does NOT worsen the discomfort (helps rule out corneal abrasion).<br /></br>• Pupil slightly dilated &amp; sluggish = elevated intra-ocular pressure (IOP) or sphincter muscle injury, both common after hyphema.<br /></br>• No diplopia, no crepitus, no restricted gaze → argues against orbital floor fracture.<br /></br>Cognitive approach: On Step/NBME exams, “visible blood level in the anterior chamber” = hyphema. The question may try to distract you with other traumatic eye terms; anchor on the objective finding.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step 1: Blunt trauma → think of 3 big internal eye injuries: hyphema, lens dislocation, globe rupture.<br /></br>Step 2: Look for specific clue. Here, obvious anterior-chamber blood = hyphema.<br /></br>Step 3: Correlate secondary signs (sluggish pupil, blurred vision) with likely raised IOP from blood clot.<br /></br>Take-home core concept: Hyphema = blood layering in front of the iris after blunt trauma; requires head elevation, eye shield, and urgent ophthalmology follow-up to prevent rebleed or glaucoma.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls for a real encounter:</br>• Visual acuity (documented in every injured eye)<br /></br>• Description of blood layer height (e.g., “1/3-chamber hyphema”)<br /></br>• IOP measurement (tonometry)<br /></br>• Pupil shape/reactivity<br /></br>• Extra-ocular movements, orbital palpation<br /></br>• Plan: shield, elevate, restrict activity, ophthalmology consult.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>Blunt impact transiently deforms the globe → tears in the fragile vessels at the anterior root of the iris or ciliary body → blood accumulates in the anterior chamber. Complications: re-bleeding (3–5 days), clogging of trabecular meshwork → acute angle closure &amp; optic-nerve damage.</p></br><p>Risk factors: sickle cell trait or disease (erythrocytes sickle in the hypoxic aqueous → worsened outflow obstruction and IOP spike).</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests</br>First-line:</br>1. Protective eye shield (no patch, which exerts pressure).<br /></br>2. Bed rest with head of bed at 30-45° to let blood layer inferiorly.<br /></br>3. Daily IOP checks; topical β-blocker (timolol) or carbonic-anhydrase inhibitor (acetazolamide) if pressure ↑.<br /></br>4. Cycloplegic (e.g., atropine 1%) to relieve ciliary spasm.<br /></br>5. Pain control; avoid NSAIDs/aspirin → ↑ bleeding risk.</p></br><p>Second-line / indications for surgery: persistent ↑IOP, corneal blood staining, total (“8-ball”) hyphema.</p></br><p>Imaging: If globe integrity uncertain, CT orbit (no MRI). Sickle screen in African-descent patients.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“HY-phema = HY-drostatic blood in anterior cHAMber.”<br /></br>Visualize a snow globe: shake (trauma) → red flakes settle at the bottom layer.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>A. Concussion (traumatic iritis): Presents 1–2 days after trauma with photophobia, consensual pain, small sluggish pupil; no visible blood layer.</p></br><p>B. Corneal abrasion: Severe foreign-body sensation; pain increases with blinking; fluorescein staining reveals epithelial defect—no layered blood.</p></br><p>D. Orbital fracture: Look for periorbital ecchymosis, step-off, infra-orbital hypoesthesia, enophthalmos, vertical diplopia from inferior rectus entrapment—not isolated anterior-chamber bleeding.</p></br><p>E. Retro-orbital bleeding (orbital compartment syndrome): Rapid proptosis, rock-hard orbit, decreased vision, afferent pupillary defect; demands emergent lateral canthotomy.</p></br><p>F. Subconjunctival hemorrhage: Bright-red patch on sclera beneath conjunctiva; completely painless, vision unaffected, no intra-ocular involvement.</p></br><p>Keep laser-focused on the “blood layering in front of the iris” cue—on exams it virtually always equals hyphema.</p>
161. A 15-year-old girl is brought to the physician 2 weeks after noticing multiple areas on her chest and upper back that are lighter than the rest of the skin. She has mild itching over these areas when she plays volleyball. A photograph of the lesions is shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: I</br></br>a. Actinic keratosis</br>b. Atopic dermatitis</br>c. Contact dermatitis</br>d. Discoid lupus erythematosus</br>e. Impetigo</br>f. Rosacea</br>g. Seborrheic dermatitis</br>h. Tinea corporis</br>i. Tinea versicolor</br>j. Urticaria</br>k. Vitiligo</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer: i. Tinea versicolor  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Age &amp; setting: 15-y-o adolescent → peak age for Malassezia furfur overgrowth (hormonally driven sebaceous activity).<br /></br>• Distribution: “Chest and upper back” = classic sebum-rich areas (thorax, shoulders) affected in tinea versicolor.<br /></br>• Lesion description: “Multiple areas … lighter than the rest of the skin” (hypopigmented macules/patches).<br /></br>• Provoking factor: “Mild itching … when she plays volleyball” → pruritus increased with heat/sweating, another Malassezia clue.<br /></br>• Time course: Gradual over weeks; not acute and not inflammatory enough for urticaria/dermatitis.<br /></br>Cognitive approach: In any trunk rash that is<br /></br> 1. hypo- or hyper-pigmented,<br /></br> 2. +/- fine scale,<br /></br> 3. worsens with heat/sweat,<br /></br>think tinea versicolor first. Remember “versiCOLOR = variety of colors.”</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Narrow by distribution (trunk) and pigment change (not erythematous papules, not plaques).<br /></br>2. Hypo- / hyperpigmented patches that fail to tan with sun → Malassezia spp.<br /></br>3. Mild pruritus but otherwise asymptomatic.<br /></br>4. Photo on exams usually shows well-defined, “raindrop” patches with subtle scale.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Tinea versicolor = superficial Malassezia infection producing azelaic acid → melanocyte inhibition → variegated pigment.</p></br><p>Chart note pearls (real patient): document color variation, KOH “spaghetti-and-meatballs,” counsel on recurrence, prescribe selenium sulfide or topical azoles.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Causative organism: Malassezia furfur (lipophilic yeast, normal skin flora).<br /></br>• Trigger: Heat, humidity, sweating, oily skin.<br /></br>• Mechanism of color change: Yeast produces dicarboxylic acids (e.g., azelaic acid) that inhibit tyrosinase → decreased melanin synthesis (hypopigmentation) or abnormal melanosome size (hyperpigmentation).<br /></br>• Appearance: Sharply demarcated macules/patches with fine “powdery” scale, may coalesce. Woods lamp: yellow-green fluorescence.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>Diagnosis: KOH prep → short hyphae &amp; spores (“spaghetti and meatballs”).<br /></br>First-line therapy:<br /></br>• Topical selenium sulfide 2.5 % lotion/shampoo, OR<br /></br>• Topical azoles (ketoconazole, clotrimazole).<br /></br>Refractory/recurrent: Oral fluconazole or itraconazole (single-dose pulse).<br /></br>Counseling: Likely to recur; prophylactic monthly selenium wash during warm months.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>• “VersiCOLOR” = many colors.<br /></br>• “Spaghetti &amp; meatballs at the Malassezia restaurant.”<br /></br>• Think “Selena (selenium) sings in the shower” for topical selenium sulfide treatment.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Actinic keratosis – Rough, erythematous or hyperkeratotic papules in sun-exposed elderly skin; premalignant. None of that here.<br /></br>b. Atopic dermatitis – Intensely pruritic eczematous plaques in flexural areas; chronic personal/ family atopy history. Our patient has hypopigmented, minimally pruritic trunk lesions.<br /></br>c. Contact dermatitis – Well-demarcated erythematous, vesicular or lichenified plaques following allergen exposure (e.g., poison ivy); acute onset, not pigmentary.<br /></br>d. Discoid lupus erythematosus – Chronic erythematous, atrophic, scarring plaques on face/scalp/ears with adherent scale; uncommon on trunk and not solely pigmentary.<br /></br>e. Impetigo – Honey-colored crusted papules/vesicles on face/extremities, usually in younger children.<br /></br>f. Rosacea – Facial flushing, papules/pustules, telangiectasias; spares trunk.<br /></br>g. Seborrheic dermatitis – Greasy, yellow, scaling plaques on scalp/face/central chest, but produces erythema not pigment loss.<br /></br>h. Tinea corporis – Annular “ringworm” with central clearing, raised scaly border on extremities/trunk; due to dermatophytes (Trichophyton); not diffuse pigment change.<br /></br>j. Urticaria – Transient, edematous wheals lasting &lt;24 h; no scaling or persistent pigment change.<br /></br>k. Vitiligo – Autoimmune melanocyte destruction → chalk-white depigmented (not hypopigmented) macules, often periorificial or acral; Wood lamp bright white, no scale, no pruritus, and chronic course.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Key Takeaway<br /></br>Hypo- or hyper-pigmented, finely scaling patches on the trunk of an adolescent that itch with sweating = tinea versicolor. Identify the Malassezia pattern, confirm with “spaghetti &amp; meatballs,” and treat with topical selenium sulfide or azoles.</p>
162. A 42-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis is admitted to the hospital because of a 3-day history of fever, intermittent severe chest pain, mild abdominal pain, and malaise. She also has a 1-week history of increasing fatigue. The chest pain worsens with inspiration, and she says she now sleeps most comfortably while leaning over her kitchen table. During the past week, she has had increased joint pain and decreased appetite. She has not had vomiting or changes in bowel function. Her medications are methotrexate, prednisone, and acetaminophen with codeine. She does not smoke cigarettes, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. She is in moderate distress. She is 165 cm (5 ft 5 in) tall and weighs 59 kg (130 lb); BMI is 22 kg/m2. Her temperature is 38.6  C (101.4  F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 22/min and shallow, and blood pressure is 140/88 mm Hg. On examination, a pleural friction rub is heard over the lower lobe of the right lung, and a pericardial friction rub is heard over the anterior aspect of the left hemithorax. The spleen tip is palpated 2 cm below the left costal margin. There is no peripheral edema. There is thickening of the synovial membranes and tenderness to palpation of the hands and feet. Laboratory studies show:  </br>Hemoglobin               11.1 g/dL  </br>Hematocrit               33 percent  </br>Mean corpuscular volume  88 μm3  </br>Leukocyte count          1800/mm3  </br>Segmented neutrophils    65 percent  </br>Eosinophils              2 percent  </br>Basophils                1 percent  </br>Lymphocytes              30 percent  </br>Monocytes                2 percent  </br>Platelet count           155,000/mm3  </br>Serum  </br>Urea nitrogen            24 mg/dL  </br>Creatinine               1.2 mg/dL  </br>AST                      12 U/L  </br>ALT                      24 U/L  </br>Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Amyloidosis  </br>b. Bacterial endocarditis  </br>c. Cirrhosis  </br>d. Felty syndrome  </br>e. HIV infection  </br>f. Leukemia  </br>g. Tuberculosis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Felty syndrome  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Long-standing rheumatoid arthritis (RA): chronic joint pain, synovial thickening, on methotrexate + prednisone.<br /></br>2. Extra-articular RA inflammation: pleural and pericardial friction rubs → serositis, a well-known systemic RA manifestation.<br /></br>3. Splenomegaly: spleen tip palpable 2 cm below costal margin.<br /></br>4. Neutropenia: WBC 1,800/mm³ with 65 % neutrophils ≈ absolute neutrophil count (ANC) ≈ 1,200/mm³ (&lt;1500). Mild anemia and thrombocytopenia may also be present.<br /></br>Classic triad (RA + splenomegaly + neutropenia) = Felty syndrome.  </p></br><p>Test-taking tip: Any RA patient with otherwise unexplained fever or infections + palpable spleen + low neutrophil count → think Felty.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (stepwise)<br /></br>• Identify chronic RA → look for complications.<br /></br>• Note systemic inflammatory signs (fever, serositis) → suggests extra-articular disease, not medication side-effect alone.<br /></br>• Palpable spleen → rare in uncomplicated RA.<br /></br>• Profound leukopenia/neutropenia → narrow differential; combine with first two findings → Felty syndrome.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Felty syndrome is an immune-mediated variant of severe, long-standing RA producing neutropenia and splenomegaly, predisposing to recurrent infections.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): Document ANC, splenic size, signs of infection/serositis, current DMARD therapy, plan for infection prophylaxis and possible hematology referral.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Autoimmune destruction + splenic sequestration of neutrophils.<br /></br>• Strong HLA-DR4 association; occurs after ≥10 years of seropositive, erosive RA.<br /></br>• Extra-articular immune complex deposition → vasculitis, serositis, nodules.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment<br /></br>Diagnostics<br /></br>– CBC with differential (confirm neutropenia).<br /></br>– Peripheral smear, bone marrow biopsy if atypical counts.<br /></br>– Cultures/chest imaging when febrile because infection risk is high.  </p></br><p>Treatment<br /></br>1. Intensify RA control:<br /></br>   • Methotrexate (already on), leflunomide, sulfasalazine, or biologic DMARDs (e.g., TNF-α inhibitors).<br /></br>2. Hematologic support:<br /></br>   • Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF, e.g., filgrastim) for severe neutropenia.<br /></br>3. Refractory cases:<br /></br>   • Splenectomy (reduces neutrophil sequestration).<br /></br>4. Infection prophylaxis &amp; prompt antibiotics when febrile.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>“Felty FELT”:<br /></br>F – Fever / infections (due to neutropenia)<br /></br>E – Extra-articular RA manifestations (serositis, nodules)<br /></br>L – Leukopenia (neutropenia)<br /></br>T – Tip of spleen palpable (splenomegaly)  </p></br><p>Visualize a “felt-covered spleen” clutching neutrophils to remember splenic sequestration causing neutropenia.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Amyloidosis – Presents with nephrotic-range proteinuria, restrictive cardiomyopathy, or hepatosplenomegaly without isolated neutropenia; serositis less common.  </p></br><p>b. Bacterial endocarditis – Would show a heart murmur, positive blood cultures, elevated or normal WBC, and not explain neutropenia + splenomegaly triad.  </p></br><p>c. Cirrhosis – Splenomegaly possible (portal hypertension) but patient lacks liver stigmata, abnormal LFTs, or coagulopathy; RA + neutropenia relationship unexplained.  </p></br><p>e. HIV infection – Can cause neutropenia and fever but would not specifically link to long-standing RA nor give the classic serositis + splenomegaly triad in a previously healthy, low-risk woman.  </p></br><p>f. Leukemia – Could present with cytopenias and splenomegaly, but peripheral smear would show blasts or abnormal cells; serositis less typical; RA history incidental.  </p></br><p>g. Tuberculosis – Usually chronic cough, night sweats, weight loss; WBC often normal or mildly elevated; neutropenia and splenomegaly are not hallmark findings.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Takeaway<br /></br>Remember Felty syndrome whenever a patient with chronic, seropositive RA develops neutropenia and splenomegaly—high infection risk mandates rapid recognition and management.</p>
163. A 54-year-old man comes to the office because of a 4-month history of progressive weakness and numbness of his left leg. He has a 5-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus treated with metformin. He has worked as a plumber for 34 years; he often crawls into and works in tight spaces, and he frequently moves and lifts heavy objects. On neurologic examination, muscle strength is 5−/5 in the thigh adductor, quadriceps, and tibialis anterior muscles on the left and intact on the right. Muscle strength is intact in the iliopsoas, gluteus maximus, thigh abductor, hamstrings, and gastrocnemius muscles bilaterally. Deep tendon reflexes are 1+ in the left knee, 2+ in the right knee, and 2+ in the ankles. Sensation to light touch is mildly decreased over the medial-distal aspect of the left lower extremity and medial aspect of the left foot. Sensation otherwise is intact. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Common peroneal neuropathy</br>b. Femoral neuropathy</br>c. L4 radiculopathy</br>d. Obturator neuropathy</br>e. S1 radiculopathy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. L4 radiculopathy  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis – How the clues point to L4</p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Pattern of motor weakness<br /></br>   • Quadriceps (knee extension) – femoral n. (L2–L4)<br /></br>   • Thigh adductors – obturator n. (L2–L4)<br /></br>   • Tibialis anterior (ankle dorsiflexion) – deep peroneal n.; primary root = L4<br /></br>   • Sparing of iliopsoas (hip flexion, L2–L3) and gluteal/hamstring muscles (L5–S1) localises the deficit to a single root (L4) rather than a more proximal plexus or peripheral nerve.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Reflex changes<br /></br>   • Depressed patellar reflex (knee jerk) on the left. The patellar reflex is mediated mainly by the L4 root (with some L3 contribution).</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Sensory loss<br /></br>   • Medial-distal leg and medial foot → classic L4 dermatome.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Occupation / chronic mechanical stress<br /></br>   • 34-year plumber often crouching and lifting → predisposes to lumbar foraminal stenosis or disc herniation causing unilateral radiculopathy.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip:<br /></br>On NBME/Step questions, identify (a) one reflex change, (b) a single dermatome, and (c) a consistent myotomal weakness. If all three line up on one root, choose that radiculopathy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)</p></br><ol></br><li>Localise sensory loss → medial leg/foot = L4.  </li></br><li>Check deep tendon reflexes → ↓ patellar = L4.  </li></br><li>Match motor findings: quadriceps + tibialis anterior weakness WITH intact iliopsoas/gluteus medius → root-specific, not whole femoral or L5 lesion.  </li></br><li>Therefore, L4 radiculopathy best fits all findings.</li></br></ol></br><p>Core takeaway:<br /></br>Radiculopathies produce deficits across several peripheral nerves that share the same root, whereas an isolated mononeuropathy affects only the muscles &amp; skin served by that one peripheral nerve.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl:<br /></br>Note focal myotomal weakness, dermatomal sensory loss, and reflex asymmetry; recommend lumbar MRI if progressive.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>High-yield Pathophysiology Review</p></br><p>• L4 nerve root exits between L4 and L5 vertebrae.<br /></br>• Most common causes: posterolateral L4–L5 disc herniation, foraminal stenosis, spondylolisthesis.<br /></br>• Symptoms: pain/paresthesia anterior thigh → medial leg/foot, weakness in knee extension &amp; ankle dorsiflexion, ↓ patellar reflex.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Management Snapshot</p></br><p>Initial: NSAIDs, activity modification, PT, neuropathic pain agents (gabapentin, duloxetine).<br /></br>Red-flags/progressive deficit (as here): Lumbar spine MRI → surgical referral for decompression or micro-discectomy if large herniation or persistent/ progressive weakness.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memory Aids</p></br><p>“L4 kicks the door.”<br /></br>– L4 is the root for kicking (knee extension) → patellar reflex.<br /></br>Dermatome mnemonic: “L4 to the floor (medial foot).”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</p></br><p>a. Common peroneal neuropathy<br /></br>   • Motor: foot drop (tibialis anterior, foot eversion) YES, but patellar reflex &amp; quadriceps would be normal.<br /></br>   • Sensory loss: dorsum of foot &amp; lateral shin, not medial leg/foot.<br /></br>   • Reflexes unaffected (knee jerk intact) → doesn’t match vignette.</p></br><p>b. Femoral neuropathy<br /></br>   • Would weaken iliopsoas AND quadriceps; patellar ↓ but tibialis anterior usually spared (served by peroneal n.).<br /></br>   • Sensory loss over anterior thigh and medial leg, not medial foot. Iliopsoas strength is normal in vignette → rules this out.</p></br><p>d. Obturator neuropathy<br /></br>   • Isolated adductor weakness ± small patch sensory loss medial thigh only. Quadriceps, tibialis anterior, and patellar reflex all normal → inconsistent here.</p></br><p>e. S1 radiculopathy<br /></br>   • Presents with loss of ankle jerk, weakness in plantar flexion &amp; eversion, sensory loss lateral foot/sole.<br /></br>   • Patellar reflex normal, quadriceps strength intact. None of these match the case.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Key Take-Home</p></br><p>Combine dermatomal sensation, myotomal strength, and reflex changes; when all three align on L4, think L4 radiculopathy.</p>
164. A 5-year-old girl with spastic diplegic cerebral palsy and gastroesophageal reflux disease is brought to the office by her parents for a routine examination. She wears leg braces and occasionally uses a wheelchair for ambulation. Since her last visit 6 months ago, she has had intermittent shortness of breath when walking long distances and has tended to lean toward the right. Her parents say she has had decreased tolerance for sitting in her wheelchair. She was born at 31 weeks’ gestation by cesarean delivery to a 21-year-old woman, gravida 4, para 4; pregnancy was complicated by chorioamnionitis. The patient has undergone soft-tissue release for contractures in her hips and lower extremities. Her medications are baclofen and lansoprazole. She is alert and fully oriented, and she answers questions appropriately. She is at the 40th percentile for height and weight. Vital signs are within normal limits. Mild impingement of the rib cage on the right iliac crest is noted. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. Pelvic obliquity is present. Examination of the upper extremities shows less spasticity and moderate fine motor control when compared with the lower extremities. There is mild tenderness to palpation of the right lower rib cage where it abuts the right iliac crest. Patellar and Achilles deep tendon reflexes are 3+ bilaterally. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Bone scan</br>b. Electromyography and nerve conduction studies</br>c. Gait analysis</br>d. MRI of the brain</br>e. X-rays of the spine</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. X-rays of the spine  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test‐Taking Strategy<br /></br>1. Key clues<br /></br>   • Cerebral palsy with spastic diplegia → high risk for neuromuscular scoliosis.<br /></br>   • New symptoms: “leans toward the right,” “decreased tolerance for sitting,” “intermittent shortness of breath when walking.”<br /></br>   • PE: pelvic obliquity + rib cage impinging on right iliac crest → classic external sign of a thoracolumbar curve that collapses the trunk on the concave side.<br /></br>   • Reflexes 3+ (upper motor neuron baseline for CP) but no new focal neuro deficits → problem is musculoskeletal, not new CNS lesion.  </p></br><ol></br><li>Cognitive approach<br /></br>   Step 1: In any child with CP who develops trunk asymmetry or ↓ sitting tolerance, first rule out progressive scoliosis/kyphosis.<br /></br>   Step 2: Radiographically measure curve (Cobb angle) so you can stage severity and plan treatment (observation, bracing, or posterior spinal fusion).<br /></br>   Step 3: Imaging of choice = standing (or sitting, if non-ambulatory) AP &amp; lateral spine X-rays.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home pattern: “Cerebral palsy + pelvic obliquity/rib–pelvis contact → order spine films.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Why X-rays?<br /></br>  – Provide objective measurement of curve magnitude (Cobb angle) and progression.<br /></br>  – Essential baseline before orthopedic referral.<br /></br>  – Non-invasive, inexpensive, and fast; MRI reserved only if neurologic signs suggest intramedullary pathology.  </p></br><p>• Documentation pearls (real patient encounter)<br /></br>  – Degree of pelvic obliquity, presence of rib–pelvis contact, skin integrity, sitting tolerance, respiratory status, tone pattern.<br /></br>  – Plan: obtain AP/lateral spine films; refer to pediatric orthopedics if Cobb angle ≥20–25° or rapid progression.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Neuromuscular scoliosis (e.g., in CP) is driven by:<br /></br>• Asymmetric muscle tone/spasticity → unopposed concave‐side pull.<br /></br>• Poor trunk control → gravity accentuates curvature while sitting.<br /></br>Natural history: curves begin around age 4–6 and progress rapidly throughout growth, often leading to pelvic obliquity, rib–pelvis impingement, pain, and restrictive lung disease.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Snapshot<br /></br>1. Diagnostics<br /></br>   • AP &amp; lateral spine X-ray → Cobb angle.<br /></br>   • Additional hip films if hip subluxation suspected (not the major issue here).<br /></br>2. Treatment ladder<br /></br>   • Cobb &lt;25°: observation &amp; sitting support.<br /></br>   • 25–40° (in growing child): custom molded TLSO brace (limited efficacy in neuromuscular scoliosis but may slow progression).<br /></br>   • ≥40–50° or rapid progression: posterior spinal fusion with pelvic fixation; pre-op pulmonary eval.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids<br /></br>“CP = Crooked Posture → first Picture the Spine.”<br /></br>Visualize the rib cage sliding onto the iliac crest like a lid on a box → think “Take a picture (X-ray) before the lid closes further.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>A. Bone scan<br /></br>   • Used for occult fracture, infection, or malignancy, not needed for curvature assessment.<br /></br>B. Electromyography/nerve conduction studies<br /></br>   • Evaluate peripheral neuropathy or neuromuscular junction problems; would not quantify scoliosis or explain pelvic tilt in CP.<br /></br>C. Gait analysis<br /></br>   • Helpful for surgical planning of lower-limb spasticity or contracture release; does not assess trunk asymmetry or rib–pelvis impingement causing sitting intolerance.<br /></br>D. MRI of the brain<br /></br>   • Would identify intracranial pathology, not indicated because symptoms are mechanical/musculoskeletal without new neuro deficits.<br /></br>E. X-rays of the spine (Correct)<br /></br>   • Directly evaluates the suspected diagnosis (progressive neuromuscular scoliosis).  </p></br><p>Core concept: In a child with CP who develops asymmetric trunk posture, always start with plain spine radiographs to assess for scoliosis.</p>
165. A 37-year-old woman comes to the physician for endocarditis prophylaxis prior to undergoing tooth extraction. The patient reports mouth pain but otherwise feels well. Two years ago, echocardiography showed mitral valve prolapse and trace mitral regurgitation. She has no other history of serious illness and takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Cardiac examination shows a normal S1 and S2; there is no S3 or S4, and no murmurs are heard. Which of the following is the most appropriate oral antibiotic prophylaxis prior to this patient’s dental procedure?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Amoxicillin</br>b. Azithromycin</br>c. Clindamycin</br>d. Moxifloxacin</br>e. No antibiotic prophylaxis is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer (per key): e. No antibiotic prophylaxis is indicated  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Setting: Routine pre-dental evaluation (“tooth extraction”).<br /></br>• Cardiac history: Mitral valve prolapse (MVP) with only “trace” mitral regurgitation documented 2 years ago; currently no murmur.<br /></br>• No prior infective endocarditis (IE), no prosthetic valve, no cyanotic congenital heart disease (CHD), no prior valve repair with prosthetic material, no cardiac transplant.<br /></br>Key takeaway: The lesion is low-risk for IE; therefore, the American Heart Association (AHA) guideline does NOT recommend antibiotic prophylaxis for dental procedures.  </p></br><p>Test-taking strategy<br /></br>1. Recall the AHA/ACC “high-risk cardiac conditions” list (the only time prophylaxis is used).<br /></br>2. Check if the patient’s lesion appears on the list—if not, choose “no prophylaxis.”<br /></br>3. Recognize distractors: amoxicillin is first-line <em>if</em> prophylaxis were warranted; clindamycin/azithro are penicillin-allergy alternatives; moxifloxacin is never used for IE prophylaxis.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Is dental manipulation occurring? – Yes (tooth extraction).<br /></br>2. Does the patient have a qualifying high-risk cardiac condition? – No.<br /></br>   • MVP with or without trivial MR is explicitly excluded from prophylaxis indications.<br /></br>3. Therefore, the correct management is no antibiotic prophylaxis.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: IE prophylaxis is reserved for patients at the highest risk of <strong>adverse outcome</strong> from IE, not merely those with any structural heart disease.  </p></br><p>Documentation cue (real world): “Patient undergoing tooth extraction. Low-risk valvular lesion (MVP w/ trace MR). AHA 2020 guideline: no IE prophylaxis indicated. Discussed rationale with patient; patient agreed.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology / Guideline Review<br /></br>Dental work can cause transient bacteremia (esp. viridans streptococci). AHA guidelines restrict prophylaxis to:<br /></br>1. Prosthetic heart valve or prosthetic material used for valve repair.<br /></br>2. Previous infective endocarditis.<br /></br>3. Certain congenital heart disease:<br /></br>   • Unrepaired cyanotic CHD, including palliative shunts/conduits.<br /></br>   • Repaired CHD with prosthetic material during the first 6 mo.<br /></br>   • Repaired CHD with residual defects at/adjacent to prosthetic material.<br /></br>4. Cardiac transplant recipients who develop valvulopathy.  </p></br><p>MVP—whether or not accompanied by regurgitation—is <em>not</em> on the list.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>If prophylaxis WERE indicated:<br /></br>• First-line oral regimen: Amoxicillin 2 g PO 30–60 min before procedure.<br /></br>• Penicillin allergy: Clindamycin 600 mg, Azithromycin or Clarithromycin 500 mg.<br /></br>• Fluoroquinolones (e.g., moxifloxacin) are not recommended.  </p></br><p>Diagnostic evaluation: No additional testing is indicated purely for prophylaxis decisions beyond an accurate cardiac history and (if needed) echocardiogram.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“PROPHY for PIE” (prosthetic, infective history, extreme congenital, transplant with valve disease)<br /></br>If the heart isn’t PIE, you don’t give Pie-cillin (amoxicillin).  </p></br><p>OR visualize: A guarded VIP section at a dental office—only four badge types (prosthetic, prior IE, congenital cyanotic/repaired &lt;6 mo/residual, transplant valvulopathy) get amoxicillin; everyone else walks in unguarded.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Amoxicillin – Correct drug but only for high-risk lesions; giving it here adds cost, adverse-effect risk, &amp; fosters resistance.<br /></br>b. Azithromycin – Alternative for pen-allergic patients, but still needs a high-risk indication.<br /></br>c. Clindamycin – Same story; good coverage for viridans strep but unnecessary here.<br /></br>d. Moxifloxacin – Not guideline-supported for IE prophylaxis; unnecessarily broad spectrum.<br /></br>e. No prophylaxis – Only option consistent with current guidelines for MVP with trivial MR.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: Thinking <em>any</em> structural valve abnormality warrants prophylaxis. Remember, guidelines changed in 2007 (reinforced 2020): routine murmurs, MVP, rheumatic mitral disease, or isolated MR/TR/AR do NOT qualify.</p>
166. An 18-year-old man has the sudden onset of rapid, pounding heartbeats. A previous episode occurred while he was drinking coffee and resolved abruptly after 30 minutes. His pulse is 180/min, respirations are 15/min, and blood pressure is 105/85 mm Hg. Examination shows no other abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Atrial fibrillation</br>b. Multifocal atrial tachycardia</br>c. Paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia</br>d. Paroxysmal ventricular tachycardia</br>e. Premature atrial beats</br>f. Premature ventricular beats</br>g. Sinus tachycardia</br>h. Third-degree atrioventricular block</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>c. Paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (PSVT)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Pertinent positives<br /></br>• Sudden onset of “rapid, pounding heartbeats” → favors a re-entrant tachycardia that can start and stop abruptly.<br /></br>• Prior identical episode that “resolved abruptly after 30 minutes” → classic for PSVT.<br /></br>• Young age (18 y) and caffeine trigger → typical setting for AV-nodal re-entrant tachycardia (the most common PSVT).<br /></br>• Regular pulse 180/min; BP only mildly low (hemodynamically stable) → narrow-complex, rapid, regular rhythm.</p></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>• No mention of irregularly irregular rhythm (rules out atrial fibrillation, multifocal atrial tachycardia).<br /></br>• No structural heart disease, syncope, or wide-complex description (less likely ventricular tachycardia).<br /></br>• Not a single premature beat, not gradual onset (rules out premature beats, sinus tachycardia).<br /></br>• Heart rate, not bradycardia (rules out AV block).</p></br><p>Cognitive/Metacognitive Approach<br /></br>1. Ask “regular or irregular?” → regular.<br /></br>2. Ask “narrow or wide?” (in real life the next step) → in a young, otherwise healthy patient, narrow-complex is most common.<br /></br>3. Abrupt start/stop = paroxysmal → think PSVT.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic<br /></br>1. AV-node–dependent re-entry circuits (AVNRT/AVRT) fire at 150–250/min → sudden palpitations.<br /></br>2. Caffeine, alcohol, stress often precipitate episodes.<br /></br>3. Abrupt conversion back to sinus once the circuit terminates → “resolved abruptly.”  </p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>PSVT = sudden, regular, narrow-complex tachycardia in a young person; precipitated by stimulants; terminates suddenly (often with vagal maneuvers or adenosine).</p></br><p>Documentation points (real patient)<br /></br>• Onset, duration, precipitants, termination.<br /></br>• Vitals &amp; hemodynamic stability.<br /></br>• ECG characteristics.<br /></br>• Response to vagal maneuvers or adenosine if done.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• AV-nodal re-entrant tachycardia (AVNRT) = dual pathways (slow &amp; fast) in AV node → re-entry loop.<br /></br>• Less commonly, accessory-pathway mediated (orthodromic AVRT, e.g., WPW).<br /></br>• Narrow QRS (&lt;120 ms) because ventricles activated via normal His-Purkinje.<br /></br>• Sudden onset/termination because the circuit either catches or breaks instantaneously.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>Acute termination<br /></br>1. Vagal maneuvers (Valsalva, carotid massage).<br /></br>2. Adenosine 6 mg IV push (transient AV-node block).<br /></br>3. Alternatives: IV verapamil, diltiazem, esmolol.<br /></br>4. Unstable → synchronized cardioversion.</p></br><p>Chronic/Prevention<br /></br>• β-blockers (metoprolol), nondihydropyridine CCBs (verapamil, diltiazem).<br /></br>• Catheter radiofrequency ablation of slow pathway = curative in recurrent cases.</p></br><p>Work-up<br /></br>• 12-lead ECG during episode (narrow-complex, no visible P’s or pseudo-R’ in V1).<br /></br>• Echocardiogram if structural disease suspected.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“PSVT = ‘Push Stop Very fast Tachy’ ”<br /></br>• Push (adenosine push) stops the Very fast (150–250) Tachycardia.  </p></br><p>Or<br /></br>“SVT is a Spa Visit with an ‘on/off’ switch”<br /></br>• S = Sudden<br /></br>• V = Very regular<br /></br>• T = Termination abrupt  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Atrial fibrillation – Irregularly irregular rhythm, usually older patients; no abrupt on/off.  </p></br><p>b. Multifocal atrial tachycardia – Irregular rhythm with ≥3 P-wave morphologies; commonly COPD patients.  </p></br><p>d. Paroxysmal ventricular tachycardia – Wide QRS, usually &gt;120 ms, often unstable; structural heart disease common.  </p></br><p>e. Premature atrial beats – Isolated early beats, not sustained 30-minute tachycardia.  </p></br><p>f. Premature ventricular beats – Occasional PVCs, wide and bizarre QRS, not a sustained tachycardia.  </p></br><p>g. Sinus tachycardia – Gradual onset/offset; rate rarely exceeds 220 − age (≈202); has identifiable P before every QRS.  </p></br><p>h. Third-degree AV block – Bradycardia with AV dissociation, not tachycardia.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Young patient + sudden, regular, rapid palpitations that start/stop abruptly → think AV-node re-entrant Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardia.</p>
167. A 66-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 6-month history of severe dizziness and a spinning sensation that occurs when she looks up or turns her head quickly. She says she fell 1 week ago during an episode. At that time, she had a brief episode of double vision. She also has a 2-year history of moderate neck pain, which she attributes to “muscle spasms”; since her fall, her pain has become more severe. She has hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Her medications are lisinopril and metformin. Her mother had a cerebral infarction at the age of 70 years. The patient has smoked one-half pack of cigarettes daily for 40 years. Her pulse is 84/min, and blood pressure is 150/85 mm Hg. Examination shows no nystagmus. Visual field testing shows no abnormalities. There are no cervical bruits. On palpation of the posterior muscles of the neck, there is mild tenderness that increases when the neck is rotated gently. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. Muscle strength is 5/5 in all extremities. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's symptoms?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Carotid artery dissection</br>b. Cervical intervertebral disc disease</br>c. Glomus vagale tumor</br>d. Ligamentous injury of the cervical spine</br>e. Vertebral artery stenosis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Vertebral artery stenosis  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• 66-year-old woman → atherosclerotic risk age<br /></br>• Hypertension, diabetes, 20-pack-year smoker → vascular risk factors<br /></br>• 6-month history of “severe dizziness and spinning” precipitated by looking up or turning head quickly → vertebrobasilar insufficiency classically worsens with head rotation or extension (“Bow-Hunter’s syndrome”)<br /></br>• Brief diplopia during a fall → posterior-circulation transient ischemic attack (TIA) red flag (one of the “5 D’s” of vertebrobasilar ischemia: dizziness, diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia, drop attacks)<br /></br>• Chronic neck pain but only mild posterior tenderness; exam otherwise normal; no nystagmus → argues against classic benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) or inner-ear disease, and supports a vascular rather than vestibular cause.</p></br><p>Test‐taking approach<br /></br>1. Vertigo + posterior-circulation neuro symptoms + vascular risk factors = think vertebral artery disease.<br /></br>2. Positional component (extension / rotation) + chronic course = stenosis/occlusion, not dissection (acute) or mass.<br /></br>3. Rule out distractors by matching their “hallmark” findings (see below).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic<br /></br>1. Vertebral arteries supply the brainstem, cerebellum, and occipital lobes.<br /></br>2. Atherosclerotic narrowing can be hemodynamically significant; extension or rotation of the neck can transiently kink the vessel and drop posterior-circulation flow.<br /></br>3. Result → episodic vertigo ± other posterior signs (diplopia, ataxia, syncope / falls).<br /></br>4. No objective nystagmus or long-standing neurologic deficits because ischemia is transient.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>In an older patient with vascular risk factors, vertigo or diplopia that is precipitated by neck motion is most consistent with vertebral artery stenosis/occlusion causing vertebrobasilar insufficiency.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient)<br /></br>• Detailed description of provoked vertigo/diplopia, fall history, vascular risk profile<br /></br>• Focal neuro exam (cranial nerves, cerebellar, sensory/motor)<br /></br>• Plan: vascular imaging (CT-angiography or MR-angiography of head &amp; neck), stroke risk reduction, neurosurgery/vascular surgery referral if symptomatic stenosis confirmed.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Etiology: Atherosclerosis of the vertebral artery origin or intracranial segment.<br /></br>• Mechanism: Fixed narrowing + dynamic mechanical compression with head movement → transient posterior cerebral/cerebellar hypoperfusion.<br /></br>• Typical manifestations: “5 D’s and 3 N’s” – Dizziness/vertigo, Diplopia, Dysarthria, Dysphagia, Drop attacks; Nausea, Nystagmus, Numbness.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment  </p></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>• First-line: CTA or MRA of neck/head to show stenosis, occlusion, or dynamic compromise (Doppler US less sensitive).<br /></br>• MRI brain if fixed deficits develop.  </p></br><p>Treatment<br /></br>1. Medical: aggressive vascular risk factor control (high-intensity statin, antiplatelet—aspirin 81–325 mg daily or dual antiplatelet if recent event, BP and DM optimization, smoking cessation).<br /></br>2. Endovascular stenting or surgical decompression/bypass considered for recurrent, disabling, or infarct-producing cases despite medical therapy.<br /></br>3. Cervical collar or activity modification sometimes used as bridge.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic “Head TURN causes the VERTEBRA to VERtigo”<br /></br>• Head turn/extension → vertebral artery → vertigo/diplopia.  </p></br><p>“5 D’s, 3 N’s” for vertebrobasilar insufficiency.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Carotid artery dissection<br /></br>• Presents acutely with unilateral neck pain, partial Horner syndrome, anterocirculation TIA/stroke (amaurosis fugax, aphasia), often after trauma. Not chronic, positional, or causing diplopia/vertigo.  </p></br><p>b. Cervical intervertebral disc disease<br /></br>• Produces neck pain ± radiculopathy/myelopathy (limb numbness, weakness), not vertigo or brainstem symptoms. Positional root pain differs from spinning sensation and diplopia.  </p></br><p>c. Glomus vagale tumor (paraganglioma)<br /></br>• Slowly enlarging lateral neck mass → pulsatile tinnitus, hoarseness, dysphagia from cranial-nerve IX–XI palsies. No episodic vertigo triggered by head movement.  </p></br><p>d. Ligamentous injury of the cervical spine<br /></br>• Follows significant trauma; causes acute severe pain/instability, neurologic deficits from cord compression. Time course and provoking factors here do not fit.  </p></br><p>e. Vertebral artery stenosis (Correct)<br /></br>• Chronic atherosclerotic disease; head rotation/extension → transient occlusion → vertigo ± diplopia; matches vignette perfectly.  </p></br><p>───────────────────</p>
168. An 18-year-old man is brought to the emergency department (ED) 15 minutes after he was involved in a high-speed motor vehicle collision in which he was the unrestrained driver. The airbags did not deploy on impact. The patient was found unresponsive at the scene by emergency medical technicians, who intubated him prior to transport. On arrival at the ED, he remains unresponsive. Medical history and medications are unknown. Pulse is 100/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 105/60 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on an FiO2 of 1.0 shows an oxygen saturation of 100 percent. Glasgow Coma Scale score is 3. Physical examination shows contusions over the forehead and anterior chest and crepitus of the anterior left chest. Femoral pulses are 1+ bilaterally. Two large-bore intravenous catheters are inserted and 1 L of lactated Ringer solution is administered with no change in hemodynamics. Chest x-ray is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnostic study at this time?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. CT angiography of the chest</br>b. MRI of the right shoulder</br>c. Needle thoracostomy</br>d. Pericardiocentesis</br>e. Repeat chest x-ray with dedicated rib views</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. CT angiography of the chest  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• High-speed, unrestrained motor-vehicle collision → classic setting for blunt thoracic trauma.<br /></br>• Chest contusions + palpable crepitus over the left anterior chest → significant chest wall/mediastinal energy transfer.<br /></br>• Hypotension that does not improve with a 1-L bolus (systolic 105 mm Hg) despite 100 % SpO₂ → occult intrathoracic vascular injury must be excluded.<br /></br>• Femoral pulses only 1+ bilaterally (upper-extremity pulses not reported but commonly stronger) → pulse deficit between upper and lower extremities suggests possible injury to descending thoracic aorta.<br /></br>• Chest x-ray (image not shown on exam, but test-writers expect you to recognize the typical next step) in this context almost always shows a widened mediastinum, loss of the aortic knob, left apical “pleural cap,” or first-rib/ scapular fractures—red flags for traumatic aortic injury (TAI).</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Any high-energy deceleration + mediastinal abnormality on CXR = “Think aorta first.”<br /></br>2. Stable vital signs? → image the aorta (CTA).<br /></br>3. Unstable? → go straight to the OR or endovascular repair.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Goal: Definitively evaluate for blunt thoracic aortic injury, the #2 cause of death at the crash scene after head injury.  </p></br><p>Why CT angiography (CTA)?<br /></br>• Fast, widely available, non-invasive, and nearly 100 % sensitive/specific for aortic transection, pseudoaneurysm, or intimal tear.<br /></br>• Guides immediate management (endovascular stent-graft vs open repair).<br /></br>• Also delineates associated thoracic injuries (lung contusion, pneumo/hemothorax, rib fractures).</p></br><p>Take-home message: In any deceleration accident with suggestive CXR or pulse discrepancy, CTA chest is the diagnostic study of choice if the patient is hemodynamically stable enough to leave the trauma bay.</p></br><p>Documentation (real life): mechanism of injury, vitals, vascular exam (pulse deficits), CXR findings, decision for CTA, trauma surgery/vascular consult.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (Blunt Traumatic Aortic Injury)  </p></br><p>Etiology: Rapid deceleration → shearing at the fixed aortic isthmus (just distal to the take-off of the left subclavian artery).<br /></br>Manifestations: May be silent or present with chest pain, interscapular pain, hoarseness, uncontrolled HTN in upper but not lower extremities, or pulse deficits.<br /></br>Mortality: 80-90 % die before ED arrival; surviving 10-20 % need immediate diagnosis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment Snapshot  </p></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>• Screening CXR → widened mediastinum (&gt;8 cm), loss of aortic knob, tracheal deviation, left apical cap.<br /></br>• Confirmatory imaging: CTA chest (gold standard today). Historically → catheter aortography, now reserved for equivocal CTA.<br /></br>• Adjuncts: TEE in the OR or if unable to transport to CT.</p></br><p>Treatment<br /></br>1. Blood-pressure/afterload control (IV esmolol ± nicardipine) to keep SBP &lt;120 mm Hg and HR &lt;80.<br /></br>2. Definitive repair: Thoracic endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR) preferred; open repair if anatomy not suitable.<br /></br>3. Concomitant injuries addressed per ATLS protocol (airway, breathing, circulation, disability, exposure).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic “WIDE”:<br /></br>W – Widened mediastinum on CXR<br /></br>I – Isthmus tear (most common site)<br /></br>D – Deceleration mechanism<br /></br>E – Evaluate with CTA  </p></br><p>Visual memory: Picture the aorta as a fire hose stapled to the arch vessels; sudden stop rips it at the staple point (isthmus).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. MRI of the right shoulder<br /></br>• Shoulder imaging delays life-threatening diagnoses and offers no explanation for femoral pulse deficits/hypotension.<br /></br>• MRI is time-consuming and contraindicated in an unstable, intubated trauma patient.</p></br><p>c. Needle thoracostomy<br /></br>• Indicated for tension pneumothorax (severe dyspnea, unilateral absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation, acute decompensation).<br /></br>• This patient is well-oxygenated (100 % on FiO₂ 1.0) and has no clinical evidence of tension physiology.</p></br><p>d. Pericardiocentesis<br /></br>• Treats cardiac tamponade (hypotension, jugular venous distention, muffled heart sounds—Beck triad).<br /></br>• Femoral pulse deficits and mechanism favor aortic, not pericardial, injury; CXR usually normal or “water-bottle heart” in tamponade, not widened mediastinum.</p></br><p>e. Repeat chest x-ray with dedicated rib views<br /></br>• Rib fractures do not explain hypotension unresponsive to fluids or pulse deficits.<br /></br>• Additional plain films won’t identify the critical vascular injury and would waste time.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: In a high-speed deceleration injury with a suspicious CXR and pulse discrepancy, immediately obtain CT angiography of the chest to rule out or confirm blunt traumatic aortic injury before the patient exsanguinates internally.</p>
169. A 25-year-old woman at 32 weeks' gestation comes to the office because of a 1-month history of "pressure" under her eyes, persistent stuffy nose, and yellow-green nasal discharge. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated. Temperature is 37.2 C (99.0 F), pulse is 82/min, and respirations are 16/min. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 99 percent. Physical examination discloses tenderness to palpation over the cheeks. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in management for this patient?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Allergy testing</br>b. Antibiotic therapy</br>c. Antihistamine therapy</br>d. Sinus ostial dilation</br>e. Reassurance that colds tend to last longer during pregnancy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>B. Antibiotic therapy</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – Duration ≥1 month → well beyond the 7- to 10-day course of a self-limited viral URI (“cold”).<br /></br>  – Constant “pressure” under the eyes + tenderness over the cheeks → maxillary sinus involvement.<br /></br>  – Yellow-green (purulent) nasal discharge → bacterial rather than allergic.<br /></br>  – Afebrile/low-grade temp is common in acute bacterial sinusitis (ABS); fever is not required.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives<br /></br>  – No sneezing, itchy eyes, or clear rhinorrhea (allergic rhinitis less likely).<br /></br>  – Oxygen saturation 99 % → no hypoxia, so outpatient Rx is appropriate.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>1. Ask: “Viral, allergic, or bacterial?”<br /></br>2. Use the ABS rule of thumb: symptoms &gt;10 days ± “double-worsening” or severe (high fever, purulence, facial pain) → treat.<br /></br>3. In pregnancy, choose a β-lactam safe for the fetus (amoxicillin-clavulanate).</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise logic<br /></br>1. Chronicity (&gt;10 days) + purulent drainage + sinus tenderness = Acute bacterial sinusitis.<br /></br>2. Guideline-directed first-line therapy for uncomplicated ABS is amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg PO bid for 5–7 days (longer course if severe).<br /></br>3. Pregnancy does not change the need for treatment; it only alters antibiotic selection (avoid doxycycline &amp; fluoroquinolones).</p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>Acute bacterial sinusitis is a clinical diagnosis. When symptoms persist &gt;10 days or are severe, initiate empiric antibiotics—pregnancy is not a contraindication.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls<br /></br>• Assessment: “32 wk G1P0 with acute maxillary bacterial sinusitis.”<br /></br>• Plan: “Start amoxicillin-clavulanate 5–7 d; saline rinses, acetaminophen prn pain. RTC if no improvement in 3–5 d.”</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Viral URI → mucosal edema → obstruction of sinus ostia → impaired drainage → secondary bacterial overgrowth (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, Moraxella catarrhalis).<br /></br>• Hormonal congestion in pregnancy may predispose but does not alter pathophysiology.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Synopsis</br>Diagnostics (usually not needed)<br /></br>• Imaging (CT) only if complications, refractory disease, or red-flag symptoms (orbit/brain involvement).  </p></br><p>Treatment ladder<br /></br>1. First-line: Amoxicillin-clavulanate (safe in pregnancy).<br /></br>2. Penicillin allergy (non-anaphylactic): Cefdinir, cefpodoxime.<br /></br>   Anaphylactic allergy: Azithromycin or clindamycin + TMP-SMX (TMP-SMX only after 1st trimester).<br /></br>3. Adjuncts: saline irrigation, intranasal corticosteroid (e.g., budesonide), acetaminophen.<br /></br>4. Surgery (balloon sinuplasty / FESS) only for chronic (&gt;12 wk) or complicated cases.</p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“10-P-T” rule for sinusitis:<br /></br>10 days, Purulent drainage, Tender sinuses → Treat with antibiotics.<br /></br>Pregnancy? Pick Penicillin (Amoxicillin-Clav).  </p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>A. Allergy testing<br /></br>• Allergic rhinitis presents with clear rhinorrhea, sneezing, itching; lacks facial tenderness &amp; purulent discharge.  </p></br><p>C. Antihistamine therapy<br /></br>• First-line for allergic rhinitis, not for bacterial sinusitis. Would not resolve purulence/facial pain.  </p></br><p>D. Sinus ostial dilation (balloon sinuplasty)<br /></br>• Reserved for chronic rhinosinusitis (&gt;12 wk) unresponsive to maximal medical therapy or with anatomic obstruction.  </p></br><p>E. Reassurance that colds last longer during pregnancy<br /></br>• Viral URIs typically resolve within 1–2 weeks; persistence for a month with classic bacterial features mandates active treatment.  </p></br><p>──────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>Persistent (&gt;10 days) purulent nasal symptoms with facial pain equals acute bacterial sinusitis. Even in late pregnancy, immediate empiric antibiotic therapy—most commonly amoxicillin-clavulanate—is the correct first step.</p>
170. A 72-year-old man comes to the office because of a 2-year history of progressive shortness of breath that worsens with exertion. He also has a 1-year history of intermittent cough productive of whitish sputum, primarily in the morning; there is no blood. He has not had chest pain. He has hypertension and hyperlipidemia. His medications are chlorthalidone, felodipine, potassium chloride, and simvastatin. He has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 55 years. He is 183 cm (6 ft) tall and weighs 73 kg (160 lb); BMI is 22 kg/m2. His temperature is 37 C (98.6 F), pulse is 76/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 128/72 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 91 percent. Breath sounds are decreased bilaterally; the lungs otherwise are clear to auscultation. On cardiac examination, S1 and S2 are normal. A grade 2/6, systolic ejection murmur is heard at the upper right sternal border. There is trace pedal edema bilaterally; there is no cyanosis or clubbing. His hemoglobin concentration is 16 g/dL, and hematocrit is 49 percent. A chest x-ray shows no infiltrates or effusions. An ECG shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. CT scan of the chest</br>b. Echocardiography</br>c. Nuclear cardiac stress scintigraphy</br>d. Pulmonary function testing</br>e. Ventilation-perfusion lung scans</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>D. Pulmonary function testing (spirometry with pre- and post-bronchodilator measurements)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – 72-year-old, 55-pack-year smoker → high risk for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD)<br /></br>  – 2-year progressive exertional dyspnea + 1-year chronic productive “morning” cough of white sputum → classic COPD (chronic bronchitis) pattern<br /></br>  – Normal chest x-ray &amp; ECG → rules out obvious pneumonia, heart failure, large effusion, pericardial disease<br /></br>  – Mild resting hypoxemia (SpO₂ 91%) &amp; relative polycythemia (Hgb 16, Hct 49%) → chronic hypoxic drive<br /></br>  – Decreased breath sounds, no rales/wheezes → emphysematous lung hyperinflation</p></br><p>• Test‐taking strategy<br /></br>  1. For chronic unexplained dyspnea, start with history / PE / CXR / ECG (already done).<br /></br>  2. Next discriminator: pulmonary vs cardiac. In a smoker with classic symptoms and normal cardiac work-up/CXR, obtain spirometry—GOLD standard for COPD diagnosis.<br /></br>  3. Don’t jump to advanced imaging; confirm the most common, treatable cause first.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. What is the most likely disease?       → COPD.<br /></br>2. How is COPD diagnosed?                → Post-bronchodilator spirometry showing FEV1/FVC &lt; 0.70.<br /></br>3. What information does spirometry give? → Confirms obstruction, grades severity, guides treatment (bronchodilator, oxygen, pulmonary rehab).<br /></br>4. Therefore, the next diagnostic step is pulmonary function testing.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Spirometry is mandatory to establish or exclude COPD; history &amp; CXR alone are insufficient.</p></br><p>If this were a real patient, documentation would include: chronic symptoms, 55-pack-year history, normal imaging/ECG, plan for diagnostic spirometry with bronchodilator response.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)<br /></br>• Smoking → airway inflammation &amp; mucous gland hyperplasia (chronic bronchitis) + alveolar wall destruction (emphysema) → airflow limitation.<br /></br>• FEV1 falls; FVC relatively preserved → ↓FEV1/FVC.<br /></br>• V/Q mismatch → chronic hypoxemia → secondary polycythemia.<br /></br>• Hyperinflation → ↓ breath sounds, ↑ AP diameter, flattened diaphragm (may be seen on CT/CXR).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment Pearls<br /></br>Spirometry results expected in COPD:<br /></br>  – FEV1/FVC &lt; 0.70 (post-bronchodilator)<br /></br>  – ↓FEV1; normal or ↓ FVC<br /></br>  – ↓DLCO in emphysema-predominant disease  </p></br><p>After confirmed COPD:<br /></br>  • Smoking cessation (biggest mortality benefit)<br /></br>  • Vaccinations (influenza, pneumococcal)<br /></br>  • Short-acting bronchodilator (albuterol or ipratropium) → escalate per GOLD guidelines<br /></br>  • Pulmonary rehab<br /></br>  • Long-term oxygen therapy if SpO₂ ≤88% or PaO₂ ≤55 mm Hg</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“4-Step Dyspnea Work-up”<br /></br>1. History &amp; PE<br /></br>2. CXR<br /></br>3. ECG<br /></br>4. Spirometry (if smoker/obstructive picture) or Echocardiogram (if cardiac clues)  </p></br><p>Mnemonic for COPD diagnosis: “COPD = COugh + Obstruction Proven by Device (spirometer).”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. CT scan of the chest<br /></br>   • Indicated for interstitial lung disease, bronchiectasis, or lung mass evaluation when CXR is abnormal. Normal CXR + classic COPD symptoms → spirometry first.  </p></br><p>b. Echocardiography<br /></br>   • Used to evaluate structural heart disease, valvular lesions, or unexplained heart failure. The mild 2/6 systolic murmur without heart failure signs and a normal ECG makes cardiac cause less likely as the next step.  </p></br><p>c. Nuclear cardiac stress scintigraphy<br /></br>   • Screens for coronary ischemia when chest pain or equivalent symptoms are present. Exertional dyspnea alone in a known smoker should be evaluated with spirometry before ischemia testing.  </p></br><p>e. Ventilation-perfusion lung scan<br /></br>   • Useful for suspected pulmonary embolism or chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension. No acute pleuritic pain, no risk factors given, and symptoms are chronic—not the best immediate test.  </p></br><p>Common Pitfall: Jumping to advanced imaging before confirming the most probable and common diagnosis (COPD) with simple spirometry.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In a heavy smoker with chronic productive cough and progressive exertional dyspnea, the mandatory next diagnostic step is pulmonary function testing to confirm COPD and guide management.</p>
171. A 62-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 3-hour history of right-sided chest pain. The pain is worse with coughing and deep breathing. She was discharged from rehabilitation 1 month ago after an uncomplicated left knee replacement. Her only medication is acetaminophen for pain. She is 165 cm (5 ft 5 in) tall and weighs 114 kg (252 lb); BMI is 42 kg/m2. Her temperature is 37.5 C (99.5 F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 126/70 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 92 percent. Cardiac examination shows an accentuated S2. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities except for limited range of motion of the left knee. A chest x-ray shows plate-like atelectasis in the right lower lung base. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Serum d-dimer assay</br>b. Gram stain and culture of sputum</br>c. Spiral CT scan</br>d. Bronchoscopy with cytology</br>e. Pulmonary angiography</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c.
