Spiral CT scan (multidetector CT pulmonary angiography, “spiral CTA”)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that scream “possible pulmonary embolism (PE)”<br /></br>• Recent major orthopedic surgery (knee replacement) → very high venous thrombo-embolism risk for at least 3 mo<br /></br>• Obesity (BMI &gt; 40) → additional risk factor<br /></br>• Sudden pleuritic chest pain (worse on inspiration/cough)<br /></br>• Mild hypoxemia (SpO₂ 92 % on RA) and sinus tachycardia (HR 110)<br /></br>• Accentuated S2 (loud P2) → acute pulmonary HTN from PE<br /></br>• Low-grade fever and plate-like atelectasis on CXR—classic but nonspecific PE findings (“normal” or nonspecific CXR in PE)  </p></br><p>Take-home cognitive approach<br /></br>1. Estimate pre-test probability (Wells or gestalt). Recent surgery + tachycardia + no alternate Dx likely → moderate-to-high probability.<br /></br>2. In moderate-to-high probability, go straight to diagnostic imaging (CT pulmonary angiography) unless contraindicated (e.g., severe contrast allergy or renal failure). Do NOT waste time on D-dimer when the pre-test probability is already high.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive pearl: On exams, D-dimer is for LOW pre-test probability. Any major risk factor plus classic symptoms → jump to imaging.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Identify risk: recent orthopedic surgery + obesity.<br /></br>2. Recognize presentation: acute pleuritic pain, tachycardia, hypoxemia, loud P2.<br /></br>3. CXR nonspecific → does not rule out PE.<br /></br>4. Patient is hemodynamically stable (BP 126/70) → next best diagnostic study is CT pulmonary angiography.<br /></br>Core concept: Spiral CT is the first-line, high-sensitivity, high-specificity, minimally invasive imaging for PE in stable patients.  </p></br><p>Documentation note (real life): Record Wells score elements, oxygen sat, hemodynamics, decision to order CTA vs. alternative if contraindicated.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• Virchow triad: venous stasis (post-op immobility), endothelial injury (surgery), hypercoagulability (obesity, postoperative state) → DVT → embolizes to pulmonary arteries → ventilation–perfusion (V/Q) mismatch → hypoxemia, acute pulmonary hypertension → loud P2.<br /></br>• Pleuritic pain from pleural irritation secondary to distal emboli/infarction.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment Snapshot  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Imaging:<br /></br>   – First line: CT pulmonary angiography (spiral CT).<br /></br>   – If CT contraindicated (renal failure, contrast allergy): ventilation-perfusion (V/Q) scan.<br /></br>   – Definitive but invasive/rarely used: pulmonary angiography.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Labs: D-dimer only for low suspicion; nearly useless in high pre-test probability.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Management (once diagnosed or high suspicion):<br /></br>   – Immediate anticoagulation (e.g., heparin drip) while awaiting confirmatory tests if clinical suspicion very high.<br /></br>   – Long-term: transition to direct oral anticoagulant (e.g., apixaban) or warfarin for ≥3 months.<br /></br>   – Consider IVC filter if anticoagulation contraindicated.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“POST-OP + Pleuritic Pain + P2 → PE → go ‘C-T-A’ ”<br /></br>• Picture a spiral staircase (spiral CT) leading from the knee (surgery site) up to the lungs.  </p></br><p>Low prob → “D-dimer;” High prob → “Direct to CT.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Serum D-dimer assay<br /></br>   • Good rule-out test only when pre-test probability is LOW. In moderate/high probability, a negative result cannot reliably exclude PE and delays definitive care.  </p></br><p>b. Gram stain &amp; culture of sputum<br /></br>   • Indicated if pneumonia suspected (productive cough, focal infiltrate, high fever). CXR shows only atelectasis; symptoms are pleuritic without infectious signs.  </p></br><p>d. Bronchoscopy with cytology<br /></br>   • Used to evaluate endobronchial lesions/malignancy or persistent hemoptysis—not for acute pleuritic chest pain in a post-op patient.  </p></br><p>e. Pulmonary angiography<br /></br>   • Historical gold standard but invasive, requires catheterization &amp; contrast. Reserved when CT/VQ findings are nondiagnostic and suspicion remains high. Spiral CT offers similar accuracy with far less risk and is therefore preferred first.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>In a hemodynamically stable patient with moderate-to-high suspicion for pulmonary embolism, the next diagnostic step is CT pulmonary angiography (spiral CT). Reserve D-dimer for low-risk patients and pulmonary angiography for indeterminate cases or when noninvasive imaging is contraindicated.</p>
172. A 37-year-old man comes to the physician for a follow-up examination 3 weeks after being diagnosed with hepatitis C. He has a long-standing history of intravenous heroin use. He is currently receiving methadone therapy for heroin addiction. He has smoked one and a half packs of cigarettes daily for 20 years and drinks seven glasses of wine weekly. Examination shows no abnormalities. Serum assays are positive for hepatitis B antibody and negative for hepatitis B antigen. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation to minimize the progression of liver disease in this patient?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Abstinence from alcohol</br>b. Smoking cessation</br>c. Lamivudine (3TC) therapy</br>d. Discontinue methadone as a treatment option for heroin addiction</br>e. Hepatitis B vaccine</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Abstinence from alcohol  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues: chronic hepatitis C; IV drug use; currently on methadone; 20-pack-year smoking history; drinks “seven glasses of wine weekly”; positive hepatitis B surface antibody (HBsAb) with negative surface antigen (HBsAg) → immune from prior infection or vaccination.<br /></br>• Task: “most appropriate recommendation to minimize the progression of liver disease.” Therefore look for a modifiable factor that directly accelerates hepatic fibrosis in HCV.<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: ongoing alcohol intake. Even moderate alcohol markedly hastens fibrosis, cirrhosis, and risk of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) in chronic HCV.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: no current HBV infection (so HBV antivirals or vaccine not useful for liver-disease progression). Methadone is not hepatotoxic. Smoking’s major harms are pulmonary/cardiovascular, not liver.<br /></br>Metacognitive tip: When NBME asks how to slow chronic hepatitis (B or C) progression, think “eradicate virus” OR “remove synergistic hepatotoxins, especially alcohol.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)<br /></br>1. Identify underlying liver disease: chronic HCV.<br /></br>2. List factors that accelerate fibrosis: alcohol &gt; coinfection (HBV, HIV) &gt; metabolic syndrome, etc.<br /></br>3. Check which factors are present and modifiable: alcohol use (YES).<br /></br>4. Recommend intervention: complete abstinence. Even “light/moderate” use (≥1 drink/day) doubles cirrhosis risk in HCV.<br /></br>Take-home message: In patients with chronic viral hepatitis, the single most impactful lifestyle change is eliminating alcohol.  </p></br><p>Documentation note: “Counseled patient on strict alcohol abstinence to reduce risk of cirrhosis/HCC; offered resources for cessation program.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• HCV alone causes chronic inflammation → progressive fibrosis.<br /></br>• Ethanol metabolism (via ADH/MEOS) ↑ oxidative stress, lipid peroxidation, and cytokine activation, synergistically accelerating collagen deposition.<br /></br>• Alcohol + HCV also ↑ risk of hepatocellular carcinoma beyond additive effect.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>• First-line HCV therapy today = direct-acting antivirals (e.g., sofosbuvir/velpatasvir), but lifestyle modification remains necessary.<br /></br>• Every HCV patient should receive:<br /></br>  – Alcohol abstinence counseling<br /></br>  – HAV &amp; HBV vaccination if non-immune<br /></br>  – Screening for HCC (US q6 mo once cirrhotic)  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids<br /></br>“C-ALCoholic CIRRHOSIS”: C-virus + ALCoho l = CIRRHOSIS fast.<br /></br>Visual: a liver “double-punched” by a green HCV boxing glove and a red wine bottle. Knock out the bottle to save the liver.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Rundown<br /></br>b. Smoking cessation – Important for overall health but has no significant effect on HCV-related fibrosis progression.<br /></br>c. Lamivudine (3TC) – NRTI active against HBV, not HCV; patient lacks active HBV infection.<br /></br>d. Discontinue methadone – Methadone is not hepatotoxic; stopping risks heroin relapse without liver benefit.<br /></br>e. Hepatitis B vaccine – Already immune (HBsAb positive); revaccination unnecessary and offers no additional protection against HCV progression.  </p></br><p>Bottom line: For chronic hepatitis C, eliminating alcohol is the highest-yield, evidence-based recommendation to slow liver disease progression.</p>
173. A 47-year-old woman comes to the physician for a follow-up examination. Two weeks ago, she underwent laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication for Barrett esophagus secondary to gastroesophageal reflux disease. Since the operation, she has had upper abdominal bloating after eating. She also has had a sensation of meat sticking in her esophagus that resolves spontaneously after 1 hour. She has been unable to burp or vomit to relieve her discomfort. She has been following an unrestricted diet. She has not had any changes in bowel habits. Current medications include oxycodone-acetaminophen. She appears well. Her temperature is 36.8 C (98.2 F), pulse is 76/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 116/78 mm Hg. Examination shows well-healing surgical incisions with mild tenderness to palpation. The abdomen is scaphoid with no masses. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Dietary modification</br>b. Abdominal x-rays</br>c. Treatment with a prokinetic agent</br>d. Treatment with a proton pump inhibitor</br>e. Biopsy of the distal esophagus</br>f. Esophageal dilatation</br>g. Esophagomyotomy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Dietary modification  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Timing: Symptoms began shortly after a laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication (2 weeks post-op).<br /></br>• Key complaints:<br /></br>  – Early satiety/upper-abdominal bloating, inability to burp or vomit → classic “gas-bloat” phenomenon after a tight fundoplication.<br /></br>  – Intermittent solid-food dysphagia (“meat sticking” that clears within an hour) → expected transient edema of the wrap.<br /></br>• Exam: Afebrile, normal vitals, scaphoid (not distended) abdomen, mild incisional tenderness, no peritoneal signs.<br /></br>• No red flags: no progressive dysphagia, weight loss, hematemesis, obstipation, or imaging/lab abnormalities.</p></br><p>Test-taking mindset: After any antireflux surgery, mild bloating and short-lived dysphagia are common and usually self-limited. The first management step is conservative—small bites, chew thoroughly, soft diet, avoid carbonated drinks or straws—before pursuing diagnostics or interventions.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Identify pattern → Post-fundoplication gas-bloat + early transient dysphagia.<br /></br>2. Rule out serious complications (wrap migration, perforation, obstruction) by noting benign exam &amp; lack of systemic signs.<br /></br>3. First-line response → noninvasive dietary measures and reassurance.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Initial, mild postoperative symptoms after Nissen are managed conservatively; invasive studies or therapies are reserved for persistent (&gt;4–6 weeks) or progressive problems.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): counsel patient on small, frequent meals; avoid carbonation, gum, straws; chew thoroughly; schedule follow-up in 4 weeks.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Nissen fundoplication wraps the gastric fundus 360° around the distal esophagus to augment lower-esophageal sphincter pressure.<br /></br>• Tight wrap + postoperative edema → gas can’t vent via belching; swallowed air accumulates → bloating/early satiety (“gas-bloat syndrome”).<br /></br>• Edema &amp; transient hypertonicity of the wrap → short-term solid-food dysphagia, typically resolving within 4–6 weeks.<br /></br>Risk factors: Large bites, carbonated beverages, eating too quickly, poor chewing.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Snapshot<br /></br>First-line/acute:<br /></br>• Dietary modification: soft/semiliquid foods, small bites, slow chewing, avoid carbonated drinks and straws, walk after meals.<br /></br>If symptoms persist beyond 4–6 weeks or are severe:<br /></br>• Barium swallow or EGD to evaluate wrap tightness/slippage.<br /></br>• Endoscopic pneumatic dilation for persistent obstructive dysphagia.<br /></br>• Surgical revision rarely needed.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“BELCH” for post-fundoplication advice:<br /></br>B – Bite size small<br /></br>E – Eat slowly<br /></br>L – Liquids (soft/semiliquid diet)<br /></br>C – Carbonation avoided<br /></br>H – High chewing (chew thoroughly)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>b. Abdominal x-rays – Useful for suspected obstruction (distension, obstipation, vomiting). Patient’s abdomen is scaphoid, no vomiting.<br /></br>c. Prokinetic agent (e.g., metoclopramide) – Addresses gastroparesis, not wrap-related gas retention/dysphagia.<br /></br>d. Proton pump inhibitor – GERD prophylaxis unnecessary after an intact Nissen; doesn’t treat gas-bloat or transient dysphagia.<br /></br>e. Biopsy of distal esophagus – For Barrett surveillance; unrelated to acute postoperative symptoms.<br /></br>f. Esophageal dilatation – Consider only if dysphagia is persistent or progressive &gt;4–6 weeks or if imaging shows obstruction. Too early now.<br /></br>g. Esophagomyotomy – Surgical treatment for achalasia, not postoperative gas-bloat/dysphagia.</p></br><p>Common pitfall: Jumping to imaging or dilation before giving the normal postoperative course time to improve on its own.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Mild bloating and brief solid-food dysphagia within the first few weeks after Nissen fundoplication are common and usually improve with simple dietary adjustments; no immediate studies or invasive treatments are required.</p>
174. A 53-year-old man with alcoholism comes to the physician because of a 5-month history of loose stools and persistent swelling of his ankles. He is otherwise asymptomatic. He drinks “at least a pint” of bourbon daily. Vital signs are within normal limits. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. Abdominal examination shows distention and a positive fluid wave. Laboratory studies show:</br>Mean corpuscular volume        108 µm3</br>Serum</br>  Urea nitrogen           7 mg/dL</br>  Creatinine              0.9 mg/dL</br>  Albumin                 2.9 g/dL</br>  Total bilirubin         1.6 mg/dL</br>  AST                     64 U/L</br>  ALT                     74 U/L</br></br>Additional laboratory studies are most likely to show which of the following serum electrolyte concentrations?</br>Na+ (mEq/L)    K+ (mEq/L)	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. 132    3.1</br>b. 132    4.9</br>c. 140    4</br>d. 148    3.1</br>e. 148    4.9</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. 132 mEq/L Na⁺, 3.1 mEq/L K⁺  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• Long-standing heavy alcohol use → risk for cirrhosis.<br /></br>• Ascites (distended abdomen, positive fluid wave) + bilateral ankle edema.<br /></br>• Hypoalbuminemia (2.9 g/dL) → ↓ oncotic pressure, supports chronic liver disease.<br /></br>• Mildly elevated AST/ALT, macrocytosis, low BUN — classic additional liver findings.<br /></br>Taken together these point to decompensated cirrhosis with portal hypertension and third-spacing (ascites + edema).  </p></br><p>Test-taking strategy<br /></br>1. Recognize the organ system: chronic liver disease.<br /></br>2. Ask: what secondary hormonal changes occur? (↓ effective arterial volume → RAAS &amp; ADH activation → dilutional hyponatremia + secondary hyperaldosteronism).<br /></br>3. Pick the answer that shows BOTH low Na⁺ and low K⁺.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic<br /></br>1. Cirrhosis causes splanchnic vasodilation → ↓ effective arterial volume despite total body fluid overload.<br /></br>2. Baroreceptors trigger:<br /></br>   • ↑ Renin–angiotensin–aldosterone system (RAAS) → Na⁺ reabsorption in distal nephron, but at the cost of K⁺ secretion (hypokalemia).<br /></br>   • ↑ Antidiuretic hormone (ADH) → free-water retention that exceeds retained Na⁺ → dilutional hyponatremia.<br /></br>3. The net serum picture:<br /></br>   • Hyponatremia (typically 120-134 mEq/L).<br /></br>   • Hypokalemia (often &lt;3.5 mEq/L).<br /></br>That matches 132/3.1.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>Hypervolemic hyponatremia with hypokalemia is the classic electrolyte pattern in ascitic cirrhotic patients because of secondary hyperaldosteronism and ADH-mediated water retention.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl<br /></br>“Exam consistent with tense ascites and peripheral edema. Suspect cirrhosis with secondary hyperaldosteronism → dilutional hyponatremia &amp; hypokalemia. Plan: diagnostic paracentesis, serum/urine electrolytes, start spironolactone + furosemide.”  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Cirrhosis → portal HTN → splanchnic vasodilation → ↓ effective arterial volume → neurohormonal activation:<br /></br>• RAAS → Aldosterone ↑ → Na⁺ reabsorption/K⁺ wasting.<br /></br>• ADH ↑ → water reabsorption in collecting ducts.<br /></br>• Sympathetic tone ↑ → further renal Na⁺ retention.<br /></br>Total body Na⁺ is high, but total body water rises even more → dilutional hyponatremia.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostics Snapshot  </p></br><p>First-line ascites management:<br /></br>1. Sodium restriction (&lt;2 g/day).<br /></br>2. Diuretics: Spironolactone (100 mg) ± Furosemide (40 mg) ratio 100:40 to maintain K⁺ balance.<br /></br>3. Diagnostic &amp; therapeutic paracentesis.<br /></br>4. Consider TIPS if refractory.<br /></br>Monitor: daily weights, serum Na⁺/K⁺, renal function.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Cirrhosis SALT-ing”:<br /></br>S – Splanchnic vasodilation<br /></br>A – Aldosterone ↑ (Na⁺↑ body, K⁺↓ serum)<br /></br>L – Low serum Na⁺ (dilutional)<br /></br>T – Third-spacing (ascites, edema)  </p></br><p>Visual: Think of a salty (Na⁺) pretzel sitting in a sea (water excess) that washes away K⁺ peanuts → low Na⁺, low K⁺.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. 132 / 4.9 – Hyponatremia with hyperkalemia occurs in adrenal insufficiency or ACE-i/spironolactone therapy, not untreated cirrhosis.<br /></br>c. 140 / 4 – Normal electrolytes would ignore the powerful neurohormonal changes in ascitic cirrhosis.<br /></br>d. 148 / 3.1 – Hypernatremia typically implies free-water loss (diabetes insipidus, GI losses) or hypertonic saline; not seen in cirrhosis with intact thirst and ADH.<br /></br>e. 148 / 4.9 – Hypernatremia + hyperkalemia suggests mineralocorticoid deficiency with free-water loss, again inconsistent with this vignette.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: forgetting that “edematous = volume overload” does NOT mean “hypernatremic.” Effective arterial volume, not total volume, drives aldosterone/ADH physiology.</p>
175. A previously healthy 6-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department because of cramping abdominal pain and right-sided scrotal pain for 4 hours. He vomited once on the way to the hospital. Examination shows a distended abdomen. Bowel sounds are decreased, and there is diffuse tenderness to palpation with involuntary guarding. The right hemiscrotum is slightly discolored with swelling and tenderness superiorly. The left hemiscrotum is normal; the testicle is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: G</br></br>a. Transillumination of scrotum</br>b. Valsalva maneuver</br>c. Urinalysis</br>d. Doppler ultrasonography</br>e. CT scan</br>f. Antibiotic therapy</br>g. Operative procedure</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>g. Operative procedure (urgent surgical exploration and hernia repair)</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that should jump out immediately:</p></br><ol></br><li>Age/sex: 6-year-old boy – highest-risk group for indirect inguinal hernia due to a patent processus vaginalis.  </li></br><li>Dual complaints: abrupt cramping abdominal pain + right-sided scrotal pain.  </li></br><li>Obstruction/peritonitis signs: distended abdomen, ↓ bowel sounds, diffuse tenderness with involuntary guarding (peritoneal irritation).  </li></br><li>Scrotal findings: right hemiscrotum swollen, tender, slightly discolored, maximal tenderness “superiorly” (inguinal canal area) while the left testicle is normal.</li></br></ol></br><p>Pattern recognition: In children, the ONLY common process that simultaneously produces small-bowel obstruction and acute scrotal swelling is an incarcerated indirect inguinal hernia that has descended into the scrotum. Discoloration signals venous congestion → impending/established strangulation → bowel ischemia ± testicular ischemia.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Any test question that pairs (1) acute abdomen, (2) scrotal/inguinal mass, and (3) peritoneal signs should prompt you to stop and choose “take to the OR now.” Imaging delays can cost a bowel segment or a testicle.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)</p></br><ol></br><li>Differential for acute scrotum in a boy: testicular torsion, torsion of appendix testis, epididymitis/orchitis, strangulated hernia, trauma, hydrocele/varicocele.  </li></br><li>Rapidly screen for peritoneal signs and bowel obstruction: distention, vomiting, ↓ sounds, guarding → strongly favor intra-abdominal catastrophe (hernia with trapped bowel).  </li></br><li>Scrotal discoloration + tenderness superiorly = incarcerated hernia at external inguinal ring; not classic for torsion (pain is testicular, cremasteric reflex absent, no peritonitis).  </li></br><li>Once strangulation suspected, definitive management is emergent operative reduction &amp; hernia repair (herniotomy with possible bowel resection). No diagnostic delays are acceptable.</li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home message: Strangulated inguinal hernia = surgical emergency.</p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): time of onset, characteristics of pain, vomiting, abdominal exam, scrotal exam, attempted manual reduction?, vitals, NPO status, IV access, consent discussion, call to surgery.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review</p></br><p>Indirect inguinal hernia<br /></br>• Etiology: patent processus vaginalis allows abdominal contents to protrude through internal inguinal ring, traverse canal, may enter scrotum.<br /></br>• Incarceration: bowel loop cannot be reduced → venous congestion → edema → arterial compromise → ischemia (“strangulation”).<br /></br>• Clinically: pain, vomiting, distention, scrotal swelling/discoloration.<br /></br>• Risk of bowel perforation, sepsis, testicular infarction if delay.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics</p></br><p>Immediate:<br /></br>• NPO, IV fluids, analgesia.<br /></br>• Notify pediatric surgery → operative exploration and repair (herniotomy + high ligation; evaluate bowel viability).<br /></br>• Attempted manual reduction is sometimes tried if no peritonitis; however, presence of guarding + discoloration = do NOT attempt reduction → straight to OR.<br /></br>• Broad-spectrum antibiotics are started intra-op but are adjunct, not definitive.</p></br><p>No imaging (US, CT) when strangulation is clinically obvious—time-critical.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips</p></br><p>“ABCS = Abdomen + Blue (discolored) Scrotum ➔ Surgery.”</p></br><p>Or think: “Red, rigid, and right lower sac → straight to the knife pack.”</p></br><p>Mnemonic “HINT”: Hernia Is Not Torsion if the abdomen is distended.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</p></br><p>a. Transillumination of scrotum – For hydrocele; hydroceles are painless, no abdominal signs, no peritonitis. Would waste time.<br /></br>b. Valsalva maneuver – Used to accentuate a varicocele; varicoceles are chronic, painless “bag of worms,” never cause bowel obstruction.<br /></br>c. Urinalysis – Screens for UTI/epididymitis; these cause gradual pain, fever, urinary symptoms, no peritoneal signs, no obstruction.<br /></br>d. Doppler ultrasonography – First-line for suspected testicular torsion if exam equivocal; but torsion does not cause abdominal distention/guarding. Waiting for imaging risks bowel necrosis.<br /></br>e. CT scan – Could detect obstruction/appendicitis, but radiation + delay; high clinical suspicion of strangulated hernia obviates imaging.<br /></br>f. Antibiotic therapy – Antibiotics alone cannot relieve obstruction or vascular compromise; they are supportive, not definitive.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: A 6-year-old with abdominal obstruction signs plus acutely swollen, discolored hemiscrotum has a strangulated inguinal hernia. Immediate operative intervention is mandatory.</p>
176. A 56-year-old man with claudication is scheduled for angiography of the right femoral artery. He has hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus treated with insulin. He smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 30 years until he stopped 5 years ago. He has no history of allergy to radiopaque contrast agents. He has proteinuria secondary to diabetes. Before angiography, which of the following should be administered to prevent further renal injury?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Captopril  </br>b. Corticosteroids  </br>c. Diphenhydramine  </br>d. Increased dose of insulin  </br>e. 0.9 percent Saline</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. 0.9 percent saline (isotonic intravenous saline)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Upcoming exposure to iodinated contrast (femoral-artery angiography).<br /></br>• Established risk factors for contrast-induced nephropathy (CIN):<br /></br>  – Diabetes mellitus with diabetic kidney disease (proteinuria).<br /></br>  – Possible underlying chronic kidney disease (CKD).<br /></br>  – Age &gt; 50 yr and hypertension add risk.<br /></br>• Goal: choose an intervention given <em>before</em> contrast that actually prevents nephrotoxicity.</p></br><p>Key cognitive steps for similar questions<br /></br>1. Ask, “Is the patient at risk for CIN (CKD, diabetes, volume depletion, CHF)?”<br /></br>2. If yes, think “hydrate with isotonic fluids (± N-acetylcysteine).”<br /></br>3. Separate prophylaxis for <em>allergic</em> contrast reactions (steroids, antihistamines) from <em>renal</em> prophylaxis (saline).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Contrast media cause renal vasoconstriction + direct tubular toxicity → acute tubular necrosis peaking 24–48 h post-procedure. Isotonic saline 1 mL/kg/h started 3–12 h before and continued 6–12 h after:<br /></br>• Expands intravascular volume → maintains renal perfusion.<br /></br>• Dilutes contrast in tubular lumen → less oxidative stress / viscosity injury.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Adequate IV hydration with isotonic saline is the single most proven, first-line measure to prevent CIN.</p></br><p>Documentation note: “Discussed CIN prevention; isotonic saline ordered at 1 mL/kg/h starting 6 h pre-angiography and to continue 6 h post-procedure.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)<br /></br>1. Pathogenesis of CIN<br /></br>   – Renal vasoconstriction (adenosine, endothelin).<br /></br>   – Direct tubular epithelial toxicity; formation of reactive oxygen species.<br /></br>2. Risk factors<br /></br>   – Pre-existing CKD (eGFR &lt; 60 mL/min/1.73 m²).<br /></br>   – Diabetes, age, CHF, hypotension, high contrast volume, nephrotoxic drugs.<br /></br>3. Prevention hierarchy<br /></br>   – Isotonic saline (best evidence).<br /></br>   – Low- or iso-osmolar contrast media.<br /></br>   – N-acetylcysteine or IV sodium bicarbonate (mixed data, adjunctive).<br /></br>   – Hold nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, diuretics, metformin).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Test Pearls<br /></br>• Labs: Baseline serum creatinine; repeat 24–48 h post-procedure.<br /></br>• Drugs routinely held: NSAIDs, loop/thiazide diuretics (to maintain volume), metformin (to avoid lactic acidosis if AKI occurs).<br /></br>• Avoid volume depletion; ensure urine output ≥ 150 mL/h intra-procedure if possible.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Wet kidneys are happy kidneys.”<br /></br>Picture the nephron as a water-slide: more water (saline) lets the contrast dye zip through without sticking and burning the tube.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Captopril (ACE inhibitor)<br /></br>• May <em>worsen</em> GFR acutely by dilating the efferent arteriole → lower filtration pressure—contraindicated around contrast exposure if renal perfusion is marginal.</p></br><p>B. Corticosteroids<br /></br>• Used to prevent <em>allergic</em> (anaphylactoid) contrast reactions, not nephrotoxicity.</p></br><p>C. Diphenhydramine<br /></br>• Same as steroids—histamine blockade for allergic reactions only; no renal protection.</p></br><p>D. Increased dose of insulin<br /></br>• Tight glycemic control does not prevent the immediate hemodynamic/tubular injury of CIN and could precipitate hypoglycemia during NPO status.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>For patients with diabetes or CKD undergoing any iodinated contrast study, begin isotonic IV saline before and continue after the procedure to significantly reduce the risk of contrast-induced nephropathy.</p>
177. A 2-year-old girl is brought to the physician by her father because of a 4-day history of vomiting and diarrhea. The patient has not voided urine during the past 24 hours. She has no history of serious illness and receives no medications. Immunizations are up-to-date. She appears tired but not in distress. Her pulse is 120/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 110/60 mm Hg. Examination shows dry mucous membranes. No other abnormalities are noted. Laboratory studies are most likely to show which of the following findings?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Red blood cell casts</br>b. Serum potassium concentration of 6 mg/dL</br>c. Urine fractional excretion of sodium equal to 0.5 percent</br>d. Urine osmolality of 300 mOsmol/kg H2O</br>e. Urine specific gravity of 1.010</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>C. Urine fractional excretion of sodium (FeNa) equal to 0.5 %</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key clues:</br>  – 4-day history of vomiting + diarrhea → external fluid loss.<br /></br>  – No urine for 24 h, dry mucous membranes, tachycardia → ECF volume depletion.<br /></br>  – Normal blood pressure for age and no edema → not intrinsic or post-renal failure.  </p></br><p>These findings point to pre-renal azotemia (hypovolemia) rather than intrinsic renal disease.</p></br><p>Test-taking approach:<br /></br>1. Ask “Why is the urine output low?” → Look for volume loss, obstruction, or parenchymal disease.<br /></br>2. If volume loss, expect kidneys to avidly conserve Na⁺ and water.<br /></br>3. Anticipate classic lab signature: FeNa &lt; 1 %, urine osm &gt; 500 mOsm/kg, BUN:Cr &gt; 20, high specific gravity (&gt; 1.020).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise logic to correct choice:<br /></br>1. Vomiting/diarrhea ➜ hypovolemia ➜ decreased renal perfusion (pre-renal).<br /></br>2. Intact tubules respond by:</br>   • Maximally reabsorbing Na⁺ (hence FeNa &lt; 1 %).<br /></br>   • Concentrating urine (osmolality high, specific gravity high).<br /></br>3. Among the answer options, only a FeNa of 0.5 % is consistent with that adaptive response.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: On NBME/Step 2, FeNa &lt; 1 % is the single best laboratory clue for pre-renal azotemia.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Record signs of dehydration, urine output, and planned fluid resuscitation.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Pre-renal azotemia = ↓ renal blood flow → ↓ GFR.<br /></br>• Tubular function is intact → Na⁺ and water avidly reabsorbed.<br /></br>• Results:</br>  – FeNa &lt; 1 % (or urine Na⁺ &lt; 20 mEq/L).<br /></br>  – Urine osmolality &gt; 500 mOsm/kg.<br /></br>  – BUN:Cr ratio ≥ 20 because urea is reabsorbed with water.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics</br>1. Diagnostic confirmation: basic metabolic panel, BUN/Cr, urine electrolytes.<br /></br>2. Management cornerstone: isotonic fluid resuscitation (e.g., 20 mL/kg normal saline bolus).<br /></br>3. Monitor urine output and electrolytes; correct K⁺, HCO₃⁻ if needed.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“Pre-renal ≈ P for ‘Pro-sodium reabsorption’”</br>• FeNa Percent: “FeNa PREserves sodium → under One percent.”<br /></br>• Picture a thirsty kidney squeezing every last drop of sodium and water back into the body.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Answers Are Wrong</br>A. Red blood cell casts<br /></br>   – Hallmark of glomerulonephritis (intrinsic renal) with hematuria, edema, often hypertension—none are present.</p></br><p>B. Serum potassium 6 mEq/L<br /></br>   – Severe hyperkalemia typically occurs with intrinsic renal failure or acidosis; pre-renal states usually maintain K⁺ excretion until late.</p></br><p>D. Urine osmolality 300 mOsm/kg<br /></br>   – Isosthenuria (≈ plasma osmolality) signals tubular concentrating defect (acute tubular necrosis, intrinsic renal). Pre-renal urine should be highly concentrated.</p></br><p>E. Urine specific gravity 1.010<br /></br>   – Also reflects isosthenuria/inability to concentrate; seen in intrinsic renal failure or diabetes insipidus, not pre-renal volume loss.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>A dehydrated child with oliguria most commonly has pre-renal azotemia; the kidney’s signature response is a FeNa &lt; 1 %, here 0.5 %.</p>
178. A 29-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 1, at 32 weeks’ gestation is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after cutting her left index finger while cooking. She has been applying pressure, but the bleeding has not stopped. She has no history of serious illness, and her only medication is a prenatal vitamin. Vaccinations were up-to-date at the birth of her first child 14 months ago. The patient’s vital signs, including oxygen saturation, are within normal limits. Examination shows a 2-cm superficial laceration over the left index finger. Fetal heart tones are normal. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. The laceration is sutured. Which of the following is the most appropriate vaccine to administer at this time?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Human papillomavirus</br>b. Inactivated influenza virus</br>c. Measles-mumps-rubella</br>d. Tetanus-diphtheria-acellular pertussis (Tdap)</br>e. No vaccine is indicated at this time</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Tetanus-diphtheria-acellular pertussis (Tdap)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis (How to read the stem)</p></br><ol></br><li>Demographics: 29-year-old G2P1 woman, 32 weeks’ gestation (third trimester).  </li></br><li>Key clinical events: • 2-cm superficial laceration • bleeding controlled • otherwise healthy pregnancy.  </li></br><li>Vaccine history: “Vaccinations were up-to-date at the birth of her first child 14 months ago.”  </li></br><li>Timing: She is now in the 27- to 36-week window recommended for routine maternal Tdap boosting.  </li></br><li>Additional clue: Wound management raises the tetanus-booster question.</li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home pattern: Any pregnant patient who is 27-36 weeks’ gestation should receive Tdap once during EACH pregnancy, independent of prior immunization status. A clean, superficial wound does not need tetanus immunoglobulin, but it does not eliminate the routine prenatal Tdap recommendation.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On test day, first ask, “Is there a pregnancy-specific immunization recommendation?” THEN decide if the wound itself changes anything (&gt;5 y since tetanus booster) — here it does not because the pregnancy recommendation already supersedes it.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</p></br><p>Step 1 – Identify guideline: ACIP and ACOG recommend one dose of Tdap between 27–36 weeks gestation every pregnancy to:</br>• boost maternal immunity</br>• maximize transplacental IgG transfer to protect the neonate from pertussis until primary DTaP series begins at 2 months.</p></br><p>Step 2 – Check contraindications: Tdap is an inactivated vaccine—safe in pregnancy.</p></br><p>Step 3 – Compare alternatives: Inactivated flu vaccine is also safe, but it is not season-specific in the vignette and is less time-critical than the mandated Tdap window.</p></br><p>Clinical takeaway: In pregnancy, Tdap at 27–36 weeks is non-negotiable, regardless of wound status or prior boosters.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls:<br /></br>• “TDAP 0.5 mL IM given to L deltoid, lot #, expiration date.”<br /></br>• Counseling re: arm soreness, fever &lt;101 °F.<br /></br>• Benefits: neonatal pertussis protection.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</p></br><p>• Bordetella pertussis produces pertussis toxin causing severe coughing paroxysms, apnea, and potentially fatal leukocytosis in neonates.<br /></br>• Maternal Tdap raises anti-pertussis antibody titers that cross the placenta (IgG), peaking when given in late 2nd / early 3rd trimester.</p></br><p>Tetanus &amp; diphtheria components merely refresh waning immunity; the main public-health goal is pertussis prevention.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment / Diagnostic Tests Review</p></br><p>Vaccines recommended in pregnancy:</br>• Inactivated influenza (any trimester, seasonal)</br>• Tdap (27-36 wk EACH pregnancy)</br>• Rho(D) immune globulin as indicated (not a vaccine but test favorite)</p></br><p>Contraindicated live vaccines: MMR, varicella, live-attenuated influenza, HPV, zoster.</p></br><p>Wound prophylaxis algorithm:</br>• Clean minor wound + ≥3 prior tetanus doses: give booster if &gt;10 y.<br /></br>• Dirty wound + ≥3 doses: give booster if &gt;5 y.<br /></br>Here she is 14 months from last dose, so no tetanus need for the wound. Pregnancy indication still stands.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Memory Aids</p></br><p>“Mom’s 2-step shot schedule”:<br /></br>1 = Flu any time (if it’s in season)<br /></br>2 = Tdap at 2-thirds along (27–36 wk)</p></br><p>“TDap for the Third Trimester” → ‘T’ for Thirty weeks.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</p></br><p>A. Human papillomavirus (HPV)<br /></br>• Not recommended during pregnancy; safety data insufficient. Would be deferred until postpartum.</p></br><p>B. Inactivated influenza vaccine<br /></br>• Safe and recommended during influenza season, but stem gives no timing cue. Even if flu season, Tdap is still specifically mandated in the 27–36 wk window; the question asks for the single “most appropriate” vaccine now.</p></br><p>C. Measles-mumps-rubella (MMR)<br /></br>• Live-attenuated; contraindicated in pregnancy due to theoretical risk of fetal infection. Given postpartum if non-immune.</p></br><p>E. No vaccine indicated<br /></br>• Ignores ACIP/ACOG guideline that Tdap is required every pregnancy regardless of prior status.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</p></br><p>Give Tdap once between 27 and 36 weeks gestation in EVERY pregnancy—yes, even if the last booster was last year—and a superficial cut doesn’t change that.</p>
179. A 30-year-old primigravid woman at 26 weeks’ gestation comes to the office for a routine prenatal visit. Her pregnancy has been complicated by first-trimester vaginal bleeding. She is otherwise healthy and takes no medications. Her pulse is 90/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 100/72 mm Hg. Examination shows a fundal height of 27 cm. The fetal heart rate is 150/min. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hematocrit                  33 percent</br>Leukocyte count             9500/mm^3</br>Platelet count              220,000/mm^3</br>Blood group                 O, Rh-negative</br>1-Hour glucose tolerance test    120 mg/dL (N<135)</br>Serum anti-D antibody screening  positive 1:8</br>The blood group of the fetus’s father is unknown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Duffy sensitization</br>b. Folic acid deficiency</br>c. Gestational diabetes</br>d. Iron deficiency anemia</br>e. Rh isoimmunization</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Rh isoimmunization  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Maternal blood type: O, Rh-negative → immediately raises concern for Rh (D) alloimmunization.  </li></br><li>Anti-D antibody screen: positive at a titer of 1:8 → proves the mother has already formed IgG antibodies against the Rh-D antigen (sensitized). A clinically significant titer is ≥1:4.  </li></br><li>First-trimester vaginal bleeding → common event that can expose maternal circulation to fetal erythrocytes, initiating sensitization if Rho(D) immune globulin (RhIG) was not given.  </li></br><li>Father’s Rh status unknown → fetus could be Rh-positive, so maternal antibodies are relevant.  </li></br><li>Normal glucose challenge, normal platelet/leukocyte counts, mild normocytic anemia (Hct 33%) → no evidence for folate deficiency, iron deficiency severe enough to explain the vignette, or gestational diabetes.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: The moment you see an Rh-negative pregnant patient, scan the question for “antibody screen” results. A positive anti-D screen = Rh isoimmunization until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Identify maternal Rh status.<br /></br>2. Look for events causing feto-maternal hemorrhage (bleeding, procedures, trauma, delivery).<br /></br>3. Check antibody screen titer.<br /></br>4. If positive → diagnosis is Rh isoimmunization (aka Rh alloimmunization).  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: A positive anti-D antibody titer in an Rh-negative gravida = sensitization. Future/fetal management shifts from prophylaxis (RhIG) to surveillance for hemolytic disease of the fetus/newborn (HDFN).</p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): Record Rh status, anti-D titer, any prior RhIG, plan for serial titers &amp; possible middle-cerebral-artery Doppler surveillance of fetus.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Rh-negative individuals lack the D antigen on RBCs.<br /></br>• Exposure to Rh-positive RBCs (prior pregnancy, transfusion, procedures, bleeding) → maternal B-cells produce anti-D IgG.<br /></br>• In subsequent pregnancies with an Rh-positive fetus, IgG crosses the placenta and opsonizes fetal RBCs → extravascular hemolysis, anemia, hydrops fetalis, neonatal jaundice/kernicterus.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Management Pearls  </p></br><p>Already sensitized: RhIG no longer helpful (RhIG works only prophylactically by clearing fetal RBCs before maternal immune recognition).<br /></br>1. Serial anti-D titers every 4 weeks until 24 wks, then every 2 wks.<br /></br>2. Critical titer (often 1:16) → middle-cerebral-artery (MCA) Doppler for fetal anemia; if elevated, consider intrauterine transfusion or early delivery.  </p></br><p>Prophylaxis (for non-sensitized mothers):<br /></br>• 300 µg RhIG IM at 28 wks and within 72 h postpartum if infant Rh-positive.<br /></br>• Additional doses after any bleeding, amniocentesis, CVS, trauma, etc.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Rh = Remember Hemolysis” – If antibody screen +, think hemolytic disease.<br /></br>“28-after-8” – Give RhIG at 28 wks and within ≤72 h after delivery.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Duffy sensitization (anti-Fyᴀ/Fyʙ)<br /></br>• Rare cause of HDFN, antibody screen would specify anti-Fya not anti-D.<br /></br>• The question explicitly states anti-D positivity.</p></br><p>b. Folic acid deficiency<br /></br>• Causes macrocytic anemia (MCV &gt; 100 fL), often with low retic count; not suggested here. No RBC indices or risk factors (poor diet, malabsorption).  </p></br><p>c. Gestational diabetes<br /></br>• 1-hour 50-g glucose challenge cutoff is ≥135–140 mg/dL. Value is 120 mg/dL (normal).  </p></br><p>d. Iron deficiency anemia<br /></br>• Would show microcytic, hypochromic indices, low ferritin. Mild normocytic anemia here (Hct 33%) is common in pregnancy (“physiologic anemia of pregnancy”). No supportive labs for iron deficiency given.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>An Rh-negative pregnant woman with a positive anti-D antibody screen (titer ≥1:4) has Rh isoimmunization. RhIG is no longer protective; the pregnancy now requires serial titers and fetal anemia surveillance to prevent hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn.</p>
180. A 37-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 1, at 15 weeks’ gestation comes to the physician for her first prenatal visit. She has felt well except for mild nausea for the past 4 weeks. She delivered a healthy newborn 5 years ago; pregnancy and delivery were uncomplicated. She has no history of serious illness or operative procedures. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. The uterus is consistent in size with a 13-week gestation. The patient inquires about amniocentesis but is concerned about any adverse effects. If this patient undergoes amniocentesis, she is at increased risk for which of the following complications?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Abruptio placentae</br>b. Meconium in the amniotic fluid</br>c. Polyhydramnios</br>d. Premature rupture of membranes</br>e. Uterine rupture</br></br></br><p>Correct answer: d. Premature rupture of membranes (PROM)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Gestational age: 15 weeks. This is precisely when diagnostic amniocentesis (15–20 wk) is typically offered for genetic testing.<br /></br>• Procedure requested: amniocentesis → an invasive trans-abdominal needle puncture of the amniotic sac.<br /></br>• Key test-taking clue: “concerned about any adverse effects.” Question asks for the most likely complication specifically attributable to the procedure itself.</p></br><p>Cognitive approach for similar questions:</br>1. Lock in the procedure being discussed (ex: chorionic-villus sampling vs amniocentesis vs percutaneous umbilical blood sampling).<br /></br>2. Recall the unique timing window and the canonical complication profile for that procedure.<br /></br>3. Eliminate distractors that are obstetric complications unrelated to needle entry into the membranes.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Amniocentesis requires puncturing both the uterine wall and amniotic membranes. This iatrogenic membrane defect can:</br>• allow amniotic fluid to leak externally or into the decidua<br /></br>• predispose to premature rupture of membranes (PROM) and downstream sequelae (preterm labor, chorioamnionitis, oligohydramnios).  </p></br><p>Therefore, PROM is the best answer.</p></br><p>Take-home: The most common clinically significant complication after second-trimester amniocentesis is PROM (≈1–2%); the overall pregnancy-loss rate is ≈0.1–0.3 %.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: If performing amniocentesis, the consent note must include counseling on PROM, infection, fetal loss, and needle-related trauma.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Needle entry → small defect in amniotic membranes ± decidua.<br /></br>• Normally sealed by fibrin, but if not, leakage continues → loss of fluid → PROM.<br /></br>Risk factors that increase leak persistence: low membrane collagen, infection, multiple punctures, large-bore needle.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Pearls</br>• No lab test before the procedure predicts PROM; it is a recognized inherent risk.<br /></br>• If PROM occurs in the 2nd trimester:<br /></br>  – Confirm with sterile speculum exam, nitrazine/ferning, and ultrasound for AFI (amniotic fluid index).<br /></br>  – Management balances gestational age, infection risk, and fetal lung maturity (expectant management with latency antibiotics, corticosteroids once viability approaches, deliver if chorioamnionitis).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“ABC – Amnio Breaks the Cover”:<br /></br>A = Amniocentesis<br /></br>B = Breaks (ruptures)<br /></br>C = Cover (chorio-amniotic membranes) → PROM<br /></br>Visualize a balloon (sac) being pricked by a needle releasing water.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>a. Abruptio placentae – Premature placental separation is related to trauma, hypertension, cocaine, smoking; not a direct consequence of piercing the membranes with a fine needle. Incidence is not increased after amniocentesis.</p></br><p>b. Meconium in the amniotic fluid – Occurs with fetal hypoxia/distress, usually ≥34 wk or during labor. Amniocentesis at 15 wk does not precipitate fetal passage of meconium.</p></br><p>c. Polyhydramnios – Excess fluid results from fetal GI obstruction, anencephaly, diabetes, etc. Amniocentesis removes (doesn’t add) fluid and is not a cause of polyhydramnios.</p></br><p>e. Uterine rupture – Catastrophic tearing of the myometrium, typically during labor in a scarred uterus (previous C-section). A 22-gauge needle puncture does not predispose to uterine rupture.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Core “Shelf” Message</br>Second-trimester amniocentesis = diagnostic + small but real risk of:<br /></br>• PROM (biggest testable complication)<br /></br>• Chorioamnionitis<br /></br>• Fetal-maternal hemorrhage/alloimmunization<br /></br>• Pregnancy loss (~0.25 %)  </p></br><p>Know these associations cold for OB/GYN shelf and Step 2 CK.</p>
181. A 49-year-old nulligravid woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus comes to the office because of an 18-month history of increasingly heavy irregular menstrual periods. Today, she reports vaginal spotting. Menses occur at 10- to 12-day intervals and last 5 to 7 days; she has to change a regular tampon every 4 to 6 hours. She is 170 cm (5 ft 7 in) tall and weighs 104 kg (230 lb); BMI is 36 kg/m2. Pelvic examination shows a slightly enlarged uterus without adnexal masses. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Complete blood count</br>b. CT scan of the pelvis</br>c. Determination of hemoglobin A1c</br>d. Endometrial biopsy</br>e. Measurement of serum progesterone concentration</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>D. Endometrial biopsy  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key diagnostic clues<br /></br>• Age 49 (perimenopausal)<br /></br>• Nulligravid, obesity (BMI 36 kg/m²), type 2 diabetes → classic risk factors for chronic anovulation and unopposed estrogen<br /></br>• 18 months of heavy, irregular bleeding + current intermenstrual spotting<br /></br>• Slightly enlarged but otherwise normal-feeling uterus (no fibroid–size masses)  </p></br><p>Why these words matter<br /></br>1. “Heavy irregular bleeding” lasting &gt;6 months in a woman ≥45 y → abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB) that must be evaluated for endometrial hyperplasia/cancer.<br /></br>2. Risk-factor stacking (obesity, diabetes, nulliparity) markedly raises pre-test probability of endometrial pathology, so tissue sampling is mandatory.<br /></br>3. Uterus only “slightly enlarged” → makes leiomyoma or pregnancy less likely, pushing suspicion toward endometrium.  </p></br><p>Cognitive road map for AUB questions<br /></br>Step 1: Determine age (&lt;45 vs ≥45).<br /></br>Step 2: Look for high-risk features (obesity, PCOS, chronic anovulation, tamoxifen, Lynch).<br /></br>Step 3: If either Step 1 or Step 2 is positive → go straight to endometrial sampling (biopsy &gt; transvaginal ultrasound for definitive diagnosis).  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Goal: Identify/​exclude endometrial hyperplasia or carcinoma.<br /></br>• The only way to get a definitive answer is to sample the tissue.<br /></br>• Endometrial biopsy can be done in the office, is low-risk, and rapidly guides management (progestin therapy vs hysterectomy).  </p></br><p>Key takeaway<br /></br>Any woman ≥45 y OR any younger woman with risk factors who presents with abnormal uterine bleeding → obtain an endometrial biopsy. “45 or risk? Grab the pipelle.”  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real-world)<br /></br>• History: duration, quantity, risk factors for unopposed estrogen, meds.<br /></br>• Physical: BMI, pelvic exam findings.<br /></br>• Plan: Discuss concern for endometrial pathology; consent for office biopsy.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Chronic anovulation → continuous estrogen stimulation without luteal-phase progesterone → endometrial proliferation → hyperplasia → potential malignant transformation.<br /></br>Major risk factors: prolonged anovulation (PCOS, obesity), unopposed estrogen therapy, early menarche/late menopause, diabetes, hypertension, nulliparity, Lynch syndrome.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Overview  </p></br><p>Diagnosis<br /></br>• Office endometrial biopsy (first-line). Sensitivity ~90–95 % for carcinoma.<br /></br>• If insufficient sample or biopsy negative but bleeding persists → hysteroscopy-directed D&amp;C ± polypectomy.<br /></br>• Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) measuring stripe ≤4 mm is acceptable screen in post-menopause, but biopsy still preferred in high-risk perimenopausal women.  </p></br><p>Treatment after results<br /></br>• Hyperplasia w/o atypia → cyclic or continuous high-dose progestin (oral medroxyprogesterone, levonorgestrel IUD).<br /></br>• Atypical hyperplasia or carcinoma → hysterectomy (± BSO, staging).  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic: “BIOPSY”<br /></br>B – Bleeding abnormal<br /></br>I – &gt;45 y or risk factors present<br /></br>O – Obesity, anOvulation, diabetes<br /></br>P – Pipelle (office sampler)<br /></br>S – Sample endometrium<br /></br>Y – Yes, every time!  </p></br><p>Visualization: Picture a red calendar at age “45” with a biopsy pipelle piercing through—anything ≥45 with red flags → sample.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Complete blood count<br /></br>• Helpful to gauge anemia but does not identify the source or etiology of bleeding. Would miss hyperplasia/cancer.  </p></br><p>B. CT scan of the pelvis<br /></br>• Poor resolution for endometrial lining; unnecessary radiation and cost. Imaging of choice, if any, would be transvaginal ultrasound, not CT.  </p></br><p>C. Hemoglobin A1c<br /></br>• Necessary for diabetes management, but glycemic control has no diagnostic value for her abnormal uterine bleeding.  </p></br><p>E. Measurement of serum progesterone concentration<br /></br>• Mid-luteal progesterone helps confirm ovulation in infertility workup, not indicated for heavy bleeding evaluation, and would not rule out malignancy.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: ordering labs/imaging before sampling in high-risk AUB patients wastes time and can delay cancer diagnosis.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Take-Home<br /></br>Abnormal uterine bleeding in any woman ≥45 y OR in younger women with strong estrogen-related risk factors mandates an endometrial biopsy as the first diagnostic step.</p>
182. A 27-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of decreased bleeding with menses. She is otherwise asymptomatic. Menses occur at regular 28-day intervals and last for 3 days with light spotting. Her only medication is an oral contraceptive for the past 5 years. Physical examination, including pelvic examination, shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Reassurance</br>b. Measurement of serum prolactin concentration</br>c. Progesterone challenge test</br>d. Pelvic ultrasonography</br>e. Estrogen supplementation</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer: a. Reassurance  </p></br><hr /></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – 27-year-old woman (reproductive age).<br /></br>  – 6-month history of decreased bleeding, but menses remain clock-work regular every 28 days.<br /></br>  – Bleeding now lasts only 3 days and is light “spotting.”<br /></br>  – Using a combined oral contraceptive pill (OCP) for the past 5 years.<br /></br>  – No systemic or pelvic symptoms; normal physical and pelvic examination.  </p></br><p>• Pertinent positives: Low-dose OCP use, completely regular cycles, otherwise healthy.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: No amenorrhea, no galactorrhea, hirsutism, pelvic pain, infertility, or systemic illness—makes endocrine or structural pathology unlikely.<br /></br>• Pattern recognition: Light, predictable withdrawal bleeding while on OCP = very common, benign OCP side effect due to endometrial atrophy.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When a vignette describes a new menstrual characteristic, first decide if the overall cycle pattern (frequency, regularity) is preserved. If it is, think “physiologic variation or medication effect” before launching into a work-up.</p></br><hr /></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1: Is this abnormal bleeding?<br /></br>• Hypomenorrhea (light flow) with preserved cyclicity is usually benign, especially on contraceptives.  </p></br><p>Step 2: Could it be pathologic?<br /></br>• Pathologies that decrease flow (Asherman, hypothalamic amenorrhea, prolactinoma) typically disrupt cycle regularity or cause complete amenorrhea.<br /></br>• Normal exam + medication known to cause symptom = adverse effect, not disease.  </p></br><p>Step 3: Management<br /></br>• No red-flag symptoms, no fertility desire issues, patient is asymptomatic. → Provide reassurance and education; no tests or treatment needed.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Low-dose combination OCPs thin the endometrium, leading to lighter, shorter, or occasionally absent withdrawal bleeding. This is expected and safe.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: “Counseled patient that decreased menstrual flow is a common effect of combined oral contraceptives; no further evaluation indicated at this time. Patient reassured and advised to return if cycle interval changes or systemic symptoms develop.”</p></br><hr /></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Combined OCPs supply steady estrogen + progestin → suppress FSH/LH → anovulation.<br /></br>• Continuous progestin exposure causes endometrial atrophy; estrogen dose is deliberately low (20–35 µg ethinyl estradiol).<br /></br>• Result: thin endometrium → minimal withdrawal bleed during the placebo week.  </p></br><hr /></br><p>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review<br /></br>Not needed. If the patient desired heavier menses, switching to a higher-dose estrogen pill or stopping OCPs would restore flow, but this is elective, not medically indicated.</p></br><hr /></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“OCP = Only a Cupful of Period.” (The pill shrinks the period like shrinking a cup of coffee.)<br /></br>Or: “Thin pill, thin lining, thin bleed.”</p></br><hr /></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>b. Serum prolactin concentration<br /></br>   • Indicated for secondary amenorrhea or galactorrhea. This patient menstruates regularly and has no prolactin-related symptoms.  </p></br><p>c. Progesterone challenge test<br /></br>   • Used to evaluate amenorrhea by testing for estrogen-primed endometrium. Not necessary when menses are present and predictable.  </p></br><p>d. Pelvic ultrasonography<br /></br>   • Helpful for structural causes (fibroids, polyps) that usually cause heavy or irregular bleeding, not lighter flow. Normal exam makes pathology unlikely.  </p></br><p>e. Estrogen supplementation<br /></br>   • Reserved for hypoestrogenic states (premature ovarian insufficiency, menopause) or specific bone-protection indications. Giving extra estrogen solely to make periods heavier exposes the patient to unnecessary VTE risk.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: Ordering a full amenorrhea work-up for any change in bleeding volume. First verify whether cycles remain regular and look for obvious explanatory factors (eg, OCPs, pregnancy, lactation, low BMI, stress).</p>
183. A 27-year-old man comes to the physician for a routine follow-up examination. He has a 22-year history of type 1 diabetes mellitus controlled with daily insulin. He measures his blood glucose concentration once weekly. He has not developed diabetic complications. He does not smoke or drink alcohol. He has an active lifestyle. His pulse is 87/min, and blood pressure is 120/78 mm Hg. Cardiac examination shows no abnormalities. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk and symmetric. His hemoglobin A1c is 6.5 percent. Which of the following is the most appropriate screening recommendation at this time?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Serum lipid studies</br>b. Liver function tests</br>c. Electromyography of the lower extremities</br>d. ECG</br>e. Ultrasonography of the pancreas</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Serum lipid studies  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• Type 1 diabetes mellitus (T1DM) for 22 years → long-standing endocrine disease with well-known vascular risk.<br /></br>• Age 27 y, HbA1c 6.5 % → adequate glycemic control but still carries diabetes-related atherosclerotic risk.<br /></br>• No current end-organ complications, normal exam → time for guideline-directed preventive screening.  </p></br><p>Testing-strategy pearl<br /></br>When an NBME question gives you a patient with diabetes who is otherwise “doing great,” ask yourself, “Which evidence-based screen have I not yet done?” For any diabetic patient ≥2 years old, lipid profile at baseline and q5 y (or more often if abnormal) is recommended by the American Diabetes Association (ADA). Among the answer options, only serum lipid studies fulfill a guideline-mandated, routine, asymptomatic screening test.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why answer A)  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Identify guideline set → ADA adult-diabetes health maintenance.<br /></br>2. Compare patient’s current data with guideline items:<br /></br>   • HbA1c – already monitored.<br /></br>   • BP – at goal.<br /></br>   • Nephropathy &amp; retinopathy – not asked in choices.<br /></br>   • Lipid panel – not yet mentioned.<br /></br>3. Determine recommended interval → baseline at diagnosis and at least every 5 years if &lt;40 y.<br /></br>4. Select “Serum lipid studies” as the only answer matching an ADA-endorsed screening recommendation.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>All patients with diabetes require periodic fasting lipid profile (baseline and at least q5 years if &lt;40 y; annually if ≥40 y or if abnormalities/therapy present) to stratify cardiovascular risk and determine need for statin therapy.  </p></br><p>Documentation note<br /></br>“Initiated preventive care: ordered fasting lipid panel per ADA guidelines to assess ASCVD risk; will counsel regarding statin initiation once results available.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Diabetes accelerates atherosclerosis via chronic hyperglycemia → endothelial dysfunction, oxidized LDL, and smooth-muscle proliferation. Because macrovascular disease is the #1 cause of morbidity/mortality in diabetics, lipid screening is critical even when glycemic control is excellent.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Snapshot  </p></br><p>First-line prevention if LDL ≥70 mg/dL or age ≥40 y:<br /></br>• Moderate- to high-intensity statin (e.g., atorvastatin 40–80 mg).<br /></br>Laboratory follow-up:<br /></br>• Re-check lipid profile 4–12 weeks after statin start, then q3–12 months.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tip  </p></br><p>“ABC’s for every diabetic visit”<br /></br>A – A1c &lt;7 % (q3 mo)<br /></br>B – Blood pressure (every visit)<br /></br>C – Cholesterol (fasting lipids baseline, q5 y if &lt;40 y, yearly if ≥40 y)<br /></br>D – Dilated eye exam (yearly after 5 y T1DM)<br /></br>E – eGFR &amp; urine albumin (yearly)<br /></br>F – Foot exam (every visit; monofilament yearly)  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong  </p></br><p>b. Liver function tests – Not a routine screen in asymptomatic diabetics. Only indicated before initiating hepatotoxic drugs (e.g., statins) or if hepatic disease suspected.  </p></br><p>c. Electromyography of the lower extremities – Diabetic neuropathy screening is clinical (monofilament/vibration), not EMG. EMG reserved for atypical or focal neuropathies.  </p></br><p>d. ECG – Universal resting ECG is not recommended in asymptomatic low-risk adults; do it only if cardiovascular symptoms, long-standing hypertension, or planned exercise program in high-risk patient.  </p></br><p>e. Ultrasonography of the pancreas – No guideline recommends screening for pancreatic disease in T1DM unless symptoms or genetic syndromes.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall<br /></br>Students often over-select high-tech tests (ECG, EMG) and forget simple, guideline-driven labs (lipid panel, urine microalbumin). Always think “greatest evidence yield for least invasiveness.”</p>
184. A 4-year-old boy is brought to the physician 3 hours after his mother removed a tick from his scalp. She said that he was playing outside this morning. She washes his hair nightly and did not notice the tick last night. He has had no symptoms. He has no history of serious illness. He appears well developed and well nourished and is in no acute distress. Examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Intramuscular ceftriaxone prophylaxis now</br>b. Measurement of serum Lyme (Borrelia burgdorferi) antibody concentration now and again in 2 weeks</br>c. Oral amoxicillin prophylaxis now</br>d. Oral doxycycline prophylaxis now</br>e. Reassurance and follow-up examination in 2 weeks</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>E — Reassurance and follow-up examination in 2 weeks  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• “Removed a tick from his scalp … washes his hair nightly and did not notice the tick last night.”<br /></br>  – Attachment time is almost certainly &lt; 24 h; risk of Lyme transmission is extremely low.<br /></br>• “4-year-old boy”<br /></br>  – Under 8 y; tetracyclines (doxycycline) are usually avoided for prophylaxis.<br /></br>• “No symptoms … exam normal”<br /></br>  – No evidence of early localized Lyme (erythema migrans), RMSF, anaplasmosis, etc.  </p></br><p>Cognitive/metacognitive approach<br /></br>1. Any question about post-tick-bite prophylaxis: immediately run through the five CDC criteria for SINGLE-DOSE DOXYCYCLINE.<br /></br>2. If one criterion is missing → No prophylaxis.<br /></br>3. Remember: No other antibiotic (amoxicillin, ceftriaxone) has proven benefit as prophylaxis.</p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>CDC/IDSA criteria for Lyme prophylaxis<br /></br>1. Ixodes scapularis tick (or I. pacificus) can be identified.<br /></br>2. Tick attached ≥ 36 h (engorged or time known).<br /></br>3. Prophylaxis started ≤ 72 h after tick removal.<br /></br>4. Local B. burgdorferi rate ≥ 20 %.<br /></br>5. Patient ≥ 8 y (not pregnant or lactating).  </p></br><p>This patient fails #2 (short attachment) and #5 (age &lt; 8 y). Therefore the correct management is simple reassurance and routine follow-up. Document counseling on signs of erythema migrans, fever, fatigue, and when to return.</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Borrelia burgdorferi resides in the tick midgut; takes ~36–48 h of feeding to migrate to the salivary glands and be transmitted.<br /></br>• Early serology is useless (&lt; 3 weeks) because antibodies have not yet formed.<br /></br>• Prophylactic doxycycline (200 mg single dose, weight-based in kids ≥ 8 y) reduces Lyme incidence by ~87 %. No comparable evidence exists for other antibiotics.</p></br><p>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>First-line prophylaxis (if all criteria met): Single oral doxycycline (adult 200 mg; children ≥ 8 y: 4 mg/kg up to 200 mg).<br /></br>If symptoms later develop (erythema migrans): 10-day doxycycline (≥ 8 y) or 14-day amoxicillin/cefuroxime (&lt; 8 y).<br /></br>Routine serology only if compatible clinical picture arises.</p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“DOXY” mnemonic for Lyme prophylaxis:<br /></br>D – Duration ≥ 36 h<br /></br>O – Older than 8 y<br /></br>X – X’ed mark of Ixodes tick &amp; high endemicity<br /></br>Y – Within 72 h (Yes, give dose)  </p></br><p>If any letter missing → No doxy.</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. IM ceftriaxone prophylaxis now<br /></br>   – No evidence it prevents Lyme; reserved for neuroborreliosis/carditis treatment.<br /></br>b. Acute &amp; convalescent Lyme serology<br /></br>   – Early test will be negative; unnecessary cost and anxiety.<br /></br>c. Oral amoxicillin prophylaxis now<br /></br>   – Not evidence-based; would overtreat most patients and promote resistance.<br /></br>d. Oral doxycycline prophylaxis now<br /></br>   – Effective only if criteria met and in patients ≥ 8 y; contraindicated in this 4-year-old.<br /></br>e. Reassurance &amp; follow-up (correct)<br /></br>   – Meets guideline recommendations; avoids unnecessary antibiotics and testing.</p></br><p>Key Takeaway<br /></br>Antibiotic prophylaxis for a tick bite is indicated ONLY when all guideline criteria are satisfied; otherwise, provide reassurance and educate on early Lyme signs.</p>
185. A 67-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 10-day history of left buttock pain that radiates down her left leg. She describes the pain as shooting and burning. She has a 2-year history of multiple myeloma poorly responsive to chemotherapy. Treatment with oxycodone-acetaminophen (two tablets every 6 hours) has not completely relieved her pain; she rates it as a 5 on a 10-point scale. She lives alone and can perform activities of daily living independently. Her temperature is 37 C (98.6 F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 125/85 mm Hg. Examination shows no tenderness to palpation over the lower spine. There is hyperesthesia to light touch over the left thigh. Muscle strength and sensation are intact. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Increase oral oxycodone-acetaminophen to four tablets every 6 hours</br>b. Add oral hydrocodone-acetaminophen every 4 hours to the regimen</br>c. Add oral meperidine every 6 hours to the regimen</br>d. Add oral naproxen every 12 hours to the regimen</br>e. Add oral nortriptyline at bedtime to the regimen</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>E. Add oral nortriptyline at bedtime to the regimen</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key phrases: “shooting and burning pain,” “radiates down her left leg,” “hyperesthesia to light touch,” and “muscle strength and sensation are intact.”<br /></br>  – Together they describe neuropathic (radicular) pain rather than pure nociceptive, inflammatory, or bone pain.<br /></br>• Current therapy: oxycodone-acetaminophen 2 tabs q6 h → an opioid already at a moderate dose. Pain is only partially controlled (5/10).<br /></br>• Functional status: lives alone, independent ADLs → goal is additive benefit without excessive sedation or delirium.<br /></br>• Cognitive approach: After confirming neuropathic qualities, recall the WHO cancer-pain ladder ─ if pain is incompletely controlled with an opioid, add an adjuvant specific to the pain subtype (e.g., TCA, SNRI, gabapentin) before simply escalating opioid dose.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When you see “burning, shooting, electric shock-like” on NBME/Step questions, immediately think “neuropathic pain → add adjuvant (TCA, SNRI, gabapentin, carbamazepine) rather than just ‘more opioid’.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>1. Identify pain type: descriptors + hyperesthesia = neuropathic.<br /></br>2. Current opioid provides partial relief. Next step per NCCN/WHO guidelines → add adjuvant specific for neuropathic pain.<br /></br>3. Nortriptyline (a tricyclic antidepressant) is a first-line oral adjuvant for cancer-related neuropathic pain; once-daily dosing at bedtime minimizes daytime anticholinergic effects and sedation.<br /></br>Core takeaway: In cancer patients, neuropathic pain is best managed by adding a neuropathic adjuvant rather than simply increasing opioid dose.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: record pain character/intensity, current opioid dose, functional impact, plan to initiate nortriptyline 25 mg qHS with follow-up for efficacy and side effects (dry mouth, constipation, orthostasis).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Neuropathic pain = abnormal somatosensory processing due to nerve injury (e.g., myeloma plagues bone → vertebral collapse/nerve root compression).<br /></br>• TCAs inhibit reuptake of serotonin &amp; norepinephrine and block sodium channels, dampening ectopic neuronal firing → analgesia independent of mood effects.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls</br>First-line neuropathic adjuvants in cancer:</br>• TCAs (nortriptyline, amitriptyline)<br /></br>• SNRIs (duloxetine, venlafaxine)<br /></br>• Anticonvulsants (gabapentin, pregabalin)<br /></br>Opioid escalation is reserved when adjuvants fail or pain is predominantly nociceptive. Meperidine is avoided for chronic use; NSAIDs help bone/muscle pain, not radicular hyperesthesia.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“NEURO pain needs NE (norepinephrine) boosters.”<br /></br>Think TCA/SNRI whenever pain is burning, tingling, shooting.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>A. Increase oral oxycodone-acetaminophen to 4 tabs q6 h<br /></br>   – Dose escalation can help nociceptive pain but adds constipation, sedation, and no specific benefit for neuropathic pain. Guidelines favor adding an adjuvant first.</p></br><p>B. Add oral hydrocodone-acetaminophen q4 h<br /></br>   – Simply adds another short-acting opioid/acetaminophen combo (duplication) ↑ hepatotoxicity risk without targeting neuropathic mechanism.</p></br><p>C. Add oral meperidine q6 h<br /></br>   – Inferior potency, short half-life, neurotoxic metabolite (normeperidine) → seizures, especially in elderly or renal impairment; inappropriate for chronic cancer pain.</p></br><p>D. Add oral naproxen q12 h<br /></br>   – NSAIDs are useful for inflammatory or bone pain (e.g., metastases to bone) but do little for neuropathic radiculopathy; also ↑ GI/renal risk in older adults.</p></br><p>E. Add oral nortriptyline at bedtime → correctly addresses neuropathic pain with an evidence-based adjuvant.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Take-Home</br>For cancer-related neuropathic pain: keep the current opioid, add a neuropathic adjuvant (TCA, SNRI, anticonvulsant) before escalating opioids or adding NSAIDs.</p>
186. A 57-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by police 40 minutes after he was found wandering around a train station in a confused state. On arrival, he appears drowsy. His temperature is 37 C (98.6 F), pulse is 72/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 135/80 mm Hg. Physical examination shows bilateral nystagmus and a disconjugate lateral gaze. His gait is unsteady and broad based. On mental status examination, he speaks in fragmented sentences and is often incoherent. He can recall zero of three objects after 5 minutes. He is oriented to person but not to place and time. Serum studies show:</br>Na+ 140 mEq/L</br>K+ 4 mEq/L</br>Cl- 104 mEq/L</br>HCO3- 25 mEq/L</br>AST 70 U/L</br>ALT 64 U/L</br>γ-Glutamyltransferase 55 U/L (N=0–30)</br></br>A urine toxicology screening is negative. His blood alcohol concentration is 0 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in management?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Obtain collateral information</br>b. CT scan of the head</br>c. Chlordiazepoxide therapy</br>d. Donepezil therapy</br>e. Haloperidol therapy</br>f. Vitamin B1 (thiamine) therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>f.
